item.title: [YC Root Access] The App That Changed How Engineers Ship Code
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Conductor 的真正瓶颈不是模型，而是人类工作记忆的 7±2 法则

Charlie 提到他最多只能同时管理 3-5 个 agent——这个数字几乎完美对应 1956 年米勒提出的"神奇数字 7±2"（人类短期工作记忆容量），考虑到 agent 比单个数字更高维，落在 3-5 完全合理。

这意味着一件惊人的事：AI coding 的下一个突破口，不在 GPU、不在模型参数，而在认知科学。Conductor 在做的"上一层抽象"，本质上是一种认知卸载装置（cognitive offloading device）——和会计发明复式记账、飞行员发明 checklist、指挥家用乐谱一样，都是用外部结构突破人脑生理上限。这条路径上，他们的真正同行不是 Cursor 或 Cline，而是 Excel、Jira、空管雷达屏。

二、跨领域连接：他们不知不觉在复刻爵士乐队 / 体育队 / 交响乐团的组织学

注意几个反复出现的隐喻——产品名叫 Conductor（指挥），他们用 ultimate frisbee 队作为团队招聘暗号，Charlie 自比 hacker / Jackson 自比 gardener。这些不是巧合。

跨界看：体育队、爵士四重奏、急诊科、航母作战中心，都是研究"高可靠性组织（HRO）"和"分布式认知（distributed cognition）"的经典案例。这些组织共同的核心机制是——异步并行 + 关键节点同步 + 角色互补 + 共享心智模型。Conductor 的产品架构（多个 agent 并行、PR review 作为同步点、skill 文件作为共享心智模型）和这些组织的工作机制是同构的。

更深一层：Charlie 说"AI 工程的最佳实践和与人协作的最佳实践高度可迁移"——这其实不是一句客气话，而是一个强结构性预言：未来 AI 工具的护城河会越来越像组织设计的护城河，而不是技术护城河。会做"指挥家"的公司会赢，不是会写最强 prompt 的公司。

三、被低估的暗线：他们的真正竞争优势是"用自己造的工具造自己的工具"形成的复利环

访谈里有一个一闪而过的细节：他们在做 Chorus 的过程中"为了让 Chorus 造得更快"而附带做了 dev tools，结果附带做的工具比主产品更值钱。然后 Charlie 说"用 Conductor 造 Conductor 让我像在咖啡因上指挥"。

这是创业史上反复出现的同一个模式——Unix 是贝尔实验室的人为了写文档造的，Git 是 Linus 为了管理 Linux 造的，AWS 是亚马逊为了卖书造的。它们共享一个稀有结构：主线项目失败/转向，副产物（工具）成为本体。

这暗示了一个让人意外的判断标准：评估 AI dev tools 公司，最该看的不是 ARR、不是 retention，而是"团队自身使用产品的频率与依赖度"曲线。如果创始人离开自家产品 24 小时就觉得手脚被砍掉，这家公司就有了一种类似生物学上"线粒体内共生"的复利结构——产品和团队代谢已经融合，外部对手很难追。

Conductor 现在正处在这条曲线最早期但最陡峭的一段。这是比那 22M A 轮更值得关注的信号。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Today I'm excited to welcome Charlie and Jackson, co-founders of Conductor, fresh off of a $22 million series A co-led by Spark and Matrix. Guys, thanks so much for joining.

Thanks for having us. Thank you, Aaron.

什么是 Conductor

Yeah, tell us a little bit more. What is Conductor?

So, Conductor is a Mac app that lets you run a bunch of coding agents all at the same time. So instead of being in your IDE editing files or within your terminal uh running cloud code or codeex, conductor consolidates all your coding agents and in one click you can create an isolated copy of your codebase, tell cloud or codeex to go work for you and then you can review and merge its work.

Very cool. And you guys were the first to build kind of this new way of of uh shipping code with multiple agents at the same time within one interface. and it seems like it's really working. Like what's the state of the business right now? Tell tell us some more about um how it's been growing.

Yeah, so we're we're a small scrappy team, but we are growing really fast. So we've grown about 10 times since January and uh uh now we have everyone from like scrappy indie hackers to uh engineers at big public companies using us every day. Um but there's a lot to build and uh uh a lot to uh figure out and so we're excited to grow the team.

发布 Conductor Cloud

Yeah, very cool. And you have some new announcements of things that you're launching today. Tell us more about that.

So, we're very excited to launch Conductor Cloud today. Uh, up until today, Conductor all runs locally on your Mac. So, if you shut your laptop, Cloud Code or Codeex or whatever agent you're using will stop working. Um, but as of today, you can create a workspace that is backed by a cloud environment. So, you can shut your laptop and the agent will keep working for you.

It seems like a lot of people that are using conductor are maxing out how many uh different instances, how many different work trees they can be running at a at a single time. Is this the big unlock that's going to let people get past the bottlenecks that they've been hitting so far?

For me, I think like I can only really manage in my head like three to five agents at once. And I think this is part like partly an interface problem and one thing we're really excited to solve next. Um, I think we we we've proven that you can run more than one coding agent at a time and it will still be productive, but I think to get to like the next level where you run like more than three to five, like we'll need it's an interface challenge and like I think we'll need to like go up a a higher level abstraction to to make that work.

Very cool. Um when when we started conductor like we were trying trying to do this manually ourselves and just running into a lot of friction that made it hard to get past one or two agents and that's like why we built the app in the first place. I think even before that we were trying to build stuff like conductor and um like our very first attempts it was just like too much too early to be able to move away from your IDE. So

yeah.

Yeah, the agents had to like re reach a like sweet spot. There's kind of a tipping point where you didn't have to pay attention to everything they were doing. Um but you had to pay attention to enough of it where that made orchestration and like running multiple at once possible. Um but yeah, in our in our like early experiments with this, we were like we had like five clones of our repo and like we were running a cloud in each one and then then we discovered work trees and we were like oh we should probably be using work trees and like step by step we like started building the primitives like for conductor like to solve a problem for ourselves.

创始人相识：从大学到 YC

Yeah, that's very cool. And um maybe let's go back to the beginning and kind of tell the story of how you guys got there. How did you guys first meet?

Yeah, we met in college. I was a freshman and Charlie was uh fifth year.

Yeah. Yeah.

Which at the time was like is like the biggest age gap possible. Like if you had told me then that we'd be working together, I'd be like

Jackson's a child. Like how like that would be just crazy to me. Yeah.

Yeah. Charlie was uh he he taught me how to lift. Um he's a great teacher, great coach.

Thank you. Finally finally you have have admitted it.

All right. Yeah. I'll I'll never say it again. Um, yeah, you you were trying to convince me to take a CS class with you and I, being a stubborn freshman, I refused. Um, but

yeah, we we reconnected after college basically. Um, and we're working on a bunch of project crazy projects together. Um, Charlie moved out to San Francisco and um, I was working at Netflix on the machine learning infrastructure team. Charlie was at Replicate doing growth and like engineering and we were just working on a bunch of projects together and um uh like wanted to I guess try our hand at a startup and we didn't really know what we were going to do at all but we we got it we had an interview with with you Aaron and we uh decided to do it.

决定一起创业

Yeah.

Yeah. And talk about um deciding to to work together and start something. So you were at Netflix and you were at Replicate. Did you know that you wanted to do a startup? Did you know that you wanted to do something with each other? What was that first kind of conversation of, you know, we should leave these jobs and actually start a startup together?

I think we were having a lot of fun building stuff together. Um, I didn't even have in my head like an idea of what a startup was. I mean, I guess I had some idea, but it was not at all connected to what startups really are. So, I don't I I didn't have like a a dream to do a startup, but I I really liked working with Charlie and he um knew a lot more about startups than me. And he this was something he was interested in and I was um uh looking for a change. I was like working from home in my bedroom and going crazy. And um yeah, I wanted something to sink all of my time into.

I remember uh like reading hacker news at my first job out of college and reading the you weren't meant to have a boss and and being like yeah I wasn't meant to have a boss and so I think like the idea of a startup like working on a startup has been like in my brain for a while. It felt very natural to work with Jackson because we like he said like we were having so much fun building projects together and the idea of just like doing that all the time felt amazing. Um, we had played ultimate frisbee together in college. Believe it or not, it was a very, very serious team and we put a ton of hours into it and um, we're a really good team. A big part of me, and I think it's like a big part of our company now, is trying to replicate that feeling of being on like an elite sports team together and like putting your all into it and working against big challengers. Like in college it was UNCC. Now it now it's like it's it's a bunch of other companies, but um working really hard together on something was like was really appealing.

最初的 idea：AI 订位助手

When you guys decided that you wanted to start a company together, did did you have an idea? Did you know what you wanted to work on?

When we applied to YC, our idea was AI that books reservations for you. Yeah.

And that did not last long.

Well, I remember that your application talked about you could reserve tennis courts and

Yeah.

Yeah. Yeah. And I think in hindsight there were some cool ideas like it was like use we were playing around using Sonnet 35 to like control your browser for you but it was very much a solution in search of a problem. We're like okay it's cool that Claude can like click around in our browser like what do we what do we do with it?

So I remember interviewing you guys with that idea.

Yeah.

Um how married were you to that idea at the time?

I remember like trying to decide how much conviction do we act like we have in this idea? And we we I did I think we did a pretty good job of like thinking it through a lot and like thinking through the like the holes and like Yeah. like the how how it could get really big. It was a bigger challenge than something we had worked on, but it wasn't really like

I didn't feel like in my bones like this was the thing to work on. Yeah.

Yeah.

And as we learned many times in YC, there's not that much you can learn by just thinking through an idea. Once it you go actually try and do it, you find out all your thoughts were wrong or at least we did.

Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And even if someone

Yeah. And even if someone tells you like, "I really want this thing," you can put it in front of them the next day and they're like, "Oh, like my friend is going to love this." Like like it doesn't necessarily translate to like them actually wanting something.

Yeah.

YC Batch：寻找方向

Yeah. I remember that was my impression interviewing you guys for OIC was you guys seemed like incredible engineers that probably had a much better idea in you than the one that you had applied with. And so maybe talk a little bit about um what the the YC batch experience was like for you and and what your main focus was during during that three months.

Yeah, I think probably the first month and a half we were casting around for an idea and um we we tried all sorts of stuff. We were asking our our friends and like building random prototypes and um showing them to people who invariably hated them and like yeah building stuff we knew nothing about.

Felt like every office hours it would be like okay we're trying this new thing.

That's what I remember too. I feel like there'd be times where we'd meet you know every 3 days sometimes and you'd be like all right when we come back in 3 days

we're going to be here and then you come back in you'd be like we have a new idea.

Yeah. Yeah. Well, did you think when you interviewed us it would take us so long to to come up with something?

Well, it wasn't the coming up with something that was was the thing for you guys. It was the finding the thing that was actually going to stick. You guys came up with a lot of idea. A lot of them were were really interesting, too. Um, and I think the other thing that really stood out to me was you guys were just like the fastest builders. You built stuff so fast that you ship things every 3 days, sometimes totally new ideas, way faster than a lot of other companies would even ship an MVP for, you know, their whole thing during the batch. And so that was really impressive to see. So

credit for that goes to Charlie. I think he has just an incredible drive to like get stuff out the door as fast as possible. And I' I've hopefully some of that is rubbed off on me.

Hacker 与 Gardener 的搭档

Yeah, I I think we have a nice dynamic where there's like two archetypes of engineers. I I think of myself as a hacker where I can make something really like I like moving really fast and I can make something that's sort of like duct taped together and like leaves a trail of dust behind it. Uh, and then Jackson I is I I I think of as like a gardener where he will kind of like pick up the broken pieces behind me and then like build it into something um like some something robust. And like that same exact thing happened with like with with conductor actually like where it was like okay I'm gonna build this MVP in like two days and then like show it to Jackson and Jackson was like okay there's there's there may be something here and then

I couldn't we couldn't build the system like without without that back and forth.

Yeah. I remember seeing the first version of conductor and saying like

first Charlie this is not at all what I wanted like you have to change all this stuff and second like this is really impressive I can't believe it works this well like we should we got to be working on this.

转折：从 Chorus 到 Conductor

Yeah. So yeah that's talk about the journey to get there like you went through a lot of different ideas through the batch and even after the batch. Yeah.

Um what was that journey like from your perspective to be able to get to the thing that ultimately stuck?

I I very distinctly remember coming into an office hours like halfway through the batch and we were we were pretty down like I I I was really into BTOC ideas and Jackson like B2B ideas and we had tried like 10 different things at that point and I remember you saying I remember two things. One was um you said um I think DevTools would be a good fit for you guys and then kind of like a light bulb went off in our heads when you said that. And this the second thing you said that we still like have to this day actually is you said uh put up a poster on your wall of your guys' faces and then just say across it at the top make something these guys want and just build something that you guys want. Um and so we literally did that. Um

and then I think from there it actually like that narrowed the scope a lot. We like okay dev tools. Um and we at the time and it's funny this is still a common thread. felt like the AI coding tools weren't going far enough. Um, and so that summer, Sonnet 3.5 had come out recently. Um, we felt like there was like a big overhang in like what was possible and like what people realized was possible. Um,

uh, and so and we also became obsessed with this uh, coding tool called Ader, which was like sort sort of like the grandfather to cloud code. And we actually tried to build a version of conductor like that summer where um we were like instead of editing the files by hand in an IDE or even using like a tab model like you instead the human should be telling the a the AI what to do and it will go off and do a bunch of work and surface it to you. Um and so we tried to make a user interface that was like the thing after the IDE. Um and the problem is it didn't quite work. Uh the models weren't really good enough. Like Sonnet 3.5, you did a bit too much handholding and you would need to open your IDE too much anyway. Um tool calling was like barely a thing back then. We lost a bit of conviction in in ourselves and like in that idea because like it just the models weren't quite ready and like we weren't quite like ready to to build that. We were like, let's take a like we have a bunch of ideas around like how the UX of this thing should work, but like let's let's just apply that to chat. Um because we know that we can make a better chat app than anything that exists. And we had an office hours with you where you were like you can't like you can't pivot until you get 10 people to try something. And so we're like, okay, we're just going to put something out uh and we're going to put out a basic version of a chat app and it's going to let you talk with multiple models at once because that's again that's like solving like a problem for ourselves that we wanted. So we released Chorus in January, so like a few months after the batch actually. Um and then it was in the process of building Chorus and building the dev tools that would let us build Chorus faster that we stumbled upon Conductor.

第一个原型与 "啊哈" 时刻

How long did it take you? It sounded like you built a prototype really fast. Jackson saw it. Jackson was like, "This is not how I would want to use it." So, you were kind of building for yourselves first. How long until you got to the thing that you actually started getting other users to to start testing out and giving you feedback on?

I think it was probably like three weeks from when we wrote the first line of code. And there were couple weeks before that when we were using cloud code a lot and like developing some manual processes around it and thinking like we need a tool for this and like there's a huge opportunity to build something around this.

Yeah. We were like someone else is going to build this like like we need this tool and then we were like wait like we can build it ourselves and yeah I think it was like two to three weeks from like first line of code to releasing it. The first week was Charlie prototyped it all himself and then that worked shockingly well and we're we decided to spend we I think we thought we could ship it in another week and it probably took us two and a half more weeks

to polish it up.

And what convinced you I mean you had you had been through that process a bunch of times with with other ideas and other apps.

What was like the first thing that you saw that actually convinced you that like I think we're on to something here and I think this is going to stick.

I think for me it was the first time giving the agent a task, then pressing command N and giving another agent a task and then I seeing a little like unread dot come up in the first agent and then I like clicked back into it and it like had finished.

Yeah. And I was like like the idea of running multiple agents like even running like one cloud code was pretty new at the time. And so the fact that we could run multiple and it would actually be productive was pretty magical.

Um I mean another litmus test for me is uh because I get really excited by lots of like shiny new things. Another limus test for me was does Jackson find it useful? And it because most things I show him he's like I don't what am I supposed to like I don't there's no I don't know what to do with this but this was one of the few times where he's like wait like this was actually kind of helpful for me.

Um so that was like another like another bar to cross.

Yeah.

Yeah. I think there was also a phase when we in the early weeks developing conductor when we were you it was there are certain problems that are like really wellshaped for conductor where you have a bunch of like mostly independent features or bug fixes you want to do and I think there was a one week maybe when we were like really in that phase and we were just ripping through like so much code with conductor it was exhilarating I think

I think being able to build conductor with conductor was another like magic moment for us because I I was just like not leaving the app and I was just like I felt like yeah I was like conducting on like on a ton of caffeine and and it was actually working.

Um so yeah that was another like signal for us.

病毒式传播的能力：David Attenborough 视频与心法

I mean you guys were the first to pioneer a lot of this running um multiple agents at the same time. Um and so that obviously you know resonated with you immediately. it resonated with a lot of other people, but you also have this other special ability, this knack to of uh, you know, putting out videos that go viral. Um, talk a little bit about where that came from and and maybe any like tips or tricks or secrets that you can share with anybody else that's watching this on on how you think about that.

Yeah, one of my claims to fame is a demo I made where the day GPT4 Vision came out. Um, I wrote a Hackup Python script that uh would record uh my webcam and then watch me in real time and then use a voice clone of David Adamber to like narrate what I was doing as if I was in Planet Earth. And um uh I I just I tweeted that out on a whim and it went totally viral. and even uh Sir David Adam saw it. Unfortunately, unfortunately he didn't love it, which it was sad for me because I I'm a huge fan of his.

Um but um I think part of uh part of how I learned to um to to get attention on Twitter and to like shape posts in a way that could go viral was my role at Replicate was basically this. Um I joined as an engineer, but like our main problem was growth for a while. And so how do we get people to use our API? Well, we have to like let the first they have to know about it. And so like building demo apps and like make like creating like uh videos that like showed you what was possible was like a big part of my job.

I think one thing that that's really important to me is like being authentic and like being direct and not using like corporate speak or not trying to like act like you're bigger than you are or I don't know like saying it how it is like you're talking to a friend. And I think that's one thing that's worked well for me is just like like the David Adamberg video was just like a raw screen recording. I like like I just like showed my screen and didn't make many cuts and like it's just some Python on a screen and like showing you how cool that could be. And I and then keeping the language really direct and straightforward I think is is important. But then I also think like it's like you have to like you have to just have a ton of reps and like put out a bunch of stuff and see what resonates with people and like start to learn like the shape of a post. Um uh I don't know. I don't know if I'm doing a good job explaining this. It's it's some of the advice that I thought that you had that I thought was really interesting was like

observe what you like like what you heart and what you like seeing when you scroll and like observe the the emotions and the reasons for why that is and like try and replicate that.

Yeah, that's yeah that's one nice way of summing it up is like yeah make something that you want to click like on um similar to the YC advice. It's like make something that you want to consume and and like. Yeah.

顶级工程师在做什么

Yeah. That's good advice. You guys, I know you do a lot of uh bike tours around the city to meet with your users and sit down and watch them use conductor. Um and it's it's pretty cool because you guys have a front row seat to some of the best engineers in the world that are literally building on the frontier doing things that have never been possible before. And I'm curious what you've learned in the course of watching some of those top engineers work. Yeah. Yeah.

Like what sets them apart?

Yeah. So I think a couple things. I think one thing that I've noticed a lot is the best engineers are putting a lot of thought into their their skills files. Um, and that's something we try and do as well is like take knowledge that like uh like take React best practices that we've learned like from either talking to people like experienced React engineers or like things that are specific to our codebase and putting those into markdown files that then the agents can continually read from. Um, I think one thing also that's maybe somewhat surprising is how simple and how vanilla most of the best uh the best uh engineers uh uh setups are. Like I think you can go I think you can easily midwe meme here and like put a ton of thought into your configurations and your sort of reminds me of like people who like have amazing Vim setups and like Vim key bindings. they can do stuff like I don't know they can like I don't know they can do all kinds of crazy stuff on Vim but like not necessarily get work done. Um so I think people have surprisingly simple uh setups. Um but I do think putting a lot of thought into your uh skills has been like a recurring thing. And I also think um uh something we are working on something I've seen is having uh what we call like slot free zones where it's like you can let the AI run wild in certain parts of your codebase and then other parts of your codebase are like much more thoughtfully considered and like the human is the architect not the AI. Um but I I think we're still figuring it out like

工程的未来

Yeah. Yeah. I mean everything has been changing so quickly over the last few months even um and and we're even finding you know in the current YC batch the advice that we give is different in a lot of ways than it was even just a few months ago which makes it a really exciting time in what way

um I mean you can build so much more so much faster you know before it' be like to take on a huge you know company that has a lot of surface area in the product it'd be like well what's your tiny entry point that you're going to start with and now it's like you could build the whole thing. So like it has changed a lot for coding and what does engineering look like over the next few years even?

Yeah.

Few years is hard to say.

Yeah. Right. Who even knows at that point? Yeah.

I I do think one thing we feel confident about is that the models are going to get they're going to get 10 100 times smarter. they're going to be able to run for longer without you needing to intervene. Um, I think they're going to start feeling more and more like a human coworker would. Um, although it's like they still will have an alien brain that's very different than ours. I think that's one reason we're really excited about cloud is that uh the agents are going to be able to run for much longer. And um I think uh I do think that like you will we will need interfaces that make you feel like the CEO of a a even a mega corp of where you have like thousands of these AI employees that are working for you and most of the time you're like sitting at your desk like the CEO would and like head of engineering is giving you a report on what they've worked on, head of marketing is giving you an update um and you're staying high level but then once in a while you'll need to like drill down in the detail. s um and that's sort of like what like a PR review feels like now where you're like zooming in um and like asking what like the specific worker had done has done for you. Um

I think that goes back to your question about like what what are the best people doing. It's like they're able to understand the everything that's happening and like go look at what the agent is doing on every level and optimize, you know, optimize away the bottlenecks the agent is running into. And then also like think proactively instead of just reacting to whatever the agent is coming back with like thinking ahead of you know what are the design questions I'm gonna need to um answer to to write this or like what like which which pieces of this do I care about like which constraints do I need to give to the agent.

Yeah.

Yeah. In some ways yeah like the the best people at building with AI are like also the best people at building with other humans like the skills are actually like pretty transferable. Um, and the people who were the best engineers in the pre AI world are likely the best engineers in the AI world as well. Um,

so yeah, there's a lot that transfers.

Conductor 的下一步：更高一层抽象

Uh, what's next for Conductor after the the most recent launch here?

Right now, Conductor runs on your laptop, but the vision is that conductor can run um wherever you are and it's like the it manages all of the AIs that run as part of your organization and like you can hook into that from your phone or your laptop um or your your existing cloud infra.

Yeah, I'm also very excited for like the next level of abstraction like conductor to me like right now is in some ways very July 2025 like that's when that's when we created it and like that's that's that's the state of the models when conductors introduced but I think I'm very excited to go up another level of abstraction and uh uh let you uh control the models from um in a different way than you can and I think there's like a lot of interface product experiments to be done there that I'm really excited about.

Very cool. What do you think that's going to unlock for for your users or for engineers or anybody who's trying to build?

I I think a couple things. I think the first is like I am bottlenecked right now on my like working memory as a human which is like three to five agents max. Um

and I think we can remove that bottleneck. Um I'm also bottlenecked on reviews. Um and uh like we all are bottlenecked on reviews. Like we have to pay very close attention to like each line that Claude is adding. We we maybe differ from some other people uh on this but like we are like try to be quite like considerate about the lines of code added. Um

um and that's a that's a bottleneck. Um and so I think there's like a ton of experiments we can do with reviews. Um, we're still stuck in the 2010 GitHub PR review era and I think like there's uh there's cool stuff we can do there. So,

y

lots of exciting stuff on in the near future.

招聘与团队文化

Yeah, I mean you guys have um changed the way uh engineer engineers build and the way engineering happens to this point and I'm excited to see what you have up your sleeve uh going forward too. Um, you guys are hiring too. talk about like what are the qualities of the the best perfect people to join the conductor team.

Yeah. So, right now everyone on the team has either played ultimate frisbee or is French. Okay. Um so that's uh that that probably helps. Um

but I think um I think people who are very scrappy and either like have worked at a startup or started their own startup I think are are great fits. people who want to move really fast and also like want to be at the frontier. Um, and importantly like people who want to build uh tools for themselves and tools for the best engineers. That's what motivates us. And it's it's it's especially fun to be able to work on the tool that um to use the tool to build the tool and to like constantly be working at a bunch of different levels like working on shipping a feature and also working on like what what is the process that's going in here and how can I like ship that to our customers as well. So

yeah. Yeah.

结语

Yeah. It's super cool. You use the word fun and that's that's the word I was going to use too just cuz uh like that's I don't know. I think that's what resonates off of you guys is you're working on something that you are the user of that is being used by people that are like you that you really care about um and changing the way a lot of people are are building and shipping software which is just super exciting and and what a better time than to be doing that right now, right?

Yeah. Yeah. There's nothing I'd rather be working on and like no one else I'd rather be working on it with. So, it's uh pretty pretty awesome. Well, thank you guys so much for coming in. Um, this was this was great to to hear some of your insights on where things are going and hear more about how you guys operate uh within Conductor. So, appreciate you joining.

Awesome. Thanks for having us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Conductor 的真正瓶颈不是模型，而是**人类工作记忆的 7±2 法则**
Charlie 提到他最多只能同时管理 3-5 个 agent——这个数字几乎完美对应 1956 年米勒提出的"神奇数字 7±2"（人类短期工作记忆容量），考虑到 agent 比单个数字更高维，落在 3-5 完全合理。
这意味着一件惊人的事：**AI coding 的下一个突破口，不在 GPU、不在模型参数，而在认知科学**。Conductor 在做的"上一层抽象"，本质上是一种**认知卸载装置（cognitive offloading device）**——和会计发明复式记账、飞行员发明 checklist、指挥家用乐谱一样，都是用外部结构突破人脑生理上限。这条路径上，他们的真正同行不是 Cursor 或 Cline，而是 Excel、Jira、空管雷达屏。
## 二、跨领域连接：他们不知不觉在复刻**爵士乐队 / 体育队 / 交响乐团**的组织学
注意几个反复出现的隐喻——产品名叫 **Conductor（指挥）**，他们用 **ultimate frisbee 队*

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Ancient Civilization That Rivaled Egypt – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被低估的"文明加速度悖论"

最让人脊背发凉的不是特奥蒂瓦坎本身的宏伟，而是 Reich 顺手抛出的那个时间结构：美洲与亚洲人群在两万年前分离，与欧亚西部人群在四万年前分离——但所有这些分支几乎"同时"在最近 5000 年内独立点燃了纪念性建筑、阶层社会、天文历法。这不是文明的扩散，这是一根长长的生物—文化引信在多个隔离的容器里几乎同时烧到尽头。

这意味着所谓"古文明"并不是某个聪明族群的发明，而更像是一种潜伏期统一的相变：只要人口密度、记忆累积、农业反馈达到某个阈值，文明就会在任何拥有同一套旧石器工具包的人群中析出。换句话说，文明不是被发明的，而是被"等"出来的。

跨领域连接：Denisovan 之谜与软件工程的"幽灵 commit"

Reich 最深的困惑——全基因组说 Neanderthal 和 Denisovan 是姐妹，但工具技术、Y 染色体、线粒体 DNA 都把 Neanderthal 拉向现代人这一侧——其实是一个非常熟悉的结构，只不过出现在另一个领域：

这正是软件版本控制里"分支拓扑"与"功能继承"不一致的经典症状。当你看 git log --graph，A 和 B 看起来是同一个父分支的姐妹；但当你看实际的功能行为，A 和 C 共享了一组关键 commit。结论几乎一定是：有一次未被记录的 cherry-pick 或 merge 把 C 的特性私下注入了 A。

应用回古人类学，这就给出一个相当大胆的预测：Reich 描述的不对称很可能不是 Denisovan 走得远，而是Neanderthal 与现代人之间存在一次比已知 2–3 万年前那次更早、更深、更彻底的"文化—基因联合输入"事件——一次把 Y 染色体、线粒体、石器认知模板打包传递的接触。基因组的"主干树"正确，但有一条"暗 commit"被主干树的统计平均掩盖了。

你可能没注意到的元模式

把上面两点叠在一起会出现一个真正出人意料的结构：Reich 所有的"大困惑"都共享同一种形状——表层叙事干净，底层数据有"多余的相似性"无法被树状模型消化。

  • 文明问题：分离两万年的人群，相似得过分——同时点火。
  • 古人类问题：基因树说是姐妹的两群，不相似的方式很奇怪——技术与单亲遗传系统不对齐。

这暗示一件他可能还没明说的事：人类历史的真实拓扑不是树，而是一张反复短路的网。我们一直在用错误的几何（树）去拟合本来是图（network）的数据，所以"剩余的相似性"才会一次又一次地像幽灵一样冒出来。

如果这个直觉对，那么未来十年古 DNA 领域真正的革命，不会来自更多的样本，而来自彻底放弃"系谱树"作为默认表征——就像物理学放弃以太、生物学放弃严格的物种边界一样。Reich 现在的"perplexed"，其实是范式裂缝的第一道光。

📝 转录文本

特奥蒂瓦坎带来的震撼

We were — we should go for a a trip to Teotihuacan in Mexico, and it's like — like so impressive. Like, you know, I — when I went there when I was 20, you know, it's just like — it's totally as impressive as as ancient Egypt. You know, it's like huge. It's massive. It's without metal. And it's — um — it's even more impressive because it's not only without metal, but it's without animals and without wheels. Which is crazy, like the — the — the — the — the — the marble is just like — Right. — hauled without wheels. Right.

Like, take any person who has like an old world superiority and like take them to these places and they will not have it anymore. It's just extraordinary what's in these places.

长引信：人群分离与文明的同步点燃

And these are people who separated 20,000 years ago at least from the ancestors of East Asians, and 40,000 years ago from the ancestors of West Eurasians. And, you know, just had the same biological, you know, cultural shared toolkit from then, but there's just a — fuse — a long fuse delay until all this stuff happens. It's kind of like an amazing thing and we don't question it.

下一组大问题：古人类与现代人的关系

What are other questions you have that — um — peop- uh — yeah, you're — you're either have — are investigating right now or want to investigate, these kinds of big picture questions of human history?

I think that I'm — I mean, I'm — I'm perplexed. I don't know if we talked about it before, but like I remain very, very confused about the relationships between archaic and modern humans.

可用的数据：Neanderthal、Denisovan、现代人

We have genome sequences now from archaic humans who lived in Europe and the West Eurasia and Central Eurasia — Neanderthals. We have archaic sequences from these en- enigmatic Denisovans, who we now have uh a skeleton for, um, since we last talked. There's now a a skull from a Denisovan that's been shown to be a Denisovan. Uh, and we have data from lots of modern humans.

Uh, and there's really big mysteries about the relationships amongst these groups.

全基因组的故事：Neanderthal 与 Denisovan 是姐妹

So genetically, the Denisovans and the uh Neanderthals are sisters. They descend from a common ancestral population 5 or 600,000 years ago, and that group descends a couple hundred thousand years before — 7 or 800,000 years ago — from the common ancestors of modern humans.

And so genetically the whole genome data says that Neanderthals and Denisovans are archaic humans from a common ancestral archaic population.

但还有另一组证据：Neanderthal 与现代人之间的奇怪共享

But there are so many things shared between Neanderthals and modern humans that don't seem to be shared between — with East Asians.

  • They both share Middle Stone Age stone — stone tools level of technology. This uh cognitively unique type of way of making stone tools that wasn't used in East Asia.
  • They both have the same mitochondrial DNA and Y chromosome sequence. So the Y chromosome sequence of Neanderthals, the mitochondrial DNA of Neanderthals is actually modern human. That came through interbreeding 2 or 300,000 years ago and then shot up to 100% frequency.
  • And then Neanderthals and modern humans are both the product of mixture events that happened between archaic and modern humans 300 or 200,000 years ago, as demonstrably — through patterns of variation in ancient and modern DNA.

困惑所在

And so it feels that there's something shared between Neanderthals and modern humans that's not shared with Denisovans, even though the — vote of the whole genome says that Denisovans and Neanderthals are related.

So one wonders whether there's something connecting kind of Neanderthals and modern humans that's different from Denisovans, even though genome-wide Denisovans and Neanderthals cluster.

So I'm thinking about that all the time now.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被低估的"文明加速度悖论"
最让人脊背发凉的不是特奥蒂瓦坎本身的宏伟，而是 Reich 顺手抛出的那个时间结构：**美洲与亚洲人群在两万年前分离，与欧亚西部人群在四万年前分离**——但所有这些分支几乎"同时"在最近 5000 年内独立点燃了纪念性建筑、阶层社会、天文历法。这不是文明的扩散，这是一根长长的**生物—文化引信**在多个隔离的容器里几乎同时烧到尽头。
这意味着所谓"古文明"并不是某个聪明族群的发明，而更像是一种**潜伏期统一的相变**：只要人口密度、记忆累积、农业反馈达到某个阈值，文明就会在任何拥有同一套旧石器工具包的人群中析出。换句话说，**文明不是被发明的，而是被"等"出来的**。
## 跨领域连接：Denisovan 之谜与软件工程的"幽灵 commit"
Reich 最深的困惑——**全基因组说 Neanderthal 和 Denisovan 是姐妹，但工具技术、Y 染色体、线粒体 DNA 都把 Neanderthal 拉向现代人这一侧**——其实是一个非常熟悉的结构，只不过出现在另一个领域：
这正是软件版本控制里**"分支拓扑"与"功能继承"不一

item.title: [Lex Clips] Should programmers learn how to code in assembly? | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、汇编不是"语法"，而是一种几乎消失的"口语传统"

最反直觉的观察：Kieran 把汇编教学的失败归结为它被当成"语法书"来教，而真正有效的传承方式竟然是铁匠学徒制——师傅手把手带徒弟。这意味着今天这个看似最"机器"、最"形式化"的编程领域，本质上却是人类最古老的口传知识体系(oral tradition)，和荷马史诗、苏菲派音乐、日本刀匠传承同属一类。

这里隐藏的系统性真相是：越接近硬件的知识，越依赖人类身体性的传递。 因为它不在于"规则"，而在于一种对处理器的"手感"——Henrik 能背出每代 CPU 每条 SIMD 指令的 cycle 数，Martin 用手机虚拟键盘在公园里写 ARM 汇编。这是肌肉记忆与心智模型的合一，正如外科医生、爵士乐手、围棋九段。LLM 学不会汇编不是因为数据少，而是因为这种知识的载体从来就不是文本。

二、"重写"是程序员的认知陷阱——一个跨领域的深层规律

Jean‑Baptiste 抛出了一个被严重低估的命题："写代码比读代码容易一个数量级"，所以新人本能地想重写。这不是软件独有的现象，它是一个普适的认知规律：

  • 历史学里叫做"切斯特顿的栅栏"(Chesterton's Fence)：看到一道不知道为什么存在的篱笆，先别拆。
  • 生物学里叫做"垃圾 DNA"的乌龙：曾经被认为无用的非编码序列，后来发现承担着关键调控功能。
  • 城市规划里 Jane Jacobs 反对的"现代主义推土机"：用一张漂亮蓝图取代一个人们看不懂但运转良好的有机系统。
  • 组织管理里新 CEO 上任就重组架构的失败模式。

所有这些都共享同一个底层机制：理解一个复杂适应系统的成本，远高于推翻它再造一个干净抽象的成本，但被推翻掉的恰恰是不可见的"暗知识" (tacit knowledge)。 Rust 重写 coreutils 的"最后 1% 要花 99% 的时间"——这其实是 Pareto 原则在面对历史沉淀时的另一种表现：那 1% 不是技术债，是 40 年来用户在边缘场景里教给系统的智慧。

三、最让人意外的洞见：Rust 的"安全"在汇编面前是个接口幻觉

这是整段访谈里最锋利、也最少被讨论的点：你把 C 改写成 Rust 拿到了内存安全，但只要底层还有手写汇编（比如 dav1d/H.264 这种性能关键路径），汇编可以"跳到内存任何地方"——整个安全模型在抽象边界处瞬间崩塌。

这揭示了一个普遍的系统性真相，我称之为 "安全的最弱环路径定律"：任何安全保证只与栈中最不安全的那一层等价。这和：

  • 密码学里"端到端加密但你截屏发到群里"是同构的；
  • 金融风控里 SOX 合规但 Excel 宏后门也是同构的；
  • 航空安全里飞控冗余三套但维修工忘了拧一颗螺丝也是同构的。

更深一层的"啊哈时刻"是：Rust 社区贩卖的"内存安全"叙事，在性能敏感领域其实是一种营销学意义上的安全，而不是工程意义上的安全。 Jean‑Baptiste 提出的解法——"compile‑time checked assembly"——才是真正的系统性思维：不是把不安全的语言换掉，而是把验证机制下沉到不安全语言所在的那一层。这和 seL4 微内核用形式化证明守住 C 代码、而不是逃避 C 是同一种哲学。

一个附加的隐藏连接

Kieran 用"驾驶喷火战斗机"比喻汇编——飞行员的操纵杆物理连接着副翼。注意这个比喻的精确性：现代 fly‑by‑wire 飞机里，飞行员的输入会先经过计算机"修正"才到达控制面，这正是高级语言；而汇编是直驱。

所以汇编之美和"模拟胶片之美""黑胶唱片之美""手动挡之美"是同一种美——消除中介层带来的责任感与亲密感。这不是怀旧，这是人类在自动化加速时代对"我还能直接接触现实"这件事的本能珍视。在 LLM 即将让所有人都通过自然语言间接编程的当下，汇编的复兴可能不是技术现象，而是一种反抗中介化的文化现象——和年轻人重新买胶片相机、写信、做手工咖啡，是同一波潜流。

📝 转录文本

一、为什么传统汇编教学失败了

"There's this nice repo with the assembly lessons. First of all, do you think developers should learn how to program in assembly? And how would you go about learning it? What is this ASM-lessons?"

So, I personally wasn't happy with the way assembly is taught in books and online. Cuz it's very grammar focused, and you don't in general learn a language from learning the grammar and the structure. You learn a language by asking someone what their name is, and you start from there and you go and solve real problems that you have when you want to communicate. You don't learn sentence structure — "this is the interrogative and the adverb" — and all the assembly books seem to be doing like that: going through every instruction, even ones that aren't really relevant, explaining what they all do, and how they actually doesn't really change much.

And the other problem that we have in our community is assembly is taught sort of hand-to-hand, like person-to-person, like blacksmithing, one-by-one. That's the only logical sort of analogy, and that doesn't really scale online. It doesn't do other things.

二、FFmpeg 风格的汇编课程

So I started a set of assembly lessons in the way it's done in FFmpeg, which is a little bit different to the way assembly in general — for, I don't know, I'm trying to think — the other good big use case of assembly is in embedded devices, in really really low power cheap devices, and that's completely different to what we're doing here.

I think it would be good if you could highlight the requirements, which are quite simple. It's high school mathematics and C. And actually, not even C — really really, is pointers.

To emphasize: yes, we've talked about how brilliant this stuff is, but high schoolers like Daniel Kang have written assembly in FFmpeg. I think there's been contributions because of these lessons. So, it's really about trying to get this dying art to continue, because we've shown it's possible with David to produce something amazing.

There's still a lot of codecs in FFmpeg that are only maybe partially assembly optimized. And so it really starts with basics and continues, explains a lot of the jargon, a lot of the syntax. It doesn't really try and explain to you, you know, interrupt handlers and interrupt instructions and all of these different jump targets. It actually makes this really vector focused — and describes all kinds of registers, general purpose registers, vector registers, really nice examples.

三、汇编之美：像驾驶喷火战斗机

This is cool. It's a classic example of FFmpeg. Some of this assembly language is really beautiful, and I think it's beautiful because it's kind of like flying a Spitfire. It's really aviation at its purest, but also pushing the aircraft beyond what the designer thought was possible. So we're abusing, for example, sometimes cryptography instructions to do certain things, and there's a level of beauty and art where it's really you and the processor.

There's nothing in between. It's you and the joystick of the cockpit, and you move that joystick and it's physically connected to the ailerons. And you can push that plane beyond what it can normally do, and there's a level of —

[gasps]

— yeah, beauty and amazingness to go that.

But I don't think the sort of person-by-person assembly that — someone taught me, and I've taught multiple people — is going to work long run, because of the particular flavor and the way that we do it. It's literally now, I should going to say, wizards handing it down. I realize I look like a wizard wearing this hat. But yeah, basically just like the sages, the wise sages, handing down the craft.

四、LLM 能学会汇编吗？

"Can I ask about LLMs? Like, can they help?"

They had more of an understanding than I expected, but they are still — I've asked it questions and it still goes and starts hallucinating, not hallucinating but making modifications, and then I go: "is it bit exact?" "No, fix it." And then it just goes and does the same thing. There isn't the corpus of information like Stack Overflow to work on.

"There is not enough data to train on."

And this is the biggest issue.

五、Itanium 的故事：为什么汇编仍然重要

I started my career actually doing some assembly for Itanium, right? So the Itanium is a dead processor type, which was done by Intel and HP a long time ago when they wanted to do 64 bits well — they lost, and then we got AMD, who did AMD64, which became X64.

But Itanium was extremely interesting in the sense that those were processors who had a ton of computing power to do floats, FMAs, which is similar to what we need now for LLMs, right? And you could pack three operations per line that could be loaded. So basically you had an output of basically 6 billion of operation per seconds. But the bus, the memory bus, only allowed 1.5. So your CPU was four times faster. So you had to do crazy things to pack things in memory, reuse the registered, and those type of semantics no language could do that.

So I have the Itanium programming book — because Intel did amazing books — but that's exactly what Kieran says: if you don't know what you're going to do, it's impossible to read. It's a ton of jargon and so on, while those lessons are amazing because they are targeted to a real problem and you can do it yourself. And people have. People have their patches and they say: "Oh, I studied your lessons and here's my first changes." That's amazing.

六、X86Inc 与"理解汇编让你成为更好的程序员"

And part of that in the lessons is a framework called X86Inc, written by Laurent, who was working on X264, and it allows you to do more things about that — to create a type of like, not caring too much about different calling convention. And we had a lot of students who gave code to X264 using that a long time ago.

So it's really doable, and I believe it's necessary to understand assembly language even if you don't do it much, to understand what's going on inside your computer, and that will make you a better programmer. And I assure you that, because doing that you will understand some of the architecture of the memory inside your computer, right? Understanding register, L1, L2, L3, RAMs, SSDs, disk, and so on, which are very important, because then you have a good programming culture that will make you a better programmer.

七、关于 Rust：两种不同的看法

"What do you think about the Rust programming language? Cuz that's a bit of a meme."

We have very different opinions with Kieran.

Kieran 的批评

I think it's valuable what they're doing in terms of memory safety as a concept. Can it achieve some of the speed up that assembly achieves? Not assembly by hand, no. I think that's a given. C, potentially. But I see it — it has a very big Esperanto vibe about it. It's like "we're going to solve this and we're doing this in a particular way."

"Meaning it's a bit too utopian?"

There's a lot of focus on the self-importance rather than solving real-world problems. It reminds me of the Sinclair C5. Sir Clive Sinclair of Sinclair computers built a car. And he said, "Oh, everyone will be traveling around in one of these electric cars," and it first reminds me of that — where I think the community doesn't quite understand that in order to get people to move, you have to build something that's as good as if not better than what you have now.

Yes, people are doing Rust rewrites, but if they only do 85, 90% of the feature set of what we need, like things like coreutils — that last 1% takes 99% of the time. To use Elon's famous quote: "Prototypes are easy." Like this kind of stuff is easy. But to get a real electric car, you have to make a car as good as if not better than what we have now, and Rust isn't in that stage yet.

I don't think anyone would object to seeing Rust code in FFmpeg, but it needs to work as well and support the same unit testing as everything else. It needs to be flawless. It can't just randomly break. They can't just randomly break ABI when they want to. It needs to have — I think it still has only one compiler implementation. Yes. So it's got to be as good as if not better, and saying "hey, here's my utopia of memory safety" isn't enough, even though we probably all agree that that's the goal.

Jean-Baptiste 的视角：VLC 与 Kyber

So I've done a ton of Rust, and the two major topics I had was adding Rust modules inside VLC. One of the reasons VLC got popular — and which was one of the main architectural decisions — is that VLC is a very small core and a ton of modules. And so you can write modules in C, C++, in Objective-C, and anything that is basically interoperable with C. And so we did some Rust modules. So I have experience on that, and I wrote some of it. And also, my new startup called Kyber is an open source project mainly done in Rust.

What Rust is extremely good in, in the sense that, it's a better C++ that cares about memory and allows you to do things about memory ownership that no one else can do so far. However, it's great when you start a new project from scratch and you do everything in Rust. But it's very not good when you interrupt with existing part. And some part of the Rust community believe that they need to rewrite everything, and everything will be better with Rust. And the answer is: no.

八、不要重写：写代码比读代码容易一个数量级

Like, I'm almost always — in all my years of being engineer, manager, CTO of startup and so on — don't rewrite.

"Right. Is that the initial instinct for a lot of people when they show up to a code base, probably before LLMs — probably because they don't understand the wisdom of the way things have been done in the past — to say, 'Well, we need to rewrite it.' Hence why there's a thousand JavaScript frameworks."

But the reason is the following. And this is very important to understand: it is an order of magnitude easier to write code than read code. And you see that also with LLM. They can write code; analyzing is a lot more difficult.

And so when you arrive to a very complex piece of code, you don't understand it, because it's so much more effort to understand the code from someone else, because you don't have the sole process. And often I joke about some languages, mostly Perl for example, which has very complex syntax. And imagine I am at my maximum intellectual efficiency in programming, and I do write the best code ever — I will not be able to understanding myself 6 months later, because reading code is more difficult.

So very often you arrive, you don't understand all the wisdom, all the business logic, the reasons that were done, that is maybe not documented. And you say, "Well, I'm going to write it." And the thing is: no, you don't. Because, that's as Kieran said: "I'm going to rewrite coreutilities in Rust." And then of course you arrive very quickly at 80%, then 90% takes a bit more time, and then you got the last ones.

On the other side, for new projects, it's great. Everything related to parsing files, networking — because of the memory borrow checker — it's amazing, and there is nothing else.

九、Rust 与汇编的安全悖论

To answer a bit differently for us: imagine I take a piece of software like David or H.264, which has a ton of runtime in assembly. I rewrite the C part in Rust. So it's more secure. Yes, but then you arrive into the assembly, and you can jump anywhere in the memory, because we are doing handwritten assembly. So even if I rewrite the C part in Rust for security reason, you break all the security when you write handwritten assembly, because we can jump anywhere.

So in my opinion, we need to do something that is secure assembly — which is compile-time checked assembly, which is similar to the "checkasm" project that we're doing on David and H.264 with VideoLAN: to start instrumenting your assembly at compile time to check that it's not jumping anywhere in the memory. Because else, you might rewrite a part of C in Rust, but if you want to have the same performances, you're going to have inline assembly, and so you destroy your whole security model.

So that's a bit what I think about Rust.

十、优化的喜悦

I just want — I would say on a personal level, I'm so in awe about the assembly. It never gets old, the speed improvements to show 62x. So there are months — but on a personal level, I run our internal test suite at work and just see, I'm still in awe at the gains we have.

"There's a source of joy and happiness programming for different reasons. But I think one of the greatest happinesses is in optimization of code. And it sounds like you're like at the cutting edge of that."

That was cool.

十一、汇编界的两位巫师：Henrik 与 Martin

In the community, I want to speak about two people who are wizards of assembly. The two of them are actually working/living in north of Europe — Sweden and Finland.

Henrik Gramner knows so much about Intel x86 assembly that when we ask questions at Intel about things, they tell like, "Why are you asking us, Intel? You have Henrik. Henrik knows better." He knows all the cycles of almost all the SIMD instruction by all the CPU generation. "Oh yes, this is a P4. This is a Nehalem. This is a Core 2," etc. That person is like the best person on assembly in the world, and he's the nicest person that you've seen. He arrives, you don't see, and he's amazing.

And the other one is called Martin Storsjö, and he's doing mostly the same on ARM — so Neon, and iPhones and Androids and so on. And he codes in assembly on his phone, editing it with the crappy virtual keyboard you have, while watching his kids play in the playground. Like, this is just like wizard level.

十二、x86 vs ARM：架构的复杂性

"So apart, when you're programming assembly at that high level, a part of that is knowing the architecture that you program on. On ARM in particular, yes."

ARM in particular. But x86 — I mean, these are complicated architectures, right?

Yeah, but in some ways, x86 with out-of-order execution is not so bad. ARM, you really need to understand all the different generations of ARM processor, cuz they're all different. There's A72, etc. etc. And there's the Apple variants, there's this variant, there's that. And you need to write code that works efficiently on all of them.

x86 — but broadly speaking, you have Intel, AMD, you have sub-variants, but generally speaking, something fast is going to remain fast on all of the variants. Whereas in ARM, it's a completely much more complicated ball game.

观看原视频 →

最反直觉的观察：Kieran 把汇编教学的失败归结为它被当成"语法书"来教，而真正有效的传承方式竟然是**铁匠学徒制**——师傅手把手带徒弟。这意味着今天这个看似最"机器"、最"形式化"的编程领域，本质上却是人类最古老的**口传知识体系**(oral tradition)，和荷马史诗、苏菲派音乐、日本刀匠传承同属一类。
