item.title: [Lex Clips] Video compression explained: H.264, AV1, ProRes | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、视频压缩的本质：一场关于"时间"的赌博

最让我意外的洞察是——B帧（双向预测帧）的存在揭示了一个反直觉的真相：为了让你"流畅地看到现在"，解码器必须先偷看"未来"。显示顺序与解码顺序的分离，意味着每一段视频流其实都在进行一场微型时间旅行：编码器在过去预言未来，解码器在未来回填过去。

这其实和人类记忆的"重构性"惊人地相似——神经科学早已证明，我们的大脑并不像录像机那样存储完整画面，而是只存储"关键帧"（情景记忆锚点）和"预测差值"（基于上下文的填充）。每次回忆，都是大脑在做一次类似 H.264 的解码：用极少的真实信号，重建出连续的"主观体验"。视频编解码器，本质上是把人类感知系统的偷懒机制，工程化成了硅片上的算法。

二、被忽视的"调参文明"：编解码器之外，藏着一整个隐形产业

转录中一个轻描淡写的事实其实是行业里最大的秘密：Netflix、YouTube、Meta 雇佣的成千上万人，不是在写编解码器，而只是在调参数。 这是一个非常奇特的劳动形态——他们既不是程序员，也不是研究员，更接近"数字调音师"或"压缩品鉴师"。

跨领域的连接更耐人寻味：这与葡萄酒酿造业的"调配师"（Master Blender）几乎是同构职业——同样的原料（H.264 标准是公开的，就像葡萄品种是固定的），但最终成品的差异完全取决于一组无法言传的"风味曲线"。一个手机随手拍的竖屏视频、一部 4K HDR 大片、一段 PowerPoint 屏幕录制——它们需要完全不同的"压缩配方"。这暗示着一个反直觉的产业真相：在算法时代，最难自动化的工作不是创造算法，而是为算法寻找"合适的人格"。

三、Intra Refresh 与"忒修斯之船"：一段永远没有完整自我的视频

最隐秘的彩蛋藏在 Intra Refresh 这个技术里——一段视频流可以从头到尾没有任何一个完整的 I 帧，整个画面是通过"每帧刷新一小块"逐步累积建构出来的。换句话说，画面里你看到的"完整图像"，从来没有在任何一个瞬间真正存在过——它只存在于解码器的累积记忆里。

这是技术版的忒修斯之船悖论：如果一帧画面的每个像素都是在不同时刻被替换进来的，这帧画面究竟是在哪一刻"成为"它自己的？

更深的连接是——Kyber 这类实时视频通话系统大量依赖这种技术，意味着我们和远方亲友"面对面"的视频通话里，对方那张"完整的脸"在数学意义上从未一次性传输过来。我们每天都在和从未真正存在过的图像交流情感。这不仅是工程奇迹，更是一种现代版本的"在场即缺席"——本雅明若活在今天，他的《机械复制时代的艺术作品》恐怕要重写一章。

四、最后的循环：VLC 的"轮回"作为软件考古学样本

转录末尾那个被当作笑话讲的细节其实极具历史厚度：Google Video 时代用 ActiveX 把 VLC 塞进 IE 浏览器，二十年后人们又用 WebAssembly 把 VLC 编译进 JavaScript 虚拟机塞回浏览器。形式几乎一模一样，只是中间隔了一整个 Web 范式的死亡与重生。

这是一个绝佳的"技术轮回"样本——证明软件世界的演进并非线性，而是螺旋式的。每一代工程师都以为自己在创造新东西，实则在用新底层重新实现上一代被淘汰的旧需求。下次你看到某个"革命性"的新前端方案时，不妨问一句：这是不是又一个被换了皮肤的 ActiveX？

📝 转录文本

五大主流编解码器与容器组合

We can lay out, let's say, the top five codec/container combos would be: H.264 inside MP4 containers, AV1 inside MP4/WebM containers, and ProRes for nonlinear editing inside containers.

So for people who don't know, I guess ProRes is...

It's Apple's codec for editing, originally for Final Cut Pro, and it's designed to be fast to decode, fast to seek, because an editor will need to move very quickly. So it's a different use case to the distribution element.

There's no or very minimal temporal compression.

There's none here. There's none — so you can cut. So you can do cuts.

This is what we call intra-only codecs. Right?

I 帧、P 帧、B 帧 —— 编解码的三种角色

So I'm going to explain quickly what is IPB frames.

Yes please.

Um, so I-frames — often key frames — but is complete frames. It's like an image. It's a JPEG, right? You can start, you see everything, right?

Um, and then the next image can be a P-frame, which is a predicted frame. So you take some part of the previous image, saying "well, I need the block five and seven and 42," and you replace it and then you just give the extra information, right? But that means that in order to decode this P-frame, you need to have access to the previous I-frame, right?

And then of course you have more complex ones which are B-frames, which are be-predicted frames, which can depend on different types of frames — some in the past, some in the future.

Um, and so ProRes is an intra-only codec — for the people who can see, this is a very good one, right? So I-frames are complete frames, um, P-frames basically depend only on I-frames, and B-frames can depend on in front.

And this GOP — group of pictures — I think the default for actually FFmpeg for H.264 is like 250… 50, a frame, something like this.

Yes.

"魔法般"的预测链：几秒一次完整帧

And to me it's just — it's like magic. Like, that you could predict, you can have a complete frame every…

Several seconds, that means…

Several seconds, and then you could still — you could have this chain of predictions you make. And the fact that somebody like me can use FFmpeg to compress something and not notice that the result still plays back smoothly is like magic.

You can even have — and we use that in tons on Kyber — is what we call intra-refresh, where basically there is no frame… you have one at the beginning and you never send an I-frame.

How does that work? What is it?

You build up an I-frame gradually across, as the stream continues.

So… so you refresh certain parts of the image?

But so you never have an I-frame like this. This is intra-refresh that we use, right?

真正的脑洞炸裂：依赖未来的 B 帧

But for me, the biggest mind-blown when I started was the B-frames.

Yeah.

B-frames means B-predicted frames can depend on frames that are coming in the future.

Mhm.

That means that in order to decode this B-frame, you need to wait for the next frame that is dependent…

Buffer that, decode that one, so that you can decode the B-frame. Right?

So the way you decode the frame — the decoding order is not the same as the display order, right? That means the encoder needs to be very clever and decide that, "well, you know, I'm going to depend on things like in the future."

So this is like mind-blowing.

Yeah. The fact it works so smoothly every day is kind of miraculous in some ways.

It works. So you can have a stream that works across the world on their decoder versus one in the US versus one here, of different manufacturers, and they produce bit-for-bit exactly the same material. That's quite remarkable — and do quite complex things and getting more and more complex, and still be bit-exact. There's a lot of work that goes into that.

FFmpeg 的旋钮宇宙：参数即艺术

There's a lot of knobs you can control in this whole process. So there's a lot of really fascinating parameters that I've gotten to know more and more over the years that FFmpeg gives you complete access to. Maybe you can speak to some of them.

So first of all, obviously we can lower the resolution. We can lower the frame rate. We can use different kinds of codecs as we mentioned, from H.264 to AV1. There's ways to tune the tradeoff between bit rate and quality, as we kind of spoken to. You know, you could do constant bit rate, you can do constant quality, CRF, QP. You can do the longer or shorter group of pictures (GOP) that we mentioned. I mean, all that kind of stuff. It's crazy. Number of B-frames.

Yeah. What is crazy is that a ton of people's job is to optimize those parameters, right?

A ton of people that you see at YouTube, at Netflix, at Meta and so on — they're not writing codecs. They're just finding the right parameters for the file they have, for the format they have, right?

Because something that is for a movie, or something that is user-generated content from your phone, or a screen recording, or something that you're going to video-edit — you don't want the same things. And there are thousands of people whose job is just to optimize all that.

YouTube 作为"网页版 VLC"

Yeah, they're wizards. Hats off to them.

Uh, YouTube, like — to deliver all the streaming sites actually, to deliver at scale — and like YouTube is really magical, because it's not just doing like what Netflix does, which is one-way, like broadcasting-type thing. It's also has to upload videos from all the places. So they're also doing encoding at scale for videos that are going to be watched by like five people, and it still has to deliver them on a moment's notice. No delay, nothing. No late… I mean, very minimal latency. And also serve it in all different resolutions.

Like, YouTube is basically the web version of VLC. [laughter]

历史的回环：从 ActiveX 到 WebAssembly

Well, actually, it's funny, because like Google Video, which was something they did before they acquired YouTube, was actually using the VLC plug-in so that you could run VLC inside the web browser using the ActiveX plug-in. And so it worked in Internet Explorer, and you were actually running VLC inside your browser.

Which is funny, because today we have the opposite — where we have VLC WebAssembly, where we compile all VLC and FFmpeg to decode, to run VLC inside the JavaScript virtual machine, um, with WebAssembly.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、视频压缩的本质：一场关于"时间"的赌博
最让我意外的洞察是——B帧（双向预测帧）的存在揭示了一个反直觉的真相：**为了让你"流畅地看到现在"，解码器必须先偷看"未来"**。显示顺序与解码顺序的分离，意味着每一段视频流其实都在进行一场微型时间旅行：编码器在过去预言未来，解码器在未来回填过去。
这其实和**人类记忆的"重构性"**惊人地相似——神经科学早已证明，我们的大脑并不像录像机那样存储完整画面，而是只存储"关键帧"（情景记忆锚点）和"预测差值"（基于上下文的填充）。每次回忆，都是大脑在做一次类似 H.264 的解码：用极少的真实信号，重建出连续的"主观体验"。视频编解码器，本质上是把人类感知系统的偷懒机制，工程化成了硅片上的算法。
## 二、被忽视的"调参文明"：编解码器之外，藏着一整个隐形产业
转录中一个轻描淡写的事实其实是行业里最大的秘密：**Netflix、YouTube、Meta 雇佣的成千上万人，不是在写编解码器，而只是在调参数。** 这是一个非常奇特的劳动形态——他们既不是程序员，也不是研究员，更接近**"数字调音师"**或**"压缩品鉴师"**。
跨

item.title: [Business of Sport] West Ham on the Brink, Are Sports Teams Just Brands & The WNBA’s Big Cash Play
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个看似无关的故事，其实在讲同一件事

表面上，这期节目讨论了三个独立话题：西汉姆的财务崩塌、布里斯托熊队 CEO 的"营销公司论"、WNBA 的薪资谈判。但若把它们叠在一张图上，你会发现一个让人意外的共同结构——它们都是"所有权 vs 故事权"的不同变体。

  • 西汉姆的危机本质上不是财务问题，而是"谁在为这个俱乐部付钱"这个问题正在悄悄变更答案。Sullivan 的钱、Křetínský 的钱、Rights and Media Funding 的贷款、球迷的会员卡——四股资金在拉扯，而每一股资金都在主张不同的"叙事所有权"。
  • Tom Tatton 说"我们是一家踢橄榄球的营销公司"，球迷愤怒的根本原因不是这句话粗鲁，而是它等于公开承认：球迷不再是产品的最终买家，品牌商才是。
  • WNBA 球员要求大幅涨薪，本质上是在说："Caitlyn Clark 的现象级增长是我们创造的故事，不是 NBA 母公司施舍的。"

三件事都是同一个问题：当一项体育资产的"价值创造者"和"价值收割者"开始分离时，谁有权重新分配蛋糕？

一个让人意外的观察：West Ham 的"廉价租用奥运场馆"是一个被错估了二十年的"成功案例"

主流媒体一直把 West Ham 用 4 百万英镑/年租下伦敦碗描述为"史诗级好交易"。但 Charlie 在节目里用一组数字悄悄拆穿了它：

  • West Ham 上 Upton Park 末季的"比赛日 + 商业收入"合计 5500 万英镑 → 进入伦敦碗后涨到 9500 万（+73%）
  • Spurs 同期建新球场后，同两项收入暴涨到 3.09 亿英镑（+242%）

也就是说，West Ham 省下来的"建场成本"，其实是被埋进了"未发生的收入"里。这是一个典型的会计可见 vs 会计不可见的陷阱：人们能算清楚省下的 8 亿造价，却算不清楚没赚到的 2 亿年收入。这个洞察可以延伸成一条普适规律——凡是被反复称赞为"轻资产模式"的行业典范，往往都在隐藏一笔沉重的"机会成本债"。Netflix 不建影院、WeWork 不买楼、Uber 不养车，每一次"不拥有"都既是解放也是天花板。West Ham 的 Olympic Stadium 是体育版的 WeWork：在牛市里看起来是杠杆，在熊市里露出的是脆弱。

跨领域连接：体育俱乐部的"农场主困境"，其实是 AI 时代每一家公司都在面对的问题

Tom Tatton 的"营销公司论"和 WNBA 的"Caitlyn Clark 经济学"放在一起看，揭示的是一个比体育大得多的命题——资产价值正在从"基础设施"迁移到"叙事生产"。

  • 在传统模式下，俱乐部/球队是"农场主"：拥有土地（球场）、农具（青训）、雇佣劳动力（球员），收割稳定的农产品（门票、转播费）。
  • 在新模式下，俱乐部更像"内容平台"：自己拥有的最有价值的东西不是球场，而是注意力的分发权。Caitlyn Clark 之于 WNBA，就像某个超级 IP 之于 Netflix——平台真正卖的是她能引来的人流。

这和当下科技行业的"模型 vs 应用 vs 数据"之争是同构的：

体育 AI
球员（叙事生产者） 模型/明星工程师
俱乐部（基础设施） 云服务商
联盟（注意力市场） OpenAI / Anthropic
球迷（情感持有人） 用户社区

WNBA 球员要求重新分配 50% 收入，和 OpenAI 出走研究员要求股权一样，都是"叙事生产者"对"基础设施持有者"的反向定价。Roger Mitchell 称之为 "fugazi"，但其实这是所有从硬资产转向软价值的行业必经的估值再校准期。

最有趣的预测是：如果 Tom Tatton 的逻辑成立，那么三十年后的 Bristol Bears 应该长得像 MrBeast 而不是像 Leicester Tigers——一群以橄榄球为内容载体的创作者，比赛只是他们最长的那条短视频。这听起来荒诞，但仔细想想，曼联早就开始这么做了，只是它自己还没好意思承认。

📝 转录文本

West Ham 濒临深渊、体育俱乐部只是品牌吗，以及 WNBA 的现金大戏

开场对话：周末的连环坏消息

"Three to four years ago, West Ham were doing more winning than most and now they've been doing more losing than most." "You've got a very unhappy group of fans. You've got an ownership who well far from endeared to that group of people. They have a stadium that they don't love playing in. The execution of all of this doesn't seem great and that seems to have filtered onto the pitch." "The WNBA returns this week for their new season. The recent exponential growth has been very heavily linked to one player, Caitlyn Clark. TV audiences shot up during her involvement. When she got injured, they harved again. How much of this explosion interest is driven by one player?" "Bristol Bears CEO Tom Tatton saying that Bristol needs to be thinking like a marketing agency that plays rugby. This is the club that bought Elona Maha to the UK last year and it's reflected in their attendance levels which are really high." "What I was a little bit worried about Charlie is that this poor chap came in here have his ears filled by you with a whole bunch of stuff about sports clubs are really marketing and content agencies and he thought that sounds great and he's gone down there and then had buckets of crap pull all over his head and he's sat there at home going never listen to Charlie Stevens again."

Good morning Charlie. Well, sorry, good afternoon. Just after 12:00. How are you? It is. It's um unusually late in the day for us to be recording, but um, was rather hoping to record this on a Wednesday. It's our new stretcher after a lot of feedback from our um listeners and viewers that they want us to record as close as possible to the release date so we don't miss stuff. So, I was looking forward to recording on Wednesday this week, but we're here on a Tuesday because you've unfortunately got some dental surgery tomorrow, Charles. I do, yeah. I cracked a teeth the other day, which um — I was speculating that this teeth crack — Yeah. I was speculating to myself, I have to say, um, that this was a result of grinding your teeth. I looked at the football results over the weekend and um, uh, I have to say that they were peculiar tuned to make a Chelsea fan grind their teeth.

一连串让 Chelsea 球迷抓狂的比赛结果

Should we — should we just line it out? Just just — well, I shall line it out. The first result that would have sort of really got you going would have been — was it the Tottenham one? No, Arsenal. Arsenal. Ars— Yeah. So, Arsenal winning — Fulham an absolute embarrassment to the league for that. Yeah. Arsenal winning, um, nice and cozy, nice and comfortably. No great nerves around the Emirates. All smooth sailing to go um London forever — to go six points clear at the top. But then of course you still had the the joy of of anticipating the demise of of Tottenham — playing away at Villa um, which I think you know could broadly be expected to have been a um a Villa win or the very, you know, the very best of draw maybe. Just — it was lined up, wasn't it? It was. It was absolutely perfect for you. I was — I was worried though. I was going into that game because Villa this falling in between Villa's two European games against Notting Forest and then having lost that first leg 1-nil. I did think there's a high chance that — and they were terrible and they were dreadful. Actually, oh my goodness. I — I was watching it and I thought, is this the worst Premier League performance I've seen this season by a team? Well, you haven't been watching Chelsea recently. I'm sorry — that's jumping the gun because yes after — after Spurs finally — found some form of for back-to-back wins. I mean — back-to-back wins. I mean, disaster. Yeah, both — both of them slightly streaky, but Villa were absolutely terrible. So that was the second in the trifecta and of course the one which was always nailed on was that Chelsea would get um, would get mashed again — by — by Nottingham Forest second streak — by Nottingham Forest reserves. Josh Pedro did score a hell of a — hell of a bicycle kick to to be the first goal scored in 52 days or something I think it was by Chelsea in the Premier League. So uh, that was a, you know, nice silver lining. Um.

So — but — but you're saying that all of these terrible football results weren't the reason why you ended up with cracked teeth. No, I'm not. And then you've also forgotten Everton decided to turn up against Man City. So yeah, which of course then in turn means that Arsenal are more likely to win now that City have dropped points in a game that they would been expected to get all three points. So really, all in all — So four matches — in fact, you probably count in West Ham being beaten as well. Yeah, West Ham being beaten as well. We'll come on to West Ham in a moment. But if you were to start off that weekend, look at those five games and say, "Right, from the from the Stebings um perspective, these are the five results that he's going to want." It's quite unusual that all five would go the opposite way. It was like — it was like a mad accumulator. Okay. So, anyway, I should have done that, shouldn't I?

关于牙齿、护齿套与一段插曲

Good luck with your surgery tomorrow. You want to try one of those mouthguards that people use at night? If Chelsea carry on like this, you know, one of those protective mouthguards, but when you grind your teeth at him — I thought you were going to say, "I've been punched or something after Chelsea leads to Wembley and and that was the real reason." Uh, but no. I'm — don't worry, everyone. I'll be all right. We'll be — we'll be in good shape. I just didn't want to record with half a numb face and risk — although there would have been the upside of you not being able to speak — of just me having a one and a quarter monologue, which I know — difference, which I know some people think wouldn't be much much of a difference there. But just despite all this sporting misery, we both did watch the world snooker final last night, didn't we?

斯诺克世锦赛决赛：跨越时代的对决

Fantastic, wasn't it? Having covered snooker a few weeks ago, um, I paid a lot more attention to this whole tournament than I have done over the last few years and the final didn't disappoint, did it? It was really, really compelling. It was one of the all-time greats. Down to the last — down to the last frame and with — with two um compelling but very different characters. So, you got Shawn Murphy, the veteran English player who first won the title — in fact, the only time he won the title in 2005 as a very young man. And since then, if you read his Wikipedia entry, you'll see that, you know, he's had an up — up and down personal life. He'd been through an awful lot during that time. And this was the moment 21 years later to win his second world championship playing against the 22-year-old Chinese sensation Wu Yiza, um, who is, you know, probably the most attacking player that snooker has seen since Hurricane Higgins all those years ago.

一支让人会心一笑的香烟

And um, unlike some of the other Chinese players who are a little bit impassive, Wu Yiza wears his heart on his sleeve a little bit more. And there was one little detail which I particularly enjoyed.

And of course, tobacco advertising has been banned in all sporting events. It was an absolute disaster for snooker because it used to be the Embassy World Championships. It used to be the Benson & Hedges Masters. And all those years when the the the tobacco giants have had to sort of watch these great championships happening without being able to push their product — and all the snooker players who who they know love tabbing.

It turned out that when Wu Yer had been going through a bit of a bad trot in the run-up to the mid-session interval, that his cure for this was not a little session with his coach, was not a sort of little sort of, you know, cold tower around the head. He nipped out the back of the crucible for a cigarette, and this is what pulled him back three frames in a row after that — which I think is great, obviously as a — as a keen gaffer myself. Um, I'm very appreciative when — when certain sports stars demonstrate that it's not necessarily a bar to sporting performance.

Didn't Ben Stokes supposedly do it in the shower before the Super Over in the 2019 World Cup? Really? He went out to bat. So, you know, when — um, when England tied with New Zealand and they basically have to go out and have one over's worth, he supposedly — was uh in the shower getting a quick hit of confidence. I think it just sharpens the focus a little bit. People think of smoking as being something to make you relax. No, it's something — sharpens the focus a little bit. It's always a bit of a surprise to people who go and watch golf tournaments live as opposed to on TV to discover how many of the golfers smoke. I always remember like, um, didn't Darren — again, Darren Clark, I think, uh — I can't remember if it was when he was winning the Open, but he used to have the occasional one. Well, he used to be quite keen on cigars, as as in Miguel Ángel Jiménez. But you have very keen tabas like Ángel Cabrera. Um, and in fact, more recently there was a little bit of a sort of, um, contr— with Charlie Hull, the um, the English female um golfer, who, um, I think there was some golf tournament where smoking was banned and she was up in arms about this saying, "It's a key part of my — key part of my game." Interesting. Yeah.

Wu Yize 与中国斯诺克的全球化时刻

Um, so anyway, go Wu. And it was interesting because it was really interesting — someone who watched a lot of snooker as I have done over the years — this is the first Chinese player that the Crucible crowd really got behind. You know, the the the younger elements of the Crucible crowd. There was a lot of cheering for — for Wu. And that's what makes me think this guy could be about to become a global snooker superstar — the first one really from outside the UK.

Um, because he — he seems to engender — I think it's the recklessness of his play together with the fact he's has quite expressionful face and is quite sort of wears his heart on his sleeve more than a lot of Chinese players. And and his backstory is just terrific, isn't it? You know, where — what — what he did, what he's done to make it, what he and his family have done to make it.

We've had a few conversations over the few weeks about the importance of Chinese athletes establishing themselves in broader markets. And so this is, you know, there's no better opportunity to to test that out. No, if — if I was Barry Hearn watching that — obviously Matchroom being the controlling um entity of um World Snooker — I'd have been rubbing my hands, rubbing my hands at that one.

第一大主题：West Ham 站在悬崖边上

Excellent. Um, big day today, Charlie. We got a lot to get through. So, I think uh, we should dive into the first major topic that we were going to talk about. And I was kind of a bit nervous about doing this because, um, naturally as we know, a lot of the football clubs we talk about on the show generally don't go on to do great things. And I would quite like this football club to do some good things in the last few weeks of the season.

西汉姆的处境：庞大却脆弱

Uh, but after the 3-0 loss to Brentford for West Ham at the weekend, I think it's kind of inevitable based on a number of things that have been happening. And a thought I had a couple of weeks ago when looking at the table and really trying to understand as West Ham and Spurs really separated from Nottingham Forest and Leeds — the teams above them that were starting to break away — like how on earth these two teams had put themselves in there.

And I actually think West Ham have kind of got off a bit lightly because of the position Spurs have been in and everyone just focusing on them. This is, um, this is a club, this is a huge club. This is a club that — is that came, you know, 20th in Deloitte's money league. Um, so they have strong revenues. They have a 60,000-seater stadium — more to be said on that. They have spent hundreds of millions in transfer market and yet they have found themselves this season in particular in in pretty dire straits.

But actually, you know, they weren't brilliant last season as well. So we're — we're looking at kind of a — a gradual decline having had some what you would class as really good years and great success um within the last five or six years when David Moyes was manager and winning their first trophy in what — four decades or something, wasn't it, with the Conference League. So a team that I think is really important for us to discuss in the context of the end of the season and actually have a look at why this may have been happening or what some of those signs were.

球迷顾问委员会的不信任声明

And I wanted to kick it off with a statement. We do love a statement and we've been short on them, but a statement from the fan group who um basically there's a fan advisory board, and last year they um — there was a vote of no confidence and they released a statement around the vote of no confidence in the board, in Karen Brady and Sullivan's ownership um and it went something like this, and it doesn't really sit on the fence so so we should enjoy it. Everyone get your cup of tea ready.

It goes: "We expressed concerns around the forthcoming season. We were told we were overreacting to initial results and the club insisted in a 1,300-word statement reveling in what they saw as success — the promising and bright future led by Graham Potter. Two weeks later, that self-indulgent nonsense was rendered totally irrelevant. We are now approaching a new year mired in a relegation battle on our fourth manager in 18 months and seeing leaks from the usual sources that the forthcoming accounts will show a £100 million pound loss. The common view is one of a club currently in crisis, mired in the relegation spots, a revolving door destination for managers and about to confirm a disastrous set of financial results. We are less than 3 years on from European triumph, 3 years of UEFA cash, the Declan Rice transfer money and of course shortly before the massive Křetínský-derived capital injection. This is not a good position for our club to find itself in. We believe in facing the facts and understanding what can be done to improve things for all supporters and custodians alike. Comparisons with the 2003 sleepwalk to relegation are becoming far too frequent for comfort."

So that was released just before Christmas. And if you see there, they're prophesizing a few things now that even four months later we can see are actually true. They are deep in a relegation battle. As of recording today, they are one point from safety behind Spurs.

We'll get on to where we think the money that Declan Rice — they received for Declan Rice — and how that's influenced the cash is. But again, to my point, this is actually not long after some real moments of success for the club in the last few years. So all in all, you've got a very unhappy group of fans. You've got an ownership who don't — well, are far from endeared to that group of people. They have a stadium that they don't love playing in. You have a relatively solid business opportunity, but the execution of all of this doesn't seem great. And that seems to have filtered onto the pitch.

球迷声明的意义：不仅仅是输球的牢骚

Well, I think that's a great summary, Charlie. Um, and I think I thought that statement was excellent, by the way. Um, I'm generally sort of can get a little bit sort of pompous about um, fan statements which are really based around results. Um, because I sort of feel that it's, you know, dressing up unhappiness about your team losing as something much broader than that — when the fact, the inevitable fact is that all teams go through patches when they do lose — do lose matches.

And the reality about league football, pyramid football, is that for all that some teams win, there have to be other teams that lose, and sometimes you're the ones that win and sometimes you're the ones that lose. And as you pointed out, three to four — three to four years ago, West Ham were doing more winning than most. And now they've been doing more losing than most. Um, and therefore, you know, anything which is really based around, "Hey, we're not very good at the moment" would sort of get my sort of upper lip sneering.

Um, but that's not actually what they're saying there. What they're saying there is that the the losses on the pitch are a sort of net outcome of other bad things and are just seen as part of a bigger picture of other bad things. Um, and they're putting it in the context of not just a few matches here or there, but 6 months, 12 months, 18 months, 24 months, and so on and so forth — and they're pointing at financial and business realities which when brought together with a bad run of results could end up — and we'll go on to it in a moment — in a disastrous cocktail of business failure effectively. Because when in football you get the two coming together — when you get a really bad financial situation combined with very poor performance on the pitch leading to a material change in your divisional status — you can end up in very bad situations. And we've been discussing that with Leicester City in in recent times.

And you're probably right in saying that the the sheer size and scale of the Tottenham debacle has meant that West Ham have sort of flown under the radar a little bit in terms of people asking questions about how does this happen. You know, if I had, you know, £10 quid for every article I've read about Tottenham's demise and why that happened and how that happened, I'd be, if not wealthy, then very comfortably off. But West Ham has sort of, you know, not sort of slid under the radar, but it's sort of been rather in the shadow of that failure.

拆解数字：£104.2M 的亏损是怎么来的

Um, but as you say, the failure is — is quite considerable. Last season's um accounts — that they had revenue of £227 million and made a £104.2 million loss. Okay.

Um, so effectively you've got a club that is revenue-wise in that sort of upper mid table of the Premier League, right? So, you know, that £227 million by the way was £42 million pounds less than the previous season because you saw no European football and you saw the fact that, you know, Declan Rice's transfer had happened and and so on and so forth. So they're now coming back down onto what you might call their base revenues.

When you take out an extraordinary transfer fee, when you take out European football, what are they doing? They're doing £225 million quid of revenue, which is still, by the way, a pretty good number. Um, and that should place you firmly in the mid-table, upper mid table of Premier League clubs. And as you say, even at that kind of number, you're you're edging on to the top 20 in the world, top 25 in the world in terms of total club revenue. So there are other clubs with revenue much less than that and with wage bills much less than West Ham who are considerably outperforming West Ham.

So how have they ended up making this enormous loss? Well, you know, first of all, let's have a look at the wage bill. The wage bill is — you know, bar shouting — around about £100 million quid, right? Playing wage bill. Um, now there's all sorts of stuff on top of that and how these things get calculated, I'm afraid, is not always absolutely precise depending on — are you counting in agents' fees, are you counting in bonuses, are you counting in national insurance and all this type of stuff. But let's just bother shouting say £100 million quid.

And you've got, um, you know, clubs like Brentford and Sunderland who this season have significantly outperformed West Ham who are down more at £70 — £80 million quid on the same types of measures. So the level of underperformance on the pitch has been quite considerable. Not at the level of Spurs — and we have to just sort of mention that and sort of put that to one side and say, okay, Spurs is spectacular because their wage bill is more up £160 million, right? But nonetheless, for £100 million quid a year within the Premier League, you would expect absolutely to be able to achieve a mid — mid-table status.

转会失败：六个中卫，没有一对组合

they haven't made a profit out of those two players. No. Okay. And so I had some total numbers — There — lot — been a lot of failures as well. Mass— Oh my goodness. Do you remember? Move that to striker. I mean, they spent about £30 million on Gianluca Scamacca. Do you remember? He scored three league goals and then went back to Italy eventually for a significant loss. I think it was about £17 or £18 million. He — he went back, um, scored against Chelsea this year playing for Atalanta. So clearly a good player. Niclas Füllkrug, I think they signed for £27.5 million, and now he's on loan at AC Milan who supposedly now are not going to um take up an option to buy him, and he's been injured pretty much all the time he's been at West Ham.

So a performance side — the total numbers reportedly approximately, again depending on what — what's included — was £450 million spent in the last four years, £295 million received. So a net outlay of minus £150 —

including Declan Rice — and that is including Declan Rice — you know, which is a different matter when you — when a player's come out the academy.

Declan Rice 这笔钱掩盖了真相

Because we actually have to talk about — you know, this — this Declan Rice deal, because it is fundamental to a lot of the broad financial story. Declan sold for £105 million to Arsenal out of the academy, right? That's recognized as pure profit. So a great sale for a club.

Yeah, last year — um or sorry, the previous year. So the accounts that have just been released — the year before that, which Declan Rice's um transfer was included in, the club posted a £57.2 million profit. Now, you can imagine turning around going, "Oh, look at this. This is great. We're in — we're in great Nick." No, you're not. You're not. You've had — you've had the football lottery play a lovely — a lovely hand for you. And well done. You've you've still, you know, you've raised a player. You've had him in your team, captaining your team, delivering a trophy, and now you — you've sold him for a huge price. But you can't build a business model. You can't build a business case on being brilliant business people and successful business people on that one deal.

So that £57.2 million is an outlier. Now, as you said, we're back into that kind of recalibration. The thing that I'm looking at that's really concerning around this in the transfer strategy as well is the amount of um amortized transfer fees that they've still got to pay over the next year or so. It's like over £100 million. It's about £110 million, I think. And I also saw in this that there's the small matter of £204.3 million pounds due to creditors within the next 12 months in general.

So really, you're — you're starting to look at a very, um, poorly executed performance strategy from a player transfer perspective, and you're looking at really no development of broader commercial strategy that has put the club in a position to deal with some of these challenges. And if that doesn't happen, we know there's there's really one lever you can pull as a club, right, to generate significant revenue. And you'd be looking straight at Jarrod Bowen. You'd be looking at Crysencio Summerville and maybe Mateus Fernandes. You'd be looking at those major players saying, "Right, [ __ ] we got to extract some serious value relatively quickly." And if it's the players that get hit, then of course the broader ambitions of the club and the fans and and what that group of um supporters want — that gets hit as well.
