So well, okay. Okay, so let — let's take if they were to stay up and they were then looking at next season and thinking, "Right, we got to try and be competitive next season," then it's extremely difficult to then sell your few successful players because you're basically almost guaranteeing a relegation the following season — particularly if, like is the case at West Ham, you don't have any track record of being successful player recruiters. So if you do have three or four really good players on your books, if — if you are Brighton or Brentford and you know that behind those players you've got another four, five, six, seven, eight options either to buy or else players you've been bringing through quietly who you acquired for not very much money who are sort of ready to replace them — that's one thing. But you bet your bottom dollar that's not the case at West Ham.

仪表盘上的红色警告灯：£124M 贷款

Couple other red flashing lights on the dashboard. They've taken out a loan, right? And this loan is a big — big loan. It's a £124 million pound loan that they've taken from an entity called Rights and Media Funding, right? So that was taken out in this year — in the 24/25 accounts — and during that one year they paid £19 million worth of interest. Okay. So a £19 million pound interest payment for that loan, which effectively was covering the losses that we've been talking about here.

Interest, right? That £19 million in interest — that's what you'd get for winning the Europa Conference League. Yeah. By February of this year — okay, February 2026 — £90 million of that £124 million pound loan had been drawn down, right? You got to assume it's been drawn down because they needed — as opposed to putting it in a bank where it would be earning less interest than if it had been stayed up there. So, fundamentally, you've got a business now that is highly leveraged with significant amounts of credit. Okay? So that they're paying interest. They owe money to, you know, Rights and Media Funding. They owe money to other clubs for players that they've purchased off them in the past and so on and so forth.

降级会发生什么

And in the event that they do get relegated — and by the way, I think it's 7 to 2 on, something like that. So, it's currently looming as being highly likely they do get relegated — their core revenue, which this year is at £230 million. Okay, you're probably looking at a diminution of that.

Why do I say that? I say that because, you know, when we're looking at Spurs, Spurs' stature and size of fan base means that even with a 60,000 seat stadium, on relegation to the Championship, broad consensus was that they would still fill their stadium. Okay? Because there's an awful lot of people at the moment who can't get into the Spurs stadium even despite how badly they've been playing. There's effectively a waiting list. Okay? The London Stadium — that's not really the case. I mean, there are games when it's not absolutely rammed and certainly that you can see empty spaces and this kind of stuff. And it's not a stadium that's much beloved either, as you mentioned earlier on. It's not a stadium where people are going to say, "Right, I — even though we're in the Championship and we're playing against, um, you know, Preston North End, I really want to go and spend my Saturday afternoon at London Stadium."

So, I put a big — I put a big asterisk against their match day revenues which this year were about — or sorry, in this set of accounts were about £45 million — and I think in the Championship that might end up looking more like £25–£30 million. Obviously, the media revenue, which previously might have been, you know, from the Premier League might have been £110 million, and then previous to that when in Europe £140 million — that's going to come down to your first year parachute payment, which is, as everyone knows, more around the £50 million mark. So again, you're seeing a £60 million pound hit there. You're seeing a £20 million pound hit there. Commercially, always very hard to know what the contracts look like and what the relegation clauses look like and so on and so forth, but you'd imagine there'd be a very significant hit there.

So it's hard to think that that baseline revenue of £230 would not decrease by at least £100 million quid, right, in the first year in the Championship.

伦敦碗：被高估二十年的"廉价租约"

So the question then is — what about the rest of the cost base? Now, if you look at things like the stadium deal, for instance — now everyone's always said, "It's an amazing stadium deal." And it is, because they didn't have to go and build a 60,000 seat stadium, which might have cost them a billion quid or £800 million quid. They've been able to rent it because it's the old Olympic Stadium. For those who aren't from this geography, effectively what happened is after the Olympics, there was no constant ongoing use for the Olympic Stadium. And effectively, West Ham did a deal which was a — it was a complex deal actually, but but fundamentally for about £4 million quid a year, they get the usage of a 60,000 seat stadium, which is what has enabled them to push up their match day revenue from what it used to be at at Upton Park.

Now £4 million quid might not feel like a lot in the Premier League, but once your revenues start getting squeezed and your match day revenues start getting squeezed, actually £4 to £4.5 million quid is quite a lot when you come down into the Championship —

and you also still don't own your stadium. And this is where like this whole notion — I agree, as you said, I think we drunk on this narrative is a great deal and and yes, we're not going to push back on that to an extent. But you know, not having to build your own stadium isn't always like a great thing. Spurs did it at a time. They got some very, very good debt. I mean, brilliant deals on their debt. So I think they're paying about between £25 to £40 million a year, let's say, on that debt.

But as part of it — and this was really interesting to me — so if you think about what building a new stadium and having access to that infrastructure does generally — you're looking for commercial revenue to move a needle and obviously match day to move a needle. So the London Stadium —

and non-match day — and non-match day — and non-match day huge, right? Huge. which West Ham obviously aren't seeing the benefits of because the London Stadium is taking that from, you know, the NLB games they host there or the Sidemen game or whatever.

West Ham's last season at Upton Park — the combined match day and commercial income was £55 million. Last season it was £95 million — a 73% increase. Okay? Tottenham, by contrast, earned £309 million more from the same two revenue streams in 2024/25, which was a 242% increase on their last season at White Hart Lane. So while West Ham grew revenue by renting the Olympic Stadium cheaply, Spurs built their own and created value worth hundreds of millions more like this.

Yes. And and great, we didn't have to spend loads on it. But at the time, if as a football club, if you could get the financial structures, if you could get the right debt structure, there was still a really strong argument for them to build a stadium. This London Stadium challenge, I think, actually is the underlying thing. It sits at the heart and we're not West Ham fans and I'd love, you know, for West Ham fans to properly have some — some commentary on this. But to me, it certainly feels just like that underlying bubbling kind of tension maybe is the best word within the club. Unhappiness. Unhappiness. And we'll move into Karen Brady because she's just announced she's leaving.

Karen Brady 的离任与权力真空

Yeah. So Karen Brady, um, Baroness Brady, as she has been for a little while now. She's been um either chief executive or executive vice chair of West Ham for 16 years. Okay. So that spans all of this time when the stadium move was being thought about — the eventual move from Upton Park to the London Stadium, the last however many years it is that they've been in the London Stadium. Is it about 10 years? Something like that. Um, around about that. Um, so she's been very much the the the day-to-day, week-to-week figurehead of the club when the club was owned by David Gold and David Sullivan.

Now, as a triumvirate — David Gold, David Sullivan, and Karen Brady — first come together around about 30 years ago when Gold and Sullivan bought Birmingham City and put a very youthful Karen Brady in as the chief executive back in those days.

The youngest ever managing director of a UK limited company, right? And it was funny because, you know, their manager was Barry Fry and the whole thing was very uproariously funny. Um, but you know, they were reasonably successful and eventually they sold out of Birmingham and then subsequently bought West Ham.

Now they bought it at a good price in inverted commas. Um, they bought it back in an era when valuations were just starting to to rise, but they had not yet fully — fully — full — fully risen. Um, so we're talking about — and I'm trying to remember off the top of my head — it was somewhere between £70 million quid and £100 million quid. It was that type of thing. Um, which at the time people was quite toppy for a club like West Ham, but of course in more recent times, the club's been valued at more like £600, £700 million.

Um, and that was part of — billion actually. Well, I mean in some people's dreams maybe even a billion, but I think in reality if you spoke to people in the industry and said, "You know, what would you get for a full set of West Ham," people were talking more about £6 or £700 million for all the reasons that we've spoken about — you know, they don't own their own stadium. Their revenues aren't as big as the big, you know, the very biggest clubs you're talking about. Like Everton — Everton went for that — these kind — these kinds of sums, you know, etc. — is that type of mid-table established Premier League club.

Křetínský 与"狮身人面像"

So effectively, um, Gold and Sullivan — and Gold passed away um about 3 years ago, I think it was — and his shares are now held in trust by his family. And you know, they effectively have all quote done well out of their ownership of West Ham. If you look at the total amount they put into West Ham and then compare it against what their shares in West Ham are worth today, they are ahead of the game. They've done well, but they're not miles ahead of the game.

And I think this is the moment at which potentially things do start to bite. And Karen Brady's exit from the club um a few weeks ago after those 16 years points to — for me at least suggests, or points to — potentially a slight changing of the guard. Um, because she was very much Sullivan's person, go-to lady.

And what you've seen in the meantime is about 5 years ago a Czech billionaire called Daniel Křetínský bought into the club, buying shares from the existing owners. And the original — I think shareholding was about 25%, 27%, something like this — and he had a put and a call option, right? And w— with Sullivan. So effectively what that meant was is that at a subsequent point Křetínský could either take full control by paying a certain amount to the the the existing owners, or potentially the existing owners could say actually we now insist you take control — and those are then valued at different amounts. The put option will always be valued at a lower level than the than the call option. So you you get the right incentivization on both sides. For whatever reason, didn't exercise that.

But what has happened more recently is that as um the Gold family trust has become less of a factor, Křetínský and Sullivan have been increasing their shareholdings — and there's now a big question mark over who is going to be in the future the majority shareholder at West Ham. And the truth is just nobody knows.

Yeah, it's a lack of clarity. Like there's no transparency around it. Křetínský is known in his general business career as the Sphinx — and that's nothing to do with his football track record. That's how he is perceived as doing business. Impassive. No one knows what he's thinking. No one knows what he's doing. Sphinx. Yeah. The Sphinx. That's his business nickname, right? Um, and so no one knows what he's thinking, what his motivations are, what his time scales are, what his plans are.

Um, actually no one really knows what David Sullivan's plans are. He's got his two sons heavily involved in the club. That's Jack Sullivan and David Sullivan Jr. They're both directors of the club. Jack Sullivan's running the West Ham women's team and so on and so forth. And of course Karen Brady was very much his lady. Does Karen Brady leaving look like Křetínský starting to flex his muscles and saying, "I want more say over the running of my club because frankly, you know, of the £104.2 million pound loss, pushing on for £30 million quid of that is Křetínský's own loss." Okay. So, he might well be Sphinx-like in many ways, but there aren't that many billionaires I know who enjoy losing £30 million quid.

与 Spurs 不同：降级会真正动摇根基

Um, so there are — there's a lot of question marks out there, but I think the one thing which can't be questioned is that relegation to the Championship would be pretty seismic for West Ham, because the extent to which their um sort of the TV revenues form obviously a very large percentage of their overall revenues — almost 50% — and their match day revenues which are, I think, significantly predicated the fact that they are playing in the Premier League against these big — big other clubs — will they fill that 60,000 seat stadium at quite a high rental price against much smaller clubs?

So, can I ask you a question? Because when we talked about Spurs, we actually said, "Look, aside from the embarrassment and the shard and fraud of everyone else, as a business, as an actual club, like relegation for one year isn't the end of the world, right? If they get back up straight away." For West Ham, you think if they go down immediately, like from from year one, they've got some real challenges. There's a — there's a real danger that it can unravel in a Sunderland or Leicester type way if they don't get a very, very quick grasp on some of these broader issues.

I think the big difference is um that you know you've got Křetínský lurking there in the background who has a very valuable stake. And it seems to me unlikely that he's going to allow his stake to become significantly devalued by West Ham spending a lot of time outside the Premier League. So the question is — is, you know, how does that power — I don't know if we — don't even know if it is a power struggle. For all we know, they all get on brilliantly well. We just don't know, right? Because it's all opaque.

But if you look at the situations with Leicester City where the owner's home company King Power has been suffering corporate stress, and if you look at the situation at Sunderland where there was a single owner who had already lost an awful lot of money and who decided effectively, "I don't want to — can't lose any more money on Sunderland" — you had a situation where effectively you had ownerships who are no longer in a position to c— to keep the party going.

The question here is — is — are — is Křetínský and maybe Sullivan, but is Křetínský in a position to say, "Right, let's reset and fund the club properly and heavily, and yeah we are going to lose a lot of money but we're going to reset and we're going to go again." Or is there a power struggle going on behind the the scenes by which Křetínský thinks, "I might be able to get this quite cheap because the valuation of the club might come down quite a lot and the losses might be too much for Sullivan and Co to really keep up with — at which point I might be able to dilute them." We just don't know.

中型俱乐部，大型俱乐部的开支

All we do know is — you got a club which is basically a mid-table Premier League club that for a number of years now has been trying to behave like a Big Six, Big Seven, Big Eight club in terms of the transfer fees they've been spending. Right? So if you think about those clubs of similar or just lower revenue levels who are trying to pick off the £10 million pound — £5 million pound signings that they can then sell for £50 to £60 to the bigger clubs — West Ham, and probably Aston Villa, and Newcastle, are the clubs who have been trying to say, "No, we are the big guys. We're the guys who go and spend £50 to £60 million on a player," as opposed to the guys who are buying cheap to then sell high.

That having failed, um, and with the liabilities that that has brought with it — and there's definitely been a reputation in the market over the last few years that West Ham's transfer business has been very agent-led. They've been — you know, that it's definitely not been a club that's been renowned for data analytics or for super smart scouting. It h— the reputation is — and I can't say the reputation is definitely true, but the reputation of the market is — it has been quite agent-led.

Yeah, but it's also just chaotic because there's so much upheaval, inconsistency around a manager's position. I mean, you just look at the way the leadership of the club on the pitch has also operated. You've had managers — I mean, well, Moyes, Lopetegui, Potter, now Nuno — in a very short space of time. When you have lots of different football philosophies — we've discussed it before — all coming in, all clashing — that's basically three or four different transfer ideals and ways of wanting to play. So managers are coming in, they're picking up players from a previous regime, they don't like them — you've just signed them for £35 million quid — what do we do? You know, there's just a further — ev— further evidence of that instability. And when you've got all of this chaos happening behind the scenes, then the overall picture is not going to be very good.

Karen Brady 的 2010 年承诺

I mean, look, I — I thought it was quite interesting looking at the broader um Brady piece is, as you said, like an indication of of of where it's at. And um I'd just like — like to say, look, firstly, I think, you know, there's a huge amount to admire about her. She's done an amazing job in in many ways, uh particularly at Birmingham. Um, and you know, West Ham fans will have their own views, but there's still been an increase in, you know, 300% increase in revenues of the club since she's been there. They have moved into a stadium and we've talked about that, you know, that that is um —

and they won a European trophy — and they won a European trophy and that's where it's culminated in.

But at the beginning, um, in 2010, she wrote in her newspaper column — I don't know if you saw this — to West Ham fans:

"I'll make a single pledge: while we're on the board, we will hang in the Tower of London before your club again goes through the financial turmoil which so nearly brought it down."

Well, I mean I — looking at the time of departure — she oversaw the stadium deal that looks increasingly short-sighted. She made a solemn promise about financial stability that spectacularly failed to keep, and leaves the club potentially heading into the Championship with £24 million in short-term debt and a squad being stripped for parts. That was a very bold statement. But in in all seriousness, you know, when you actually do highlight it like that, that's been a — a failure.

Yeah, and and and what it points back to is — you can get an awful lot right on the business side of a club, but ultimately if the football strategy isn't isn't right or isn't executed correctly, you end up coming a-cropper anyway. You need both to be right. Um, and and you can probably get away a little bit better with a great football strategy and an average business operation than you can with a great business operation and a poor football strategy.

Um, yeah, would be a really good example. Um, so I think just just looking at it right now — it everything's in flux. Um, is increasingly probable they do go down. I think the next few months, West Ham fans and the rest of us actually will be looking very, very closely to see what's happening behind the scenes. Who's actually going to run the club? Who's going to step forward and say, "Right, I'm basically the owner. I'm, you know, I — I either own more than 50% or for whatever reason I have assumed control of the club. I'm appointing a chief executive. There's going to be a proper recruitment operation and all these types of things." I think that that's what the next stages should look like. If they don't look like that and if there's no obvious movement after a potential relegation, I think that's when, as a West Ham panel, I'd start to get seriously worried.

工资占比的简短计算

Can I ask you one quick question? I meant to ask it earlier. You mentioned wage bill at £100 million. Yeah. And revenues at £230. So that'd be like a 40%. Is that on — when you've broken down wages before where it's like that's — is that just the player? Yeah. Yeah. And and don't forget that um that's just the wages, right? So you have to think about net transfer spend as well, right? So if you're thinking about — total — total football — yeah, exactly. If you're talking about total football spend and you're looking at the amortized transfer fees alongside the wages being spun out on the same time frames, etc. — that number is obviously going to be an awful lot higher. I mean, if we had the whatever £100-whatever-million amortized fees due, then that's £210 million pretty much, which is — 90% of your — suddenly that's 90% of your revenue on score cost ratio. Yeah. So, I think it's a bit less than that. I think — I think the number I heard — and again, it's speculative at this point — but I think the number I heard was about £175 million um on an annualized basis, including the amortized transfer fees. So, £175 as a percentage of £225 — that's that's that's wobbly. Okay. Interesting. Um, West Ham fans, let us know your thoughts. I'm sure it's a pretty nervy — nervy time for you. Uh, as you know, as you would have heard, I'm really hoping you stay up. So — be a great reassurance. Dear endear you to to to the show.

第二大主题：Bristol Bears、营销公司论与"球队是品牌吗？"

That feeds very nicely into a second topic, particularly with how you were rounding off "good business, Charlie, and performance on the pitch" — because the second topic we want to talk about, um, is off the back of something that's been bubbling in the news over the last week relating to Bristol Bears, won the Prem Rugby team CEO Tom Tainton saying at a football industry event that Bristol needs to be thinking like a marketing agency that plays rugby — and that recruitment of some players who are maybe more appealing as brands is the way that the club should work. And uh, as you can imagine, fans, the the pure fans, should we say, the the fans steeped in their g— hasn't gone down well.

Sound like a bucket of cold s—. Um, and well, for a couple of reasons, it's quite interesting to me. Firstly, I vehemently disagree with a lot of the critique — and and we'll discuss that. Secondly, we've actually — if you look back just three days, released a show with Tom which he came and filmed with us just after — after this event — and we had a fantastic conversation. It was after the event, wasn't it? Was after the event.

一个朋友被泼了冷水

What I was — I mean, what I was worried — what I was a little bit worried about, Charlie, is is that this poor chap came in here and he already had a speech prepared about, you know, all the usual bromides — and he came in here to be — have his ears filled by you with a whole bunch of stuff about, you know, well basically sports clubs are really marketing and content agencies — and he thought, "That sounds great. I must add that into my speech." And he's gone down there and then had buckets of crap pulled all over his head, and he's sat there at home going, "Never listen to Charlie Stevens again." So true. If it was the other way around, I'd be in trouble. Um, but — you'd have to wear a fake mustache if you went to Bristol. Yeah, true. That's the only thing I get away with it.

上下文很重要：他是对体育从业者说的

Um, there's there's a few points to make on this. Firstly, in the context with which these comments were said, I think it's pretty unfair and actually poor practice that is pulled out into the mass audience. Because it is very, very different to say something like this to a panel of sports industry professionals in a room about the commercialization of sport and driving value through individual stars and talent, to how he would communicate with a fan base talking about how Bristol is going to develop.

And what — when we were chatting about it and I've still always seen — is this has never been positioned as the expense of performance on the pitch for rugby. He sat in — in the show, and you can listen to it now — saying silverware, winning our first Prem title, silverware is the next step for us, the priority. Now, the question of how we get there can then include broader work around making ourselves more marketable, more investable, more of an — more of an attractive proposition to brands. This is all kind of part of the same ambition.

You just in this day and age have to pull a few more levers to do that. Because — and this is something I very firmly believe and I'm sure this will delight some of you who who don't feel like this — but you know, no club, no sports team has a right to exist. You have to — you have to still fight for your audience. You have to fight for people to come and turn up and watch you on TV. And — but it is not unheard of for teams to — Wasps, Wasps, Worcester, um London Irish.

球迷为何反弹

My key point really, and this is the discussion Charlie, because we've got interesting insight across sport here. While maybe — maybe there could have been a slightly more sensitive phrasing to the point being made by Tom, bearing in mind the content, the context it was actually given in — and bearing in mind this belief that we often talk about of sports being content businesses having to think like that in this new world of sports consumption — to to deny this, to to have significant issue as fans is really just to put the asset which I'm sure you treasure in danger. Let's say that — in danger. You have to think about progression, you have to think about progressive strategy.

So, I guess um, you've just said you've just used the word "you you have to think about progressive" but by which I think you're addressing that to the fans. Sorry. Yes. But of course the truth is is that the fans don't, right? And I think that's the problem with this whole thing — which is that if Tom Tatton was having a conversation with me and you about how, as a commercially focused executive (because he's not the director of rugby, he is the head of the business operation of the club), how do you start thinking about ways to um supercharge your revenues to enable oneself — the club — on a sustainable basis to afford the kind of playing budgets which enable that club to be competitive at the top level, and I'll come on to that in a moment in terms of Bristol's competitive situation with other top rugby clubs — that is the type of conversation that happens all the time, right?

So hate to disappoint any just general sports fans out there who think that we're all there sort of kissing the badge and wearing bubble hats as executives. That's not how it works, right? So, we are fully aware of our responsibility to try to deliver success on the pitch, and we are under intense pressure as sports executives to provide the money to do that whilst asking our owners for as little money as possible.

"如果是老板付钱，那就是老板的俱乐部"

Right? Now, I know that fans don't care about the drain on an owner's pocket, right? But they should. First of all, because actually it's — as I've said before, I think on my on my first interview I ever did with you guys was — if the owner is funding it mostly, then it's not your club, it's his club, and you don't really have any rights over it then.

So if you actually want to have the traditions of your club valued, that money has to fundamentally mostly come from fans, which in turn then gives you the right to challenge and question the direction of the club because it's mostly your money being spent. We had that discussion about Celtic the other day where basically all the money comes from Celtic fans, and therefore Celtic fans have every right to question the way in which it's being spent. There are other clubs where basically dare I say it like under the previous ownership with Chelsea where the majority of the money is coming from one guy and frankly it's his way or the highway and the fans shouldn't really demand have too much of a say. It's not really mostly their money.

Tom Tatton 的真实任务

I love that. Okay. So exactly. So here we are at Bristol, right? And you've got a business focused executive who's been in the Bristol sports family of clubs um for a decade. Originally, I — I think you'll correct me if I'm wrong, as a PR guy, working up through the marketing and PR department and then into more senior roles, and then eventually into a role at Bristol Sports, which is the umbrella company looking at all of the Bristol clubs — Bristol City, Bristol City women, Bristol Bears, Bristol Bears women — and then finally into the hot seat as as chief executive.

Now, he's done that at a time when um Steve Lansdown, who is the ultimate owner of all of those clubs, has made it very publicly clear that now that he is retired and in his mid-70s, that the days of him munificently funding all of these clubs are drawing to an end and all of these clubs need to become more sustainable and more investable to people other than him.

So, it's absolutely clear that Tom Tainton would have been given the commission and the mandate by Steve Lansdown on his appointment: "Can you try and make some sense out of this? Can you try and make sure that I'm not having to spend £5 million pounds a year — which is what it has been — of my own money propping the rugby club up? Can you try to make more sense out of it?"

已经做出的成绩

And as an energetic, imaginative young executive, he's done some really interesting stuff. And we have to recognize that. You know, this is the club that bought Ellis Genge — Manu Tuilagi — to the UK last year. This is the club that did the deal to bring Louis Rees-Zammit back from American football. Okay. And it's reflected in their attendance levels which are really, really high.

Okay, so this is why I think it's important to to to get some nuance into this. Okay. So the the attendance numbers in English professional men's rugby — you got Leicester Tigers who are the the biggest historic club of English men's rugby (sorry to Bath fans, but that is generally speaking how the rest of us would view it), who are getting 21,000 a week at Welford Road. Then you got a whole group of clubs — Harlequins, Bath, Gloucester, Northampton — who are around 14 to 15,000. But Bristol are up at 19,000. So they're the only ones now who are pushing on to try and challenge the attendance levels that Leicester are getting.

Um, but that has not necessarily all translated straight through into revenue. Their revenue levels are — or have been — around about the £13 million pound level compared to Leicester and Northampton, you know, single club cities where rugby's quite heavy and quite dominant. I know Leicester City is very, very big and Northampton Town exists, etc., but they are big rugby cities and they also sort of speak for their county. Leicester and Northamptonshire get behind their rugby clubs. So, they're both up at the sort of £21 million type area and Bristol's been down at £13 million. Okay. So that — and that was a loss, a book loss of £5.5 million.

So what Tom Tatton's challenge here is is — how can I get more people through the gate? How can I do more interesting things commercially? How can I um engender a broader audience beyond even just the ones who come through the gate and and pay for tickets? Um, I need to start thinking of this business in a in a different way, because if I just run it like a traditional rugby club, then ultimately, um, in the end Leicester squash me, um, and the French clubs squash me, and I end up not being able to — particularly if Mr. Lansdown eventually decides to sell the club or move on or whatever it might be. Once that cash float is no longer in place, then at that point the spending that we have available to spend on players starts to come down and we become less competitive — which is not the aim of the exercise. As he said, you — we want to win silver — silverware.

那个引起争议的具体引语

So he's trying to square this circle and it's a difficult circle to square, and coming up with inventive ways to doing it. And his way of explaining that is to say that the way that I look at it is that we are a marketing agency that plays rugby. Of course he's not saying it's not a rugby club. Of course he's not. He knows it's a bloody rugby club. He runs a rugby club. But he's saying that's how I think of it. And he's saying that to a group of other sports executives.

So like you, I want to be 90% right behind him and his way of thinking and the things that he's doing to try to make his sports club sustainably competitive at the top level. The one quote which I thought was injudicious — and I must put an asterix saying that I've given plenty of injudicious quotes, right? So —

have you got — have you got any of them to hand? Well, you — you won't have to — just look on Wikipedia. It's all there. Um, right. So he gave a quote which I think was injudicious. He said:

"Our players have to be competent, but if they are not bringing any value off the field, then that genuinely factors into our recruitment conversations."

Brian Moore 的反击

Now, this is the bit which was then picked up by Brian Moore, the veteran former England hooker who is a columnist for the Daily Telegraph and a great sage of rugby these days. And this is the bit that he picked up on. He didn't have a big issue with some of the rest of it — because Brammo's pretty smart guy. He — he kind of gets it. But he did say:

"What players do on the park must come first and nothing off the field will make up for not doing their primary job."

Now, you know, is there a false distinction being made here between — well, you can have players who are outstanding on the park who are also outstanding off the park, and Tom Tainton might well say, "I want — I'm targeting the ones who are both, and maybe some other clubs aren't. Maybe some other clubs are basically saying, well, it doesn't really matter what they do off the park, whereas I'm saying it does matter what they do off the park."

But I think that Brian Moore is right in saying that — that there needs to be, you know, saying our players have to be competent — I think probably one would want to replace the word "competent" with "excellent," right? So that the bar — you can't imagine like recruiting a player who is just competent to a club that's aiming for top silverware. You know, you you are trying to push the bar higher than that.

主动型 vs 被动型的体育高管

But overall, I think his general direction of travel is the correct direction of travel. His way of thinking is the correct way of thinking. Because one big gripe I have with sports club executives is effectively they sort of sit there and when the team does well more people turn up and more money comes in or whatever it might be and they go, "Well, that — because we're doing a good job." And then when the team doesn't do well, they say, "Well of course the reason why the numbers down is because the team aren't doing so well." And you sort of think, "Well, what's the point in you then?" I mean, effectively the head of marketing at the club is simply that the first team manager. Um, so why is — why are you being paid a fat salary for sitting there basically just doing nothing — whereas he's turning on his head saying, "No, I want to be proactive. I want to do stuff. I want to make a difference." It's just come across in the wrong way.

英格兰 vs 法国橄榄球的财务鸿沟

But I just wonder whether this is the moment, Charlie, just have a look at the financial — you know, the the challenge that English professional rugby is facing vis-à-vis French rugby. Because, you know, we're in the middle of the, you know, the Champions Cup sort of demon where obviously there is not an English team in the final. Um, it's um being played between an Irish team and a French team, and and that's been quite consistent over the last few years, which is that the big French clubs have sort of dominated, with Leinster sort of sticking their foot in the door, you know, and saying, "Well, we're here as well." Um, etc.

And I was just sort of looking at this and the problem that English rugby faces is quite similar to the problem that European topflight football faces vis-à-vis the Premier League's TV revenues. Um, and hence why the Tom Taintons of this world are really having to work very, very hard to even like vaguely keep up. Because if you look at um what Prem Rugby's TV revenues — have totally flatlined over the last 15 to 20 years. They're basically getting £30, £35 million a year from their TV deal. In 2013 to 2014, it was £38 million a year. Okay. Okay. Now, if you split that out between the clubs, the amount that each club is getting from its TV revenues is minimal.

Now, if you're looking at France, they're getting €110 million domestically from Canal Plus. Okay? And then they're getting an extra €20 or €30 from their foreign rights. So, in other words, the French league is getting almost as much from its foreign rights as the English league is getting from its domestic rights.

And that then leads to totally different cost caps. So, you know, if you look at the English league currently — £6.4 million per club in terms of total squad cost. They can push that up using various allowances to about £7.5 million quid. That kind of thing. In the French Top 14, it's already at €10 to €11, rising to €14 million. So, in other words, they're effectively able to spend almost twice as much as the English clubs. And yet effectively our top clubs are being judged somewhat by their own fans on, "Okay, well, how are you — you know, how are you competing in European competition against the top French clubs?"

So it's it's a big challenge. It's a huge challenge. As I said, it's the same challenge that, you know, Spanish football clubs and German football clubs are facing when it comes to competing against English Premier League clubs.

中央分账少 = 必须自己生钱

Yeah. So at the same time, with that — quite often if you do have a less of a reliance on central revenue — let's say it was, I don't know, £3 to £4 million of TV deal money coming down into the Prem clubs, and you're then getting clubs that are doing well £20 to £25 million — they're having to generate a lot of that on their own — you look at the Premier League clubs — you'll have a few Premier League clubs in there, some of the smaller ones whose reliance on central media will — well, central revenue streams will be up at maybe 75 to 80%. So in some ways for rugby, they're being forced to to look at this like European football clubs in different ways. They're being forced to extract more value out of the club that they've got and try and find ways to to generate more cash. But that does lead to then situations like this. So the purity of that rugby fan, you know, the the diehard um passion that exists for everything that is called rugby — and I think it probably does more in rugby than any other sport.

给球迷的硬问题

I — I think I think rugby is really holding on to its traditions and its amateur past, which I understand. But I always sort of say to fans that circumstances — I say the same in football as well — which is, if that is your priority and you're cool with that, that's okay, right? But let's make it very clear that you won't be competitive as a club. Are you okay with that bit as well, right? So that's the question I'd be asking Bristol rugby fans — which is, okay, he could have perhaps put it a bit differently, but fundamentally he's trying to drive your club forward so it can compete, right? Are you — if you're saying you don't want his mindset and his way of looking at things at your club, are you okay with being a mid-table club that doesn't compete in Europe, that can't compete against the French clubs? Because if you are okay with that, do you know what? Absolutely fair enough.

And you can see this in in English men's football where Wimbledon — the Dons Trust — won't sell a majority of their club. Now, if they did sell a majority of their club, it being a central London club and all all that brings, they could probably find a very rich new owner who could come in and who would more reliably be able to provide the funding to take Wimbledon to the next level. But the fans say, "Okay, that's fine, but actually it's not fine because unless we keep control of this, we might lose the bits that really, really matter to us."

So, that's the question I'd ask Bristol fans is — do you want to be a bit like AFC Wimbledon? Do you actually want to try and keep — not be as competitive but be very clear that you want to keep your heritage — or do you want to be competitive? So anyway, I think we agree. Um, could have been put better, or at least could have been — I think one thing that you have to understand is as a chief executive, everything you say in public, even something you say in private pretty much, you have to say, "Would this stand public scrutiny?" because it could well come out in this day and age. Anyhow, there we are. Moving on.

第三大主题：WNBA 的现金大戏

Moving on to Charles — the WNBA. Oh, fantastic. Women's basketball — area of expertise. Um, which is Charlie's abiding passion. I'm not a huge basketball fan. What? Men's, women's — name. I mean, I love The Last Dance — which is horrible. And you know that — that's literally probably the thing that every basketball fan hates to hear. Thought it was brilliant. Really interesting. I — I said it before on the show. I struggle with some American sports. Um, but I know you are. But it's — but we're not — it's not going to stop us discussing them. We're not — and and the WNBA returns this week for their new season.

30 年的渐进式增长

Um, now what's really interesting — I find the WNBA was set up um 30 years ago, 1996. And unlike the mistakes that are being made by um professional women's football in Europe, they were much more careful to um start start low um and build slowly. Um, and they deliberately have a season which is the opposite of the men's season. So just as the men's season is coming to an end, the women's season starts. So that if you are a passionate basketball person, you have the opportunity both to focus on the men's season when it's happening and then to focus on the women's season when it's happening — as opposed to being forced constantly to either focus on both, which is very difficult, or else to choose between the two.

And one of the main cornerstones of their um gradual growth has been a very strict wage cap, as happens in most US sports. So um back in the day when the thing was starting out, your average um female basketball player was being paid $20 to $30,000 bucks a year. Um, and you know, it was really — you had to make a serious choice. I know Mrs. Asmaifin had to make this choice — which is, "Am I willing to basically be paid less than I would be in what I would probably be doing in the rest of my life?" Because don't forget, all these athletes coming out of colleges, so it's not like they're sort of 16-year-olds who, you know, pretty much this is their entire life. They they do have other avenues they could pursue. Or, "Am I just so passionate about basketball that this is what I'm going to do?"

Um, and over those 30 years, there has been a gradual growth. You know, they started off playing in secondary arenas. The crowds would be 1,000, 2,000, 3,000. And over about a 25-year period, gradually it grew. They started to to play more often in the associated men's arenas, because often the franchises were associated to NBA men's franchises. It was very much a baby born of the NBA, as you'd expect with it being called the WNBA. And it was pretty low cost um, and inasmuch as, you know, it was heavily subsidized effectively by the NBA during that entire period.

罢工威胁与新薪资上限

Um, in more recent times, uh, there's been a lot more noise around the WNBA — and it's perceived (and we'll get into whether it's true or not) that there's been an explosive growth, exponential growth, has sort of come from nowhere. Um, and that — that then in turn has meant that players who by this point were being paid about $100,000 bucks a year — so still quite modest, $120,000 bucks on average, that kind of thing — so we're talking about of those in a UK audience about £80,000 a year — that they should be being paid a lot more, a bigger slice of the pie.

And their union, um, the WNBPA, took a strike ballot and they voted to go on strike in advance of this upcoming season. Um, and then there's several months of negotiations with the WNBA about what the wage cap might move to, and they've ended up um moving it quite a bit. Right. So, so, so the wage cap has really flown um from sort of average player earning about $120,000 bucks a year to more like $500,000 bucks a year, with a clear view on potentially going to more like $800,000 bucks a year by 2031.

Now, the league said something quite interesting during this whole process and they said that the WNBPA's um — he said that "the proposal is unrealistic and will cause hundreds of millions of losses for our teams." And that is true — that that is broadly true, right? Which is that these increases are not actually affordable as things stand right now.

金州 Valkyries：第一只十亿美元女子球队

Can I ask you a question straight in there? Because what I saw — and the other side of this uh that came out recently is — all big noise, and we — we know the the lovely metrics of valuations — but there was a big headline of the first billion dollar women's team valuation with the Golden State Valkyries. Yeah. Um, alongside the billion dollar valuation was their revenue figure of $78 million in their first year. Now, actually, can we leave the re— the valuation aside just for that? $78 — million in revenue in a debut season is an impressive feat for a — and is the highest in the league sports organization, and is the highest in the league. But they will still — but they will still live in under the same rules and regulations of the salary cap, right? So, $1.5 million salary cap with revenues of $78 million.

From that standpoint, do you not think that there's a legitimate discussion to be had with those players, some of whom I read said that, you know, they last year struggled to pay their rent, but the team, you know, can make X amount, that that should have been raised, that the players should have been getting more — but that's linked to team success. Again, I'm — because you're going to tell me that the average is far lower, which I'm sure is true, but —

do you not think in that situation that that is right, that the players get paid more?

Caitlyn Clark 效应

Yeah. And I think there was an absolutely a case for the players to be paid more. Um, but to quadruple it in one go I think is punchy or bold um brave or whatever it might be foolish — um because the recent um supposed exponential growth has been very heavily linked to one player, Caitlyn Clark.

And for those who don't know, this is a young basketball player um who came out of college two or three years ago, two years ago, and was a very, very famous player at college as being the generational talent, outstanding talent — who then the moment she came into the WNBA brought a huge upswing of interest in the WNBA from general men's basketball fans following her. And there was almost like a control study because TV audiences shot up during her involvement and then when she got injured they halved again.

The Messi effect at Miami. Right? So it — you know, the question is here is — how much of this explosion interest is driven by one player?

Now, there's no doubt by the way that there has been slow and steady growth anyway, right? And we discussed recently how the real big flashing red sign about the women's super league football is that attendances are actually declining rather than increasing. Whereas in the WNBA they have been increasing, and the leaguewide average now is around about 11,000.

So league just landed a new $2.2 billion media deal this season. The the the media deal is um an interesting one because it's effectively a subset of the NBA deal. So effectively the NBA negotiates their deal with all the big companies and then they negotiate at the same time what share of that is going to go to the WNBA. So working out exactly what a full — a normal commercial rate would be for the WNBA is quite difficult to do because it's all done as part of the NBA.

增长的真实程度

So this growth over a long period of time has been 3%, 4%, 5% — good, steady — and starting to build an environment where these franchises can make money. Um, I think when you start to move towards a situation where the players are being paid a million bucks a year, that is going to put quite a lot of stress on the entire system. And you, I think, would be well advised to consider carefully the extent to which this is very heavily driven by one athlete who is — it's almost hard to describe how famous she is in the US. She is extremely famous within that US market. Like, so she is the one female basketball player who has elevated herself onto a totally different level by which normal sports fans will go, "Oh, is Caitlyn Clark playing today? I'd like to watch her." Right? That's that's what's happened here.

So look, I think it's a sort of — it's a nuanced picture which is that women's basketball is growing. Even without Caitlyn Clark, it already was growing. Even if she were to get, heaven forbid, badly injured again, it would still be basically growing. There are more um expansion franchises coming. So by 2030, there will be three more franchises. The cost of those franchises is $250 million.

And I just mentioned the Valkyries — they were bought a few years ago, a couple years ago, I think — born. Uh, yes, but as in the license went for $50 million. $50 million. That was their expansion franchise. So it's gone from $50 million to $250 million.

"Fugazi" — 一场骗局还是一个泡沫？

Now I — I took the time um before discussing this to consult um the sports industry sage Roger Mitchell, who has been talking about this for the last two or three years. And he describes women's professional basketball as a — what he calls a "fugazi" — which is basically a bluff, right? Is the whole thing is like a Ponzi scheme where, you know, lots of confidence is sprayed around in lots of different directions and really rich people buy into that confidence and say, "Yeah, it's worth $300 million, $500 million, $700 million, $900 million." It's like being sales ring at Tattersalls during when they're auctioning off one of the top yearlings, you know, from from one of the big sort of stud farms.

And scrape it all back and say, well, this is a mature product. This is not a new product. It's been there for 30 years. And on a revenue basis, we're starting to talk about revenue multiples at 20x —

20 — tech multiples, basically. Right? We're we're looking at, you know, tech unicorn type multiples being applied to a mature legacy product which has been showing a steady growth profile like many sports have been during that entire era — and at a time when other sports franchises (for instance the English Premier League, being the premier football franchise in the world, revenue multiples 4x). So Premier League 4x, women's basketball 20x.

And so the question is — which people like Roger raise — is not should women's basketball exist, not should women's professional basketball players be reasonably well paid. It's: are we starting to look like the inflation of a bubble here which all sorts of things are being sort of, you know, dreams and hopes are being piled into when in actual fact there was a perfectly reasonable steady slow growth legacy product that was going on there which over time indeed would have, could have, should have led to a sensible growth pattern.

50% 收入分成的数学题

But so are you basically saying that for them to be able to get paid what really we're talking about here, we just need to see the collective value of that league actually increase from a revenue standpoint, i.e., if a league is making a lot of money and can support the salaries of those players to a percentage in line with how the NFL or the NBA operates, which are generally on 50% distribution of finances to the players from the league and the clubs?

Right? Don't forget there are certain basic — when you're operating a sports club there are certain basic costs that have to be covered off first which kind of apply to all all sports. So it's fine to share 50% when your revenues are so big that the 50% kept by the club or the franchise is big enough to cover all that type of stuff without batting an eyelid. But if your revenues are more like — like most WNBA franchises around about $40 million bucks a year — and let's just say for example that the franchise holder was only receiving $20 million bucks of that, and out of that they were having to put on the whole show, all the travel around the country, they were having to pay all the coaching staff and the general sort of commercial staff, etc. — there isn't much left um at the end of all that.

So the the the kind of levels that we're moving to here mean that the group who are scooping the gain from the current hopes and dreams of where women's basketball is going to go are the performers — and the people who are having to take it on trust that eventually the percentage that the franchises get to keep will be sufficient to justify the kind of valuations they're playing are the franchise holders.

So the balance has totally changed, and um because obviously, as you said, three years ago even buying a franchise was so relatively inexpensive that actually you could afford to be a little bit more sort of, uh, you know, generous with your view in terms of how much should be going to players. But when you when you're when you're paying $250 million for the right even to have a franchise, you're doing that because you think that there is going to be — it's going to be a profitable business. You're not doing it because you think that every single time that there's a bit more revenue, it just gets shoveled straight to the players.

NWSL 的镜像

We saw this in the NWSL, didn't we? I mean, in 2019, they were trading at about $2 million a franchise — and then Angel City sells for $250 million plus maybe even valuation. So, it's not the first time this type of multiple is being attributed to some of these teams in women's sport in the US. That's because there's a rising — a real rising hype, as you've said, around some of these now. But also most importantly, I — I always worry with this that it's just built in comparison with what the men's is. And therefore, that always adds a particular premium to it because there's just this underlying belief that it should be up higher because the men's is so high. I saw reading some of the stuff before, right? If you're looking at a comparison between those wages and a top player um could earn, it was about $249,000. Next line was the top NBA player earns $50 million. It's like, well, yeah, but again, bring it back to the what the NBA is and has created and what the WNBA is and has created. It's very hard to compare those two because it's apples and oranges.

It is apples and oranges. It's absolutely apples and oranges. But there is this sort of lingering thing of saying fundamentally it's unfair that female athletes get paid less than male athletes. And this is not something which is replicated this — this sense and a feeling is not something that's replicated across other other segments. Right? So, in other words, if I was to ask you the question, who do you think earns more in the fashion industry, a female supermodel or a male model? Oh, yeah. I listen to like David Gandy stuff before and he's been paid like a million a year for some campaigns, and the female I think like Gisele Bündchen's only got $30 million for the same thing.

三股力量在推动估值

So it — the for me that whole direction of travel of saying, "Well, there needs to be equality in everything" — it's just totally nonsensical, and there's no doubt that that there's a bit of that that's driving this. Then there's the sort of "this could be the next big thing" — what Roger calls the "fugazi" element of it — which is the the hype and the bubble around it. And then underlying it actually there is some growth, right? Which is what means that even people who are very cynical about it have to accept, "Yeah, but it is growing, right? So in 20 years time, if it kept on growing at 3 or 5% a year, you know, could end up being something." So anyway —

I mean, if it is tied into a media deal, right, at $2.2 billion regardless of how they're doing it. I mean, if they're still — if that's there, then that's good revenue. Yeah. I mean, I'll — I'll come on another show. I'll come back on unpacking that TV. We don't have time right now. That TV deal is not a genuine — that's not a genuine — But it's still revenue coming to the league or not. No. Right. Not in the same way. Doesn't work. It doesn't work quite that way, but I'll come back on to that.

与英格兰联赛一组队的对照

Okay, fine. I mentioned $78 million for Golden State Valkyries and you said that was the top by a considerable amount. Do you do you know what the kind of what some of the other teams do? I believe that the average is about $45 million. But then again, I — just say even at $45 million, $7 million to a cap to your player, that's 17th, just under. Yeah. But so that's manageable. But — but that's very similar to similar kind of numbers to what we call a big League One football club here. And that's about what they sort of pay their players and they lose money as well. What 17? What? Onth of their revenue. Well, yeah. Uh, so if you look at a — a big League One club like Bolton Wanderers or something like that — they'll be doing about £21–£22 million pounds revenue a year. So talking about $30 million bucks, a bit more than that — which is similar to the sort of bottom half um WNBA clubs etc. And their players will be paid £8–£9 million a year, that kind of thing. So — so 40%. But you're not paying $250 million for an expansion franchise. You can pick up these clubs for a tenth of that.

美国体育的核心：必须赚钱

And this is where the disconnect happens for me, which is American sport is a business. It's not a passion play. It's not a lifelong fan of the Golden State Valkyries going, "I've been watching the Golden State Valkyrie since I was 5 years old," etc., etc. These are driven by business fundamentals at some point. Right? Franchise owners expect that their franchises will make money.

And there is nobody at the moment in the entire industry, including the players, net unions, who are expecting WNBA franchises to make money right now. And for me, that's dangerous. You know, the American system is built off the basis they will make money — and that's what justifies these huge expansion fees and indeed general valuations.

Okay, interesting. Yeah.

收尾

Um, the season starts this weekend. So if you are interested in checking out the product, go and uh, go and take a look. I wish I could tell you which uh platform to to go and access it on, but I don't have a clue. Um, but one to watch. I — I find that really interesting to to to take a look at. And um, Charlie, a really — a really enjoyable chat today. I think we covered some — some good stuff. So, thank you very much. Thanks, Charlie.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个看似无关的故事，其实在讲同一件事
表面上，这期节目讨论了三个独立话题：西汉姆的财务崩塌、布里斯托熊队 CEO 的"营销公司论"、WNBA 的薪资谈判。但若把它们叠在一张图上，你会发现一个让人意外的共同结构——**它们都是"所有权 vs 故事权"的不同变体**。
- 西汉姆的危机本质上不是财务问题，而是"谁在为这个俱乐部付钱"这个问题正在悄悄变更答案。Sullivan 的钱、Křetínský 的钱、Rights and Media Funding 的贷款、球迷的会员卡——四股资金在拉扯，而每一股资金都在主张不同的"叙事所有权"。 - Tom Tatton 说"我们是一家踢橄榄球的营销公司"，球迷愤怒的根本原因不是这句话粗鲁，而是它**等于公开承认：球迷不再是产品的最终买家，品牌商才是**。 - WNBA 球员要求大幅涨薪，本质上是在说："Caitlyn Clark 的现象级增长是我们创造的故事，不是 NBA 母公司施舍的。"
三件事都是同一个问题：**当一项体育资产的"价值创造者"和"价值收割者"开始分离时，谁有权重新分配蛋糕？**
## 一个让人意外的观察：West

item.title: [Lex Clips] How CIA spied on people using fake VLC video player | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、VLC puzzle 滤镜的“无用之用”：开源世界的进化论

最让我意外的并不是 CIA 的 Vault 7，而是那个拼图滤镜的故事。一个高中数学老师为了教 Bézier 曲线写了它，5 年后有人来抱怨“拼图太简单”，于是 16×16 被改成了 256×256。这看似荒诞的小插曲，其实揭示了一个被主流软件工程严重低估的真相：

在用户基数足够大的开源项目里，"无用功能"不是技术债，而是生态多样性的标志。

这与生物学中的"中性突变"惊人相似——大多数基因变异既无害也无明显用途，但正是这些"冗余"构成了物种应对未知环境的弹性储备。VLC 的 ASCII 输出最初看似玩具，最终成了网络工程师 SSH 调试组播流的利器。一个系统是否健康，不在于它能否做最重要的事，而在于它能否容纳那些"显然无用"的事。 商业软件砍掉这些功能以"提升 KPI"，恰恰是在剪掉自己未来的进化通路。

二、CIA 的 VLC 与德国假站点：被忽视的"信任拓扑学"

表面看，Vault 7 是一次精彩的间谍故事。但真正让我"啊哈"的是 JB 不经意提到的另一件事——德国那个假 VLC 站点存在了 12 年，Google 知情但不处理，理由是"安装包太大无法扫描"。

这暴露了一个比 CIA 更结构性的问题：现代软件分发的信任，并不建立在密码学上，而是建立在搜索引擎的排名算法上。 我们以为 HTTPS、代码签名、哈希校验构成了安全边界，但 99% 的用户其实是通过"Google 第一条结果"来判断什么是真的。这是一种被外包给广告公司的信任——而广告公司的激励函数与用户安全并不对齐。

跨领域看，这与金融体系中的"评级机构问题"完全同构：穆迪、标普理论上是独立的信任中介，但 2008 年我们才发现他们的激励结构使其无法承担这个角色。Google 之于开源软件、评级机构之于债券——同一种结构性失灵，只是介质不同。

三、沙箱化 VLC：一种"自我不信任"的工程哲学

最深刻的一段其实是 JB 谈 VLC 沙箱化时说的那句话——"保护我们自己不受我们自己的影响"。注意：他不是说保护用户不受恶意攻击，而是说 VLC 进程内部跑着 FFmpeg、GPU 驱动、第三方插件，这些代码不是 VLC 团队写的，因此 VLC 必须不信任自己。

这是一种罕见的工程伦理姿态。它的反面是大多数应用程序的默认假设："我的代码是好的，所以我可以拥有所有权限"。

把它跨界联想：这与斯多葛学派的"前摄反思"（premeditatio malorum）惊人一致——预设自己会犯错、会被攻陷，然后据此设计行为。Chrome 的多进程架构、Linux 的 capabilities、Kubernetes 的命名空间隔离，本质上都是同一种哲学：成熟系统的标志，是对自己内部组件保持怀疑。

而更隐蔽的洞见是：VLC 沙箱化困难的根本原因，是它需要"既封闭又开放"——既要隔离崩溃，又要每秒传输几个 Gb 的 memcpy。 这恰恰是所有复杂系统面临的悖论：免疫系统既要识别异己，又不能阻断营养交换；城市既要安检，又要保持物流速度。VLC 团队在毫秒级别上重演这场永恒的张力。

📝 转录文本

一、Vault 7：CIA 的"魔改版 VLC"

Lex： Okay, there's this legendary story that you pointed me to that it was discovered via WikiLeaks release of all sorts of documents — the CIA was using a modified version of VLC to basically try and trick people, to steal their data?

JB： Yes, exactly.

Lex： So can you explain what the heck happened with— [laughter]

JB： So this was a surprise, right? Because at some point WikiLeaks mentioned some documents. There were a few ones with something related to Blu-rays and VLC, but the most interesting one was the CIA Vault 7, which if I understand correctly — the CIA had like a custom version of VLC where they had a specific plugin.

Lex： Yeah, exactly.

JB： This is — like, we had to write a press release on that. VideoLAN wrote a press release saying the only safe source for getting VLC media player is the official VideoLAN website.

Lex： I mean, I suppose that's a security vulnerability for basically any piece of open source software — somebody can trick you to download in a fake website or targeted advertisement.

JB： Like, that was a targeted advertisement: to watch a specific file you need to watch with this custom version of VLC. And it was the normal binaries of VLC except they added one DLL. I think it was psapi.dll, which was basically reading your document folder, encrypting that and sending that.

And the thing is, this is very clever to be honest, because once you're watching a movie, right? You're going to do that for 2 hours and you're not going to touch your computer. And sometimes it's normal because it's HD that your fans are going up, and there is ton of TCP usage because you're using VLC, right? That's normal. But the thing is, what you don't see is that actually a powered version of VLC that is used by CIA.

二、印度封禁事件：被冒用的 DLL

We had exactly the same problem with Chinese hackers that were targeting Indian people, and that got VLC banned from India, until I had to fight in court in India — the Indian government — to unban VLC.

They didn't use VLC. They took just one DLL, because we signed the DLL correctly. And they used that DLL to do another program. So you had the vlc.exe and it was calling libvlc, but it was calling it into a fake one, and they used that to target.

There is not much we can do actually to block those type of hacks.

Lex： Yeah, and I think people should — for all open source software, for all software in general — people should pay attention where they download the thing.

JB： Yes, because that means that they were not downloading it from our website.

三、搜索引擎的失职：德国的 12 年假站点

Lex： Do the search engines help you?

JB： No, they don't.

Lex： Just to clarify, 'cause you can, you know, to prevent threats from people manipulating SEO to get up there on the links —

JB： Absolutely not. Right? We have a big issue for like more than 10 years: there is a fake version of VLC in Germany that was reported for now for 12 years, and Google basically decides to not — they know what's in it, but the binary is too big for their virus analyzer to analyze it.

And so, well, if you're in Germany, you can go to a website that is a fake version of VLC with a custom installer, and it's very popular in Germany because the website is in German, and Google's mentioned that before VideoLAN.

And the weirdest thing is that it doesn't do anything on your machine for 3 weeks.

Lex： Mhm.

JB： Because that's how they do the detection. And after 3 weeks there is a small program — that is a service that installed at the same time — that wakes up after 3 weeks, and it starts downloading spyware and adware. And Google knows about it. They decided not to do anything. The guys used dark SEO in Germany to do that at some point.

And this is very damaging, right? Because one of the things that they are downloading is actually something that is replacing your ads inside your machine, right?

四、社会工程的精妙：假"安全更新"

Lex： It's actually quite surprisingly effective. Whoever is doing it with Twitter and X — with X, I'll get emails about "your X account has been hacked." And however they phrase it, it gets me to at least click on the email, not to follow the thing. And then you're like, man, whatever they're doing with the psychology to try to trick you, they're quite good.

JB： There is a "security version of VLC," right? You receive an email saying: "Hey, there is a security version update on VLC. Think about updating right now because it can hack your computer." You come — it's a website that looks decent — and you download it's a new version of VLC. Great. You don't know a month later you're hacked. You have no idea. You're part of a botnet.

Lex： Yeah. [snorts] So make sure wherever you're downloading stuff is legitimate. Part of the botnet.

五、为什么 VLC 沙箱化如此困难

Speaking of which — so you've mentioned that VLC sandboxing is something you're working on, and it's actually something quite challenging. Why is it important? Why is it hard?

JB： So VLC is a core with around 500 plugins, right? One of them is FFmpeg, but we support so many other formats. We support new protocols. We support new filters. We support weird architectures.

And in this release of VLC you have modules that are going to call your drivers, right? Mostly the hardware decoders, which are going to call your Intel, your Nvidia, your AMD driver. And all calling FFmpeg, right? And there might be a security issue. There might be a security issue in the shader. There might be a security issue in VLC, in FFmpeg, that is going to basically crash.

The issue is that you're running VLC like every other program — like Adobe, right? You're running it on your machine and it has access to all your documents, right? So the idea is to be sure that you do a sandbox, so that we can protect from ourselves — because inside the VLC process is running some code that is not even ours. It's open source other projects that we integrate in VLC, or it's your GPU driver, or something that is provided by someone else inside.

And so when we crash, we want to not allow people to do bad things, right? Because one of the common way of hacking people is to crash a program — very often done with a web browser, very often done with PDF files, less often with multimedia, but it could happen. And when you crash, you launch something on the machine of the person. Could be a ransomware. Could be a botnet, right?

六、桌面与移动的安全鸿沟

So security of desktop application is important. On mobile it's a bit different because most of the mobile applications are running inside their own sandbox. But for VLC we could run it inside one sandbox, but the problem is that we need access to so many things that basically — we would have all the permissions, right? And so if you have a sandbox and you put some holes everywhere, it defeats the purpose, right?

So what we are trying to do — and we're actually doing — is splitting VLC into several processes. One is decoding. One is demuxing. One is filters. And all of them run into their own sandbox. So that the whole VLC — a part of VLC crash, like Chrome crashes on some tab, right? It crashes — crash, but it did not crash the whole program. And this is what we're trying to do.

And it's difficult because it's a sandbox that needs to sustain gigabits per seconds of mem copies. No, it's not a website which is 5 megabytes or 10 megabytes. We're talking about hundreds of megabits per seconds. So this is why it is quite challenging. And this is a research topic that we are working on, in order to have multimedia player that is secure.

七、海量用户的副产品：每个奇葩功能都有人在用

Lex： This is all the kind of stuff you have to think about when millions of people are using it. You mentioned something somewhere where, like, all the different features of VLC — when you have that many people using it, somebody will use every single feature and they will tell you about it.

JB： Best feature in VLC is called the puzzle filter. Right? So you click the puzzle filter and it transforms your video into a jigsaw puzzle, right? And you can click and move the pieces, right? Yeah.

It's very, very useful when you're watching a French movie, right? You're bored —

Lex： Oh, yeah.

JB： — because it's like very long things, or love triangle, like you've seen that so many times. Right? But you need to watch it, because someone — your wife, or — yeah — told you to do that, or your boyfriend told you to do that. So you're doing that, right? Yeah. And you can click and move the pieces around. Yeah.

It's absolutely useless, right? Like who cares about that? First, it was done by a math teacher in high school in South of France to teach his students about Bézier curves, which is something that everyone should know about, right? It's very useful. But the code was clean. So it got in VLC. It was merged in 2010.

5 years later, I received an email saying: "Hello JB, I have a problem with VLC. The puzzle is too simple." And I was just like — what? And yes, the puzzle was, in the UI, maximum 16 by 16, right? Only 256. And he says: "I'm sorry, but in a movie I love puzzle. This is too simple, right?" So there is a commit of me — you can check it online — which is JB changing that the dimensions are 256 by 256.

Lex： Brilliant.

八、ASCII 艺术与组播调试：被低估的奇技淫巧

JB： My point is, so many features are used by a few people, right? There is a way to watch VLC movies in command line without any UI, right?

Lex： I saw that — you can do ASCII art. Is it useful?

JB： Very useful. Imagine you're debugging — imagine you're debugging a multicast network, right? You have very complex networking stack, right? You can SSH to all of the routers and put VLC on it with no UI. And you're going to see whether it's black or it's not black, right? So you see if — "Oh, it's all green or not all green," right? So you can see.

Right? People don't realize there is so many things in VLC that are useful, and they have users — because once you have hundreds of millions of users, you have people who use every feature.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、VLC puzzle 滤镜的“无用之用”：开源世界的进化论
最让我意外的并不是 CIA 的 Vault 7，而是那个**拼图滤镜**的故事。一个高中数学老师为了教 Bézier 曲线写了它，5 年后有人来抱怨“拼图太简单”，于是 16×16 被改成了 256×256。这看似荒诞的小插曲，其实揭示了一个被主流软件工程严重低估的真相：
**在用户基数足够大的开源项目里，"无用功能"不是技术债，而是生态多样性的标志。**
这与生物学中的"中性突变"惊人相似——大多数基因变异既无害也无明显用途，但正是这些"冗余"构成了物种应对未知环境的弹性储备。VLC 的 ASCII 输出最初看似玩具，最终成了网络工程师 SSH 调试组播流的利器。**一个系统是否健康，不在于它能否做最重要的事，而在于它能否容纳那些"显然无用"的事。** 商业软件砍掉这些功能以"提升 KPI"，恰恰是在剪掉自己未来的进化通路。
## 二、CIA 的 VLC 与德国假站点：被忽视的"信任拓扑学"
表面看，Vault 7 是一次精彩的间谍故事。但真正让我"啊哈"的是 JB 不经意提到的另一件事——**德国那个假

item.title: [Lex Clips] FFmpeg drama on Twitter/X | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一场表演型冲突的隐藏经济学

这段对话表面是公关洗白——"其实只是说唱对决啦"——但底层揭示了一个反直觉的机制：开源项目的可见度危机，往往无法通过技术叙事解决，只能通过冲突叙事解决。FFmpeg 维护者一直在地下室里默默工作了二十年，没人捐款；和 Google 工程师吵了一架，捐款立刻飙升。这不是巧合，而是注意力市场的物理定律——和平的奉献是无声的，对立才能产生频率。他们其实在用"被冒犯"作为一种营销燃料，并且他们自己都知道。

跨领域连接：FFmpeg、WWE 与中世纪的"司法决斗"

最让我意外的是 JB 主动用了 "WWE" 这个比喻——这不是随口一说，而是一种深刻的自我认知。WWE 的本质是 kayfabe（剧场化的真实），观众心知肚明却乐在其中。但更有趣的跨领域连接是中世纪欧洲的司法决斗（trial by combat）：当社区无法通过常规渠道解决"谁才是真正的开源精神继承人"这种身份争议时，公开冲突就成为了一种合法化机制。Theo、Google 工程师、FFmpeg 账号——他们其实在 X 上重演了一场仪式化的身份审判，只不过武器是推文。软件世界正在悄悄重新发明它以为自己早就抛弃的中世纪社会工具。

一个你大概没想到的系统性真相

注意叙述者反复强调的一句话："Google 不是一个实体，而是很多人。" 这看起来是在缓和矛盾，但它其实泄露了一个更深的洞察：大型公司在开源生态中扮演的是"分裂人格"角色——同一个 Google，既是 FFmpeg 最大的免费使用者（YouTube/Chrome），又是它最大的批评者（那位发推的工程师），还是它的潜在赞助者。叙述者的"calm everyone down"不是在调停冲突，而是在协调 Google 内部不同人格之间的张力。

这指向一个少有人讨论的事实：开源项目维护者真正的政治工作，不是写代码，而是充当跨国巨头内部派系的非正式调停人。他们比 Google 的 PM 更清楚 Google 内部的权力地形。这是一种隐形的、未被定价的劳动——而它，恰恰是现代数字基础设施得以延续的真正粘合剂。

📝 转录文本

对相关方的致意

I just want to put a little bit of love out there, even to the beer community. Much love and respect to Google engineers. Like you said, they're some of the best software engineers in the world, and they do contribute a lot, even on the security front.

And also, you know, I'm a big fan of Theo. Much love to Theo. He was part of this debacle and drama a little bit.

戏剧的正面外溢效应

I think when you just zoom out on the grand arc of human history, the drama contributed positively to everybody involved. Donations went up. It brought more attention to the topic. Allowed everybody to bicker in a way that ultimately got them to figure out what a fan base is all about.

So the way we looked at this is: it's a rap battle at the end of the day.

[laughter]

No, but it is — we say stuff, we say stuff. Yeah. But we can leave it on the X as a perfect place for, you know, international rap battle. You say stuff, I say stuff about your mama, but it doesn't mean, you know, I'm going to have an actual personal issue with her.

Theo 事件与"娱乐化"的边界

Yeah, and that's what it looks like. The Theo situation — you know, JB can maybe expand — went a little bit too far. You know, it was a little bit… You know, it's just a bit of fun. It's just a bit of rap battle. It's a bit, it's WWE, you know — everyone's having a bit of fun on X. It doesn't need to be taken seriously.

You know, the teenagers thing — that guy was a Google employee saying, "Hey, you know, there are other ways to run an open source business." You know, go and just have a bit of fun. That's what the point of this account is.

教育意义与开源现实的揭示

And furthermore, if you can teach people about the ways of open source projects, assembly, etc. by doing that, I think there's a lot to be offered here. It's not donking on people for donking sake. It's showing — actually the story that I think X learned — is: these are not big corporate open source projects. This is not Kubernetes where there's, you know, hundreds, maybe thousands of people paid to develop this stuff. These are just people in their basements, in their spare time.

And if you can address that topic in a fun and entertaining way, I think that's the good thing. And that's the value of X and the reach we have.

Google 内部的多样性与事后调停

And to be honest, right? Like, even at Google — Google is one entity, but so many different people, right? And there is a ton of Google engineers we work with all the time, and even like Google from YouTube to Chrome, to Chrome media, to the rest of Google. Those are very different types of entities, but what we do is efficient.

And for example, for Theo, right? It went a bit too far. I had him — like, I calmed everyone down. I had him on the phone. We said, "Okay, like, this goes too far," and so on.

But in the end, yeah, it's a rap battle, but it's positive for the project. Like, the awareness we have on open source — and I mean true open source, from communities, right? — has increased dramatically in the last 2 years, and this is useful.

观看原视频 →

这段对话表面是公关洗白——"其实只是说唱对决啦"——但底层揭示了一个反直觉的机制：**开源项目的可见度危机，往往无法通过技术叙事解决，只能通过冲突叙事解决**。FFmpeg 维护者一直在地下室里默默工作了二十年，没人捐款；和 Google 工程师吵了一架，捐款立刻飙升。这不是巧合，而是注意力市场的物理定律——**和平的奉献是无声的，对立才能产生频率**。他们其实在用"被冒犯"作为一种营销燃料，并且他们自己都知道。

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Surviving Twitter's Growing Pains: Ex-CEO Dick Costolo
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Twitter 的"慢"，本质是一种被伪装成"民主"的恐惧

表面看，Twitter 早期的"圆桌投票文化"是一种扁平、协作的美德。但 Costolo 揭示的真相恰恰相反——集体决策不是民主，而是责任的稀释装置。当一群人共同决定"retweet 要不要变成原生功能"时，没有人真正承担后果，于是没有人真正在乎速度。

更隐蔽的是后来的"Dick said"现象——一个中层经理用 CEO 的名字当令箭推动决策。这看似是 CEO 权力过大，实际是整个组织在借 CEO 之名逃避自己的判断责任。Costolo 因此意识到：所谓"velocity"不是让人跑得更快，而是让每个人停止把决策权外包给"上面"。这跟禅宗的"截断众流"是同构的——速度的敌人不是懒惰，而是逃避主权。

二、跨领域连接：Pixar 的"无导演会议"与森林防火学的同一个秘密

Costolo 偷师 Steve Jobs 在 Pixar 的做法——把导演请出会议室，单独和插画师们聊"什么进展不顺利"。这个动作和他后来用"森林防火"比喻教导事必躬亲的 director，其实在解决同一个信息论问题：组织规模越大，信号在传递中越被既得利益者过滤。

把这个洞察放进生态学就更妙了：森林防火学有个反直觉发现——过度扑灭小火，反而酿成毁灭性大火，因为可燃物不断累积。Costolo 让 director 不再扑火、转而做"林相规划"，本质是允许小型火灾发生（即让团队犯错），以避免组织级的森林大火。这又和他的"bias to yes + 不惩罚错误"形成闭环：一个不允许小事故的组织，必然在准备一场大灾难。

这条逻辑甚至能往生物学延伸——免疫系统过度活跃就是自身免疫病。Twitter 的 14 道审批 = 系统性自身免疫攻击。

三、最容易被忽略的悖论：Costolo 的"adult in the room"人设，恰恰建立在他做过即兴喜剧演员之上

人们会理所当然地把"成熟 CEO"想象成西装革履的 MBA 类型。但仔细听他讲在 Adelaide 凌晨被一千个醉鬼喝倒彩、在 SNL 试镜失败——这些"非严肃"经历，反而是他后来面对 Yahoo Finance 把他列入"年度最差 CEO"时心理免疫力的真正来源。

这里有个反向选择效应值得警觉：今天大量 25 岁就成为 CEO 的年轻人，其人生最缺的不是"商业训练"，而是 Costolo 所说的"那个对家人都没给过反馈的盲区"——他们没有过公开失败、被嘘、被取消的肌肉记忆。所以一旦遇到媒体反扑，要么过度防御（变成 Elon），要么内伤崩溃。

最妙的连接在这：他不去 Vanity Fair 奥斯卡派对的那个小决定，和他在公司"bias to yes"是同一种自我管理——严格区分"哪些邀请是给 Dick Costello 的，哪些是给'Twitter CEO'这个角色的"。这不只是谦逊，而是一种心理财务上的对冲：他从一开始就在防止自己被身份俘虏。这种身份-角色的清算能力，可能是高压 CEO 真正稀缺的品质，远比"focus""velocity"更底层。

一个让人后背发凉的预测：今天的 AI 创业公司之所以普遍被建议"少招人、保持精简"，表面理由是 AI 让一人能干十人活；但 Costolo 的经验暗示了更深的原因——组织一旦越过 200 人门槛，"Dick said"现象就会自动启动，速度就开始指数级下降。AI 时代真正的护城河可能不是模型，而是让公司永远停留在"人人都能直接听见 CEO"的那个临界点。这意味着创始人最危险的庆祝时刻，不是 IPO，而是签下第 201 名员工的 offer 那天。

📝 转录文本

开场：Twitter 的"戏剧女王"年代

Dick Costolo： I just felt like Twitter was such such a drama. Twitter was like the drama queen of hyper growth companies.

You know, it was like when I took over as CEO that year, the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two different magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker, and I was like: "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal — no dead birds on magazine covers." You know, low bar. Yes.

主持人介绍

Hey everybody, we have Dick Costolo on the show. He's a rare bird in Silicon Valley these days. Uh, he was CEO of Twitter, but he was the third CEO. The first one was Jack Dorsey, who you probably met on the pod a couple weeks ago. Uh, the second was Ev Williams, the other founder, and Dick took over as COO and then ultimately as CEO of Twitter, and he ran it for 5 years.

It was a really extremely fast growing organization. They created a category. Uh, didn't have a revenue machine and was famously dysfunctional. I think Dick did a really nice job — and he was sort of the adult in the room — and he kind of ran this scale-up CEO master class.
