He talks all about in this podcast: he talks about increasing velocity of decisions and execution, which is tricky as you scale. He talked about how he managed by walking around — this is something I did, I think is a really good idea. He talked about: how do you give very tough feedback to very tough people? There's some gems in there. Dealing with the press, particularly press that doesn't like you. He talks about focus and how much focus you really need in the era of AI when you can build stuff so quickly. He talks about how do you manage the forest instead of fighting all the fires.

He's got a lot of wisdom in here. He also talks about his regrets — including a whopper, his regret in not acquiring Instagram. Got one hurt. If you're scaling your 100 employees, you're ripping growing fast, you've got a lot of growing pains, maybe you feel a little out of control — this episode's for you.

Welcome to the show. — Thanks for having me. — Appreciate it.

加入 Twitter：那场"代班"如何变成永久职位

主持人： I want to wind the clock back to Twitter, of course.

Dick： I watched a documentary recently —

主持人： Was it the one I was in? Yes. — I thought you were really good in it.

Dick： Oh, thank you. Great. You're great. You were great in the documentary. You came off as rational in knowing what the hell you were doing. But you're in a long line of interesting CEOs — of Jack Dorsey and then Ev Williams and yourself and Jack Dorsey and Parag and Elon Musk.

主持人： Elon — and don't forget, you know, the Elon hired hand Linda Yaccarino.

Dick： Okay. Don't forget, of course. Of course.

主持人： You joined as COO. What was the pitch?

Dick： What was the pitch to me?

主持人： Yeah, Ev pitched you.

Dick： I mean, yeah, pitched me. The initial pitch was: "I'm taking paternity leave for a couple weeks." Ev Williams was taking paternity leave. "I need someone to come in and just hold down the fort for a couple weeks while I'm out." Yeah, interim. Would you do that?

But then while we were having that discussion, he's like: "Well, what if it was permanent? What if you were COO?"

And I was going to go start another company. So, rewinding the clock a little bit further: I'd sold my previous company, FeedBurner, to Google. It's where I rebumped into the Twitter guys, because they were all there from Blogger and getting ready to leave and start Odeo, which became Twitter.

Anyway, I'm leaving Google a couple years later. Ev texts me. I was going to go start another company again with one or more of my FeedBurner co-founders. And then Ev said: "What if it was permanent? What if you were COO?"

And Twitter was already — it's summer 2009 — it was already, like, Ashton Kutcher and Oprah Winfrey were fighting for who's going to get a million followers.

主持人： I can see why you wanted to do it.

Dick： But zero, zero revenue model, zero business model. All the articles were: "Sure, Twitter's interesting, but they don't have a business model — never make money." And I just kind of had the — man, you don't get an opportunity to get on these rocket ships, and when you get the opportunity, you should do it. So I did it.

进入"集体决策"的混乱

主持人： It's was famously dysfunctional and chaotic. I assume you knew that going in. Was it much more so than you thought, or was it about what the press was saying?

Dick： It's always both, you know.

The things that were different were — it was very much a collective, by which I mean decisions were kind of made as a group. It wasn't, "Okay, well, you're responsible for engineering — figure out what the right answer is." Or, "Should we make retweet a native thing, or should it be — you know, just you write RT at the time and rewrite the tweet." I remember that.

There was a discussion about: "Well, should we just have retweet be a thing you can do in Twitter?" And it would kind of sit around the table, and the group would decide.

So things moved methodically — is a diplomatic way of putting it — and decisions were reached over a period of time. It was chaotic, and it was also just... it didn't move quickly at all.

被任命 CEO：在风雨夜里被告知

主持人： In the documentary that went through pushing Ev out and giving you the CEO job — it sounded awful, the whole process.

Dick： It was awful.

主持人： What was going on?

Dick： I don't really have a side of it other than: it was awful. These things in hindsight happened very, very quickly — and happened super slowly and painfully.

I was informed, like, in the driving rain on the road home by Evan Jason that the board has decided that you should be the CEO. Yeah. But I had been told — and then Bill Campbell called me, like, "You should just quit, you know." And then, "Why am I quitting?" And like, anyway, went…

And then this went, and then like, the board decided actually no, Ev is going to remain CEO, "but you've been great and we're going to vest a bunch of your options, and so long." Okay. And that — I mean, no kidding — over many, many days and weeks.

And finally I was like, "Listen, just write up the separation agreement and I'm good. I'm good. Whatever. I'll go find something. I'll go do something else."

And then, to make a long story short, the board got back together. I was like, "Oh, no, no, no, no." Remember — Bijan Sabet from Spark texted me, "Don't quit." Okay. I was like, "I'm not quitting. You guys told me you wanted me to leave 'cuz I was going to stay." Anyway, it was super crazy.

There was obviously a ton of stuff going on, and long story short, they were finally like, "Look, you got to do it." And really long story short, I was like, "All right, I'll stay and do it."

接手后的第一刀：Velocity, Velocity, Velocity

主持人： Okay, you take over. You're the adult. What did you do initially? It's very tricky — the existing founders were hanging around, probably looking over your shoulder if not breathing down your neck.

Dick： Well, Jack was the chairman of the board still. Ev had made Jack chairman of the board when he made himself CEO. Ev's still on the board. So two of the founders, Ev and Jack, are still on the board. And Biz was in the company. And Jason Goldman's in the company running product, and a bunch of other people.

The first thing that I just felt we had to do was: velocity, velocity, velocity. And that remained a concern of mine throughout my time there — faster, faster, faster. Cadence, cadence, cadence. Like, how can we go faster? Why do things take so long? "If this takes six weeks, what would have to be true for it to be two weeks?"

We just — I did stuff like that constantly because I was always like, "Man, this is like dental surgery." But it was starting from a position of: we're not going to sit around the table and vote on things anymore.

I remember vividly — the status line changed from "What are you doing?" or "What's going on?" to something else, maybe "What's happening?" I don't even remember. I wasn't even involved in it. And one of the front-end engineers, Britt Selvitelle, came into my office. He's like, "Hey man, what's going on?" I was like, "What are you talking about?" He's like, "The status bar changed to 'What's happening?'" I was like, "Okay." He's like, "No one asked me what I thought about that."

I was like, "Oh, man. Yeah, we're not doing that anymore. There's going to start being a lot more of that."

砍掉空洞口号与"明年再说"文化

So that was thing number one. Okay. Thing number two: there was a set of operating principles that were written on a whiteboard that Biz had been involved in — either on his own or with other people — but they were like, you know, sometimes you'll get on a United Airlines flight, and they'll say, "Here at United Airlines, we believe in integrity and honesty." I'm like, "Okay, who cares?" That doesn't help me. It doesn't help me make any decisions, and these things are meaningless — and they're usually weaponized against management by, like, "Well, it says right here…"

So it's like, okay, we got to get rid of these things. They're not useful.

主持人： Did the founders push back?

Dick： Uh, no. They didn't really. And so good for them. We got rid of those.

I remember everything was just kind of messy. There was this kitchen, and it was just kind of a mess — dirty dishes in the sink and everything. I think I sent out an email: "This is why the [bleep] site crashes. Like, you may not get it, but this is why the site crashes — because everything's just kind of, 'Oh well, someone else will clean it up.'"

There was a poster on one of the floors that was like, "We'll make better mistakes next time," or something. And I was like, "Get that poster out of here." It's just sort of indicative of: "Oh well." And then the site would crash again, and people would go like, "Oh well, we'll figure it out. Hopefully won't happen again next year, whatever."

So it was a lot of cleaning that up, and a lot of velocity, velocity, velocity.

组织"藤壶"：为什么大公司越长越慢

主持人： All anybody — CEO — wants to talk about now is speed. Companies seem to slow down as they earn hyper-growth. You get bigger and get less done.

Dick： Yeah. Because barnacles build up on the — you get organizational barnacles.

A couple things initially when we were smaller — this is sub-200 people — what I did to speed things up was:

One, we're not going to make decisions as a group. We're going to push decisions down the stack. By the way, "Don't come to me and ask me what you should do about this unless you have no idea and want my opinion. Figure it out, you know — and don't tell your team what to do. Tell them to figure it out, and push as many decisions down the stack as you can."

I remember telling Jack Dorsey at these dinners we would have from week to week — when he was chairman and was CEO of Block down the street from us — we would get together on Tuesday nights and have dinner. And I remember telling him, "God, I hate hearing 'Dick said' in the company as a reason for things."

"Dick said" 的故事

Because what would happen — I'll give you an example. The Android app started taking longer and longer to boot. So we'd have a metrics meeting. I was like: "Hey man, you can see the usage numbers going down almost linearly with the Android boot time going up. People are firing up Twitter, and it's taking too long, and they're moving on to something else. Over here on iOS, that doesn't happen. It's got to take just as long to boot Android as it does iOS."

Two, three nights later — week later — I'm walking around one of the engineering floors after hours, and this Android engineer comes up to me and goes, "Hey, can I ask you a question?" I'm like, "Yeah." He goes, "Hey, did you say remove the JSON file from the Android boot system?" I was like, "I know what a JSON file is, but I couldn't tell you what's in the Android boot JSON." And he's like, "Okay, because I was told 'Dick said remove that.'"

And I was like, "Aha — I think I know what's going on here." This would happen throughout the company. Some manager-director decides, "Here's a way to solve this problem," and the engineer pushes back against them, and they just go, "You know what — Dick said do it." And it became this reason for doing things.

So part of the speed was: no, we're not going to have someone say that. Push the decisions down the stack. And by the way, if your team makes a decision and you can't defend it to your manager — don't go, "Well, so-and-so said." Have the engineer talk to the director, whatever.

"Bias to Yes"：从 Bezos 偷来的解药

The second thing I did as we got bigger and bigger: I started realizing the organization is slowing down because there are all these FFTs in the organization that want ball control over their stuff. Very common.

Here's how it surfaced. We ran an experiment — "Oh, this is actually really super interesting. What are the hypotheticals about what this experiment would do in production?" The engineer runs through it in an experimentation meeting. "Great. Let's go ahead and launch this to x% of users and see what happens."

One week, two weeks, three weeks go by. I'm like, "Hey, where's this engagement experiment we're running?" And I find the engineer. He's like, "Oh, I'm waiting for Trust and Safety to sign off on it." I'm like, "What?" He's like, "Oh yeah, I asked so-and-so if we could launch this. It was ready to launch. They didn't say no, but they said, 'You have to go get approval from yours of security.'"

I started realizing that when people would try to go do something, they wouldn't be told, "No, you can't do that." It was something much, much worse and slower than that: "You need approval from security. You got to go ask these 14 people."

主持人： Everyone's got this problem. How do you fix it?

Dick： What I did was — it's very similar to what Bezos used to do at Amazon with bias to action. From my improv background, I was like: we're going to have a bias to YES.

Bias to yes is: only the person you report to is allowed to tell you you're not allowed to do that — or legal can tell you, "If you do that, you'll be fired because it's illegal and a violation of the privacy policy." But no other organization, period, is allowed to tell you you can't do that, or that you need to go ask these people for permission. Just your direct manager.

That means if you have an idea for an experiment and your manager signs off on it, and you have time to do it because you're done with whatever else you're working on, no one can tell you you can't do that — the marketing team's not allowed to tell you you can't use the Mailchimp software we license because that's the marketing team's, right?

Bias to yes was: "I don't want to hear about something being stalled because organization X said you have to go get approval from organization Y." And that was great. Experiments started flying out. In fact, engineers started going like, "Wait, I don't have to necessarily get design to build all these wireframes for something. I can just do it myself, since we're only launching it to 1% and see if it works."

I would repeatedly get on stage at all-hands meetings and talk about bias to yes — what it means and what it doesn't mean. It doesn't mean you can stop doing what you're supposed to do, because it's a priority — and do whatever you want to do — because your manager is going to fire you.

不要用流程解决问题

主持人： No group decisions. DRI. Bias to action. Anything else that all these CEOs I work with are like, "We're slowing down. How do we speed up?"

Dick： Don't solve problems with processes.

It happens all the time in organizations: some customer problem happens, the customer churns out or the advertiser quits. And the CEO goes, "That can never happen again." And so what happens? Some step gets added to some process.

I remember at Google famously the launch checklist got to be like 17 pages long or something, and Larry was like, "This is insane." I was like, "Yeah, it's because — it's like the FDA: never another thalidomide. Or the TSA: make sure there's no water bottle on the plane." And pretty soon you're not managing to outcomes, you're managing to processes. And you got to go through nine steps — instead of just managing to the outcome of "make sure there's not a bomb on the plane."

The way you do that is: you solve problems with DRIs, operating control, and not processes. You should probably only really have a couple of organizational processes, and everything else should be span of control and decision-making.

When you do that — and this was something I had to come to understand and adapt to — you can't punish people when things go wrong. You want them to take risks. You want them to be bold. You want them to have this bias to action and not tiptoeing around asking for permission.

But one of the implications is: you can't punish people for making mistakes. I would tell the team: it's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening. It's the job of leadership to correct mistakes quickly when they happen.

If you screw something up, by all means, tell us so we can help you fix it. But no one should get in trouble for screwing something up or making a mistake. I didn't come to that immediately, but I came to realize that was an important artifact of pushing decisions down the stack and bias to yes.

沟通架构：从 50 人到 150 人的鸿沟

主持人： Love it. So many of the CEOs I work with — I was just at a company in New York City and spoke at their company meeting. Here's how I would describe every company: when it's under 50 employees, everybody knows what's going on all the time. When it's over 150 employees, nobody knows what's going on anytime. How did you scale communication, keep people on the same page? And tell me a little bit about management by walking around.

Dick： Well, I did two things.

First of all, I taught this management class at Twitter. It was actually based on Ben Horowitz teaching a management class at LoudCloud.

主持人： Fascinating.

Dick： And Ben had in his outline from his management class a statement that I, like, highlighted and underlined and circled three times: "Make sure everybody understands what you understand."

So I would always tell my managers: "Your job is to make sure everybody understands what the priorities are and what it means for them and their team — how they fit into those priorities and, more importantly for your team, what it means for them personally and professionally if we achieve what we have to achieve."

砌砖工与大教堂

I remember somebody telling me a story of taking a factory tour at a Tesla shop and seeing some guy who's screwing a widget into the car. They asked the guy, "What are you doing?" And the guy didn't just say "screwing the widget into the car." He said, "Well, if I do this, and we're able to build these kinds of cars, and these kinds of cars can become more popular in America…" The guy understood the story.

主持人： It's like the analogy — somebody asks a brick layer, "What are you doing?" And the brick layer says, "Well, I'm laying bricks." And the other bricklayer says, "I'm building a beautiful cathedral."

Dick： I love that. How much more motivating is it to work in a company where, if I'm successful in my work, I understand what the implications are for me personally and the company?

Priorities and context. Good news travels fast; bad news travels slow. As the org gets bigger, the signal gets distorted.

主持人： How does the CEO deal with that?

Dick： I said earlier — it's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it's to help correct them quickly. So I would always communicate: "You got to tell leadership if something's screwed up." If your situation is screwed up, or your team, or some decision is a mess — you have to communicate that constantly.

And secondly, I would regularly communicate the priorities to the team over and over at all-hands meetings. "Here's what the priorities are that we're working on. This is how we're going to measure them. This is why they're important, and this is what they mean for us as a company if we're successful."

I always wanted people to understand not just what we had to do, but why it was important to do it.

偷师 Steve Jobs：导演不在场的会议

One last thing I picked up — a Steve Jobs trick.

主持人： Okay. I love a Steve Jobs trick.

Dick： When Steve was managing both Pixar and Apple — so he's CEO of Pixar still, but now he's back in the seat at Apple. So now he's only at Pixar one day a week, because there's all the stuff he's got to go fix at Apple.

So they're making Toy Story 2. What Steve would do — he's got one day to come up to speed on what the heck's going on at Pixar — he'd go into the office on Friday and say, "All right, today I want to sit down with the illustrators, without Lasseter, the director of the movie, John, in the room. I just want to sit down with the illustrators."

He'd go around — there's like eight or nine of them — and say, "Janet, tell me something that's not working well at Pixar." And then, "Bob, do you agree with that?" — taking notes. "Okay, Phyllis, tell me something that's working well at Pixar. Not on the movie, not about your job."

At the end of the meeting — 45 minutes, one hour — he'd say, "Okay, here's what I heard. People are having these kinds of issues. This is going really well. I'm going to go talk to John about it, and he'll deal with the rest of this on Monday. You can follow up with him."

So he's not saying, "Oh yeah, that sucks," or "No, you're wrong, whatever." He's taking notes, making sure he hears everything, and then going to talk to Lasseter about it.

I started doing that with teams. You quickly find out if that team knows what the priorities are — is in sync with what you're hearing from the leadership of that team. And some of the leaders of the company hated it. And I was like, "Well, now I'm concerned about why you hate it." But I wouldn't — you're not in that meeting throwing the director under the bus. You're going, "Okay, here's what I heard. I'm going to go talk to my management team about it on Monday, and Chris or whoever will follow up with you guys."

优先级：什么时候该改，什么时候不该改

主持人： Let me switch to you on priorities. All these CEOs I coach — they have their list of priorities. It's January 1, but oh my god, there's so much opportunity, so much interesting stuff, and this employee has this thing going on. How often did you rejigger those priorities? Were you disciplined about it?

Dick： Yeah, you can rejigger them once in a while. Once or twice a year you can go, "Huh, this one — let's actually change that one to this other thing."

If you start doing it regularly, people are like, "Well, we can get to work on the direct messaging architecture, but Dick's probably going to — 50/50 he's going to change it." You just can't do stuff like that. By the way, that's another way companies slow down: people are like, "Look, I'm not going to bust my ass on this thing when there's a 50% chance Dick's going to throw it out the window in a month."

单向门 vs 双向门：聚焦在 AI 时代是否过时？

主持人： Let me test something on you. A lot of these hyper-growers — it used to be that there were a lot of one-way doors in the software industry. Stuff took a long time to get out the door, even to test. And you had to do things serially. Now you can do so much so fast. So they're just doing more, and it's more parallel. A lot of those one-way doors are two-way doors. Is focus overrated now?

Dick： No. I don't think focus is ever overrated. However, however — the fact that you can do more quickly, and since they're two-way doors, you're like, great.

This is the old Jeff Bezos: only take a long time to make decisions that are existential. We might die as a company if we make the wrong decision. There's fewer and fewer of those. Decisions that, if we screw it up, we can go back and do the other one — do those right away.

So focus is still important, but it's also true that you should feel like you can do a lot more now with less, because you can.

Bezos 与 Steve Jobs 的两种成功

There was a point in 2011 when we wanted to try to get Jeff Bezos to join the board of the company. We had an independent seat open. Who would be the best possible independent director? Jeff Bezos.

So Jack and I go meet with Jeff Bezos — I think it was at the Allen & Co. conference. Jeff asks a question about strategy, and Steve Jobs was still alive at the time. And Jack said, "Well, Steve says the most important part about being a CEO is focus. And you should say no to almost everything. In fact, it's the CEO's job to be the editor-in-chief. You should say no to almost everything and do very, very few things."

And Bezos looks at Jack and goes, "Oh, yeah. Well, I like to do everything." And he does the big Bezos laugh. He goes, "My team has to talk me out of stuff. There's lots of ways to be successful."

I remember that like it was yesterday. And he's right.

主持人： There's many ways to climb the mountain.

Dick： Today he would say, "Great, I can do even more now. We can still be focused, but I can do even more things." Those things can both be true.

巡场管理：晚上 9 点的走廊

主持人： Just want to get back to some of your management techniques. This management by walking around — a very old-school HP concept. It sounds like you did it the same time every week.

Dick： I did it at all hours. And mostly late night. It started because — Bill Campbell, for people who don't know listening to the podcast, Bill was on the board of Apple and famously coached Larry, Sergey, and Eric at Google. I wrote a book about him called Trillion Dollar Coach. The Benchmark folks had also brought him into Twitter before I got there.

Bill famously said when I took over as CEO: "You could roll a grenade into Twitter at 5:30 p.m. and only take out the cleaning people." And he was right.

So I was like, "Okay, you can't just tell people, 'Work harder.'" They're like, "Okay — you work harder."

So what I did was — it's such a stupid thing, but I was like, "All right, I'm going to go out, get dinner, come back at 9, 9:30 p.m. and walk around. And whoever's here, I'm going to talk about the stuff they're doing at the next all-hands meeting, and I'm going to bump up the prioritization of that."

I remember Marcel Molina — at-no-radio on Twitter; that's a little promotion for Marcel there. Marcel was always working late on stuff, and I would go, "What are you working on?" — "I'm working on native retweet" or whatever. And I would get up in front of the company and go, "The work Marcel is doing…" And people were like, "How does he always know what Marcel is doing?" And other people would go, "Oh, Dick comes back after dinner at night and walks around."

People slowly, slowly started to change the culture. "Okay — the people who are around when Dick's walking around are getting their stuff prioritized."

I walked around Friday afternoons. And I learned a lot. I would just go up to engineers and go, "What's going on? What are you working on?"

森林防火学：从战术到战略

主持人： You have an interesting expression that I don't understand about forest fires in forestry management. What the hell are you talking about?

Dick： I was trying to help a director in the company be more strategic. They were super tactical. And I was finally like, "Your job as a director, as you grow in this company, is — you can't just say, 'Well, I put out this forest fire, and I put out…' Your job is to map the territory and start to do forestry management."

"What are we going to do so that next year, if there's a fire over here, that highway is not going to be in trouble? You can only work so many hours a day, and stamping out fires isn't going to work as we scale. You need to push those down the stack. Your team should be fighting the fire that's over there, because it might burn a house down or something. You need to be focused more on thinking about this whole pie — what has to be true 24 months from now so this whole territory is in better shape."

That was the metaphor I had for helping people try to think strategically — versus, "Well, I'm chopping down the trees. Look at all the trees!" That's not leadership and strategy — that's tactics.

主持人： I like that. Okay, now I get it.

关于"Dick 不是 passive aggressive，而是 aggressive"

主持人： One of the people I connected with before interviewing you said something interesting about you.

Dick： Oh boy, I can hardly wait. I'm sure most people did, but anyway —

主持人： Both sides of this are interesting. He said: "You know, Silicon Valley, San Francisco, everyone's passive aggressive." And so I'm curious your take on that — because I don't live in Silicon Valley. And the second thing he said: "Dick's not passive aggressive. Dick's aggressive."

Dick： I'm super impatient — I know this is a personal flaw. People would come in and go, "Here's why I'm sad," and they would start talking to me, and I'd be like, "Okay, stop. It seems like you don't like working here. You should quit."

"No, I want to tell you I'm sad." "Okay, I don't care. Who cares? I'm sorry, I'm not trying to be a jerk, but we have a million things to do."

I'm just direct and blunt with people. I'm not a jerk. A lot of times — especially maybe engineering leaders who came up in the organization coding and programming on their own, and now have to manage people — they think when you tell them, "Look, you have to manage performance in your team and you have to manage poor performers out," they think you have to be a jerk when you do that.

You don't have to be a jerk about it. You just have to be direct, forthright, and not, you know — "No one's sadder about this than me." Don't say stupid stuff like that. You can be empathetic and still be straightforward.

给艰难的反馈：写下来，然后照念

主持人： A lot of these CEOs I coach are in their mid-late 20s. In their whole life, they haven't given — they don't go to Mom, "I got some feedback on dinner." Or, "You're hogging the basketball." You've just never given a homo sapien feedback. It's unnatural. Advice for that CEO?

Dick： Bill Campbell actually gave me this advice early on. He was like: "Write down what you want to say, and then say that — and don't say anything else."

The night before — if you write down the honest feedback you would give Brian, especially if I have to give Brian a bad performance review — the night before, if you write it down, I guarantee you, 99 out of 100 people, when they go into the meeting the next day, don't say that.

They're like, "Listen, Brian — you know, it's really a hard time here for everybody. We're all working…" Just shut the [bleep] up. The person knows you're about to hit them over the head or something.

And you also don't have to say, "Hey, Brian, that's just the way it is. Too bad if you don't like it." Just write down what you want to say, and be forthright and honest. You can say, "I understand, if you don't want to deal with it anymore, I get it." Say those things, and don't add a bunch of fluff around it.

I thought that was great feedback, and I would do that.

主持人： Sit with the silence.

Dick： Sit with the silence.

主持人： Sit with silence is hard. It's unnatural.

Dick： It's unnatural. The best person — I used to do a thing in my management class. It was a six-hour course. One of the things I would do was about giving people feedback. Like: "Brian, you're going to give Dick feedback. The feedback is, you've been working on this thing for six weeks, busting your ass, here till midnight every night — and we have to cancel the project, because it's no longer a priority."

Something horrible that no one wants to hear. The only person who did the role-play that was ever good at sitting in silence was Elad Gil, current venture capitalist. Elad sat in the chair and gave the person the feedback.

I would say to the IC before the role-play — pull them aside and go, "You're — this is [bleep], and you're going to tell Dick, and you don't want to hear this, and it shouldn't be cancelled. They need to look at the data, whatever."

And Elad was great at just sitting there. It's hard. And then eventually, the other person runs out of gas and stops talking. Elad was great at that — and the only person who ever did it.

招聘哲学：从内部提拔 + 非共识聘用

主持人： One of the things I want to ask you about — you had some very talented folks. Some of which you brought in: Adam Bain, Kevin Weil, Anthony, Elad. How did you guys recruit? What did you look for? Are you looking for slope or experience? What was your filter?

Dick： It's a great question. I actually tried to promote from within as much as possible, because you want people inside the company to feel like, "We're not going to get capped out — at which point, I can only get to this level, because anything above that, Dick just goes out and finds someone."

So Kevin Weil, for example, was promoted from within from an IC when I got there in 2009. There were only 40 people or so in the company when I got there. He eventually ran all of product, after running all of ads product. So he was promoted from within.

Anthony Noto：从高盛挖来

Noto — I just felt like Twitter was such drama. The drama queen of hypergrowth companies. When I took over as CEO that year, the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two different magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker: "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal — no dead birds on magazine covers."

It was just constant drama. And Noto was so good at Goldman Sachs, where he ran technology and media at dealing with investors and communicating with them, etc. I had been trying for a while to bring him into Twitter, and I was finally able to. And he's fantastic.

Adam Bain：非共识聘用

主持人： How about Adam?

Dick： Adam was funny. Adam was a non-consensus hire when I first recruited him. It was while I was COO, and I went to the board and Ev and said, "I got the perfect person to run revenue at Twitter. It's this guy Adam Bain. He's in LA and he works for Fox Interactive."

And everyone was like, "You're going to bring in someone who works selling ads — at Fox in LA — and bring them to San Francisco to run our revenue side of the business?"

But he was the perfect person. I must have flown down to LA for five or six months before I could convince him to do it. I had met him while we were in the process of selling FeedBurner. His employer, News Corp, was one of the interested parties before Google bought it. And I got to know him. I was like, "Man, this guy is tireless, and we're going to be in a constant dogfight, fighting from behind against Facebook for social ad dollars." And Adam is one of those people who's got a motor that doesn't stop. He was the perfect person for the role.

"Hire Slow, Fire Fast" —— 但他做不到

主持人： There's an old adage: fire fast, hire slow.

Dick： I always said that out loud. I didn't do that. I fired slow — and almost every time, I was like, "Man, I should have. At six weeks I knew."

主持人： Were you disciplined about that?

Dick： I tried to hold myself accountable to that. And it's horrible. There's nothing worse than having to do something like that.

I always am a little bit like, "Come on" — when people are like, "I like firing people, because it's the right thing for them." That just sounds like you're an [bleep], because it's horrible. But you have to do it.

6AM 冷汗醒来

I asked David Rosenblatt — one of my board members — because I had to terminate someone with global operating responsibility. I was like, "Wait a minute, if I fire him in the morning, the people in Japan are going to be in bed. But if I fire him in the afternoon, then the people in… you know."

I was talking to my board about it. David Rosenblatt, who had been the CEO of DoubleClick before it sold to Google, was like, "Oh, I had to do that. I actually saved the calendar date from that, because I had a whole series of communications I had to roll out." This is — we're now a couple-thousand-person company, etc., with 30 offices around the world.

So I get his "day-of" communications, and the only thing I remember from it — it was a whole layout of how to go about it — the only thing I remember from it is: "6 a.m.: wake up in a cold sweat." It's just horrible. You can't wait till it's over. But you have to do it.

Jeff Weiner 的"投手类比"

Most — Jeff Weiner's old analogy from when he was LinkedIn CEO when I was running Twitter. Jeff Weiner would call it — for those of you who are baseball fans — "You know, sometimes you're watching a baseball game, and you see the pitching coach or the manager walk out to the mound and talk to the pitcher. The pitcher's like, 'No, no, no — totally, don't worry, coach. I still got it.' And the manager walks back to the dugout, and this guy cranks one out of the park again. And you can see the manager walking out, like, 'I knew it. I knew it.'"

Jeff was like, "I always tell myself, don't be the manager having the first conversation with the person, when you know what you should be doing is having the second conversation with the person."

主持人： As a Red Sox fan, that really hits home.

Dick： I think his example is actually Pedro — Pedro Martinez from the Red Sox.

主持人： Grady Little — take him out.

Dick： Yes.

超高速增长公司的陷阱

主持人： I work with all these hyper-growth companies — I think you do too. What are the pitfalls when you're in hypergrowth, and what are your tips for the CEOs listening today who are growing incredibly fast?

Dick： One of the real pitfalls — especially for these hyper-growth AI companies that are going from single-digit-million revenue to 100 million — is it's very easy to take your eye off the ball when you raise too much money. By taking your eye off the ball, I mean: thinking about operating leverage and operating efficiency.

Once the money is there, you're like, "I don't have to worry about that. I'll go worry about this thing over here." And next thing you know, the burn is — you know — "We have…" "What are you worried about? We have…" It happens all the time.

And Brian wrote this great post saying, "Look, I thought I was Mr. Efficiency. We're going to be super — it's not going to be the same for us, we're going to be super capital-efficient." But when you don't have to worry about it at all, and there are a million other things to do, you stop worrying about it.

主持人： Are people raising too much money?

Dick： I think so. In many, many cases. I don't know that the models — the models probably need all the money for compute. But for an app company — yeah, I think people are raising too much money. A lot of companies are raising too much money, because raising a hundred-million Series B is the new $20 million Series B.

雇佣失控：A+ 玩家的神话

主持人： What else than hypergrowth — what breaks in hypergrowth?

Dick： Hiring. My favorite thing is — you talk to a CEO who's like, "I don't know, whatever. Pick some number. First company they were lead PM at — awesome fintech company X — and now they're CEO." And they're like, "We're only going to hire A+ players." I'm like, "Yeah. PS, guess what — everyone says that."

Come back to me when your customer success team has to hire twice as many people next quarter, because you just churned a bunch of customers because no one checked in with them last quarter, because you're growing like a weed. And then tell me. You just end up not doing that.

So one of the things you have to do, when you come to the realization that we don't only hire A+ players, is: you have to get religious about — we have to manage out low performers. But as you move down the stack, and you've got inexperienced managers who are first-time managers, first-time directors, there's nothing they want to do less than let someone go.

They'll do anything to not do it. You prevent them from "transfiring" people. Transfiring is like, "Janet would be great on the ads team. She loves ads. You need people."

So we had performance reviews. I was like, "If your review is below an X number, you can't switch teams." Like — that's a good idea. Let's say it's one through four. Four is amazing. One is, "You got to go." If you're a two, you can't move teams until you're no longer a two.

PS: if you go two-to-three-to-two, I'm going to talk to your manager again and go, "Don't let me guess. This person's going to be a three again this next term. So you don't have to put them on a performance plan."

We would, after performance reviews, always go through as a senior team: "Okay, I can see from the way these eight people are bouncing from two to three to back to two to back to three — like, you're just trying to not fire people. I can see your curve is inflated, blah blah blah." So we would just be super hardcore about that.

与巨头竞争：怎么打 Facebook，怎么打 OpenAI

主持人： Let's talk about competition for a second. You were competing with Facebook. They were the juggernaut. We just had the hardest time. Tomorrow I'm speaking at another company meeting — they're going to be competing with OpenAI and Anthropic. Almost every boardroom I'm in is talking about that. Everyone's anxious. Their employees are anxious. Advice?

Dick： First of all, you have to acknowledge it. And then — one of the things I would always do is, like, they're huge, and Mark is the most competitive person on the planet. Trust me when I tell you — they don't say to themselves, "Oh whatever, Twitter won that thing, that's fine." They're like, "That can never happen again."

So we have to think — and we're way smaller than them and have far fewer resources — we have to think about them, talk about them, understand what they're doing, and try to figure out how to out-hustle them.

Adam Bain 的销售反间计

Adam Bane on the revenue team was great at that. He would come into customer meetings at Consumer Electronics Show at the beginning of the year — let's say it's Unilever — and he would sit down with the CMO from Unilever before the meeting and say, "How'd the Facebook meeting go?" — "Oh great, blah blah." — "What are they psyched to show you this year?"

And then he would learn all that, and go into the meeting with Unilever and say — even if we had a bunch of things to show them — "We have some things we want to show you, but we really want to spend the next two hours today just understanding your business: what you guys are concerned about, what your problems are." Knowing she had just told him the Facebook people came in and showed us this and this, and weren't asking those kinds of questions.

Now, Facebook wised up to that over time, etc. But Adam had come up with a great way of thinking about how to compete with this juggernaut. The juggernaut's rolling out new things every quarter. It's just good salesmanship. He's like, "I can't compete with their eight new things and their micro-targeting, blah blah blah. So I'm going to totally come at it from a different angle." And it was just smart about learning how to do that by talking to the customer beforehand.

商业模式的反思

主持人： You guys picked the advertising model to make money. As you look back at that —

Dick： Good call. Yes. Advertising is undefeated. The ARPU is higher than subscription revenue, etc. I think we would do the same thing over again.

最大的遗憾：错失 Instagram

主持人： Anything else as you look back, like, "Man, I wish I had a do-over on that one"?

Dick： Oh yeah. We tried to buy Instagram the month — month and a half — before Facebook bought it. And by the way, for a much higher percentage of the company. Facebook's offer is famously a billion dollars — I think it was 700 of stock and 300 million of cash. They only had eight or nine employees at the time — Mike Krieger and Kevin Systrom.

But we knew well before that, like, these guys are — Kevin is an extraordinary product manager. Kevin and Evan Spiegel I think are probably two of the [great] product thinkers in Silicon Valley. So I knew that, and well before Mark made his offer to them, I pulled Kevin — went out to dinner with Kevin and Mike — and told them we wanted to buy the company. Would end up giving them basically well over 10% of Twitter, and something equivalent to $700 million in stock. And we were maybe valued at $5 billion at the time.

If I could go back there and talk to Kevin, I would say: "You can run the company when I leave. Congrats — you'll get the BC." I mean, may still not have done it. And I would also say: "Also, I'll go borrow $300 million from JP Morgan and give it to you." And you can have it all, and we'll pay them back later.

Because that was a game-changer. Once they had Instagram, I was like, "Oh man, we're boxed out — that is the future of rich-media social media feeds. We have Vine, but it's much smaller, and they're not going to come at us on Instagram with video" — which of course they did. And that was sort of checkmate.

内容审核：ISIS 与《纽约邮报》

主持人： Other regrets? Any regrets on — man, the content moderation stuff has been in the news over the last 5 years.

Dick： The thing I'll say about content moderation that people get wrong all the time is, "This is really easy. Just prevent people from doing that." You're like, "Okay, well, that is a lyric in a Kanye song. Can you not tweet his lyrics?"

I'll give you a specific example that I always think highlights why it was such a pain in the ass. These companies try to come up with a set of rules — "If it violates the rule, you have to kick it off; and if it doesn't violate the rule, you can't kick it off." You just can't think about it that way. These things are so subjective.

So — ISIS, the terrorist organization, captures three pilots from some Air Force, puts them in cages basically on a beach, lights them on fire and kills them — basically burns them to death. And they post these to Twitter — photos and videos.

My general counsel comes to me and goes, "Hey, these terrorist accounts are…" I'm like, "Suspend the accounts, right?" "Yeah, of course." Right away. Anyone that posts the media of these pilots being lit on fire — suspend the account.

Well, the freaking New York Post an hour later posts the picture — huge photo of this pilot being lit on fire. And we suspend it. And then I get this: "Hey, why'd you suspend the New York Post?" "What? We didn't suspend the New York Post." "Yeah, you did."

And you're like, "Okay, wait. I guess the New York Post can do it — but now who can't do it?" That was one tiny example. It was just so infinitely hard.

The big "if I could go back again": you just can't have these hard and fast rules. There has to be operating control and somebody with an understanding of the situation, like our GC. You're going to just have a bunch of judgment calls, and you're going to get a bunch of them wrong — and that's life.

关于政治倾向

主持人： And after your tenure — I don't know what to believe, but it looks like there was a lot of conversation between the Biden administration and that group, and a lot of accusations from Elon that that group was just very left of center in their orientation. Any reaction to that? Was that the case when you were there?

Dick： Probably it was left of center. But I don't think when I was there that it impacted the way we thought about abuse. We didn't have any "Oh, you're not allowed to dead-name trans people" — that stuff was all post me being there.

The stuff we were dealing with when I was there were ridiculous amounts of Russian bots — which at the time were not election stuff. Maybe they were preparing the groundwork for 2016. But it was mostly phishing attacks and massive bot farms doing phishing attacks on people, trying to hack into their accounts or get credit card information.

怎么承受坏新闻铺天盖地

主持人： Most of the CEOs who have been around a while have got a [bleep] ton of bad press. You got a metric ton of bad press. It must — I got a bunch of bad press. It impacted me. I tried to show a face like it didn't, but it was soul-crushing to me. How did you handle it?

Dick： I had had the fortunate opportunity to be a performer and do improv comedy and stuff, and auditioned for SNL a couple times before I went back into business.

I remember I was at the Adelaide Comedy Festival with my improv group. We had this midnight show at the Adelaide Opera House. And the crowd is hammered. Drunk — maybe a thousand people. And it's an hour-and-10-minute show, completely improvised. We get on stage, and like five minutes in, the crowd starts yelling, "You suck! Get off!" And I'm like, "Man, we got 80 more minutes."

So I just became resilient.

主持人： Did it hurt inside?

Dick： I don't know. The good press never hit for me. I'll tell you — my daughter called me. This is at the end of 2014. So 2013 — we go public, IPO successful. Everyone's super excited. Yay us. And there's some stupid awards that come out — "CEO of the Year, Dick Costolo."

End of 2014, my daughter texts me. She's like, "I have bad news and good news." I was like, "What's the bad news?" She's like, "Yahoo Finance says you're one of the five worst CEOs of 2014." I was like, "What's the good news?" She goes, "Nobody reads Yahoo Finance."

不去 Vanity Fair 奥斯卡派对

I'll say one last thing. I got invited to go to like the Vanity Fair Oscars party one of the years.

主持人： By the way, that must have been cool.

Dick： Well, I got invited to go. I didn't go. I told my — good question — I told my daughter, "I don't want to start going to a bunch of stuff that I get invited to because I'm the CEO of Twitter, because then I'll be like, 'Oh, I don't want to not be the CEO of Twitter, because I won't get invited to…'"

So I went to the NBA All-Star Game because I knew Adam Silver, and he invited — I felt like it was a Dick Costolo invitation. I'll go to things I get invited to because I'm Dick Costolo. I don't want to go to things that the CEO of Twitter gets invited to, because then I'll be like, "Wasn't it part of your job?"

I mean, going to the White House — sure. "Hey, the President wants to talk to you about Edward Snowden, and abuse on the platform, and what are we going to do about terrorists using the services?" Yeah, you got to go to that as part of the job. But going to the Oscars or whatever — I don't know any of these people. They don't care about [me].

主持人： Totally would have done it.

Dick： Yeah, I totally would have.

主持人： Well, you would have been standing over in the corner going, "No one's talking. Tom Cruise won't talk. Tom Cruise isn't talking to me."

创始人 vs 成熟 CEO

主持人： It's exceptionally rare these days that somebody brings a founder company — replaced. They don't hire the adult external CEOs. Should it be happening a lot more?

Dick： I think it goes in waves.

主持人： It doesn't happen at all these days.

Dick： Yeah, I know. I think it goes in waves. There will continue to be — something will happen. People will start going, "You know, we really need, for these companies that are making $4 billion in revenue after three years, and the 26-year-old founders are…" Something will happen, and there will be a wave of operating executives coming back in to run some of the companies.

It happens a little bit — like Snowflake, you know, pretty rare.

主持人： Even the public companies — like MongoDB, my Workday recently — but maybe one a year.

Dick： It's true. I do think it goes in waves. We'll see if it happens.

想成为 Dick Costolo？

主持人： Okay. You're a VP at blah blah company, and you want to be a Dick Costolo. You want to be that adult.

Dick： Do what Anthony Noto did, when he was CFO at Twitter. Start telling executive recruiters: "I want a CEO role. When you start CEO roles — I might not get one right away, but I want to talk to you about CEO roles when you get them."

Get your name out there. I never took a risk that I wasn't super psyched I took. Even — I was trying to get on SNL and wasn't successful — it ended up benefiting me down the road. Because it was like, "All right, like, live TV on CNBC, are you okay doing that?" I'm like, "Look at the camera. I got booed at midnight in Australia by a thousand people. I can look in the camera and talk about this."

主持人： Should all CEOs take — my co-founder took a comedy class in business school. It really helped him.

Dick： Being on stage and being uncomfortable being on stage — I mean, you stand up at an all-hands meeting. "I know exactly what I'm going to say at the all-hands meeting. I'm just going to talk about the priorities." That's easy after doing that.

旧金山真的"被动攻击"吗？

主持人： Okay, let me give you a fun one. Is San Francisco passive-aggressive? Is everyone passive-aggressive? Are CEOs passive-aggressive?

Dick： I think there's a lot of passive-aggressiveness. I know the person you talked to, and I know the other companies he's been at. I think he's generally right.

But there are some great exceptions. The Collison brothers at Stripe are that rare combination of really, really widely-read polymaths — have a broad perspective on humanity and biology and the human condition — and are forthright communicators, neither passive-aggressive nor hyper-aggressive. They're forthright, straightforward, and blunt. I think those guys are great.

And I feel the same way about Reed Hastings — totally different executive at Netflix. Reed is always like, "This is just how it is. I'm not trying to be mean about it. This is just the way it is."

主持人： You rhyme with Reed in my head.

Dick： I take that as a huge compliment. I love that guy.

I always thought those three people — John and Patrick at Stripe, and Reed at Netflix — are extremely self-aware. I was at a table with Reed at a dinner once, and he went around the table and said, "I want to hear everybody's opinion on this. How much of your success do you think is based on your DNA, and how much of it is based on, 'I just learned how to kick ass and be amazing'?"

I was like, "I think I know where he's going with this." Of course, when he got around to himself, he was like, "I think it's basically like 80% DNA."

主持人： Interesting. How would you answer that?

Dick： Similarly. I think that's a result of him being — luck versus skill. There's certainly a bunch of luck. But I don't think he was saying luck versus skill. I think he was saying, "It's basically encoded in you to be this way, and it's ended up helping you. And sure, there's like 20% luck along the way that put you in the position to get this role you might not have otherwise had — but you were always going to do something like this."

没有唯一剧本：黄仁勋的 60 个直接下属

主持人： You brought up some good CEOs. The CEO rulebook feels to me to be changing. Your take on how Jensen Huang runs that with like 60 direct reports, no one-on-ones, criticizing? He's got a whole different playbook. The thing that surprises me is — I work with all these CEOs — nobody's copying him. Which I think is interesting. Your take on that?

Dick： I go back to the Jeff Bezos comment to Jack Dorsey. Jack — there are lots of different ways to be successful.

The mistake CEOs make is they read the Walter Isaacson book on Elon Musk, or they read the one on Steve Jobs, and they think, "This is the archetype." And Walter just left out a whole bunch of stuff.

主持人： Did you have an archetype?

Dick： No, I didn't have an archetype.

主持人： Steve Jobs was mine. I tried to copy everything he did. I did it quite poorly.

Dick： It goes again to: "Well, Steve says you have to be an editor," and you say the name, and Jeff saying, "Oh yeah, well, there's lots of different ways to do it."

The reason I say that about the books is not just because they're more mythology than they are what really happened. Some of it of course happened — but it leaves out a bunch of stuff. More importantly, if you try to be someone you're not, nobody is fooled. Like, you think people are fooled — nobody is fooled. Everyone knows you're spinning them, or you're playing some role, and you're not yourself. And nothing is more miserable than trying to be someone you're not.

哈基姆·奥拉朱旺 vs 查尔斯·巴克利

I used to do this in my management class — actually during one of the NBA Finals back in like the 90s. There's a reporter in the Houston Rockets locker room. It was at the time when Charles Barkley did this ad for Nike where Charles dunks a basketball, looks at the camera and goes, "I ain't no role model" — something like that.

This reporter is in the Houston Rockets locker room and asks Hakeem Olajuwon —

主持人： He wasn't wrong.

Dick： He wasn't wrong. Although he appears to be on Ozempic or something. He's got to have his suits retailored.

The reporter goes up to Hakeem and says, "Charles Barkley says that kids shouldn't idolize NBA players — look up to them as role models. What do you think about that?"

And Hakeem Olajuwon goes, "I'm happy to be a role model. I love being a role model for kids." She's like, "Well, what do you—" And Hakeem says, "Well, here's the deal. Charles is a different person in public than he is in private. And so he's got this anxiety and tension in his mind about whether he's supposed to be public Charles in this moment, or private Charles in this moment. I'm always the same person. So if people want to look up to me as a role model, that's fine with me."

I just think that it's nothing but misery if you're trying to look at some written-down playbook of how Steve Jobs did things, and "I got to go do that" — when it was almost impossible for Steve to do it, and you're certainly not going to be able to do it, and you'll be miserable trying.

董事会建议：早一点引入运营经验

主持人： Okay, last question I have. The Twitter board, man — there was a lot of freaking drama there.

Dick： Tons. So much.

主持人： You're building that. You're a $100 million company going to 400 million. You're building a board. Advice — should you bring in independents early? Should you wait?

Dick： I had two board members that were immensely helpful to me. Peter Chernin, who I had brought in from News Corp as an independent director, because he'd managed tens of thousands of people. I needed someone with operating experience — global operating experience. Peter was great at always doing the sort of Jeff Bezos "let's talk about this from the user's perspective" instead of "How are we going to do XYZ?" He would always back up and say, "Wait — if I'm the chief user officer at this company, and I'm thinking about this from the…" That was immensely helpful in lots of cases.

And secondly, Peter Fenton from Benchmark would always help me interview candidates and either close them, or help me with, "Hey, how should I think about this candidate?" Having someone on your board who's not in the day-to-day at the company — and who can also give the candidate a third-party perspective on, "Why I think this is going to be a monster, and why I think you should do this" — was crazy helpful and helped convince numerous execs like Adam Bain and Anthony to come in.

主持人： Okay. You're at 100 million, doing your Series B — do you bring in independents? What happens now is they wait right before the IPO.

Dick： No. I think it's helpful to have operating people with operating expertise in there much sooner than that.

公司应该晚一点上市

主持人： While we're on the IPO — you took Twitter public. It's been incredible — companies going public have become very unfashionable. Take companies public — your [view]?

Dick： I would have kept it private longer if I could go back and do anything over again. It's just so much easier running a private company — and not having to like, "Okay, well, that's going to mean we're going to miss the revenue number by a percent. It's the right thing to do, but the employees are going to be running around like, 'What's wrong?' when the stock goes down 8% after-hours."

You don't have a daily ticker that the employees are freaking out about when you're a private company. It's a lot easier to focus on the long term — as opposed to, "Okay, now we have to have a long-term-versus-short-term discussion about this, because everything's going to go sideways if we do this in Q1 instead of…" You get it.

It's just a lot easier to run a private company.

主持人： I wanted to talk to you for a long time, because you are kind of a rare species these days — the adult CEO brought into a hyper-growth company, and you delivered. Thanks for coming on the podcast.

Dick： Thanks for having me.

主持人收尾：复盘要点

Okay, I thought Dick was great. It's a master class on how do you scale and be a terrific scale-up CEO. There's a few things he talked about that I think are worth reviewing — some of which I did, some of which I wish I did when I was CEO of HubSpot.

Management by walking around: One thing he did that I totally did. I'd wait until like 7 o'clock at night and I'd walk the halls of HubSpot through the dev organization, support, sales — who was around — and really hear what was happening. That was super useful for me. Great context, and also good to see who was burning the midnight oil.

Tough feedback: I liked his feedback around giving tough people tough feedback. I was never very good at this, and his advice is good. The night before you're going to give feedback to that tough person — it's not great feedback — write it down the night before. Practice it in the mirror. I think that's good advice. And when you deliver it, you deliver it and you sit in silence. No fluff. You sit in silence. Really hard to do — but I think that's great advice.

Killing committees, using DRIs: A lot of the CEOs I've interviewed are talking about similar things. Particularly important as you've got silos in your organization, and the important stuff cuts across boundaries.

Ben Horowitz's gem: He gave a great quote from another podcast guest, Ben Horowitz: "Make sure everybody understands what you understand" — not just the what, but the why, and what success means for them personally. Priorities without context are useless. I thought that was a gem from Ben, and I thought it was really cool that he repeated it.

Aggressive vs passive-aggressive: He accuses Silicon Valley of being passive-aggressive. He said he's not. He's aggressive. I think that's good advice.

Hire fast, fire slow — but he didn't: Like a lot of CEOs, he says you should hire fast and fire slow. He didn't fire slow. Almost no other CEOs don't fire slow. I did not fire slow. And so he wishes he did that.

Some gems in there. Hope you enjoyed it, and looking forward to having you in the next pod.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Twitter 的"慢"，本质是一种被伪装成"民主"的恐惧
表面看，Twitter 早期的"圆桌投票文化"是一种扁平、协作的美德。但 Costolo 揭示的真相恰恰相反——**集体决策不是民主，而是责任的稀释装置**。当一群人共同决定"retweet 要不要变成原生功能"时，没有人真正承担后果，于是没有人真正在乎速度。
更隐蔽的是后来的"Dick said"现象——一个中层经理用 CEO 的名字当令箭推动决策。这看似是 CEO 权力过大，实际是**整个组织在借 CEO 之名逃避自己的判断责任**。Costolo 因此意识到：所谓"velocity"不是让人跑得更快，而是让每个人停止把决策权外包给"上面"。这跟禅宗的"截断众流"是同构的——速度的敌人不是懒惰，而是逃避主权。
## 二、跨领域连接：Pixar 的"无导演会议"与森林防火学的同一个秘密
Costolo 偷师 Steve Jobs 在 Pixar 的做法——把导演请出会议室，单独和插画师们聊"什么进展不顺利"。这个动作和他后来用"森林防火"比喻教导事必躬亲的 director，**其实在解决同

item.title: [Lex Clips] VLC video player can open anything - VLC lead developer explains | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的事实：VLC 不是播放器，而是一座"数字方舟"

你以为 VLC 是软件，但它其实是一种反熵装置。世界上绝大多数软件都在追求"规范化"——让用户使用标准格式、标准协议、标准输入。VLC 走的是相反的路：它把所有人类制造出来的"格式垃圾"都接住，包括那个有人为《星战》游戏开场十秒钟手工实现的 bit-exact 解码器。

这件事真正惊人的地方不是技术，而是经济学的反常：在一个边际成本被严格计算的时代，居然有一群人花一整个夏天去支持几乎没有人在制作的 DVD-Audio。这不是工程决策，这是文物保护行为伪装成了开源项目。VLC 团队事实上扮演了"数字考古学家"的角色——他们维护的不是代码，是人类被遗忘的编码记忆。

出人意料的观察：那个橙色雪糕筒，是开源界唯一"打赢了品牌战"的反设计

注意这个数据——25% 的官网流量来自搜索"cone player"而非"VLC"。这意味着用户记住的不是产品名，而是产品的"荒谬感"。

这违反了所有现代品牌设计法则：好 logo 应当抽象、可缩放、传达功能（YouTube 的播放按钮+电视框就是教科书答案）。VLC 反其道而行——它选了一个与功能完全无关的物体（路障雪糕筒），用了不讨喜的橙色，且比例怪异。结果它击败了所有遵守规则的对手。

这里有一个跨领域的连接，让我想到 Rin Tin Tin（电影狗明星）效应 和 可口可乐弧形瓶：当一个符号无法被功能性解释时，它反而获得了文化耐久性。功能性 logo 会随着功能的消亡而消亡（想想软盘"保存"图标的尴尬处境）；而反功能 logo 是抗时间侵蚀的。Cone 之所以能"meme 化继而文化化"，正因为它从一开始就拒绝了"理性"。

这给所有产品设计者的启示是反直觉的：有时候你品牌的护城河，不是你做对的事，而是你坚持做的那件荒唐事。

跨领域连接：从 VLC 到博尔赫斯的图书馆，再到末日方舟

那个把 RFID 芯片塞进塑料雪糕筒里当"实体 DVD"的粉丝，做了一件比他自己意识到的更深刻的事——他把 VLC 从软件逆转回了实体媒介。这正好闭合了一个圈：VLC 诞生于救助实体媒介（VHS、DVD），而它的粉丝又把它的虚拟符号重新实体化。

把这个现象放到更大的尺度上：你提到的"千年之后只剩 VLC 能打开的视频"——这其实和Svalbard 全球种子库、Arch Mission Foundation 把维基百科刻在月球上是同一类项目。区别只在于：种子库是有意识的文明保险，而 VLC 是无意中成为了文明保险。

更让我意外的是这种非对称性：人类花数十亿美元建造正式的"数字保存"机构（国会图书馆、Internet Archive），但真正在第一线和"数据死亡"搏斗的，是一群免费工作的志愿者，用一个雪糕筒做 logo。这暴露了一个隐藏的系统性真相——人类文明的关键基础设施，往往不是被规划出来的，而是被一群古怪的人因为不肯放手而意外保住的。

阿帕网最初是军方的，但拯救它的是 BBS 上的极客。维基百科不是大英百科全书的延伸，而是边缘业余爱好者的产物。严肃文明的延续，往往要靠不严肃的人来执行。VLC 就是这条规律最纯粹的当代样本。

📝 转录文本

VLC 能打开的"奇葩"东西

So the legend goes VLC can open everything. What's the weirdest thing that you know that it can open?

You know that there is a ton of people who are using VLC to record VHS videos, right? Like it's just like you plug it with a capture card and you can basically record VHS video.

Well, how does that work?

Basically, it's you know those type of capture card where you can put a peritel in or VGA and you put that and actually VLC can play those type of card, and there is a module which allows to control directly some of those VCR camcorders.

DVD-Audio 与 Lucas Film 的冷门编码

We support DVD audios lately, right? We spent the summer working on DVD audio support. And like there is no one's making any DVD audio support. There is a custom encryption schemes.

What about Lucas Film?

Oh, yeah. And there is of course all the weird codecs support — game codecs supported by FFmpeg. The one Star Wars video game, the first 10-second opening sequence, someone has gone and implemented that and make sure that's bit-exact on one disk that existed at one time of one little sequence in the game.

"最烂文件大赛"

And then funnily, there was at one VideoLAN conference we made a competition to make the weirdest and most horrible file ever and see if VLC could play it.

What did it end up being? What's the file?

It was an MKV file made by Derek which each of the frame was changing resolution, aspect ratio, rotation and then — and it was like…

Did it work?

Yes. And there was another one where the whole video was actually animated subtitles, right? SSA, right? So this one was — and so each frame was a black frame, but on top of that there was a subtitle that was animated for each frame.

There was a file that's a valid zip and a valid MP3 at the same time or something like that. So yeah, we made a competition of stupid files.

And it worked. It opened all of the stupid files.

Yes.

雪糕筒：最糟糕也最伟大的 logo

By the way, for people who are not familiar, I am wearing a hat. Would it be fair to say this is the best worst logo of all time, the cone?

Yeah, by far, right? The logo of VLC is so iconic, right? Like we are a team with a small number of people and the icon is known everywhere. I go to middle of nowhere in India or in China, people know the cone, right?

And 25% of the website traffic that comes to our main website is "cone player", right? So so many people don't know VLC, right? They know the cone player. That's the thing they Google for — it's "cone player". Yeah, they go on Google and they put "cone player" and they download VLC, right? So that's iconic.

一次愚人节玩笑引发的 1 万封抗议邮件

And once we tried to change it as a joke, right? We said it was going to be a type of caterpillar construction, and we said that during April 1st we had around 10,000 emails saying "no, don't change the logo" and so on, right?

So it's so iconic, right? It's so distinctive, right? If you want to do a video player, you're going to put a play button on a TV, right? And that's a YouTube logo, right? It's an original. This one is orange, right? Very bright and it's weird. And it's ridiculous and it's absurd and it's hilarious.

It becomes meme, and meme becomes culture. And you keep it and you know about it and you know that in 20 years, like, you still have going to have the cones and remember "oh yeah, that was a video player."

VLC 作为人类文明的"档案守护者"

Yeah, and we'll talk about, you know, the mission of FFmpeg being a kind of the archival aspect of it. So you could think about 1,000 years from now we'll have all these videos that only VLC can open. Humans — human civilization has already destroyed itself multiple times and the only thing that will remain is, you know, the cockroaches will be crawling around and it'll be the VLC logo with some of the archival footage that VLC can open, and the aliens will show up and they'll press play and they'll get to see…

Well, we really really hope so, right?

给煎饼塞进 DVD 驱动器？

But there is also so many memes where people say, well, I'm sure I can put a pancake inside my DVD drive and VLC will play it. No, we tried it, doesn't. But we actually have a video of us trying that. Didn't work.

A codec for physical reality, I don't know what that would even look like.

用 RFID 雪糕筒当"实体 DVD"的极客

Guy who did that, right? He printed a small cone, right? Like the ones we distribute as goodies, and inside he put an RFID chip which was his way of playing a movie, right? And so he put this on RFID player, and when he put that it was playing like the last Star Wars and so on. So instead of having like DVD boxes, he had like VLC cones all around, and he plugged that and there was like physical objects.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的事实：VLC 不是播放器，而是一座"数字方舟"
你以为 VLC 是软件，但它其实是一种**反熵装置**。世界上绝大多数软件都在追求"规范化"——让用户使用标准格式、标准协议、标准输入。VLC 走的是相反的路：它把所有人类制造出来的"格式垃圾"都接住，包括那个有人为《星战》游戏开场十秒钟手工实现的 bit-exact 解码器。
这件事真正惊人的地方不是技术，而是**经济学的反常**：在一个边际成本被严格计算的时代，居然有一群人花一整个夏天去支持几乎没有人在制作的 DVD-Audio。这不是工程决策，这是**文物保护行为**伪装成了开源项目。VLC 团队事实上扮演了"数字考古学家"的角色——他们维护的不是代码，是**人类被遗忘的编码记忆**。
## 出人意料的观察：那个橙色雪糕筒，是开源界唯一"打赢了品牌战"的反设计
注意这个数据——**25% 的官网流量来自搜索"cone player"而非"VLC"**。这意味着用户记住的不是产品名，而是产品的"荒谬感"。
这违反了所有现代品牌设计法则：好 logo 应当**抽象、可缩放、传达功能**（YouTube 的播放

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1430期】必看，恐高的应对策略！PLTR为何下跌？DIS仍然被严重低估✨20260506#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：恐高的真相不是"高"，而是"快"

你以为大家在恐高？错了。视频里藏着一个被忽视的关键洞察——当前标普500的前瞻市盈率比去年10月还要低，但去年10月没人恐慌，现在却人人恐高。这戳穿了一个市场心理学的本质谎言：

投资者从来不是怕"贵"，而是怕"快"。

去年4月到10月那段缓慢爬升的阴跌式上涨，让所有人在不知不觉中被时间催眠——同样的估值，配上6个月的爬升，叫"舒适"；配上1个月的拉升，叫"泡沫"。这其实是行为金融学里适应性预期和神经科学里多巴胺敏感度衰减的合体——大脑对慢变量脱敏，对快变量过敏。

跨领域连接：估值修复 = 生物学的"伸长生长"

主播提到一个绝妙但他自己没有点透的概念——英伟达过去一年价格横盘但估值从40倍降到30倍。这不是传统意义上的"调整"，而像极了植物学里的等容生长（isotropic growth）：表面上植物没长高，但细胞内部在重组、致密化。

把这个映射回市场： - 价格下跌式修复 = 砍枝（破坏性） - 横盘式估值修复 = 内部细胞分裂（建设性）

这就解释了一个被99%散户忽视的事实：最强的牛股从来不靠回调消化估值，它们靠"装睡"消化估值。这意味着，对芯片、光、储能板块来说，未来几个月如果它们"什么都没干"——那才是最 bullish 的剧本。

隐藏的系统性真相：Disney 的估值悖论是一个"分类学事故"

主播算出 Disney 流媒体业务按 Netflix 估值法值 1170 亿，剩余传统业务只被市场定价 5.7 倍——这数字本身已经够震撼，但更深的洞察在这里：

Disney 的低估，本质上是一个"语言学问题"，而不是"金融问题"。

市场有一个隐秘的认知机制：它给公司贴标签，然后用标签的乘数定价，而不是用业务本身定价。Disney 被困在"传统媒体"这个标签里，就像柯达被困在"胶卷公司"标签里失去了数码相机的故事，就像亚马逊被困在"书店"标签里直到云业务被独立看见。

这指向一个跨行业的元规律——所有伟大的重估行情，本质上都是"重新分类"行情： - Tesla 从汽车股 → 科技股 - Meta 从社交股 → AI 基础设施股
- Palantir 从政府承包商 → AI 平台

Disney 现在卡在分类切换的"中阴身"状态。真正的催化剂不会是某个财报数据，而是某一天某个大行分析师把它从"Media"板块移到"Streaming/Tech"板块的那一刻——估值跳跃将以小时计算发生。

一个让人后背发凉的元洞察

主播在Palantir案例中无意中暴露了一个估值的时间悖论： - Palantir 估值从 400 倍降到 154 倍降到现在 PEG 2.4 倍——公司变好了，股价却跌了 - Tesla 5年价格不动——公司变好了，股价却没动

这两个案例放在一起看，揭示了一个残酷的真相：在高估值股票里，"基本面变好"和"赚钱"经常是负相关的。基本面越好，估值修复就越快，股价越被压制。

这意味着你买高估值高增长股的真正风险，不是公司做得不好，恰恰是公司做得"刚刚好"——好到能 justify 部分估值，但不够好到突破估值。这是一个反直觉的"中间地带陷阱"，比纯粹的失败更可怕。

📝 转录文本

开场与本期主题

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场奉闲小陶，欢迎大家来到周三的更新。因为昨天没有为大家更新，所以今天帮大家补一期。

那财报季度还是我会放比较多的一些重点在各家公司的一个财报数据上面。今天主要的一个切入的一个视角是——不同公司的一个估值，怎么去看待估值到底是贵还是便宜。那么包括了在周一发布财报的 Palantir、然后周二发布财报的 AMD、周三发布财报的 Disney 等等，我们会聊比较多的一些财报。

另外一点是当下市场，我知道最近过去一个多月，市场走势非常的、就涨幅非常的快、非常的急速，在非常短的时间之内，所以大家都有一个恐高的一个心态。怎么去克服这个恐高的心态？或者说有哪一些策略本身嘛我们可以去应对？ 我提了很多的一些思路和思考的一些方向，希望大家可以去看一下。看完之后，也许会对于大家现在恐高的这个心态有一定的一个帮助；或者看完之后，希望大家可以知道接下去应该怎么做。

好，那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，拜托大家记得留言、点赞加转发。好像最近这段时间留言不是非常的多，你们的留言其实是我最大做视频的一个动力，好吗？

一、周三市场走势回顾

先来看一下周三资产价格的走势。美元和美债市场都出现了下跌，美债收益率都出现了下跌。三大指数的反弹幅度非常的大：标普 1.5、纳指 2.0、道指 1.2，感觉好像是完全停不下来的感觉。

黄金上涨了 3.1%。呃，黄金其实在过去一个月是非常弱的，只上涨了 0.5，而且还是在美元出现下跌的情况下。三大指数的反弹是非常的大，那么标普 500 上涨了 12，然后纳指上升了 18%。那主要还是因为黄金在几个月之前的那一波拉涨之后，基本上把买家已经全部吃掉了，就没有额外的买家再进来继续把黄金给托上去了。

那过去这一段时间，即使是有宏观利好的情况下，黄金也没有出现上涨，说明什么？说明在不断的换手、不断的消耗，就类似于比特币其实是一样的道理。

然后原油出现了 6.8% 的下跌。所以周三的一个反弹不单是芯片板块走的好，然后受到原油价格影响的这些标的、这些板块也出现了反弹。比如说我提到了几个标的：Home Depot 出现了反弹，然后呃 GE——我特地一周之前强调过的 GE，我说 GE 也是一个机会，因为起码从估值这个角度已经是不贵了。

那么还有没有下跌的空间？你可以认为有下跌的空间，但是有下跌的空间你可以加仓。但是一周之前的话已经是一个比较好的一个进场点了。我说只要你相信战争不会持续下去，战争未来的时间点一定会结束，原油也不会长期保持在一个非常高的位置，那只要原油下跌——因为 GE 是受到原油的价格的影响嘛——只要原油价格下跌，那 GE 就非常有可能会出现这一个反弹。那其实在周三也得到了这个验证了，稍后我会再去更新一下。

IGV 出现了下跌，然后罗素 2000 上涨了 1.5。那么所以从板块这个角度去看，除了能源板块、公共事业下跌之外，保险板块也相对弱一些。保险板块现在是在一个下行周期，呃，主要是因为保费增速的这个放缓，接下去我们将会看到赔付率的这个上升。

呃，前七大科技股上涨了 2%，标普等权指数上涨了 0.8%。那前七大科技股和标普等权指数的这个回报差 YTD 现在来到了 2.5%。我在过去这一段时间一直和大家强调的是，我觉得 at one point 这两者之间的一个回报差应该是 10% 以上了。我说我仍然看好大科技，我说大科技是非常的便宜——但这句话可能要将近一个月、可能一个多月之前了。现在我们可以看到大科技和等权指数的这个回报差在慢慢的拉近，说明什么？说明在过去这一段时间，大科技走势非常的强劲。

从板块这个角度，最强劲的肯定是芯片板块了——过去一年上涨了 170%，YTD 上涨了 68%。

二、市场参与度：恐高心态的真相

我们来看一下市场的参与度，这是周三收盘的价格。你可能难以相信市场的参与度——纳指 20 均线 55%、50 均线 57%，中性几乎是在一个中性相对偏乐观的一个位置。罗素 2000 会更乐观一些，66% 和 73%。

标普，你看一下 20 均线才 51%。说明标普 500 里面将近有一半的公司，现在连 20 日均线都没有做到。 你想象一下，就是说明这一波的一个涨幅确实指数走的非常的快，无论是纳指也好、标普也好，但是这一波的一个涨幅非常的窄——它并不是一个大范围的一个涨幅，而非常窄的、非常窄的。

这一波涨幅啊所涨的这些标的，其实我个人看法还很大一部分是来自于这一次这个财报或者他们的一个基本面。今天其实特地花时间去看了一下那 49% 的公司——连 20 均线都没有走到是哪一类的公司呢？可能很多都是和一些受到以高油价影响的这些价值股的板块、这些公司。

所以从标普整个情绪面或者参与度这个角度去看，现在市场绝对不在一个泡沫的一个阶段，或者说非常高估的一个阶段，还没有到。因为整个市场的参与度其实非常的低，那指数做的非常的好。

三、应对恐高的五个策略

我知道现在很多人的心态可能就是恐高了。怎么去应付这个恐高呢？有一些看法、有一些建议。

策略一：享受当下，做好移动止盈

首先如果我们做投资的话，就从投资这个角度去出发的话，我认为我们现在应该享受当下。那么起码很多的一些标的的一个上涨，起码它的一个方向是和我们原来预期是完全一样的话，我们应该——对吧——给自己一些鼓励嘛。起码当时这个判断现在是在验证，那现在就是一个验证的一个过程之中。

那只是可能部分的一些标的它的一个涨幅超出了我们原本的这个预期。那如果超出的话，我们就需要去思考一下，或者特别去看一下这些标的它的一个估值是不是有到非常夸张的这种位置。如果是真的非常夸张的话，那去做一些操作层面的话，可以去做一些行为，比如说移动止盈——不去猜顶在哪。如果从最高点回调了 10%，那你可以去减一部分的一些仓位。

那这样的话可以做到两点： - 第一点，你根本就不用担心它什么时候回调。一旦有比较深的一个回调，你一定能够保住你一部分的利润。 - 第二点，它如果继续上涨的话，你将会继续持到接下去的这个整个涨幅。

那就可以了。只要你接受——你不可能永远把所有的仓位全部卖在最高点的——这种事实就可以了，对吧。所以这更多的是一个心态上的一个问题了。

所以我说做投资的话，应该享受当下，然后只要去关注一下、去更新一下现在手里持仓它的一个估值就可以了。

策略二：盈亏同源，接受满仓回调

从心态上来讲的话，如果大家和我一样，这一路反弹、然后一路几乎都是满仓过来、完全没有动过的话，那我们也应该接受：接下去如果有任何的回调，我们以满仓的这种状态去接受回调。

这其实就是盈亏同源嘛。仓位也是一样的。 如果你是满仓上来的，那接下去满仓出现一部分的回调，这难道不正常吗？我们不可能期待你是满仓吃到了一个上涨、吃到了一波反弹，然后接下去的回调你是清仓——或者说我这边的"清仓"是打错了，我"清仓"是"轻重的轻"，而并不是"轻仓"，而并不是"清零的清"。所以但没关系是一样的嘛。

所以我们既然是满仓吃到了上涨，那就期待起码要满仓吃到一部分的一些下跌，这是合理的。那么我们要接受一个下跌。所以不要去幻想"满仓吃到上涨、然后清仓去承担回调"，凭什么有这种可能性？ 对吧。所以放弃这种幻想，这是心态上要去做这个调整。不可能做到这样。如果能够做到这样的话，我们就是股神了。市场里面是没有股神的。

策略三：管住手，对抗 FOMO

另外一部分，如果现在的仓位比较轻、比较少的话——20%、30% 或者你空仓的话——有 FOMO 的这种心态，一定要管住手。

我在昨天在 APP 上直播的时候，我举了一个例子，我说现在标普 500 是 7200 点。即使在今年某一个时间点到达了 8000 点，那短时间之内到达 8000 点，市场可能就有泡沫了，市场可能就会比较的高了。

那到比较高的时候，你说今年第四季度或者下半年出现 10% 的回调，可不可能？不一定是一个小概率的事件。那么如果因为一些宏观数据，或者因为一些一级市场现在的标的上市之后对于二级市场这个吸血都有可能、或者一些通胀反弹等等加息，都有可能出现一些风险。

在年初的时候，也没有人想到 3 月份会有战争吧？会导致或会使得 3 月份出现那么大幅度的下跌，没人会想到嘛。所以 7200 点到 8000 点再下跌 10%，那你还是会到 7200 点。你错过什么？你什么都没有错过。 只是这中间等待的这一段时间，是非常的煎熬的。

而恰恰在整个市场里面最难的是什么？最难的就是耐心。无论是相对比较低的这个仓位、或者抵到比较低的这个贝塔，去等待加仓的这个机会；还是说现在仓位比较大，去等待自己选择的这些标的开花的这个过程——这个等待很多时候没有那么快的，我们都需要时间的。

之所以会有 FOMO 的这个心态，其实就是没有这个耐心，等不住了要追进去。那之所以很多时候很多标的会卖飞，也是因为等不下去了。你买一个标的买进去之后，等了半年、等了一年，它没有任何的反馈，价格没有任何的上涨，其他的一些标的又在上涨，然后你就抛弃它，去追入其他的一些标的。然后你刚刚卖出之后——半年或者几个月之后、或者一周之后、一个月之后——这个标的出现反弹了。

所以市场里边两方面，等待机会和等待开花的这个耐心，其实是最难的。没有那么简单的，好吗？这是心态上的一些调整。

策略四：判断市场——涨幅会扩大，资金会轮动

然后接下去是我对于市场的一些判断了。

我认为这个涨幅会扩大的，我的"扩大"就是它会有一个 rotation。它可能从比如说接下去可能会从芯片板块、从光啊，然后转移到其他的一些板块。因为资金要赚钱的话，很多标的炒的非常高的话，他可能会选择其他的一些没有炒的一些标的。当然这可能需要故事线上的一些配合。

什么时候会发生？我并不知道。就像你去猜顶——很多人现在都想知道，到底这些标的芯片、存储、光什么时候会回调——我说这本身就是一个非常荒谬的想法和问题。对吧？就像前面说，市场里面没有人可以知道这些标的什么时候会见顶。即使你认为当下有泡沫的话，你也不用去左侧太顶，你不可能猜到这个顶在哪。

我认为资金会出现这个轮动。然后你如果担心短时间之内会出现非常大幅度下跌的话，这个概率其实非常的小。因为你去回看历史上美股，一年之内出现两次比较深的 5% 至 10% 或者以上的这个下跌是有的，并不是说没有。但是非常短时间之内、间隔只有两三个月之内再次出现，我们叫做 back to back 这种非常大幅度的下跌几乎是没有。所以这个概率非常的低。

主要的一个原因是每一次的下跌、每一次市场的恐慌，其实都伴随着仓位的这个下降。仓位下降完之后，再次的这个积累是需要时间的。

策略五：时间维度——舒适感的秘密

那么这也引到了第四个就是时间维度。

为什么很多人现在恐高、就现在恐慌，然后在去年的 10 月份却不恐慌？而且即使现在当下，无论去看标普 500、还是去看纳指、包括进去看芯片，向前看的这个市盈率都比去年的 10 月份要低，但是现在市场却恐慌——为什么？

恐慌是因为去年 4 月份到 10 月份整个 6 个月的上涨，几乎不带任何深度回弹的上涨，让大家舒适度都上来了。随着时间的这个推移之后，大家觉得股市不会下跌了。所以这种舒适度它是需要时间的这个维度在那边的。

因为现在没有这个时间的维度，很多人对于 3 月份的这个下跌依然是历历在目。那么只是短时间之内上涨的幅度非常的大，所以大家恐慌。但实际上你从估值这个角度去看，根本就没有去年的 10 月份要高。所以现在市场并没有像去年的 10 月份那么的舒适，主要是因为时间的跨度还不够。

那接下去如果出现小幅度的一些回调或者资金的这个轮动，那么其他的一些标的开始追回来的话，那市场就会比较舒适了。这个舒适度慢慢建立之后，其实就是为了下一波市场的一个下调或者回调去做了这个 setup 在那边了。对吧？因为到时候人人都觉得股市不会再下跌了，然后大家又非常的舒适，那时候反而股市的下跌就有了这个空间和这一个、整个市场的一个情绪面就在那边了。

策略六：个股仍有机会——以 GE 为例

那么然后最后一个就是第五个就是个股。

我认为仍然是有机会的。前面那组数据我告诉大家，标普 500 里边 50% 的标的，现在价格仍然是在 20 均线以下的。只是很多人我们不愿意去碰、也不会买。为什么？因为这些标的走的非常的弱。走的非常的弱，一定有它背后的故事性和理由。而我们常常希望、或者市场喜欢去做一个线性的外推——现在这些标的走的不好的理由将会持续下去，所以这些标的将会持续走弱。

所以这时候就和我们 3 月份的时候一样：3 月份的时候你敢加仓，那么 4 月份的时候你能够不去考虑战争的这个影响，必须要退后一步去看，站在一个更高的视角。我一直和大家强调，从价值投资这个角度去看，一定要站在一个更高的视角才能够看清楚，然后利用短期之内市场的一些波动去进行加仓和减仓。

我上一周之前，我举的 GE 的这个例子——GE 的财报发布了，然后 GE 财报发布完之后，你如果你去听他们管理层的讲话，包括对于未来的这个指引，其实基本面是完全没有任何变糟糕的。如果不是说因为战争的这个影响，第一个季度财报发完之后就会上调全年的这个指引，得是有多么的 bullish？刚刚过了一个季度，它就要上调整个全年的这个指引了。但是因为战争管理层还是相对会保守一些，且给了一个相对比较悲观的下半年的这个展望。

那如果战争出现缓和的话，那 GE 的一个下半年的这个 EPS 的增速马上就会回来了。这也是为什么周三出现了油价的下跌之后，GE 马上出现了 6% 的这个反弹。

一周之前我是在这个位置和大家分享。我而且我分享的是同样这张图，图里蓝色这条线是油价，白色这条线是 GE。那么过去 YTD 到现在为止的走势，基本上你可以看到从 2 月份之后，这两者是非常的负相关。那么因为 GE 里边有一部分的业务是和油价是有关系的，是和传统的航运是有关系的。

那我们知道了这个相关性之后，对吧，我们先不去判断战争什么时候会结束，但是只要我们相信长期战争会结束的话，然后你从基本面看一下这家公司基本面有没有问题，然后从估值这个角度去出发——那一周之前我和大家分享 GE 的估值已经在合理的位置了，向下的空间是一个加仓的一个位置。但是合理的位置你就已经可以建仓了。所以 GE 是一个例子。

四、Palantir 财报与估值分析

然后我们来看一下财报。Palantir 周一的一个财报，他们这一次的一个财报、这一季度财报其实非常的炸裂。

营收增速作为一家 SaaS 公司，营收增速 85%。你基本上市场里边你是看不到的，除了现在的两家大模型公司以外，其他任何的 SaaS 公司、这样的一个体量你是看不到那么高的一个增速的。

美国这边在 Commercial 这一块的一个同比增速 100% 翻倍了，所以非常非常的高。且毛利润 87% 在远也是远超同行的一个 SaaS 公司，运营利润的 46%。

运营利润 46% 是什么概念？ 呃，Meta 的运营利润就差不多就是 40% 多。所以它的运营利润率已经达到了像 Meta 这一类的公司、平台这一类的公司了。

那运营利润率这一块，5 个季度之前还刚刚是 1%。那么两年之前，他们刚刚从不赚钱到赚钱的这个阶段。然后接下去利润率从 5 个季度前、就一年多前才 1%，一年前才 20%，一年之后来到了 46%。这一定是伴随着营收非常高增速的这个增长，给他的运营开支的这个增速是远远低于它的一个营收增速，才会出现这样的一个结果。所以它的一个基本面其实是在变得越来越好，而并不是变得越来越糟糕。

但是为什么价格出现了下跌？ 很简单，就是一个杀估值或者一个估值修复的一个过程。那么过去一年、可能八九个月之前嘛，Palantir 的估值是 400 多倍，甚至可能 at one point 可能是 500 多倍吧。

所以很多时候我觉得 Palantir 的一个下跌，恰恰是证明了为什么我们要看估值、为什么从估值这个角度我们要有一个模糊正确。我们知道一家公司你是很难长期去保持四五百倍的这个 PE 的估值，不可能的。未来 at one point 它这个估值是一定会进行修复的。

Tesla 的对照案例

呃，特斯拉也是一个非常好的例子。到现在特斯拉还在 400 多块钱左右，2021 年的时候特斯拉也是 400 多块钱，将近 5 年吧——特斯拉的一个价格还是站在原地不动。

为什么？因为 2021 年特斯拉刚刚赚钱的那时候、刚刚开始赚钱的时候，EPS、当时是 1000 倍的 PE。我记得当时的特斯拉老外社区里面，呃，Rob Maurer 他是一个老外社区里面，他现在不做了，他以前是非常受欢迎的，我也经常看他特斯拉的一个财报会议——当时他就去 justify 那么特斯拉为什么 1000 倍的估值是合理的。

那但现在我们站在当下，但是当然中间我认为 Elon 网有一些运营上我认为它是方向上是错了，也导致现在可能特斯拉的 EPS 增速没有那么的快。但是没有关系吧，但是反正从结果你我们就可以看到了，特斯拉在过去 5 年的时间里边就是也是在一个修复估值的一个过程之中。

回到 Palantir：PEG 估值法

那回到 Palantir 这边，那它的 TTM PE——就是 trailing twelve months 过去 12 个月、过去一年嘛——它的一个 PE 现在来到了 154 倍。这是我在周一的时候发了一条推，然后后面有一些数据上的一些更新。

那根据周一的一个价格，154 倍，然后 2.8 倍的这个 PEG ratio。其实 2.8 倍的这个 PEG ratio，我说已经不算是非常非常高了、非常非常夸张了。

通常来说： - 2 倍以上的 PEG 就算贵的 - 相对便宜一点的话，2 倍到 2.5 倍 - 2.5 倍以上就是非常、就是相对比较贵了 - 2 倍以内是属于合理偏贵的 - 然后 1.5 倍——那取决于你的一个增速多少——1 倍至 1.5 倍就是属于相对于比较便宜的了

我觉得在几个月之前吧，对，我和大家分享过，我说 AMD 的一个估值、包括英伟达它的这个估值就是 1.1 倍 PEG ratio、1.2 倍。我说这两个标的就是非常的便宜——但如果他们能够执行出来的话。我特地聊过 AMD 的一个估值、特地聊过 AMD 的一个 PEG ratio，说我预估呢，如果接下去的 EPS 增速超出现在市场的这个预期的话，那 2.4 倍的这个 PEG ratio 到底是不是贵？

我说绝对没有像在去年 8 月份或者 11 月份那么的贵了。我认为现在 Palantir 的估值是属于有点贵，但是绝对没有到非常离谱的位置。

怎么算出这 2.8 倍的一个 PEG ratio？ 其实很简单：你拿到现在市场对于未来他们 EPS 这一块同比增速的这个预期——75% 26 年、41% 27 年、45% 28 年。你把 75 加 41 加 45 全部加在一起去除以 3，然后把它现在的一个 PE 去除以这一个得出来的一个平均的未来 EPS 这个增速，就可以得到 2.8 倍的这个 PEG ratio。

这是我周一拿到的数据：EPS、市场预计 EPS 26 年的这个增速是 75%。我们记住这一组数据。我今天再去做更新的时候，市场已经抬升了 26 年整个 EPS 这个体量。 那么现在已经来到了 1.43 了，和去年的同期 0.75 去做对比的话，同比增速 90%。前面周一的时候是 75%，我现在已经来到了 90% 了。

那么我们把 90% 给带进去了嘛，90 加上 41、加上 44 的话，除以 3 就是 58% 的这个增速。140 倍的 PE 除以 58%，得到 2.4 倍的 PEG。所以因为市场抬升了 Palantir 的这个 EPS 的一个体量，所以现在 Palantir 的一个估值反而就出现下降了。

那在这个基础上呢，如果管理层能够做到比现在市场所给的这个预期还要好的话，就未来就假设现在还能够做到更好的话，那可能现在那个隐含的这个 PEG、乐观一点，这个 PEG 可能就是在 2 倍左右了。

好吧，我个人认为当 Palantir 的这个 PEG ratio 进到 2 倍以内——因为它的一个增速非常的快——它可能 2 倍以内的 PEG ratio 就已经会进入一个击球区间了。如果能够去到 1.5 倍的话，无论是以什么样的一个方式，如果能够去到 1.5 倍的话，我认为 Palantir 将会是一个非常吸引人的标的。

五、AMD 财报与估值

同样一个方法，我们来看一下 AMD 的这个 PEG ratio。我前面说到可能几个月之前吧，和大家分享过 AMD 这个 PEG ratio 就是 1.1 倍。我记得当时 1.1 倍 1.2 倍，呃好像 AMD 是 1.2 倍，AMD 1.2 倍，然后英伟达是 1.1 倍，反正一个是 1.1、一个是 1.2 了。就在一个非常低的一个位置，导致他们如果能够做到、如果能够实现现在市场的这个预期，这两家公司都是被低估的。那经过这一波的一个上涨之后，我讲一下。

啊，AMD 和瑞米的这个财报我就不多做展开了，我去扫了一下。那主要还是 CPU 的这个故事线。那和英特尔上涨是一样的——英特尔发布完财报之后，AMD 和瑞米也同时上涨；然后 AMD 发布完财报之后，这三家公司也同时上涨。那么基本上都是一个故事件就是 CPU 的一个故事验。

所以主要对于这类标题，我认为只要更新一下估值，对于这一类标的一个估值稍微去有一个概念就可以了。

那经过这段时间的一个上涨，然后你看市场也上调了他们 EPS 这个增速的一个预期，来到了 26 年 71%、27 年 70%、28 年 34%。这三个非常非常高增速：26 年基本上是非常确定的；27 年的上半年可能也是确定的；唯一的一个风险可能是 28 年和 27 年的下半年。但是市场不会看的那么远的。

那根据现在市场对于未来 3 年 EPS 增速这个预期，然后现在算上 92 倍的 PE 的话，PEG 1.6 倍。1.6 倍仍然是不贵的，我认为是在一个合理的阶段。

六、最佳调整方式：横盘式估值修复

那在过去一个多月的一个时间里边，无论是芯片也好、光也好、还是储能也好，走势走的非常的好。那我认为接下去因为没有永远上涨的标的嘛，那接下去这些标的一定会调整的。

我认为最好的这种结果调整的这种方式可能可以参考一下英伟达。

呃，我这张图里蓝色这条线是英伟达的这个价格，然后白色这条线是英伟达的一个估值。就是接下去我认为这类标的应该是要估值上的一个修复的，修复的方式有很多种：单纯的是价格上的一个下跌；但是另外一种是它可以是价格出现不动，就经过一定时间的一个修复，价格基本上是不动的，但是估值上的一个修复——就随着财报出来之后，它价格可以不动，因为财报变得越来越好，未来的预期保持续保持乐观，那它的 PE、它的估值就自然就会下来了。

所以我举了——我拿了英伟达——在这段时间 2024 年的年终然后一直到 2025 年的年终，基本上一年的时间，蓝色这条线英伟达是横盘的，股价基本上是没有任何的上涨、还下跌。当然中间是有波动的，包括了 2024 年这一波的一个下、2025 年 4 月份的这一波关税的一个下跌等等，对吗？

无论是这一波加上这一波波动之后，将近一年的时间吧，因为它的这个价格是完全没有任何改变的，但是你看一下白色这条线、因为它的这个估值却从 40 多倍直接下降到了 30 倍左右。你看右边的话就 40 倍到 30 倍，所以估值下降了 20% 多，价格完全没有任何的变化。

为什么？因为 EPS 增速、EPS 的这个体量上来了。

所以我认为过去这一个月，什么芯片啊、光啊、储能啊，最好的这种调整方式就是横盘，通过慢慢财报发布完之后、EPS 抬升上去之后，估值自然就下来。这可能是最好的一种结果。

七、Disney：一家被严重低估的"流媒体公司"

最后我们来聊一家公司——Disney。

那 Disney 这家公司我已经卖了。我为什么卖了？是因为我和大家分享过，我拿了 3 年多。为什么卖了？三年多的质感其实不是那么的好，还是赚钱的，但是没有赚那么的多。我是 80 多块钱买进来，然后我忘了多少钱卖了，可能 100 多。反正赚了可能 30% 左右、二三十吧。三年二三十其实就远远是跑输了标普了。
