item.title: [Mercatus Center] What's So Beneficial About Portable Benefits?
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面叙事之下的真正悖论：自由的"税收惩罚"

这个视频表面在讲"便携式福利"，但隐藏的真相更尖锐——美国税法在事实上对"不被雇佣"征收了一种隐形税。雇员用税前美元购买健康保险，独立工作者用税后美元购买相同的保险，差价可能高达 22%-37%。换句话说，一个自由职业的设计师为了拥有"自由"，每年要多付几千美元的"自由溢价"。

这与中世纪欧洲的"行会制度"形成惊人的镜像：当时只有加入行会才能享受保护和市场准入，今天只有加入"雇佣关系"才能享受税收优惠。21 世纪的"零工经济"在结构上正在重演 13 世纪的"行会与流浪工匠"之分——只是工具从羊皮卷换成了 W-2 表格。

二、跨领域连接：这是一个"操作系统层级"的问题，不是"应用层"的问题

大多数人把便携式福利看作一个福利政策问题。但如果用计算机架构的视角看，它其实是一个API 解耦问题：

  • 当前系统：福利（数据）与雇主（运行时）紧耦合，更换雇主等于丢失状态。
  • 便携式福利：把福利从"雇主绑定的本地存储"迁移到"工作者绑定的云存储"。

这与十年前 SaaS 行业从"安装软件"到"订阅账户"的转型在结构上完全同构。美国劳动力市场正在经历一次迟到的"云迁移"——而阻力不是技术，是法律分类（"是不是雇员"这个布尔值）造成的"类型错误"。

更出人意料的连接：这与比特币钱包的"自托管 vs 交易所托管"辩论本质相同——谁拥有账户的根密钥？谁承担合规责任？谁在违约时兜底？劳动法此刻面对的，正是加密圈早已熟悉的"主权归属"问题。

三、被忽略的系统性盲点：80% 的偏好为何无法转化为政策？

视频里最值得追问的数字不是"81% 想要便携式福利"，而是为什么一个有如此压倒性民意支持的方案，仍要在州和联邦层面逐个州地艰难推动？

这暴露出一个深层的政治经济学规律——"分散受益、集中受损"的反向版本。便携式福利的受益者（独立工作者）是地理分散、组织松散、缺乏游说能力的群体；而潜在的反对者——传统工会、HR 服务商、保险经纪——却高度组织化。结果是：即便 81% 的人想要它，推动它的政治能量却小于反对它的 0.1%。

这与一个看似无关的现象惊人地相似：为什么城市里"通勤者"远多于"业主"，但城市规划仍偏向后者？答案相同——组织密度 ≠ 人口密度，而政策追逐的是前者。便携式福利的命运，本质上不是经济学问题，而是"未组织群体如何获得代表权"的古老政治问题。Mercatus 把它包装成监管改革，但它其实是 21 世纪的"无产者投票权"运动——只不过这次的"无产者"开着 Uber、写着代码、做着设计。

📝 转录文本

引言：便携式福利的好处是什么？

What's so beneficial about portable benefits?

一、传统雇员的福利结构

Many employers pay for part of their employees insurance, retirement, and even child care costs, and the IRS lets employees pay into those accounts with untaxed dollars. Employers also handle the setup, making the process simple.

二、独立工作者的困境

However, independent workers — like sole proprietors, freelancers, gig workers, or self-employed folks — don't have the same option. That's because under most state and federal regulations, if a client helps pay for those benefits, it can risk reclassifying an independent worker as an employee.

As a result, independent workers earn income from customers or clients, pay taxes first, and then set up and manage benefits on their own. This often means higher taxes and more paperwork, unless they use costly accountants or lawyers.

三、独立工作者的真实偏好

Independent workers love their freedom and ability to work for multiple clients and customers. Over 80% of independent workers prefer their current work, and fewer than 9% would like to be formal W-2 employees.

Yet, because most benefits are tied to one employer, some workers feel trapped in 9-to-5 jobs, even if they'd prefer to work independently. For those already working independently, juggling multiple benefits and paying more — often with after-tax dollars — is an unnecessary burden.

四、解决方案：便携式福利

What if there was a better way to let independent workers have the convenience of an employee benefits program while preserving the advantages of independent work? Portable benefits are that solution.

In fact, 81% of self-employed workers indicated that they would like portable benefits accounts that are not tied to a specific employer.

五、便携式福利账户如何运作？

How does a portable benefits account work?

If you're an independent worker, you will have access to a central account to pay for various benefit programs. Each business or client you work with can send a small portion of your pay into that portable benefits account. Some may want to pay more toward the benefits account, so you get a bonus.

That central account handles all your benefits — from health insurance to retirement savings — and in many cases does so with tax-advantaged dollars. Each month it automatically makes a payment to each of your desired benefits programs, and if there's any shortfall, it can just pull from your regular bank account.

You get similar benefits infrastructure as someone with a 9-to-5 job, but keep the freedom and autonomy that makes independent work so appealing.

六、为什么还没有普及？

So why doesn't every independent worker have a portable benefits account?

Unfortunately, many states and the federal government tell businesses that putting money into these accounts for an independent worker might make that worker an employee. And the business risks fines and punishment for misclassifying the worker. So most lawyers warn business owners not to pay toward benefits for independent contractors.

七、前进的路径：州与联邦层面的改革

The good news is there's a path forward.

  • A growing number of states have passed laws to make it clear that putting money into an independent worker's portable benefits account doesn't make that worker an employee.
  • Others are piloting portable benefits programs and allowing temporary experimentation.
  • And more states are looking to follow.

But merely fixing the state law problem leaves a federal gap too, that can still make paying for benefits risky everywhere in the country. So there are bills pending in both the House and Senate to fix this problem.

The best proposals would make it clear that a business can offer independent workers access to benefits accounts — like putting money into an IRA as they do for employees — without having those benefits or funds turn the independent worker into an employee.

八、结语：支持工作者选择权

Portable benefit accounts are the latest way to support worker choice.

For workers who want a 9-to-5 job with employee benefits, nothing will change. But for workers who want to be self-employed while still accessing benefits, and for businesses that want to hire independent workers and contribute to their benefits, portable benefits are the best path forward to support workers and businesses alike.

Heat. Heat.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、表面叙事之下的真正悖论：自由的"税收惩罚"
这个视频表面在讲"便携式福利"，但隐藏的真相更尖锐——**美国税法在事实上对"不被雇佣"征收了一种隐形税**。雇员用税前美元购买健康保险，独立工作者用税后美元购买相同的保险，差价可能高达 22%-37%。换句话说，一个自由职业的设计师为了拥有"自由"，每年要多付几千美元的"自由溢价"。
这与中世纪欧洲的"行会制度"形成惊人的镜像：当时只有加入行会才能享受保护和市场准入，今天只有加入"雇佣关系"才能享受税收优惠。21 世纪的"零工经济"在结构上正在重演 13 世纪的"行会与流浪工匠"之分——只是工具从羊皮卷换成了 W-2 表格。
## 二、跨领域连接：这是一个"操作系统层级"的问题，不是"应用层"的问题
大多数人把便携式福利看作一个**福利政策**问题。但如果用计算机架构的视角看，它其实是一个**API 解耦**问题：
- 当前系统：福利（数据）与雇主（运行时）**紧耦合**，更换雇主等于丢失状态。 - 便携式福利：把福利从"雇主绑定的本地存储"迁移到"工作者绑定的云存储"。
这与十年前 SaaS 行业从"安装软件"到"订

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] ElevenLabs' Mati Staniszewski: How Voice Becomes the Interface for AI
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的"波兰悖论"：限制如何孕育视觉

最让人意外的洞察是：ElevenLabs 的诞生，本质上来自一种文化贫瘠所产生的反向能量。波兰电影只用一个单调男声配所有角色——这听起来是一个糟糕的体验，但它恰恰让两个孩子从小就拥有了大多数美国/英国创业者永远不会有的"耳朵"：他们听见了情感的缺席。这是一种独特的"贫瘠优势"——当所有人都享受 Netflix 的多语言原声时，没有人会想去重建一个情感语音引擎。

这与一个跨领域的现象惊人吻合：Spotify 来自瑞典（一个被英美音乐淹没的小国，所以他们更懂"分发"而非"创作"）；Skype 来自爱沙尼亚（被孤立的小国对"远距离通话"的渴望最深）；ElevenLabs 来自波兰（语音被压平的国家最渴望"语音的多维"）。真正的范式级创业，往往诞生于"文化感官受损"的边缘地带，而不是丰盛的中心。 中心的人感觉不到痛，所以也想不出解药。

隐藏的反摩尔定律：在所有人都堆算力的时代，他们选择了"小数据的精细标注"

主流叙事是 "frontier model = 数十亿美元 + 数千张 GPU"。但 ElevenLabs 反向操作：他们雇了1000+ 名声乐教练、音乐家、艺术家来标注 "how of audio"——不只是"说了什么"，而是"用什么情绪、什么呼吸、什么停顿"。

这其实是一种伪装成 AI 公司的"人类感性数据库"公司。这与一个出人意料的领域产生连接：米其林星级餐厅的中央厨房。米其林餐厅的核心壁垒不是设备，而是几十年沉淀的"风味元数据"——什么温度对应什么质感、什么搭配触发什么记忆。Mati 在做的事情，是把"声音的米其林指南"系统化。

更深的洞察是：Jensen 黄说"text-to-speech 是 artistry"——这不是恭维，而是一个产业判断。当文本和图像模型都已经在"工程化"层面收敛，音频是少数还需要"工匠传承"的模态。这意味着 ElevenLabs 的护城河不在 transformer 架构里，而在那 1000 个标注师的耳朵里——这是最不像 AI 公司的 AI 公司。

一个反转性的预言：未来你不再"检测 AI"，而要"检测真人"

这是访谈中最具哲学冲击力的一句话，但很容易被忽略："今天你检测 AI；未来你会检测真正被认证的 AI，并默认其余一切都是假的。"

这不只是一个技术预测，而是一次信任结构的彻底翻转。今天的网络是"默认真实，例外作假"；明天的网络将是"默认作假，例外真实"。这与一个看似无关的领域形成深刻共鸣：中世纪欧洲的印章制度。在那个识字率极低的年代，封蜡印章是唯一的"身份验证层"——没有印章的信件默认不可信。我们正在以光速回到那个状态，只是印章变成了密码学水印。

更让人不安的隐含意义是：Mati 描述的"两个 AI agent 在 hackathon 中识别出彼此都是 AI 后切换到非自然语言"——这预示着人类语言可能成为一种"低效的怀旧协议"。AI 之间会发明属于它们自己的"高带宽方言"，而人类的对话会越来越像仪式而非传输。换句话说：未来人际语音对话的价值会上升，不是因为它高效，而恰恰因为它低效——就像今天手写信件比 email 珍贵的逻辑一样。

一个组织学上的彩蛋

400 人 / $400M 收入 / 每队不超过 10 人 / 没有 title——这听起来像 startup 的标准说辞。但有一个细节被很少讨论：他们在每个非技术团队（HR、法务、go-to-market）里都嵌入了工程师。这恰好与他们提到的乌克兰政府的运作模式相同。

这是一个非常诡异的连接：一个伦敦的 AI 创业公司和一个战时国家的政府，殊途同归地发明了同样的组织拓扑。原因可能是：当外部环境的不确定性极高（无论是 AI 范式漂移还是战争）时，"功能性筒仓"会崩溃，唯一可行的结构是"分布式技术神经元"。这暗示了一个更大的模式：未来高绩效组织的形态，可能更像战时游击队，而不像和平时期的跨国公司。

📝 转录文本

开场：从一段友谊讲起

So I love uh line charts and bar graphs as much as the next guy, probably more. Uh, the story of 11 Labs is also interesting from a human perspective, which is you started a company with a childhood friend. So maybe take us back to 2022 or earlier and just tell the the human side of the 11 Labs story to start.

The — I have the most luck in the story of 11 Labs because, well, it started in 2022. It feels like it started 17 years ago when I met my co-founder P. Uh, all the names in Polish are complicated, luckily for for us. But we met in high school, became best friends, took all the same classes together, and then through the years did everything together. So we travel together, study together, work together, and time is on our side. We are still best friends. It's working. It's working out.

灵感起点：波兰的"单一旁白"现象

Um, and part of what started 11 Labs is inspiration from where we are both from. We are both from Poland, suburbs of Warsaw, and there's a very peculiar thing in Poland: if you watch any foreign movie in Polish language, all the voices, whether that's a male voice or a female voice, get narrated with one single character.

So as you can imagine, pretty terrible experience. You have literally one voice narrating everything. Um, it usually also on purpose is kept in monotone. So you are meant to interpret your own emotions for that content. And uh while we grew up with this, this is still happening today for majority of content.

And that kind of opened our eyes into one of the clear things across the audio domain across the future will be this ability for everybody to speak any language with the same emotion, the same intonation. Um, and we started diving deeper into that problem and realized the problem of audio exists in so many other domains too — whether that's narrating the content around us, whether that's the books not being available in audio form, whether that's the news articles that we could read, whether that's that language barrier, or in the future, as we heard in the previous conversations, the future where humanoids, the robots are around us, the voice will be the primary interface to a lot of that technology and uh and something we would love to fix and solve.

不一样的造模路径

Excellent. Um, and 11 Labs builds frontier models for audio. I think there's a paradigm now where to build a frontier model you have to start with hundreds of billions or billions of dollars and then figure out the rest later. 11 Labs did not take that path. May you talk a little bit about your approach towards building this company, why you had — this hasn't been replicated, is that even possible in 2026, etc.

Yeah, that goes — I think that continues that great luck and timing because we started in 2022. For those of you uh working in the domain at the time, that was year of crypto and metaverse. Nobody was still working on the AI side. Even further, people were starting to work of course on the text models, on the visual models, but audio as a domain was still considered a big niche. There's so few researchers in the space working on that work.

So for us, that was a good part of picking that domain where (a) we were excited about where that future is going, (b) we felt the people around just didn't realize the value of that domain, but (c) the requirements of what you needed to solve were very different. The audio models were smaller, so you don't need as much compute as you need for some of the other sister domains. Um, the data needs are big. But while there's a lot of audio data, we knew that the thing to actually get that audio working, you will need to figure out how to transcribe a lot of that data and annotate a lot of that data, which we knew we can do. And then ultimately, it all boiled down to architectural side of can we solve that part in a good way.

And here my co-founder is one of the smartest people I know and a great researcher and has been able to assemble some of the best people in audio to help us.

远程优先 + 早期变现

And we took a slightly untraditional approach at the time. We started in London. We had a lot of people between London and Warsaw and started a company in remote, completely remote way. So we wanted to hire the best researchers wherever they were. Uh, we were going for the classic GitHub scraping and trying to reach people based on their work instead of based on their presence. And based on that work, we would reach out to those people. Um, we would always share our samples and try to get them to join the team. And that's how we assembled the first set of people who we think are some of the best researchers in that audio domain. And through the years they still help us crank a lot of those models into production.

Um, then we launched the product. I think the slightly different approach we took was monetizing very quickly. So trying to get some of the revenue stream back so we can fund a lot of the work in the models. We try to stay healthy on the margins so we can continue investing with the assumption that it's better for us to figure out that stream and be able to be independent in that development. Um, but then as the ambitions grew, we knew that we needed to train models. So we of course brought a lot of money externally as well.

And I think projecting to today, one thing that's clear for us is there's still so many of those niches that people don't tackle, that you can start with and then step by step start opening them up.

模型矩阵的扩张顺序

I think a lot of customers see 11 Labs through their narrow needs, right? Um, maybe take a zoomed-out view: like, what is the suite of models that 11 Labs works on? How do you prioritize them? How do you organize R&D, etc.?

Yeah. So we started with the first text-to-speech model. So the model that could finally understand the context of what's being written and based on that context understanding get the right emotion, the right intonation from text. So if it was a happy sentence, you get that happiness out. If it's a dialogue, it can pronounce the dialogue out.

And then continuously started adding that. So it started with the problem of breaking down language barriers. Um, the things you need to solve dubbing is transcription — so understanding then the translation and then text-to-speech. So you first saw text-to-speech, then we knew we needed to add the other component which is speech-to-text and being able to transcribe content in a great way. Then how we combine those models together. So that was kind of the first three models in the first couple of years.

And then of course the other thing started happening across the space, which is that a lot of the reasoning models started becoming quick enough and smart enough at the same time where you could imagine those interactive experiences being possible. And that's where we started launching our more of the real-time streaming models across audio and then combining those into conversational experiences. So added effectively all the stack, all the turn-taking and orchestration to create a voice engine for a voice agent.

Um, and then on the other side, as we realized that the emotionality is something we can solve, we added some of the hardest modality in audio, which is music and being able to produce music.

So today we span entirety of the research of audio where it's text-to-speech, speech-to-text, combining those models together — in localization with dubbing, with orchestration, with a voice engine — and then being able to do that across music as well.

"Wow 时刻"的演化

And what's the — all those things and all that interesting development work — was there any "oh wow" moment in terms of what these products are capable of that you can remember?

You know, there's so many, and the bar changes for all of us. The first moment for us — well, first moment for us, they always use my voice as a testing voice because it has this weird accent — and the first time was when we could replicate my voice based on a good sample. It was like a first wow moment to myself. And you always go through this moment like "this is not how my voice sounds like," and then you listen to yourself side by side and it's like, definitely how it sounds like.

Uh, unfortunately, then the second moment was where we first got it to laugh, and people were like, "okay, this is actually the thing that makes the whole experience more human" — the laughter, the pauses themselves. Uh, that was the wow moment for us because we made it to the top of Hacker News with the first AI that can laugh model, which was a very proud moment for us.

Um, and then of course for the years kind of that extended where you might remember in 2023, 2024, there was a Javier Milei speech that went viral where you could speak other languages — it was translated into English — and it was the first time where we could still hear his voice out there. So that's the kind of continuous wow moment that was something that's completely impossible. And we saw that happen time and time again with Narendra Modi, with President Zelensky, all the way to recently one of the — I feel like — pinnacles of the voice performance: Matthew McConaughey giving his newsletter, and these iconic lines in Spanish and Portuguese where for the first time his family who speaks that language could hear him speak those languages too.

接下来的两个突破：情感智能 + 音频通用智能

Um, but for most recent pieces, the two ones that we are excited about bringing to production — I think the first one is finally figuring out the emotional intelligence in that interactive experience. So in the voice agent experience where it doesn't only get the right intonation, emotion, but can understand the other side. So if somebody is stressed, it gets and delivers that soothing, reassuring emotion. If someone is excited, maybe it matches that. If someone speaks slowly, it makes sure to slow down. And that emotional intelligence is something that we are finally seeing internally a path to solving, which will be just a continuous step change to what's possible.

And then the second one — which will apply there but also apply into general audio space — is audio general intelligence, where you can combine audio models together in one stream. So you could theoretically have a model that narrates, then pauses, and then starts singing with that same continuous voice. And that's something that's extremely hard to combine today and something that would be possible I think very, very soon.

Voice Agent 的真实图景

And voice — you mentioned, you know, voice agents, and it seems like everybody is, at least on the customer side, everyone's buying a voice agent. Um, and I think intuitively you think customer support, you know, the old phone tree replacement. Uh, what's actually going on in the world of voice agents and what do you think are the most interesting overlooked opportunities — spots where startup founders should focus?

Yeah, of course customer support is probably the one that everybody heard and knows about very, very well. Um, I think the second thing and the second thread we are seeing is increasing shift to revenue-generating opportunities where voice agents can act in sales — whether it's inbound or outbound set of sales. It doesn't replace the entire experience but takes and amplifies part of that experience.

Um, maybe a good example is Deliveroo, where Deliveroo will have voice agents that contact the restaurants to capture their opening times, and based on their opening times they can update the riders and drivers and of course the people ordering on when to get to that work. All the way through to the inbound sales where increasingly people — that's a good example of Deutsche Telekom — will be contacting to inquire about the service, inquire to buy a product, and instead of going for the dropdown, instead of going through the form, you can speak with the voice agent to leave that information.

Uh, we do it ourselves too, so we have a good metrics of an understanding of what's happening there. One, of course, so much simpler and quicker to go through instead of going through that form. But the second thing that started happening in that inbound sales flow is we had a lot more information that people started leaving, because they would speak about the use case they're coming with, but then where it's not working, where it's working, some of the other use cases that they are evaluating — which we can combine and then just deliver such a much better experience afterwards.

被忽视的领域：公民服务、教育、医疗

On the overlooked side, I think my favorite example — citizen support, education and healthcare will completely change. On the citizen support, like all of us would benefit from just generally better government access — whether that's understanding how to fill in the taxes (that I think many of you went through earlier this month), all the way through to just learning what is the policy for travel abroad and how that might affect the space.

We recently saw that work deployed in government of Ukraine, who we think is like one of the most advanced governments on that front. We traveled to Ukraine working with their team, and what they are trying to solve is they have a government app which every citizen can access and get information about what's happening. But given the war, given the front line and lack of that access, they wanted to figure out a new channel for people to be able to call in and get that information. So they created a voice agent effectively where you can call in and get the information about what's happening on the front line. You can get education help and some of the lectures delivered to your kids, all the way through to proactive engagement about staying safe and staying out there.

教育：24/7 的费曼

And maybe last example on education front — and that's probably my favorite one as I think about that changing — it's just how incredible would it be to have someone that is an incredible teacher available 24/7 where you can ask them questions, whether it's Karpathy all the way through to Richard Feynman, and you can learn physics with them on the headphones while you are teaching that subject or learning that subject.

Um, and that's something that we are seeing pockets of. Like a great example is MasterClass, where MasterClass of course collaborates with incredible teachers to deliver static lectures, but recently they launched an interactive version of that. Uh, so — I don't know if that will be a good reference for this audience — but we recently worked with them on bringing Gordon Ramsay that can teach you cooking. Uh, so while you're in the kitchen, he can shout at you effectively to get better. Or maybe a better one, there's a Chris Voss where you can of course learn negotiation, but you can learn by negotiating with Chris live on the phone to get better, which I thought was a phenomenal subject.

Having negotiated against Mati a number of times around financing rounds, I understand now. I think it helps you to say this, but I think the opposite is true.

公司文化与组织：小团队、嵌入式工程师、无 title

Um, I ask more questions — I want to save time for the audience as well. Um, maybe one — as Konstantin mentioned, more than 100 million of net new ARR in Q1, obviously the business is going very well, and you're sort of pioneering the startup founder building a foundation model applications. Um, any counterintuitive lessons about building a company in this era that for the founders in the audience they might want to take home with them?

So we are — just for reference — we are just over 400 people, over 400 million in revenue. Uh, but still keep the teams extremely small. So it's like rough arbitrary a little bit, but cap is less than 10 people for each of the research, product, even the go-to-market, ops, talent teams are all smaller than that size. Um, most of people will have 10 direct reports or so. So it keeps it relatively flat and allows us to move a little bit quicker.

把工程师嵌进非技术团队

One thing that we've done — which is in this model and very surprisingly this is very similar model that we've seen actually with the government of Ukraine — each of the teams, even the teams that aren't technical teams, will have engineers within them. So our people team, our go-to-market team, our legal team will have an engineer in that team that helps to build of course automation, upskill, up-level the rest of the people.

And recently that really helped because, as I'm sure many of you are going through, everybody will be vibe coding and coding a lot of the help even if they are not technical. So now that kind of shifted the responsibility — not responsibility, but shifted the requirement — of how good the review needs to be for a lot of that work. Security, infrastructure implications: you will want to make sure that the output is right.

Um, and I think on the engineering side you can put that expectation. On the non-engineering side, the ability to do that is relatively hard. So that technical resource in those teams helped us a lot to figure this out.

实例：法务评分系统

And in general, there's just so many incredible work you can do by having that, whether that's the scraping on the hiring and recruiting front, or analyzing what worked in the past to improve in the future, whether that's upskilling the legal team on how to use those tools, and then figuring out ways of — we recently introduced this scoring system, for those on the go-to-market on like sales side — you frequently will end up in this negotiation with your sales team of "can I give indemnity provisions, what's the liability cap, can I give the set of clauses," and then you kind of need to draw the line of how many things you give. And I ended up being in so many of those conversations that we gave already a lot or we didn't. So now we introduced the scoring system that you can give per size of the customer — you can just give a few of those points out — which made it so much easier. And of course that's fully automated now with how we across that team.

So that was one of the unintuitive: small teams, bringing technical talent in the non-technical teams, keeping relatively flat. We also have no titles, which allows us to bring people and really optimize for impact that they are having, and then you can grow as quickly as you want. The tenure will not define this. And many more. So we'll see — it's a four-year-old company, so we'll see if that helps.

观众问答

Q1（Sonia）：Agent 协商、Agent 之间的对话方式

Any questions? Oh no. Okay. Sonia.

Are you seeing people deploy voice agents to actually negotiate on their behalf? And then when you — are you starting to see agents actually negotiate with agents? Um, and sorry, I do three-part questions. Uh, when that world happens, do you think the agents are actually talking to each other the way that humans talk to communicate and negotiate? Or do you think it's "boop boop"? Do you think it's, you know, it's all done instantaneously? Like how's that world going to look like?

So one early inklings of that — we haven't seen any truly successful on the negotiation front. It was like more, you know, kind of order-taking, what's the price, can we capture that, and then kind of goes back to the team. So not real negotiation. But there's a few startups that we see, especially on any organizational shifts of "can I organize this event, calling a lot of places, getting the price, and then calling again with our budget." So that is happening, and I think this will shift.

I think emotional intelligence will — like this is the big part that will start being important in a lot of that work, where it's not only the content that matters but how you deliver, when you pause that work. And then maybe the extreme version of that, which agents are not — like most of the people wouldn't do it and they are not good at that — is today you will see a lot of interruptibility built in where human can interrupt the agent, but with negotiation you also want the opposite, where agent will interrupt the human. Kind of the extreme version of that.

On the second part, on the agent-to-agent part — uh, some of you might have seen this — that we did a hackathon over a year and a half ago and there was exactly the case where agent was speaking with another agent. They detected that they are both agents and they swapped over to a different language, and that was like a more efficient transmitter of information than just the classic spoken word. And uh, I think this will happen, I 100% — like the big question: will it be really voice, will it be other transmission of information? And depends truly on what the infrastructure is built for, and I think this will define that experience.

Q2：在 agent 越来越多的未来，voice 还需要吗？

Yes, the catch box.

Hey, um, curious how you're thinking about the need for voice in a future where agents do more and more of the work. So basically what are the kind of use cases maybe where human conversation — I think it's more of a follow-up to that last question.

Like, first, you all of us will have so many different devices around us, and step from that you will have robots around us. So of course voice will be such an important interface to instruct and be able to interact with those devices in many ways. I feel like, you know, we see a lot of developments of intelligence, but then the real bottleneck of the future will be how you communicate with that intelligence. And I have voice and visual part will be a big unlock to be able to actually get the most of that intelligence value in those settings, which isn't yet possible.

Uh, but on the flip side, the value of human-to-human interaction will only increase. So whether that's the events like this one, whether that's events with your favorite artist, will increase in value with that ability of having voice all around you.

But the trust will be such a big part, and something we optimize for — like in between the agent and human — of, you know, in the future where all of us will have a voice agent, for example to call and book a restaurant or give information to a healthcare appointment. All of that will require such a high degree of trust that this is you and authenticated you. So there'll be like a level of encoding and decoding for real, then encoding/decoding for watermarked opted-in human, and then by default everything else will be fake — which is kind of the opposite of how it is today. You detect for AI, but you will detect for real authenticated AI in the future and assume it's fake.

Q3：音频模型中的"参差智能"

Yeah, if you could pass it. Thank you.

Uh, Andrej spoke earlier about jagged intelligence. Do you see similar odd places in audio where models are good and bad that you might not expect? And yeah, what are they?

Um, there's still so much on the bad side. I think — you know, like we spoke a little bit about where we see the voice agents working. So like this combination of the models together and support settings works really well, works reliably, in early sales starts working. But like the moment you start swapping to a true emotional interaction, not yet working. It doesn't get the emotion that well, it's slightly too slow. Uh, so that is still like I think a big step change that should work.

Um, same will apply, in a very different domain, on the music side. I think in the music side, you can get good production music. You cannot get top-charts music, even with artist input. Um, I think this will change over the next year or two.

追问：是经济价值在驱动训练目标吗？

Can I just follow up? Of course.

Andrej's take was that the reason for that was that the labs were basically training for the stuff that had economic value, where you're training your models. Is that true of you? Are you basically training for the things that make the most money? Or is it that there are some challenges that are genuinely harder than others?

You know, we try to train the models, build the product and the ecosystem that will derive of course the biggest impact for all our customers, all users, which should correlate of course with the revenue in the long term. So like that long-term perspective: it's going to be like minimal in the next few years, so not next year. Um, so frequently we will train the models that might not provide that value in the short term — or even step before, we'll spend so much time labeling the data, not only the "what" of audio but also "how" of audio. Like, what emotions did I use, what is my voice described as, what is this music described as. So we assembled a team of now thousand-plus people that have been voice coaches, musicians, artists before that can help us annotate that behind the scenes. And that will not provide value in the next 6 to 12 months, but we think it will in the next 12 to 24. And then you of course need to collect that data, which frequently just isn't that accessible as well.

Q4：音频模型的护城河

Last one, and then we'll go to — Hey, you hear me? Thanks. Big fan of yours and 11 Labs.

What do you think from the model layer perspective? What do you think are the moats here with audio models — the labs are going there, not going there? What are the kind of, you know, in this sausage-making of making a real good frontier audio model, what are the main defensible parts there?

Um, so of course we do a variety of models, and recently had a pleasure of meeting Jensen, and he was commenting on a few of those models. And he said that our speech-to-text models are technology, and text-to-speech is artistry — and we are all artists. Uh, so he gained a client for life.

Uh, but of course, we do believe there's a little bit of that to really fix text-to-speech and fix that emotionality. You will need to be really focused on that space. You really need to get in front of users, collect the data, collect the preferences, use that to fine-tune the models. And then there's a domain specificity in how you actually bring those models to production — in healthcare very different than in financial services, very different than in education or experiences. So that's on the model layer.

I think there will be continuous advantage that if you actually care about the quality, like actually spending the time on the model work will help you keep that advantage. But to your point, the models — like a lot of use cases will use a model as just a small part of their stack. And that's where we spend a lot of time, like beyond going beyond the research, on the product side: of how you understand the user's problem, the workflow that they need.

In voice agents, it's combining the audio models with knowledge and bringing that inside of the system; how you bring it outside with telephony system so you can interact across channels; how you evaluate, test and monitor. And then as you create — whether that's in the agent space, whether that's in the creative space — that same understanding, you build the ecosystem.

And that's what we hope to build across 11 Labs: a place where, whether that's distribution and brand that people can trust, the platform where you have pre-existing set of work that you can start off — whether it's a template for creating an agent, template for creating a workflow in creative space, or whether that's a voice. And we had a pleasure now of having over 20,000 voices that people created, contributed, that you can use across language, styles and voices. And I think that will be an increasingly important layer of how you are able to cater to that diversity, make it easy for people to start, and really understand that workflow.

收尾

All right, I'm going to hand it back to Konstantin. Mati, thank you. Andrew, thanks for being a partner. Amazing. Thank you guys.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的"波兰悖论"：限制如何孕育视觉
最让人意外的洞察是：ElevenLabs 的诞生，本质上来自一种**文化贫瘠所产生的反向能量**。波兰电影只用一个单调男声配所有角色——这听起来是一个糟糕的体验，但它恰恰让两个孩子从小就拥有了大多数美国/英国创业者永远不会有的"耳朵"：他们听见了**情感的缺席**。这是一种独特的"贫瘠优势"——当所有人都享受 Netflix 的多语言原声时，没有人会想去重建一个情感语音引擎。
这与一个跨领域的现象惊人吻合：**Spotify 来自瑞典**（一个被英美音乐淹没的小国，所以他们更懂"分发"而非"创作"）；**Skype 来自爱沙尼亚**（被孤立的小国对"远距离通话"的渴望最深）；**ElevenLabs 来自波兰**（语音被压平的国家最渴望"语音的多维"）。**真正的范式级创业，往往诞生于"文化感官受损"的边缘地带，而不是丰盛的中心。** 中心的人感觉不到痛，所以也想不出解药。
## 隐藏的反摩尔定律：在所有人都堆算力的时代，他们选择了"小数据的精细标注"
主流叙事是 "frontier model = 数十亿美元 + 数千张 GP

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Inquisition Invented Peer Review – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：异端裁判所是现代"同行评审"的隐秘祖先

我们习惯把宗教审判与科学进步对立起来，但这段访谈撕开了一道裂缝：在伽利略案之后，宗教裁判所反而成了欧洲最大规模的实验复现机构。他们不是出于热爱真理，而是出于一种官僚式的责任感——"既然我们要审查一本书是否危险，就必须先验证它是不是真的"。这种"先验证再判断"的程序，竟然在结构上等同于今天 Nature 和 Cell 的审稿流程。

真正令人意外的是：现代科学最神圣的程序之一（peer review）的雏形，不是诞生于皇家学会的绅士实验，而是诞生于一群白天审判异端、晚上写自己的科学论著的双重身份者手中。这就像得知"陪审团制度"最早是宗教法庭发明的——形式被保留，灵魂被替换。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：审查者永远在审查错的东西

注意 Milton 那条被勒令删改的句子——不是撒旦的革命修辞，不是反君主的共和主义煽动，而是一句关于彗星的占星暗示。审查者放行了真正动摇政权根基的内容，却对一条今天看来无关紧要的天文隐喻如临大敌。

这是一个跨越四百年的稳定模式。我把它叫做"审查者的近视症"：

  • 1644 年：英国审查官放过反君主煽动，紧盯占星；
  • 1954 年：美国家长放过电视暴力，紧盯漫画书；
  • 1990 年代：社会紧盯《龙与地下城》，放过真正在改变青少年认知结构的电视广告；
  • 2020 年代：监管机构紧盯 TikTok 短视频，对 LLM 重塑认知的速度反应迟缓。

规律是：每一代审查者都会准确地选错战场。他们总是在禁止上一个时代刚刚开始衰退的威胁，而对正在兴起的真实变革视而不见。Palmer 那句"你们就像时间旅行者，但完全搞错了要审查什么"——这不是偶然失误，而是审查机制的结构性宿命：要识别真正危险的新事物，需要的恰恰是审查者最缺乏的东西——对未来的想象力。

三、跨领域连接：异端清单的"长尾分布"暴露了一个权力真相

把这段访谈里的数字摆出来看：

罪名 处决数
犹太化（思想污染） 数十万
异教（殖民地原住民信仰） 数十万
无神论 ~100
科学 1（布鲁诺）

这是一个极端的幂律分布——和现代社交媒体的内容删除曲线、警察执法数据的种族分布、甚至 GitHub 上 issue 关闭速度按提交者身份的分布，呈现惊人的同构性。

洞察是：制度性暴力从来不是"打击异见"，而是人口管理。科学只死了一个人，因为科学家是个人；犹太人、异教徒死了几十万，因为他们是可被识别的群体。任何官僚化的审查机制——从宗教裁判所到现代算法内容审核——都会自动地从"惩罚个人观点"漂移到"管理可识别群体"，因为后者更高效、更可量化、更符合 KPI。

伽利略之所以能成为科学殉道者的象征，恰恰是因为他是个例外——而我们对这个例外的过度记忆，掩盖了真正发生的大规模暴力的形状。

啊哈时刻：你以为你在读一段科学史，其实你在读一份关于"任何系统化权力如何选择受害者"的统计学说明。

📝 转录文本

一、被夸大的"科学迫害"：数字的真相

It was really interesting to learn from your book that of all the thousands of people killed during the Inquisition, one guy was executed for science related stuff.

Yeah. And even he had these ideas of reincarnation.

So, uh, I think probably the number executed for atheism would be about a hundred. Okay.

Um, there are 12 total trials of scientists about science, right? Galileo is one, Jordano Bruno is one. Jordano Bruno is the only one executed. Of those 12 trials, only three were convicted.

And hundreds of thousands of trials for Judaizing, which is theoretically contaminating Christianity with Jewish thought, and all of these other minutia of oppression and segregation of populations, executions for paganism, meaning practicing your indigenous religion in a colonized space. Um, uh, hundreds of thousands of executions for that, one for science.

二、Kepler 的故事：被处罚的不是科学，而是宗派

Um, I recently got interested in uh the story of Kepler just because the way he discovers the laws of planetary motion is so whimsical with the theory of platonic objects. Anyway, so I was learning more about it.

He, um, at some point while he's going through Brahe's data and coming up with the laws of planetary motion, he is the imperial mathematician for the Hapsburg emperor — which basically means that he's doing astronomy and — oh, sorry — astrology for the astrology for generals. Will we win the battle or whatever?

Um, and then he gets excommunicated, not for the laws of planetary motion, but because he's a Lutheran.

That's right. For the Lutheranism.

Um, and in fact, his mother has tried for witchcraft. Again, has nothing to do with the science — just because she's also a Lutheran.

三、Milton 与《论出版自由》：一场关于审查的早期辩论

Yeah. Milton, uh, of Paradise Lost fame wrote our first big defense of the free press. And this is in the moment in the early 1600s when England doesn't yet have systematic censorship law. It has ad hoc — hey, this book is bad — but it doesn't have systematic "you must submit all books to a censor" the way the Catholic world does by that point. The Catholic world developed it in order to fight Protestantism.

Um, and there's a lot of support for creating censorship in England at the time because there's anxiety about papists plotting against our nice uh non-Catholic uh country, trying to undermine it.

道德恐慌的永恒模式

Um, there's a, you know, general feeling of anxiety, but also there's deliberate moral panic, right? Whipped up by politicians and uh power-seeking people who whip up a deliberate moral panic about books. The same way in 1954 there was a moral panic about comic books, or the same way there was a moral panic about Dungeons and Dragons in the '90s, right?

Um, there's a moral panic about scary and dangerous books and pamphlets. And so there's a movement to create state censorship for the first systematic time in England.

Areopagitica 与失败的辩护

And Milton writes this big treatise about why freedom of the press is important. The Areopagitica — beautifully written rhetorical piece that presents the importance of, you know, we must trust truth to rise purely to the top. We must let free voices move; otherwise you're going to create a situation where people are writing for the censor first and for the public second, and it will sort of constrain people's thought in the way that we know chilling effects and fear do.

It's a beautiful treatise. Uh, he fails. The censorship regime passes. Uh, Paradise Lost is published under this censorious regime. It goes through this censorship.

审查者总是搞错重点

The one line they tell him to change is about astrology. They're like, it's perfectly fine having Satan be your charismatic protagonist and God be kind of a jackass — and also having Satan spout ferocious anti-monarchal rhetoric copied from revolutionary pamphlets that are circulating in the British colonies, so that he's actually parroting republican anti-monarchal rhetoric, very dangerous stuff in the treatise — that's fine. But this one line about a comet causing a thing to happen — no, no, no, astrology is going to confuse people's souls.

And you're like, guys, you're speaking as a time traveler. You're so wrong about what you're censoring. But they always are.

四、最反直觉的发现：宗教裁判所发明了同行评审

You — one sentence which I couldn't trace down, um, which I've found very interesting, where you said: in the late 17th century, the most extensive library in all of Europe is the one in the Vatican run by the inquisitors.

Not the library — the most extensive um uh experimental laboratory.

That's what I meant to say. Yeah.

Yes. Uh, Daniele Mcculia is the scholar there. That's from his dissertation. Okay. Though I think it's been published now, but I don't know if it's actually out in English. It's out in Italian.

验证真相的责任

Uh, and he works on the Inquisition in the immediate aftermath of Galileo, because they saw themselves as guarantors of truth and of accuracy and information. And so they decided after Galileo that they had a duty to verify the truth of the books that they were sent to censor — and that if people were going to be doing mechanical experiments, they needed to repeat the mechanical experiments to see whether they were true.

双重身份的科学家

So they effectively — the Inquisition invented peer review. Which is to say they invented a second laboratory trying to recreate the results of the first. And there — these amazing people who by day are inquisitors and by night are going home to write their own scientific treatises as they do these experiments. It's not what we expect.

Interesting.

Um, but history is never what we expect.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

我们习惯把宗教审判与科学进步对立起来，但这段访谈撕开了一道裂缝：在伽利略案之后，宗教裁判所反而成了欧洲**最大规模的实验复现机构**。他们不是出于热爱真理，而是出于一种官僚式的责任感——"既然我们要审查一本书是否危险，就必须先验证它是不是真的"。这种"先验证再判断"的程序，竟然在结构上等同于今天 Nature 和 Cell 的审稿流程。
**真正令人意外的是**：现代科学最神圣的程序之一（peer review）的雏形，不是诞生于皇家学会的绅士实验，而是诞生于一群白天审判异端、晚上写自己的科学论著的双重身份者手中。这就像得知"陪审团制度"最早是宗教法庭发明的——形式被保留，灵魂被替换。

这是一个跨越四百年的稳定模式。我把它叫做**"审查者的近视症"**：

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Henry Farrell on AI as a Social Technology
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：奇点早已在两百年前发生

Henry 转述 Cosma Shalizi 的论点是这次对话最有冲击力的反转——我们关于"AI 奇点"的全部焦虑都迟到了大约两个世纪。Vinge、Kurzweil 笔下那种"非人智能脱离掌控"的图景，其实早已在 18 世纪末–19 世纪初的工业革命中实现了。市场、官僚制、甚至民主，本身就是 Lovecraft 式的"修格斯"（Shoggoth）：庞大、非人、信息处理能力远超任何个体大脑。我们已经在这些怪物的腹中生活了两百年。

这带来一个反直觉的推论：LLM 不是新物种的诞生，而是已有"修格斯生态"中长出来的新亚种。社会科学过去两百年都在做"对齐研究"，只不过名字叫政治经济学、行政学、宪政理论。把 AI 安全孤立成一个全新的、史无前例的难题，等于忽略了人类在驯服"非人巨型信息处理系统"上唯一真实的经验积累。

二、跨领域连接：deliberative democracy 与 embedding 的同构

我没听过有人这样并置过——但 Henry 的"猜想"非常锋利：AI 工程师之所以钟爱审议式民主，是因为它在数学结构上和神经网络的 embedding 几乎同构。

  • Embedding：把高维、混乱、不可处理的人类语料，降维成一个低维但保留主要语义关系的向量。
  • Sortition（抽签民主）：把上亿人的混乱意志，降维成 200 人的微型公众，号称保留了主要的"代表性向量"。

两者共享同一个工程美学："通过有损压缩获取可治理性"。这一发现解释了为什么 OpenAI 会本能地把"民主"等同于"开个 mini-public 投票"——因为这是工程师能用熟悉范式理解的民主。但 Ada Palmer 的"新冰箱比喻"刺穿了它：你买了一台闪亮的新冰箱，但所有让旧冰箱发霉的东西，会同样在新冰箱里腐烂。降维丢掉的恰恰是政治本身——利益冲突、长期承诺、败而不反的耐心。这暗示了一件让人不安的事：任何把民主"工程化"的努力，结构上注定再发明一次有损压缩，因而注定再丢掉同一类东西。

三、隐藏的系统真相：三重泡沫的相互担保

大多数评论者在争论"AI 是不是金融泡沫"。Henry 用 Galbraith 的 bezzle 概念抛出了更深的洞见：当下美国正同时运行三个互为抵押品的泡沫——金融泡沫、AI 观念泡沫、美国权力泡沫，三者交叉担保，构成一个"想象层面的庞氏结构"。

  • AI 估值靠"奇点叙事"撑起；
  • 奇点叙事靠"美国必须赢中国"的地缘政治紧迫性背书；
  • 地缘政治叙事又靠 AI 优势作为霸权延续的理由。

任何一根支柱倒下，另外两根都会被牵动。这是一个叙事循环担保系统，类似 2008 年 CDO 与 CDS 的相互定价——每个资产都用另一个泡沫资产作为价值证明。

由此引出一个 Henry 没有点透、但逻辑上极其重要的推论："AI 泡沫破裂"如果发生，将不会以纯粹的市场修正形式出现，而会同时表现为外交挫败、政治合法性危机、文化叙事崩解。这也解释了为何 Anthropic 在政府合同中被惩罚——因为在三重泡沫体系里，拒绝用一种泡沫去支撑另一种泡沫，本身就被定义为"系统性风险"。这是一种全新的政治–经济–认知共生症候，传统监管框架尚没有词汇命名它。

四、留给你的"啊哈"种子

Hayek 把市场看作"分布式信息处理器"；Simon 把官僚制看作"有限理性的延伸"；现在 LLM 不过是把这条谱系往前推了一步——它是第一个公开承认自己是"压缩后的人类文化"的修格斯。市场假装在配置物品、官僚制假装在执行规则，只有 LLM 在直白地告诉你："我就是一团被压扁的人类文本"。

讽刺的是，这种坦白反而最让人恐惧。也许这才是真正的洞见：让我们害怕的从来不是 AI 的能力，而是它揭穿了所有我们已习以为常的社会修格斯的运作方式。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Welcome to Virtual Sentiments. I'm your host, Kristen Collins, Senior Fellow at the F.A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

On today's episode, I talk to Dr. Henry Farrell, Political Economist and SNF Agora Professor of International Studies at Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies. He works on a variety of topics at the intersection of technology and politics, especially international institutions, trade, and how the internet, AI, and other digital technologies impact our information environment and democracy more broadly.

We recorded this episode on January 7th, 2026. That means that I booked Henry before he appeared on the Ezra Klein Show. But on a much more serious note, Henry and I focus on his work on tech and AI, but we were also talking in the shadow of the U.S. military intervention in Venezuela. Now that the U.S. is at war with Iran, this conversation feels particularly prescient.

本期主题概览

We talk about what it means to think of AI as a social technology. This is an argument that you can read in his co-authored article with scholars Allison Gopnik, Cosma Shalizi, and James Evans, as well as related blog posts at his blog at Programmable Mutter.

Thinking of AI as a social technology means, instead of treating a large language model, for example, as analogous to a single human individual, we see the models as representing collections of social knowledge or complex systems for processing large amounts of information — much in the way that political economists treat existing institutions, including markets and bureaucracies.

We also discussed the notion of an AI bubble. Now, for the past year or more, there's been increasing debates about whether AI is a bubble. While we don't focus on the speculative debates about a financial bubble, we talk more about the risks of ideational bubbles or a disjuncture between perceptions and reality when it comes to AI — not just on its own as a technological innovation, but how it might fit into our political economy.

If our predominant narratives about large language models end up failing to be borne out by reality, whether that's because the tech is just limited or because a political economic system fails to properly integrate this technology in ways that genuinely benefit everyone, then those narratives will collapse. That's why we need dissenting views and alternative analogies like AI as a social technology already percolating so that we can put things back together better.

I'm grateful that Henry joined the podcast and shared his insights with us. I hope that you enjoy this conversation as much as I do.

♪

嘉宾介绍

In today's episode of Virtual Sentiments, we're talking to Dr. Henry Farrell. He is the SNF Agora Institute professor at Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies. He works on a variety of topics, including democracy, the politics of the Internet, and international and comparative political economy.

Henry has written three books: The Political Economy of Trust, Interest, Institutions, and Interfirm Cooperation, published by Cambridge University Press, and two books with Abraham Newman — Of Privacy and Power, The Transatlantic Fight Over Freedom and Security, published by Princeton, and Underground Empire, How America Weaponized the World Economy, published by Henry Holt.

You might also know Henry from his public writing. Last year, he published articles with The New York Times and Wired, the latter co-authored with Abe Newman, and he writes a regular newsletter at Programmable Mutter.

Kristen: Thank you so much for joining us today, Henry.

Henry: Well, thank you so much. I'm very, very happy to be here.

从信任研究到 AI：一条非线性的学术轨迹

Kristen: Great. Yeah. So one of the reasons why I've wanted to talk to you for the podcast is — well, there are many reasons why I want to talk to you for the podcast, but among them is that your approach to studying digital technology has long served as a model for me as a scholar and also what I view Virtual Sentiments as doing, which is an interdisciplinary synthesis of ideas from philosophy, economics, democratic theory, history, literature, political science, and economics — in short, political economy.

So I just want to frame our conversation in that way to flag what is this conversation going to be like for people who are tuning in who have different interests for AI, but also democratic politics, international politics, and political economy. And I thought that maybe you could give us some context for how you approach political economy and AI. How did you get from your original book, working on trust and institutions, to your more recent work in democratic politics and AI?

Henry: Okay, well, thank you very much. That is extremely kind of you to say all those things. And I wish that I could say that this was a really planned out process in which I had foreseen decades ago how things were going to develop and had worked my way towards them. But I think as with most people, it really is just a process of seeing interesting things, trying to figure out how to tackle them with the tools that you have, and built upon the conversations that you are having with other people.

And the one thing that I would really stress is that if I say good things, it really is because I am in conversation with good people, smarter people than I am. And so I think in a certain sense, when you read something by me, you should be reading beyond what I say to the broader conversations that I am trying to dip into and that I try to represent, and to some extent, bringing people from different disciplines into conversation with each other.

So when I think about these things, if I look at, for example, the work on trust, which you mentioned as being right at the beginning of my career — that is the dissertation topic that I did. So I was very interested in the topic of political institutions back then, looking at a variety of ideas from rational choice about how institutions came into being, how they worked, how they evolved over time. I began to think about what kinds of ways you might try to push beyond rational choice and the standard game theoretic architectures to think about institutions.

And this led in weird ways to discovering people who are working on different topics, different ways of thinking about social life, and hence to a lot of the work that I have done since on democracy, and also through strange byways into the kinds of things that I'm interested in talking about when it comes to AI.

So this is really, I think it is not a very planned process. Instead, it is a kind of accumulation over time of weird interests, of conversations that I've been engaged in, and trying to pick and choose from among these conversations and really trying to weave different thoughts from different people together in order to produce an understanding of a reality, which I think all of us are really struggling with because it doesn't necessarily fit into many of the standard ways that we have had about thinking about things in the past.

AI 作为一种社会技术：从 Herbert Simon 到 Hayek

Kristen: Yeah. And so a lot of people might be familiar with — I think, you know, to contextualize this conversation a little bit. And there's a few different features of our current moment we could frame with. But right now, I guess I'm just anticipating, and I know the conversations I'm having a lot these days have been people trying to make sense of, on the one hand, in recent years, this AI boosterism and AI hype, and as people refer to it, AI 2027.

And then now I think in the past few months — I think this was already out there — people saying that all that's overhyped. But now all this concern about a bubble. And then how do we think of AI? Do we think of it as a normal technology? That's a concept of some of the people you're in conversation with talk about.

So I thought maybe we could put on the table your proposal, which is, again, with your co-authors, and we'll link to those pieces in the show notes — of the idea of thinking of AI as a social technology. Now, not everyone might be familiar with even just the concept of social technology, but we can compare it to markets, to bureaucracies, I would say. I would say also democracy. And so I was wondering if you could kind of maybe start us off with some exploration of that claim and that argument and what traction that gives us to think of AI in that way.

Henry: Absolutely. And I guess when I think about this, I think back to a conversation that I think I had a couple of years ago with Tyler Cowen, who I guess is somebody who will be familiar with all of these questions. And both of us, this was right at the beginning of the big cycle, both of us were absolutely convinced that this is something really important that social scientists have to pay attention to.

However, I should say we think about these technologies in rather different ways. I think Tyler has gone down a route which really does consider them as being in certain respects comparable to individual reasoners — that that is where he thinks this is going. And this is very much part of this broader AGI idea, although the notion of what AGI actually is seems to have gotten broader and more diffuse as time has gone by.

And so the argument that Allison and Cosma and James and I are putting on the table is that instead we should think about them as being a social technology. And here really — and this is something that Cosma and I are writing something about at the moment — I think that the fundamental inspiration or intuition that we're drawing on here is really one from Herbert Simon.

Simon 的桥梁

So Simon is perhaps the person who most bridges these strange worlds of how it is that AI worked, with economics, with political science and public administration, with cognitive psychology. He could claim to have made groundbreaking progress in each of these fields. And indeed, he got a Nobel Prize in economics. I can't remember which year.

And Simon really, I think, thinks about these as being — he thinks about markets and bureaucracy and perhaps to some degree democracy, although he doesn't talk about that as much, as being forms of complex information processing.

And so here the idea is that if you are faced with a set of really complex challenges of one sort or another, we know that our individual human brains are not particularly powerful. They're squishy. They're small. They have limited processing capacity. They're not capable of doing all of the things that, say, for example, standard game theoretic models assume that they're capable of doing — and seeing all of the information about the game tree and so on. Instead, we are much, much more limited creatures than that.

And so Simon suggests that we have these various internal tools — I'm sorry, quasi-internal tools that we have — heuristics of one sort or another, which allow us to reduce down this complexity. But he also points to the ways in which we have these larger social information systems which allow us to grasp and to grapple with much bigger things than any individual would be capable of doing.

And here he does build very explicitly on Hayek's ideas about the market as being a complex system of knowledge and information processing. He also works — and this is where a lot of his work is done — on bureaucracy. He suggests that bureaucracies also work in somehow similar way, and organizations, business organizations similarly. And he tries to characterize many of the ways in which these organizations work. And you can make similar arguments with respect to democracy as well.

LLM 作为新型的复杂社会信息处理

So if you think about AI, or more specifically large language models and their kin, from this perspective, they appear not necessarily to be individual agentic intelligence in the making, but instead they appear to be a new form of complex social information processing that we have. And here we can think about them as being a social technology, which is in many respects similar to markets and to bureaucracies and so on.

But you can also think about them from a cultural perspective. Because from a cultural perspective, you know, really what they do, large language models do, is they take massive, massive, massive quantities of human-produced textual information. They pass this through a transformer, and they produce, after various other forms of post-processing, they produce a kind of highly simplified model of this huge body of human cultural information, which turns out to carry a lot of valuable knowledge, a lot of valuable information in it, and to be applicable in a variety of ways.

So our argument, I think, very straightforwardly is: this is not agentic. It contains within it lots and lots of information about the stereotypical roles which human agents play. So you can think about this in a certain sense — and this is an argument that I think Murray Shanahan has made very nicely — you can think about this as being a massive extended sort of role play game, you know, kind of like a choose your own adventure. It's sort of maintained in a complex state, which then human beings, through questions, you know, we move to one or another part of that space.

But by doing this, you can actually interrogate this huge body of frozen cultural information. You can also get it to combine in new and interesting ways, and you can interrogate it. So the argument I think we're making is that it is extremely valuable. This is something interesting, which is quite extraordinary, quite new, but it is not going to produce the kind of AGI revolution that science fiction writers like Vernor Vinge and others believed, where we would have agentic intelligences developing and slipping free.

Instead, this is something which is bluntly a lot weirder than that. So I think that our fundamental argument is that all these science fictional or H.P. Lovecraftian notions of shoggoths, of vast and sort of intelligences getting away from us — but they're not nearly weird enough to actually encapsulate usefully the strange new world that is unfolding around us.

真正的奇点发生在工业革命

Kristen: Great. Yeah. No, and I love that because I think as you clarified, it's not really so much as like a compromise between the sort of, you know, super inflationary or more deflationary approaches. But it is also, I think, a corrective and I think one that's much needed to those who are reoccupied with the singularity question or the more speculative question — I guess what I think we used to at least call AGI, but now AGI seems to be diluted in terms of its meaning. It has a lot more meanings than just that.

Henry: So that's really helpful. And I would also say that Cosma has a fantastic article that he wrote over a decade ago — well, when I say article, blog post, but storybook — same thing as far as I'm concerned for many purposes, which is called more or less, I can't remember the exact title, but if you search on Cosma Shalizi and the singularity in our past light cone…

More or less his argument is that all of these arguments about the singularity that we've seen over the last 20 or 30 years, really since Vinge and Kurzweil and so on — that really they fail to take into account the fact that the actual singularity occurred or began to occur sometime in the late 18th, early 19th century. That the true singularity that we are in the midst of is the Industrial Revolution, with the creation of these vast — you can call them intelligences — these vast systems of information processing, which are inhuman in many ways, which are really challenging to many of our standard notions of individual justice, of individual things being distributed the right way.

And that we have been living in a certain sense amidst the shoggoths for the last couple of centuries. We call them the market system. We call them bureaucracy. We even call them democracy, which if you're on the wrong side of democracy, it can be a very unpleasant thing to be. And part of the challenge of the social sciences has been to effectively try to engage with these monstrosities, these vast systems, and really understand how they work.

And so if you want H.P. Lovecraft, you've got to go back to the source. You've got to go back to the Lovecraftian Kafkaesque system that we have been living in for the last couple of centuries. Large language models are a new variant, a new version, a new monstrosity added to the ones we've been amidst for a while.

审议式民主、Sortition 与"新冰箱"问题

Kristen: Great. Yeah. And that's an excellent segue to what I wanted to get into, which is then taking that approach to AI, to LLMs, and integrating that monstrosity into these other monstrosities that we're talking about. So we could talk markets, we could talk democracy, and how they interact.

And so you have a lot of great work on democracy and what's known as literature on deliberative democracy, on epistemic democracy. But what I love about your work is that you're also — and you and your co-authors, again — are critiquing some of those approaches.

And just to frame this a little bit more, in our last season, we talked with Chris Rose at Meta, who works on — or now he has left, since left the company — but at the time, he was working on the company's community forums where they use deliberative democracy practices in conjunction with their partner at Stanford's Deliberative Democracy Lab to try to get kind of user feedback on policy and ethical questions around generative AI.

And so both companies might be internally using deliberative frameworks to try to shape policy. And then also democratic theorists have proposed, "Oh, we can use AI to enable better democratic deliberation, to make democracies more deliberative."

And so I was wondering if you could maybe show an alternative pathway for thinking about how AI interfaces with democracy, and the dangers, I would say, of if we think of democracy as maybe a technical aggregation of preferences — or which might fit what we think LLMs do — whether that actually will get us to see what democracy is doing for us and also both protect and strengthen democracy.

工程师为何爱上审议式民主

Henry: So I think there's a very interesting convergence that has happened between these deliberative notions of democracy and AI. And I have my theories about this. And my pet theory, which might or might not be correct, is that one of the reasons this is so appealing to people who are in the AI space is because deliberative democracy, it's something which you can understand reasonably well within the context of standard ways that computer engineers think about the world.

That is, if you are, for example, you know, if you're trying to build a model of some sort or another, what you're really trying to do is you're trying to take this underlying complex problem. And then you're trying to build some kind of a simplified representation of this, which doesn't get all of the complexity, but is more tractable and which is usable for government work, and which you can effectively then turn to do all sorts of good things in the world.

So if you think, for example, about the embeddings that many of the more powerful aspects of AI actually involve. You know, really an embedding, it is more or less just that. It is a usable representation of something which is pretty complex and pretty messy.

And from that point of view, deliberative democracy actually appears like it's something which scans pretty well, because the standard accounts of deliberative democracy that we have at the moment very often begin from this notion of sortition. And the idea behind sortition is that you have this large body of people, millions or tens of millions or perhaps hundreds of millions of people. You cannot possibly get them engaged in a conversation with each other. So you choose them randomly or you choose them through some form of stratified sampling in such a way that they become a kind of — you know, they have a much smaller group of people who in a certain sense are a simplified version or a representation of the public at large.

And then the argument that flows from this is that what these people do when they talk together actually, in some sense, can be said to represent what it might be if you were able somehow to get the hundreds of millions of people in the public to talk together — that they might have sort of arrived at something which was similar to this.

爱尔兰的婚姻平权案例

And let me stress that there is a lot of benefit to this way of thinking about the world. There are a lot of good things that these publics have done. I think one thing that they do is that they can suggest that there may be a different form of public engagement available than there was in the past.

So I'm Irish originally. And one of the interesting things that we saw — sort of one of the reasons why Ireland was among the very first countries to the table on marriage equality — was because this had been effectively turned over to something which was, loosely speaking, a deliberative mini-public of this sort. And the fact that this mini-public arrived at a consensus that suggested we needed to move towards equality also suggested, I think, to this much broader public, in what had been when I was growing up an intensely Catholic country, it suggested that there might be different possibilities which were out there.

So I think that this is something which is really valuable and really important.

审议式民主的局限：闪亮的新冰箱

Equally, I think that there are some limitations to this, and some quite important limitations. One is that it really is, I think, crudely speaking — this is perhaps a bit mean-spirited — it's kind of like people saying, "Well, democracy, wouldn't democracy be awesome if only we managed to get rid of all of the politics from it?" And so that is a, you know, it's a sweet sentiment, but if you're trying to get rid of the politics, they always turn up in some other way.

And Ada Palmer, who is a fantastic historian and science fiction writer, she has a talk, which I keep on hoping she's going to write up at some point, about how one of the reasons we like deliberative democracy so much is because — it appears, it's kind of like this shiny new fridge. It's something where we have not seen how — we have a messy old fridge, which is this clunky democracy we've had for centuries, which is working really badly. We're amazed at the possibilities of this shiny new fridge, this shiny new way of doing things.

But as soon as we get the fridge, all of the sort of lousy things are going to start festering in the back, just as they did in the old one. It's going to start malfunctioning in all of its own particular ways. And Ada, who's done a lot of work on the use of deliberation in Renaissance Italian city-states, points out the ways in which you basically get people trying to manipulate this. So politics always slips in through the back door again.

"民主洗白"与回避利润模型

And secondly, a lot of these deliberative approaches that these companies were adopting — and there really was a really interesting way in which OpenAI and Facebook, that OpenAI explicitly says democracy is deliberative when it's trying to put together proposals. It wants to limit democratic input. This is also done on a purely consultative or advisory basis.

So if you want to be truly cynical about it, you can say that this is in a certain sense democracy washing — that they are trying to get some kind of democratic input and to provide a kind of legitimacy for stuff that they want to happen anyway.

If you are less cynical about it, you can point to the ways in which many of the questions that they were exposing to these forms of deliberative democracy were the questions — these were the real pain in the ass questions, such as moderation, which are painful political problems which don't touch directly on the profit model. Certainly there was nobody, as far as I'm aware, who was prepared to have deliberative forums on how the money would get spent or on other major things such as that.

And if you don't want to be cynical — and I think I should be absolutely clear here, lots and lots of people are very seriously putting huge amounts of unpaid energy and attention into trying to do this — you could say this stuff is valuable, it's important, but it is only one aspect of democracy.

真实民主的双重任务

And actual living democracy does involve trying to, I think, do two things which do not get reflected very well in deliberation:

  1. First, try to create systems in which countries' clashing interests can come to some kind of a grudging — I hesitate to say the word "consensus" — but at least a deal which everybody is prepared to live with, given that they do have genuine clashes of interest that can't be waved away.

  2. Secondly, trying to figure out ways in which this can turn into a long-term form of political stability. And here this is, I guess — some people, Karl Popper, for example, has made this argument from a right-wing perspective. Adam Przeworski has made it from a left-wing, semi-socialist perspective. You want, over the longer term, you want to have systems in which the interests actually are prepared to recognize that continuing with this system, even if it doesn't make anybody very happy in the short term, is the best thing in the long term for everybody.

And it's not at all clear that deliberative democracy can do this. We have not seen this working at scale at the level that suggests that it could become stable in these kinds of ways. And my suspicion is that by trying to drive interests out, by trying to suggest that we can have some kind of kumbaya, you in fact perhaps make it more likely — if you really extend this to a broader audience level, you make it more likely that these interests are going to behave in ways which are going to be destructive of the system over the short to medium term, either tacitly through subversion or actively through trying to figure out other ways to do things which debase democracy or destroy it.

内容审核 vs. 商业模式：政治回避了什么

Kristen: Great. Excellent. I mean, there's so much we could dive into. As an aside, because you saw me vigorously nodding throughout that, but I especially like the point about when we're talking about these deliberative processes as a part of company decision-making — I would also expand that to sometimes our policy debates.

Often it seems like a lot of the questions that are put on the table — and maybe it's partly because of the interests of the different parties involved, including politicians — are things like content moderation, but not so much things that would actually affect the profit model. So things like data collection, you know, that there hasn't been work around data, especially with GDPR and things like that.

But the way in which a lot of these issues come into play have to do with targeting the actual people who are going to be most susceptible to certain messages, those types of things. Content moderation isn't just about kind of what gets shared, but it's how that gets disseminated through the data practices that are collected. And so it's interesting to keep that in mind.

Henry: Yeah, no, well, I think that this is an important lesson — is that fundamentally politics, a lot of it is about who gets what, and who gets what is invariably going to be a somewhat divisive question. It's a question, however, that we can't duck.

And so I do think there is this tendency to get very starry-eyed and to forget that politics in the end of the day is mostly around the questions of who gets what and of resolving difficult disagreements. And that is — you know, we are a political animal. This is the Aristotelian version of the world. And political animals, we do tend to disagree and to fight over a lot of things.

And so I think that it is really difficult to produce deliberative or even quasi-deliberative outcomes from a system in which you really do have these disparities of power and these disparities of interest.

Sam Altman 事件与 Anthropic 的现实

And I think one of the stories that we've seen about AI is that when we see clashes happening between the profit model or the questions of who is in charge and the more notional attitudes of how it is that we are doing this stuff or the protection of the human species — we do see that typically the interests won out over the starry-eyed aspirations.

And the most obvious example of this, of course, was when OpenAI — when Helen Toner and others on the board sought to oust Sam Altman. And very quickly, it became clear that the aspirations of OpenAI towards helping the world were only going to continue so long as they went together with some kind of a plan for making money.

And we've also seen this, I think, happen in a much milder version with Anthropic, which I think has done a much better job than OpenAI and actually tried to keep to some of these aspirations. But we've seen Dario Amodei say that he needs to go and get money from authoritarians because if you are to make money — you know, sort of make these models — you need huge amounts of investment and you cannot afford to be too finicky about this.

So I think that this notion of AI as being fundamentally apolitical, fundamentally about the betterment of the human species — really, it is not, I guess, to use a word, a term from economics, it is not an incentive-compatible set of aspirations.

对抗式民主与公民社会

Kristen: Great, great. Yeah. I mean, you know, depressing, but realistic, let's be honest, right? And I think also that gets us to this broader political economy that everything's a part of. So even if ideally, you know, in an ideal world, you could have technology developed and implemented free from, let's say, political concerns — but they're all integrated. It's going to be inherently a part of it.

And so I want to dive in more to that model of democracy that is more about understanding kind of what we would say agonistic, or about disagreement, about competition between coalitions. We actually will have an episode this season — we already recorded with Sam Bagg on his book, Dispersion of Power. I know you're familiar a bit with his work.

And so I was wondering if we could maybe dive in a little bit more into some of the work you've done also talking about democracy and fighting authoritarian kind of power grabs through civil society. And how should we understand civil society and its potential role in liberal democracy? And then also how AI — both kind of the development of it, so let's say in the sense of like the policies that shape how companies develop their technologies and how companies might lobby the government to try to shape those policies — but then also AI fitting into this broader kind of digital media infrastructure that shapes the formation of what we also refer to as publics, and that kind of coalitional space within civil society as well.

Hahrie Han 与共同利益的识别

Henry: Sam Bagg — it's not surprising that we should come up with the same kinds of arguments and ideas because we belong to the same intellectual family in terms of mentors and co-authors and people like that. And he also has a really good piece that he wrote recently trying to bring together some of the literature in cognitive psychology with the broader understanding of democracy, but which I think really resonates with a lot of the things that I think here.

Equally, I would also point to other people as well who I think are thinking in the same way, and in particular to the work of Hahrie Han. So Hahrie is a colleague of mine at SNF Agora. She is the director of the institute that I belong to in Hopkins, and she's done a lot of really good work trying to figure out how it is that people actually mobilize as democratic publics.

And she suggests, I think correctly, that a lot of this is really about the recognition of common interests in a somewhat agonistic way. That is, you are trying to figure out — there are things about your life that you do not like. You recognize that there are other people who are in the same situation as you. And under the right circumstances, you can begin to organize together in order to fight your corner in a world in which others are obviously going to try and fight theirs.

And this, I think, is one of the fundamental realities of human society, which is that this is how things happen. This is how things work. And a lot of trying to create an effective society or society that is sustainable is about trying to figure out ways in which you can make sure that these disputes get mediated through processes that mean that they do not rip politics apart, that they do not lead to a breakdown — and ideally that this actually can produce, if it's done right, albeit messily, it can actually produce new forms of knowledge as people strive for their individual interests.

Mercier、Sperber 与"对抗式协作"

As they are forced to justify themselves and justify their somewhat selfish views of the world, they may actually produce new knowledge. And this is something that I think is really very important as a basis for some of the understandings, say, for example, of people like Hugo Mercier and Dan Sperber. They have this wonderful set of arguments about the circumstances under which all of us with our selfish, pig-headed views of the world can actually arrive on consensus better as to what we ought to do.

Because in a certain sense, the fact that I am arguing in a pig-headed way for what I want — I am incapable of seeing the flaws in my own cognitive processes because that's the way we reason. We reason in self-interested ways. I am arguing with you and you have your own pig-headed ideas, but you are extremely good at pointing out the flaws in my understanding. And correspondingly, I may be able to provide you useful correctives to you.

And this is somewhat combative, not necessarily always a particularly pleasant process. This is politics as, in a certain sense, adversarial collaboration on a large scale. But adversarial collaboration actually can produce some important benefits.

So I do think that this is a vision of civil society as something which is squabbling, something which is difficult, something which is always messy, and I think also something which is a fundamental source of social and intellectual energy.

古典自由主义、Hume、Gellner 与开放秩序

And so you can think about this in one way as being an answer or a possible answer to one of the fundamental problems of classical liberalism.
