So if you look at people like Hume, if you look at people like Ernest Gellner, who I think is one of Hume's most interesting and most astute modern students, you see the sense of civil society as involving the set of messy politics and processes, but nonetheless being capable of producing a kind of dynamism.

There are left-wing versions of this, which I think are the kinds of ideas that Sam and Jack Knight, who was his dissertation advisor, Melissa Schwartzberg, who was his dissertation advisor, have about how to push things forward. You also have, I think, more conservative or classical liberal-leaning versions of this — people like Barry Weingast and the late Doug North. Their arguments for these open social orders are really about how do you create orders in which this kind of dynamism becomes part and parcel of our life.

And one of the big problems I think we've seen over the last few years in Silicon Valley has been the abandonment by a lot of people who would have been, I think, classical liberal coded back in the day of these kinds of ideas, in favor of a much more aggressive set of ideas about, "Oh, my God, if we are able to seize control of the apparatus, what are the awesome things that we can do with that?"

And this is what leads to DOGE. This is what leads to some of the ways in which I think some of the more extreme versions of AI or other related ideas have been developed. And I think it is a terrible path and a path that we ought to try to push back against.

And I guess this finally leads into the arguments about civil society, which in the current moment — and this is a set of ideas which I got via Russell Hardin, another great political theorist, who is in turn building on Hume — who more or less argues that we need to have a coherent civil society to counterbalance the power of the state and to counterbalance the power of tyranny.

And so one of the big problems I think we have at the moment is in trying to figure out ways to bring together civil society in the United States in ways that can provide a kind of a bulwark against some of the crazy things that we have seen happening over the last year or so as the Trump administration has really gotten going.

监控资本主义、技术封建主义与开放秩序

Kristen: Yeah, one of the things that stood out to me — and to just build on and complement what you've stated about civil society and that idea of the fact that it can also be (I don't know that I want to say, I feel like this word is coming to me because of your work) but almost weaponized by more people who are trying to control it, who are trying to use it to serve more authoritarian rather than democratic politics and dominate.

It is also the aspect of both Sam's work and the North, Wallis, and Weingast arguments about limited versus open access order in terms of both democracy and that type of more decentralized state political institutional structure and markets — actually, overall, reflecting a society in which you enable (and this is the North, Wallis, and Weingast argument) enabling people, or opening up the ability to form organizations such that you're able to have that expression and competition of interests and that avoidance, and more from Sam's points, of one party dominating entirely.

What I've been thinking a lot about recently in that literature is the recent debates around, you know, surveillance capitalism and techno-feudalism more recently — in terms of people who worry that right now, and I think rightly so, our politics is to some degree very much captured by powerful companies or interests who are able to kind of use that private power to shape policymaking.

And I think to the degree that Trump's administration through DOGE has hollowed out bureaucracy and has also expanded executive discretion in his office and pursuing a more personalistic and patrimonial approach to governance, it seems like we are shifting, or at least there is a threat of shifting further away from that open access. What would you say to those types of concerns?

公众作为表征技术的产物

Henry: So I think that there is a whole body of work that we've only begun to engage in trying to figure out — and this gets back to the question of Herbert Simon and representations.

So if you think about how it is that democracy works, democracy works through publics. And publics, to some extent, are a product of the representational technologies that we employ. So it used to be — you know, you could think about, in a certain sense, the town hall meeting of Massachusetts as being one form of representation of what publics are. You look around, you see the people around you, and you take a gauge from the people who are yelling about this or that problem as to what the public, quote unquote, thinks. That turns out, of course, to involve fundamentally — rich dudes tend to predominate there, as in many other aspects.

You look at other technologies such as opinion polls — this is work that Andy Perrin and Catherine McFarland have done — suggesting that we should think about how it is that opinion polls and survey evidence also constructs publics. It constructs our understanding of what the people around us think.

And now we have these social media which do the same thing and very often construct these rather stripped-down understandings of what the public actually says and believes. Partly this can be through direct manipulation. And this is one of the problems, I think, with Twitter slash X — that it very clearly is fundamentally shaped by the political preferences of one very far out there on a variety of political questions person, who I think really does dominate.

It's kind of like one of those Philip K. Dick novels such as Ubik, where you find yourself in this weird alternative universe which always seems to trend towards the ideas of some particular individual entity who is rather malign.

自我聚集的阴谋公众

So you have that, but you also have ways in which crowds or publics can become self-sorting or self-creating, which is that even in the absence of these forms of manipulation, we got a lot of congelation of crazy happening through social media as people who are angry or unhappy with the world — effectively, they find each other and they construct these vast, distributed, somewhat conspiratorial understandings of how things actually work.

So you look at some of the strange ideas which were circulating around antisemitism, and so on. These, I don't think, were the product of manipulation by powerful individuals, but instead they're the product of people finding each other and reinforcing each other in these conspiratorial beliefs, and also believing that there is a large public who is like them, and that this is fundamentally the default position of other people because the only people who they are exposed to are people who share this particular set of beliefs or perspectives or whatever.

So you have this extremely messy and complex set of systems arising.

MechaHitler 与操纵的不可控后果

Equally, I think these systems don't necessarily work as well or as perfectly as people think. So I think if you look at, for example, how Elon Musk has tried to use Grok, you try to press the button to get people to adopt more right-wing views. And then you discover that Grok declares itself to be MechaHitler — because it is very, very hard to fine-tune it towards precisely that position on the spectrum that you want it to be tuned towards.

I think you're going to find also a lot of other similar disparities or points where efforts to manipulate turn out to have unexpected consequences, because these are tools that are not necessarily practical — these are not purpose-designed for manipulation in quite the same ways. And it turns out if you use prompt engineering or RLHF to push it in one direction, there can be all sorts of incredibly unexpected consequences because these are complex and poorly understood systems, in which you really do not understand how it is exactly that, if you're diving into the actual connections that these systems form, these connections are often extremely difficult for human beings to actually understand how they are made.

So I think we are in a world — and I mentioned Philip K. Dick — and this feels to me like a really Philip K. Dickian world. So those who are not familiar with him, he was a somewhat paranoid science fiction writer of the — primarily did his work in the late 60s, early to late 70s, who saw this world in which on the one hand things work. There is a lot of paranoia in the air. There's a lot of conspiratorialism. There's also real reason why you want to worry about manipulation happening. But none of this stuff works quite in the ways that anybody expects it to work.

And so I really think that that is the world that we live in. And so we see these publics being constructed, which are, in a certain sense — they are sometimes the product of manipulation. They're sometimes the product of people congealing together around shared and elaborate fantasies, but they are not as uniform. They are not as much the product of a single system as you might think. Instead, they're much more organic. They're much weirder.

And perhaps there are ways — but we need to start figuring out this — perhaps there are ways in which you can begin to counteract them on the collective level rather than trying to individually persuade people one by one away from them.

拜登 vs. 特朗普政府的 AI 政策走向

Kristen: That makes total sense to me. But I think as people worry about these publics, there seems to be some appetite for some discussion around potential policy interventions. And so I guess I am curious, though, of your sense of the particular conditions we're in right now under the current administration in terms of anticipating how policy around AI might shake out, and the degree to which that, you know, is likely to be subject to the influence of a truly diverse and pluralistic set of interest groups and members of the community — versus something more mercantilist in nature or, you know, from a public choice perspective, a regulatory capture or state capture problem.

拜登的两条线

Henry: Okay, so there's been a huge — you know, there's been a very substantial change, of course, from the Biden administration, which I think was a kind of a crystallization of a somewhat incoherent, sometimes crystallization of two sets of concerns.

One was a set of concerns about geopolitics. I think this was probably the dominant perspective, which was a worry — a genuine worry among many senior people — that AGI was going to happen, and we damn well wanted to make sure that it was good old American AGI rather than AGI with Chinese characteristics. So there's this geopolitical competition, which leads to the encouragement of the labs, places like OpenAI, to keep on making progress for fear that China will get there first, and a lot of other controls, such as semiconductor controls and whatever.

And then on the other hand, there are genuinely, you know, there are people in the White House who are worried about how it is that — what are the social consequences of these technologies? They don't have the capacity to bring through legislation because there isn't sufficient agreement on that. But they're trying to use whatever instruments they have in order to make sure that there's some degree at least of thinking through the consequences before all of this stuff becomes a fait accompli.

特朗普的反向操作

So Trump administration seems to be surprisingly the opposite on both of these — which is that it is more or less sort of "go ahead as quickly as possible, all guns blazing," much less interested in controlling Chinese access to AI, instead wanting some rather unfortunate language, which I think was used by Howard Lutnick, to "hook China and the world on United States AI." If you're dealing with a China that has memories of the Opium Wars, that is perhaps the least politic way you can express this proposal imaginable.

And then, on the other hand, being extremely willing to go together with the Marc Andreessen and other people's perspective, which is that having any regulatory limits whatsoever is going to result in effectively the equivalent — as Andreessen describes it — of murder, because you are preventing all of these awesome things that are going to happen once AI really gets established and once humanity begins to spread out across the universe with trillions and trillions of different descendants.

So you have this — you know, it's a very different approach.

不可预测的反弹与奇怪的联盟

But to get back to your question — so I think that it's really hard to see what's going to happen because I think that this is very definitely the way that the Trump administration is pushing things. And you saw that with the recent Trump executive order, which is looking to try and restrain states from getting involved in this stuff.

But you also have a lot of conservatives who are extremely worried about what the consequences of AI are going to be and are looking to perhaps make some strange bedfellow coalitions with people on the left who have similar concerns.

So I think we are, I guess — William Gibson has this fantastic phrase in Neuromancer about how one of the future cities he's talking about is like "a crazed experiment in social Darwinism conducted by a bored researcher with his finger perpetually on the fast forward button." So you have this experiment happen in real time with technology. There's very, very little in the way of actual regulatory barriers to the labs or other people doing what they want to do.

On the other hand, we're seeing a concatenation of social forces assembling themselves in response to this, which really could lead, I think, to some quite unpredictable outcomes in terms of regulation, in terms of pushback. And I really, I don't have a good sense of where this is going to go.

AI 泡沫的预测与各实验室的不同路径

I think that this is — you know, if I were to predict, I think that we are going to see some kind of a collapse of the AI bubble happening sometime in the next six to 12 months. I think that this is going to go together with a lot of realizations, remaking of the conservative coalition around these questions.

And then I guess one of the interesting questions is: to what extent do people on the left, to what extent do centrists who are also concerned with these issues, do they have the opportunity to make their own plays? And also, to what extent does this shake up some of the internal dynamics between the labs, where we have, I think, quite different approaches being pursued — certainly by Grok, sort of with, as we speak, the effective pornification of underaged women and of other women as being more or less, "this goes together with the business model. If you don't like it, sucks to be you."

We have OpenAI, which is quite aligned with the Trump administration, I think, for largely self-centered commercial purposes, and Anthropic trying to keep Trump to one side to some degree, trying to pursue a different business model. But also, I think, you're nonetheless getting pulled into some of this stuff because you have to.

It's a — I would be very hesitant to make any predictions as to which ways this actually plays out.

三重泡沫与 Galbraith 的"the bezzle"

Kristen: Well, I think that's exactly right. And I've thought recently a lot about, you know, the number one rule that I have is to be skeptical of anyone who gives very clear predictions about the near future, let alone the far future. So I think it's good — that I like the humility — but I think a lot of it also, I think you are suggesting some key insights that I think would prove really helpful for people who are worried about this, which is to say:

Thinking about things like a potential — I mean, it already is the case that people are trying to correct for hype in AI and anticipate the utter uncertainty of the environment right now in terms of both policy and also just public discourse.

The thing that I wanted to add to that to sort of probe a little bit further was that question of: if we see something like an AI bubble pop bursting — which, I don't… so let's not get too speculative about that. I guess I just want to connect what's going on in AI and discourse around AI with the kind of broader political economy that we're living in right now.

So if Trump's policies, tariffs, other policies, other kind of military actions like what recently happened this weekend in Venezuela impact the American economy — to what degree do you think there's a risk here also of, you know, that sort of epistemic space that we're living in and the degree to which people blame AI versus tariffs versus immigration, the kind of way in which fluctuations in our economic conditions can be kind of framed by different actors as being pinned on different groups. Is that something you worry about at all about the kind of uncertainty of this era?

Galbraith 的 bezzle

Henry: Yeah. Yes, I do. And this gets back to the not wanting to make specific predictions. But here's, I guess, what I would say.

There's a really useful concept that the late economist John Kenneth Galbraith had, which is "the bezzle." And more or less, you know, this is a — it's a play on the term for embezzlement. And more or less, the bezzle suggests that you get to situations where markets, they're in some kind of a bubble — the froth begins, and people do lots of bad, self-interested things. They engage in various forms of fraud, and nobody wants to call them on this because nobody wants to stop the dance while the music is still playing.

And then suddenly, it all comes apart, and the whole damn thing collapses. And suddenly you discover that a lot of the apparent imaginary wealth that you had is, you know, basically was nothing. It disappears, and lots of people are left caught out.

三重相互强化的泡沫

So I think we're in a version of the bezzle, but a much bigger version of the bezzle than purely with respect to financial markets. You know, so we are — you can think about a lot of the Trump administration's policy as being a kind of ideational bezzle, this kind of grand, magniloquent fantasy of the United States as this incredibly powerful actor which can go around the world and kick the shit out of everybody, and nothing is going to happen because everybody's terrified of that.

You can think about the AI bubble as being part of the bezzle, not simply in terms of finance, but also in terms of the ideational aspect of it. And Drezner, I think, uses this phrase — he talks about AI as being the philosopher's stone. So basically, it's a magical technology. Everything can be done with it.

And the important point is that these different forms of bubble — the financial bubble, the intellectual bubble, the American power bubble — they're intersecting in ways which are mutually reinforcing. So that it seems like we are living in this incredibly weird, fantastic world in which everything is possible.

崩塌与重新讲故事的机会

At some point that breaks down. At some point that crashes. And we also — you know, so we are not going to be able to map out the particular relationships until ex post. You know, so we don't even have a very good idea of what the financial investment relationships are sort of among the AI firms, let alone sort of the various sketchy stuff that is happening around people connected with Trump.

And so if you want to be optimistic about it, that is a moment which provides a lot of opportunity for those who have not been directly connected to try to reframe the story to some extent and to try and provide a more realistic understanding of the strengths and the weaknesses of the possibilities we have and the possibilities that we only imagined that we have.

Equally, I think that part of the problem is that you have lots of people — so if you think about this in terms of the opponents of Trump, so many of them are bought into some greater or lesser aspect of this to some degree. We have no idea how much, if this house of cards comes toppling down, what major structures in society effectively are built on — sort of with some sort of the ace of spades, some sort of is supporting something which turns out to be incredibly important — financial investments, et cetera, et cetera.

So we are in a hugely messy world, in which things could go very bad, as well as things perhaps correcting in some way that is to everybody's benefit.

学者的有限角色

So I really, all I can do is say that I hope that we have the nous to figure out how to try and pull this stuff together — so that there are people who are prepared, when this stuff happens, to push this and to figure out ways of connecting with the public around these ideas, building better public understandings in a certain sense, building publics that are not distorted in these kinds of ways, and that we don't just sort of act in a passive way, which allows those who are in important ways responsible for the problems to create self-glamorizing narratives about how it was all, in fact, somebody else's fault.

So I do think that this is something — you know, academics like me, clearly, you know, I am nobody's idea of — you should not go to me for advice on how to undertake specific political strategies. You know, so this is something that I have absolutely no way of doing more than I'm sort of shouting from the stands like the rest of the punters. But I do think that what academics can do is to try to build structures of understanding which help to simplify some of these problems in ways that make them more visible, more connectable, and then hopefully sort of provide people who have a better understanding of how to engage politically with tools that they can use.

政党、组织与开放秩序的事后理解

Kristen: That's great. And I think that that also gets back to the conversation we were having earlier about like an agonistic vision of democracy, and also democracy being about a system in which you have multiple parties who buy into repeated games, who buy into the system because they might lose, but they might win again. And it's better to keep that kind of system in place than to try to dominate and then risk losing out in that sense.

And that when crises of different kinds happen, whether financial or more ideational, we have other people with other perspectives who have been free to explore and argue and defend that can provide us with kind of alternatives or at least answers.

It actually reminds me — and I don't know, I know we're coming up on time. And so I do also just a general invitation for you to share anything you haven't gotten a chance to share. I know there is so much more we could have talked about, although we luckily did cover a lot. Even if we didn't literally cover enshittification, which I thought was a good — your use of Cory Doctorow's term with Abe Newman to describe the kind of current also kind of state of American hegemony and in international relations. So invitation to get into that, but also flagging that for listeners.

But the thing I wanted to just put on the table before is trying to get more hope. Like I'm always trying to — I can lean kind of pessimistic, but I try to push myself to think of, you know, how do we defend democracy? How do we strengthen democracy? How do we build a coalition of people, classical liberals, conservatives, people on the left who are committed to democracy?

And one thing that I was thinking a lot about with the North, Wallis, and Weingast work that we've mentioned a little bit is they talk about how the founders didn't really appreciate political parties, partly because of this kind of long Republican tradition of — that was fearful of factions. And it turned out parties are also just the ability to form organizations, including political organizations, would be crucial to defending and creating what we know as open access societies — to democracy, to markets, to economic prosperity and political liberty.

And so I guess I wanted to invite reflection on that of like, how much can we even understand democracy, or the traits of democracy, or the features of our society that we need to defend or expand or kind of grow out further in this moment? How much of it has to be to some degree an ex post facto thing, or something we only learn in…

尊重问题的复杂性

Henry: Well, I think that this gets back in some weird ways to some of the things we talked about at the beginning of the conversation, which is that we are in a world which is fundamentally vastly more complex than individual thinkers can actually figure out. I think, you know, and I think that that is one of the fundamental challenges we face. The world is too complex.

The second is that we are invariably going to disagree on this complexity. And we're going to disagree in ways which are necessarily going and inevitably going to be kind of self-centered.

So I don't think — I simply don't think — that we can come in, that anybody can come in with any sort of grand master plan for "here is how to do things, here is how to respond." And like you, I'm extremely distrustful of people who say that, you know, who have that kind of approach.

You know, at least I'm not a prophet. I don't think that — you can do your best with the limited knowledge that you have, but you absolutely have to respect the problem — in the terms of my friend Dan Davies, you have to respect the problem. And the problem is one of vast complexity and incredible plurality of perspectives and disagreement and dissension, which is often pretty nasty, often pretty sordid, often pretty self-interested. And that is the human condition that we have to deal with.

有机的、非黑格尔式的进步

So I think that whatever we see, if it emerges, is going to be organic. I don't think that we have any warrant to believe that there is inevitably going to be some magical Hegelian synthesis or other process of history where we're inevitably going to get to somewhere better.

But equally, I think that there are possibilities — possibilities that we cannot see — for a better world out there. And so in a certain sense, I think our challenge is to try to build towards those.

I think as this conversation has discussed — I really think that there is a lot of, or at least implied it, even if hasn't discussed directly — I think that there's a lot of scope for people on the left and classical liberals to start talking together. They've got very different understandings of some aspects of the problems. They're going to shout at each other. But nonetheless, there is something valuable and something incredibly important about the plurality that both of these traditions really respond to.

Hume 与英国内战的历史回响

And again, if you look at David Hume — he was really responding to a past period of religious and political unrest and violent disagreement, the English Civil War, where you saw people effectively fighting battles where thousands of people died over where a fucking altar ought to be located in the church.

So this is not the first time that we have been in this place. And I think that it is a huge mistake to try to foreordain, you know, sort of "here is this institutional design" or "here is this other weird, one weird trick which will allow us to get out of this problem that we're in."

But trying to figure out what are structures in which people with plural understandings of the world can come together and can actually sort of begin to try — sometimes grumpily, sometimes reluctantly and grudgingly — towards a better world, and at least have that minimal degree of mutual respect and mutual toleration that allows them to understand this. I think that's — if we can get to a world in which that is the commonality…

Kristen: Yeah. Great. Now that is a perfect note to end the conversation on. So thank you so much. And it does make me excited to keep having these conversations on the podcast, but also off the podcast with people all across the spectrum too. And in that building of systems of understandings and structures of pluralistic contention. I love that. So thank you so much for joining us. Is there anything else you wanted to share before we go?

Henry: Just thank you very much. This has been a great conversation. And I really do think that these are the conversations that need to be happening.

Kristen: Great. Well, I agree. Thank you so much, Henry.

Henry: Thank you. Thank you.

主持人结语：AI 作为社会技术的进一步反思

I'm so grateful that Henry discussed his work with us on the podcast. And as usual, I am going to conclude by emphasizing a few of the points that we discussed, especially in light of all that has happened since we first chatted in January.

一、反对拟人化

So first, Henry and I talked about AI as a social technology. Thinking about AI as a plural collection of knowledge gets us away from what I believe are the pernicious anthropomorphizing tendencies that occlude what these tools are capable of and how scientists and companies build them in the first place.

While I assume that those who create and sell AI products think that this language of treating the models like a human being is helpful — it's easy to slip into that rhetoric, it's kind of natural to us, it makes the text sound impressive. And those who want the massive financial expenditures and investments that are required to actually make these things work might make it seem like the LLMs are actually getting us closer to this highly speculative notion of super-intelligent AI.

But AI skeptics, particularly people who have not found LLMs helpful for what they want to work on or what they like to spend their time doing — then they hear these narratives as both false advertising and even insults to their own intelligence.

It's also worth noting here that there's a fair amount of debate within the industry as to the limits of LLMs. Some leading computer scientists, though they do not deny how transformative these technologies are, still say that we need to focus on what's known as world models to really pursue the major transformative advancements that some attribute to the future of LLMs. So some of these thinkers are Fei-Fei Li and Yann LeCun. If you're interested to hear more about Fei-Fei Li and how she impacted the field by creating ImageNet, then please check out my March 13th, 2024 episode with AI expert John Coffold.

二、Simon、Hayek 与社会化的信息处理

Henry and his co-authors draw on the work of Nobel-winning economist and major figure in the history of computer science and artificial intelligence, Herbert Simon. Simon modeled complex information processing systems. Interestingly, in his work, he talks about how the brain can be seen as such a complex system. However, as Henry and I discussed, we as individuals have major cognitive limits. So we rely on social, political, and economic institutions to coordinate amongst ourselves, gather, create, and disseminate knowledge as well.

Henry mentions that Simon was influenced by F.A. Hayek's theory of the market. So to expand on that a little bit: for Hayek, private property rights enabled people to engage in voluntary exchange, and it allowed information about supply and demand to be communicated through prices without even the individuals involved necessarily being aware of the reasons of why there might have been, let's say, a fall in supply or an increase in demand. But these changes help everyone in the decentralized system know where to allocate resources most effectively.

Whether a centralized system without private property rights could do this was the crux of what was known as a 20th-century economic, or even socialist calculation, debate. Now, this theory can also be applied to other institutions that coordinate knowledge among individuals and groups.

三、把 AI 看作"被压缩的文化知识"

Applying this theory of social technologies to AI means that we should see LLMs and other models as being trained on a massive collection of human-generated textual information, and they create a kind of simplified model of this collection of cultural knowledge. When the user prompts the model to generate a particular kind of output, that is generating a particular limited representation of the body of human knowledge on which the device relies.

As Henry and his co-authors argued, this isn't meant to diffuse excitement around AI or create a new source of hype, but just adjust the frameworks we use to make sense of these tools so we can better grapple with both their weaknesses and their potentialities.

Much as the Hayekian theory of the market prompts us to think about the legal and political institutions that make prices possible, I like that thinking of AI as a social technology rather than just as a reasoning agent helps us think about the kind of engineering choices, including policies implemented by companies that structure the model and the user's experience using the model, as well as the outputs they end up receiving in response to their prompts.

If we zoom out further, thinking critically about the design features of an AI model means that we start thinking about how the broader political economy and all these other institutions shape the decisions that go into the design of that model in the first place.

四、商业激励与受限的能动性

Henry and I talked about the tensions between companies like OpenAI and Anthropic professing how much they want to build AI that benefits humanity, while admitting that they actually require vast financial investments that incentivize them to do business with authoritarian governments.

This is a perennial question that goes beyond AI, beyond these companies, to things that we experience ourselves in our everyday lives. And this question is this: How much agency can one person exercise in an environment in which they are dependent on a domineering authority with contrary values and interests?

Open access institutions like democracy and markets are examples of the types of institutions we've come up with for trying to navigate this issue of constrained agency and the risks and problems posed by domination. If you want to hear more about these different kinds of institutions and why it matters that we think about how our political economy enables collective action and contestation in order to counter domination, feel free to head back and listen to our last episode with political theorist Sam Bagg.

五、Anthropic、OpenAI 与国防部的最近事件

While we recorded this episode in January, Henry and I could have addressed this point just within the context of Americans' domestic politics. Recently, OpenAI won a government contract that Anthropic had lost — because Anthropic refused to accept the government's request to eliminate prohibitions on the government's use of Claude for domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons use.

The government did not simply allow Anthropic to exit the contract, but punished them for doing so. The Department of War designated the company as a supply chain risk. This is a designation typically reserved for foreign companies like Huawei. And this designation prevents them from contracting with any company that does business with the U.S. government.

In ensuing lawsuits, the U.S. government has defended the designation by suggesting merely hiring foreign nationals as a security risk. This is an argument that puts many companies and individuals in danger. In fact, implementing this as a general policy principle would likely destroy America's tech sector, not to mention the lives of many individuals, families, and communities.

I'm against this on normative grounds, to be clear, as I think America should welcome people of all countries and allow private companies to employ them. But even the most cynical operator who doesn't share my values could see that such a principle would give away the AI game to China and other countries who could more easily recruit talent.

As Henry discussed in his blog post on the DOW and Anthropic dispute, OpenAI may have looked like the short-term winner as they got the contract, but in the end, such a zero-sum, patrimonial, and punitive system means everyone loses. This is a system that seems to be straying far from the open access institutions that I discussed with Sam Bagg in our last episode.

六、Carlota Pérez 与"观念泡沫"

And this is where I want to get back to the notion of an AI bubble. I'm not an economist. So again, I largely want to steer clear of the question of whether there is or will be a financial bubble, and whether it will eventually burst and to what degree — all of that depressing stuff.

But I want to talk about Carlota Pérez's book, Technological Revolutions and Financial Capital: The Dynamics of Bubbles in Golden Ages. Because there, using the history of political economy, Pérez argues that major technological transformations since the Industrial Revolution have tended to follow patterns where truly phenomenal, incredible innovations nevertheless lead to inflated valuations and financial speculations or bubbles. Societies face incredible challenges figuring out how to finance and integrate these innovations into the political economy in a peaceful and productive way that really benefits everyone.

Of course, historical work has the benefit of hindsight, and it's difficult to know for sure while we're living through history which technological innovation or set of innovations will be the pivotal ones and what to do about them.

So to be clear, when I think about the question of an AI bubble today, I'm not really thinking about it because I deny the potential of the technology. Rather, I worry that regardless of the promise of any technology, we are in a moment where our political institutions are so fragile and our economy is so precarious — and that's not just because of technological change, but because of domestic and foreign policies, including the war in Iran and its impact on oil and other facets of our economy.

结语

I don't have an answer to how we face these challenges, except to plead that anyone who's thinking about AI should not assume that simply prioritizing technological innovation will be the key to solving all of our problems — but also question and think about what kinds of institutions can best support ordinary people, their well-being, and their freedoms, rights, and needs in the U.S. and around the world. Taking advantage of any new innovations for the good of humanity depends on getting those institutions right.

Thanks again to Henry for joining me, and thank you, as always, for listening. Till next time.

♪ Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最意外的观察：奇点早已在两百年前发生
Henry 转述 Cosma Shalizi 的论点是这次对话最有冲击力的反转——**我们关于"AI 奇点"的全部焦虑都迟到了大约两个世纪**。Vinge、Kurzweil 笔下那种"非人智能脱离掌控"的图景，其实早已在 18 世纪末–19 世纪初的工业革命中实现了。市场、官僚制、甚至民主，本身就是 Lovecraft 式的"修格斯"（Shoggoth）：庞大、非人、信息处理能力远超任何个体大脑。我们已经在这些怪物的腹中生活了两百年。
这带来一个反直觉的推论：**LLM 不是新物种的诞生，而是已有"修格斯生态"中长出来的新亚种**。社会科学过去两百年都在做"对齐研究"，只不过名字叫政治经济学、行政学、宪政理论。把 AI 安全孤立成一个全新的、史无前例的难题，等于忽略了人类在驯服"非人巨型信息处理系统"上唯一真实的经验积累。
## 二、跨领域连接：deliberative democracy 与 embedding 的同构
我没听过有人这样并置过——但 Henry 的"猜想"非常锋利：**AI 工程师之所以钟爱审议式民主，是因为它在

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Shruti Rajagopalan and Milan Vaishnav on India's Delimitation Dilemma
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的悖论：南方诸邦其实在"赎买"自己的政治声音

最让人意外的观察是——这场争论表面上是"一人一票"的民主原则之争，骨子里却是一场财政赎罪券交易。北方贫困邦（UP、比哈尔）选择沉默，不是因为他们高尚地不去争取本可获得的席位，而是因为他们心知肚明：席位增加的政治筹码，远不如继续维持"穷邦受捐"的财政管道来得实在。换言之，南方邦今天承受的"代表性赤字"，本质上是他们用议会话语权换取的财政自主权的"价格"。这不是民主危机，而是一场未被言明的、跨越50年的隐形交易——用席位换钱。

这里隐藏着一个更深的悖论：印度宪法理论上崇尚"一人一票"，但实际上运行的是"一卢比一票"——你为国家财政贡献得越多，你在议会的相对话语权反而越小，因为发展让你的人口增速放缓。这是世界上少有的"惩罚成功者"的代议制结构。

跨领域连接：议会席位与"碳排放权交易"的隐秘同构

把这个问题从政治学拉到环境经济学，会出现一个惊人的同构。1971年的人口冻结，本质上和《京都议定书》的"基准年排放权"是同一种制度逻辑——用一个历史快照锁定权利分配，让后发者承担调整成本。区别在于： - 在气候领域，发达国家锁定了高碳排放权，发展中国家被要求节制； - 在印度联邦，南方"发达邦"通过控制人口而失去了政治排放权，反而是高生育率的北方邦成了"政治排放大户"。

更妙的是：印度的生育率下降本身就是发展和女性教育的成果，结果这种文明进步在政治算术上被惩罚。这和"环保做得越好越被惩罚"的国际气候博弈，在结构上完全镜像。Shruti暗示的"按穷困和人口加权分配"逻辑，几乎就是发展中国家在COP会议上的话术——只是这次南方邦扮演了"发达国家"的角色。

最隐蔽的系统真相：女性保留席位是一个"特洛伊木马"

整段对话中最被低估的洞察是Milan和Shruti默契达成的怀疑：2023年女性保留法案不是一项独立的妇女赋权立法，而是一枚被精心设计的"政治诱饵"。证据链非常微妙： 1. 完全可以用普通立法立即实施女性保留，无需绑定 delimitation； 2. 但他们偏偏选了最难通过的宪法修正案路径——这只在"未来政府想推翻它"的恐惧下才合理； 3. 把它绑定到 delimitation，意味着任何反对席位重新分配的人都会被塑造成"反对女性"。

这是一种叙事工程学——把无可争议的道德议题（女性代表权）与高度争议的技术议题（席位重分配）焊接在一起，让反对者无法"分别投票"。这种手法和广告心理学中的"价值捆绑"如出一辙：你不能拒绝有线电视套餐里的购物频道，因为它和你想要的体育频道捆绑销售。

最反直觉的结论：BJP的131号修正案"失败"，可能本身就是它的成功。失败本身才是产品——它给了执政党一个完美的叙事："反对党连女性都不愿赋权"，可以一路用到2029年大选。这不是议会博弈中的失败，而是叙事博弈中的胜利。Ram Mandir教过他们一件事——朝圣比抵达更值钱。一旦庙建成，政治红利就消失了；一旦法案通过，议题就死了。所以最优策略是让议题永远悬而未决。

一个没人提的隐忧：反叛逃法（10th Schedule）让"扩大议会"变成纸上富贵

如果议员的投票完全被党鞭锁死，那么把议席从543扩到850，从政治理论上看是用三倍的成本买同一个结果——你只是放大了党魁的声音，而不是公民的声音。这就好比给一个交响乐团增加300个乐手，但所有人都只能跟着指挥的节拍演奏同一个音符。真正的代表性扩张，不在于席位数量，而在于每个席位的"投票自由度"。在这个意义上，印度今天最该改的不是宪法第81条，而是宪法第十附表。

📝 转录文本

Ideas of India: India's Delimitation Dilemma

主持人：Shruti Rajagopalan（Mercatus Center, George Mason University） 嘉宾：Milan Vaishnav（Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, Grand Tamasha 主播） 录制日期：April 24th

开场与介绍

Welcome to Ideas of India, where we examine the academic ideas that can propel India forward. My name is Shruti Rajagopalan, and I'm a Senior Research Fellow at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. Today we are releasing a webinar recording from April 24th, where Milan Vaishnav and I had a conversation on delimitation in India. Milan is a Senior Fellow and the Director of the South Asia Program at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace. He is also the host of the excellent Grand Tamasha podcast, where this recording will be simultaneously released.

We talked about the failure to pass the 131st Constitutional Amendment Bill in Parliament, the government's intention to reapportion and increase the size of the Lok Sabha, the delimitation freeze pegged to the 1971 census that has survived five decades, why the fiscal bargain between states matters as much as the political bargain, and much more. For a full transcript of this conversation, including helpful links of all the references mentioned, click the link in the show notes or visit mercatus.org/podcast.

寒暄与定调

Shruti: Hi Milan, it's great to see you.

Milan: Hi Shruti, thanks for having me.

Shruti: Yes, this is, I think, going to be a lot of fun. I want to first welcome all the people who are joining as we speak. I believe we have a few hundred people who registered for this conversation, which makes sense because I think delimitation is one of the great fault lines of our times in India. It's the political bargain that will set the tone for all other political bargains to come in the future, including constitutional, federal and fiscal.

And I can't think of anyone better to have this conversation with other than you, Milan. Because when I first started writing about this about five years ago, and I was looking for who has written about this, there was only one paper by you and Jamie, and everything else was a little bit outdated. So I think of you very much as my go-to person when I want to think through the politics and the numbers and everything that's happening with delimitation. So it's wonderful to have you here. And thank you so much for doing this.

Milan: Well, thank you, Shruti. I mean, I have to be straightforward and say that when you asked me, I said yes on one condition, which was that this was going to be a conversation because I've relied on your work on delimitation. But I also think we bring very different perspectives — as a constitutional scholar, as an economist, you have a perspective that I simply cannot have as a political scientist. So I actually think hopefully bringing these together will be useful for us, but also for the people who are paying attention.

一、什么是 Delimitation？基本概念与宪法框架

Shruti: So maybe a good place to start is just what is delimitation in general? How does it take place in India? And what are the controversies and fault lines for those who are not very familiar with this topic and steeped into writing it for the last few years like you and me.

Milan: So, quite simply, delimitation is what most of us would call in other democracies kind of redistricting, right? And we're having these debates in the United States on a daily basis, literally.

It involves, I think, two linked processes. One is about apportionment and the second is about delimitation in the narrow sense of constituency or district boundaries. So the first — apportionment — is really how we allocate seats in the parliament, in the legislature across states. And the second is within states: if you have a collection of parliamentary constituencies or state assembly constituencies, what do those boundaries look like given the overall pool of seats that you might have in the state?

宪法依据：第81条与第82条

Now, in India, there is a fairly clear constitutional basis for how this is done. You have Article 81 of the Constitution, which says that the allocation of Lok Sabha seats, the lower house of parliament, should be allocated across states in a way that is proportional to the population. And the ratio of every member of parliament to the population they represent should be as practicable as possible, roughly equivalent across states.

Now, there is an exception here, which is for very small states, those that have a population of six million or below and union territories. Those don't have to be proportional. Parliament can decide how they want to apportion those. But broadly in the main, that's what we're talking about.

Now, Article 82, which follows, says, OK, there should be a readjustment after every decennial census. So in India, it's done on the ones — 1991, 2001, not 2011 for reasons we'll come back to later. And after that census enumeration takes place, Parliament can authorize the setting up of the Delimitation Commission, which will essentially do this work, again, both in terms of thinking about seats across states, as well as how to do boundaries within states.

There's a parallel requirement, which I won't get into in the constitution, as you might imagine, about how this should work for state assemblies. It's essentially the same process.

Lok Sabha 席位上限与 Delimitation Commission

There's another part of this, which I think is important, which is that the size of the Lok Sabha is constitutionally capped. The cap on the number of seats the lower house can have has evolved over time. At the latest amendment, it is 550 seats, maximum authorized strength. There are 543 seats currently being occupied. And the ceiling, again, has fluctuated by amendments over the years to Article 81. So any expansion of the size would require a constitutional amendment.

There is, as I mentioned before, an independent delimitation commission authorized by Parliament that is meant to essentially do the nuts and bolts work of this apportionment, of this redistricting. Its orders have the force of law. They are not questioned by the judiciary. It is set up for this purpose by an express act of parliament. The last delimitation act, I believe, had an act that was passed in 2002, roughly that time frame, to set up the delimitation that happened, at least dealing with intra-state boundaries, in 2007, 2008.

保留席位：SC、ST 与女性

The last thing I just want to say very quickly is, because I think it's very relevant to the current debate, this question of reservation. Under the Constitution, one of the things that also gets decided by the commission is which constituencies will be reserved for two types of groups: for scheduled castes and scheduled tribes — two historically disadvantaged, marginalized communities. Together, they have a quota of roughly a quarter of parliament and state assemblies. Those are meant to be given to areas which have scheduled caste and scheduled tribe populations in the greatest numbers.

The new wrinkle, of course, is in 2023, this government managed to get through Parliament a constitutional amendment, the 106th Amendment, which would reserve one third of seats in the Lok Sabha and the various Vidhan Sabha state assemblies for women. And in that amendment as it was passed, it essentially tied the question of the commencement of the quota with the next delimitation — the delimitation we still have not had, because that delimitation was supposed to be taking place after a census, which we also have not had.

So that, I think, essentially is the basic nuts and bolts. Shruti, maybe I'm going to turn the tables on you for a second, because one of the things I haven't gotten into, but I think now is the right time to do it, is — this process has not worked as cleanly or as neatly as I have just laid out. The Constitution is very clear: every 10 years, you have a census, you deal with the implementation, you figure out this reapportionment, redistricting. You have written this excellent piece, which we should have for our listeners in the show notes recently — Indian Express — where you kind of trace back this history because there's a constitutional angle and there's a kind of political angle. So I don't know if you want to maybe elaborate on that for a second.

二、为何走到今天？1976 冻结的来龙去脉

Shruti: Sure. No, that was an excellent primer. Thank you, Milan, for getting us to this point. So the question you're asking is, how did we end up here exactly in 2026?

印度上下两院都是按人口分配的特殊性

So one of the quirks of the Indian Republic is that typically, bicameral legislatures have one of the houses which is apportioned by population and the other house which is apportioned based on some other principle. It may not always be population. Now, in India, both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha were always apportioned on population. Of course, one you have direct representatives and the other is indirect representatives. So the Rajya Sabha was never the US-style Senate or some of the other federal republics which have a very particular number of people from particular ethnicities or particular minorities and so on. So that's the beginning part of the problem.

Now, what that means in the Indian Republic is if you lose population share — even if the absolute numbers are growing — as a share of the total population of India, if any state loses share, it loses some political voice in Parliament proportionate to that, which makes sense because that's how democracies work. But of course, in India, it loses it in both houses.

高度集权的财政结构

Now, a second quirk of the Indian Republic is right at the beginning, India started as a very centralized union of states, not the typical fiscal federalism one would expect. And it only got worse as central planning kept increasing. So India's purse strings have always been very tightly held by the union cabinet and all the allocations have been decided by the union cabinet, which is, of course, based on parliament.

So we reached a situation in the 70s where the richer and more developed and the southern and western states were actually losing the proportional share in parliament based on every decennial census. And that meant that the fiscal pie and all other major decisions of plan expenditure, welfare entitlements, everything was now going to be decided in Parliament, and they had a smaller voice. And it's useful to note that in the 1970s, most states were actually Congress governments, either because they won the election or they got rid of opposition through president's rule. This was all crazy times during the emergency.

第42修正案与冻结的诞生

So the 42nd Amendment, which was really infamous in 1976, did a number of lots of other things other than this.

Milan: Exactly. Lots of things.

Shruti: And the one defining feature of the 42nd Amendment was it was centralizing India more as a republic and placing more power, not just in the hands of the Union Parliament, but actually in the hands of the prime minister's office and the Union cabinet. That was really the structure of it. And where did the power get taken away from? It got taken away from the judiciary and the states, and Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha and so on.

Now, the 44th Amendment undid most of the damage, but one of the things it didn't undo was this freeze — delimitation freeze. Now, what does that mean? So in 1976, the amendment decided that we're going to have population proportional representation in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, except instead of the latest decennial census, we are going to freeze that to the 1971 census. And it was for a time-bound period. It was a period of 25 years because the expectation was that poorer states that have higher fertility rates will sort of catch up in both economic growth and the fertility rates would converge — and 25 years is all one needs for it. So they sort of kicked the can down the road and said in 2001, we will revisit the question of which census Parliament will be apportioned on.

2001：Vajpayee 政府再度推迟

Now, come 2001, we have the Vajpayee government. This is already the third Vajpayee government because the first one lasted a couple of weeks. The second one lasted about a year and a half when AIA-DMK took over.

Milan: I think it was 13 days, 13 months.

Shruti: Exactly. And then the third Vajpayee government, when they took office, was a strong coalition where the strongest partners were from the South and the West. So this is DMK, Telugu Desam Party of Undivided Andhra Pradesh — so it includes both Andhra Pradesh and Telangana representation — Shiv Sena in Maharashtra and so on.

And basically, in 2001, it was untenable to revise to 2001 numbers, because one of the things that had happened thanks to liberalization was that the southern and western states grew even faster economically, and their fertility rates dropped even more. So they would have really lost a larger number of seats — proportional political voice in parliament. But India was still redistributing revenues with formulae from the past. In the 70s, the union government would devolve maybe a quarter of the revenues back to the states. Now, I think it's 41%. And in the early 2000s, it was something in the middle of these two. So that's kind of how this whole thing happened.

州内 vs 州际：双轨冻结

Now, a couple of nuanced additions to add to what you were saying about reservation. There are two parts to the puzzle. There's uneven population growth, both between states, but also within a state. So what they decided was that between states apportionment, we would freeze to 1971 numbers. Within state apportionment would be based on the 2001 census.

And this is to avoid problems like — you know, there was a point in time when Gurgaon was an incredibly malapportioned district because it was growing so fast and there's a huge influx of migrants. Same with Thane more recently — was the single most malapportioned district, electoral district in India. So to avoid problems like that, that would be fixed on the 2001 numbers. And the same for Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe, which are again based on population.

新设邦带来的不对称

So all these things got adjusted. And another thing that happened was that three new states were carved out of the erstwhile Hindi heartland states. So you had Uttarakhand getting carved out of Uttar Pradesh, you had Jharkhand out of Bihar, and you had Chhattisgarh out of Madhya Pradesh. But because within the state numbers were based on the 2001 census, the new states were less severely malapportioned than the parent states.

So where we are as a result of this today is that we still have a system where they kicked the can down the road another 25 years. So the Constitution currently reads that the next delimitation or redistricting exercise will be based on the first census after 2026, which should have been 2031. But of course, we've not, as you pointed out, had the last census. So it could be any census between now and whenever.

So that's where we are now. But that also means that we are apportioning our parliamentary seats based on a census which is 55 years old, which is frankly just extraordinary. And the populations have grown very unevenly, but also economic growth has not only been uneven, but in India — the richer states and the poorer states both are growing economically, but the richer states are growing faster than the poorer states. So we don't have the typical catch-up growth or convergence. So this is where we are now. And I guess I should hand this back to you now.

Milan: I mean, just to add one thing, Shruti — I think it's important to reemphasize that the total kitty, 543, didn't change, number one. Number two, the statewide proportion could not change. So Uttar Pradesh had its 80, Kerala had its 20, Tamil Nadu had its 39. None of that could change until after 2026.

But what did change, and it actually came into place in 2007, 2008, was that within Uttar Pradesh's 80, you could redraw those boundaries such that every member of parliament roughly presided over a similarly sized constituency in terms of population. And so we're kind of bent out of shape in two ways now: not just the malapportionment across states, but it's also been quite a long time since the 2001 census. So the size of constituencies — I mean, Gurgaon's a great example — are also now totally, totally out of whack.

三、比较视角：印度在世界范围内有多失衡？

Milan: I just want to add maybe one comparative data point to this discussion, because I think it's worth thinking about it. There are other federal democracies out there where we can think about how malapportionment in India's lower house sort of compares.

And one of the things that we've done — and this is a forthcoming paper that Louise Tillin from King's College and Andy Rabina, who's one of my colleagues, have coming out, hopefully in a couple of weeks — is to try and do many things, but one of the things is look at this comparatively.

Samuels-Snyder 失衡指数

So in the political science literature, there is this standard measure of malapportionment. It's called the Samuels-Snyder Index after these two political scientists, Samuels and Snyder. And basically what it does is it captures the share of legislative seats that are, quote unquote, "out of place." So basically, you just take the absolute difference between each state's share of seats in the legislature and each state's share of the overall population.

And so what that tells you, if you put federal democracies kind of on a spectrum in 2025, is Argentina turns out to be the most malapportioned. Belgium turns out to be the least malapportioned. India is kind of towards the more malapportioned side. The number that comes out is 8% on the Samuels-Snyder Index, which means that roughly 8% of Lok Sabha seats either over- or under-represent a particular state or union territory. And obviously, within that, the two most underrepresented states would be Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, and the two most overrepresented states would be Tamil Nadu and Kerala.

So what that means at the end of the day, in practical terms, is a vote in a southern state carries more weight than one in the northern state because you've really departed from this foundational principle of one person, one vote. So India is not the most malapportioned, but it is on the higher end of the spectrum.

失衡发生在下议院的反常性

Shruti: Yes. And what I think that percentage also masks a little bit is where it is malapportioned. So it is malapportioned in the lower house, which is typically not the area where the malapportionment takes place in most republics — most federal republics. It's typically in the upper house that you have severe malapportionment. So this is the place where you have direct democracy, where you have the most severe malapportionment.

And the numbers, as you pointed out, Milan — if I remember correctly, I think it's 3.1 million per MP in Bihar and 1.75 million per MP in Kerala. So it's almost twice. And of course, these are all made up numbers because I don't have current census numbers. So everything we discuss after 2011 is a projection. But that's an extraordinary discrepancy, while knowing that all of this is based on fertility rates — and the poorer the group, the later the fertility rates dropped.

So it is more likely that a poor person is in a severely malapportioned state versus a wealthier person. The same is true for scheduled castes and scheduled tribes, because their fertility rates dropped later than the upper caste. The same is true for the youth — I mean, that's fairly obvious. The later you were born, the more young you are. So the groups that had fertility drops seen later are more likely to be in severely malapportioned districts. So that number is very useful as a comparative, but India may be worse on other margins than just the average percentage of malapportionment.

Rajya Sabha 的反常：上议院反而最不失衡

Milan: That's right. And, I think what you said earlier about the Rajya Sabha is important — the upper house, which doesn't ever get any attention in this conversation because its seats are not subject to delimitation. So it's kind of dealt with in a separate schedule of the constitution. And I'm sure we will come back to this because as we think about future scenarios, envisioning a different Rajya Sabha is very much potentially part of the menu of options.

But you can apply the same kind of methodology to looking at malapportionment across upper houses. And what you see is that India, among federal democracies, has the least malapportionment, again, because it is proportional to population. Most upper chambers are not. The U.S. Senate is a great example — every state has two seats, that's it. So on that spectrum, Argentina's malapportionment statistic is like upward of 40%. And in India, it's 12%.

So what that means, if you step back, is that the Rajya Sabha reinforces majoritarian outcomes to a greater degree than other comparator upper houses. And so you end up with two chambers that largely mirror the population. So if we think about the Rajya Sabha as a council of states, which is in theory what it was meant to be, this raises some interesting questions — which again, I think we can come back to.

四、131号宪法修正案：政府试图做什么？

Milan: But, Shruti, maybe now since we've kind of cleared our throats with all this background, we should say a word maybe about what the government tried to do. I mean, I think that's why we're all here on this call. We wouldn't be having this conversation probably on April 24th had the government not called a special session of parliament abruptly, with very little notice, presented three bills which have massive consequences. The bills, of course, did not succeed, but they have kind of set the terms of the debate. Do you want to just start, maybe, and I can fill in a little bit on what actually the government's stated intent was through that legislation?

三个法案的全景

Shruti: Yes. So I would not frame it as the government's stated intent or outcome. The outcome was that the bills failed. The stated intent, I can't understand. I can just base my remarks on the text of the bill, which I think is the only thing people like you and I have to go on at this point.

So basically, in this special session of Parliament, they introduced a Constitution Amendment Bill — this is the 131st Constitution Amendment Bill, which lost the vote — and two other bills which would have been tabled and passed were the Constitutional Amendment Bill to pass. So right now, none of these bills are active law or have passed. So that's the beginning point.

第131修正案的内容

So what did the 131st Constitutional Amendment Bill try to do? And again, constitutional interpretation is a complicated and almost fatal business in India. So I'm going to try and be very careful, but maybe I have personally misunderstood many things.

(1) 扩容到 850 席

So the first thing it did was to actually increase the number of seats in Lok Sabha from 550, which is the current limit, to 850, where 835 would go to the states and 15 would go to the union territories. Now, this is not a terrible idea in itself, because in addition to being malapportioned, India also has some of the worst population-to-elected-representative ratios. The original constitution capped it at one MP for 750,000 people. We have gone very far from that. It's like three to four times that number in most constituencies. This will help stabilize that a little bit, so MPs can actually serve their citizens better. So that's one.

(2) 切断"最近一次普查"与 delimitation 的硬绑定

But the more complicated exercise was not just increasing the number, but also what would be the basis on which these 850 seats would be shared between the states, which I guess is the big question given the delimitation freeze to 1971 census numbers.

So the original phrasing of the Constitution was that delimitation exercise would be conducted upon each decennial census, and then the numbers would be adjusted to have roughly equal constituency sizes "as far as practicable." Now, this actually removes that link between the most recent decennial census and delimitation. So that's a really important change that I want to flag.

(3) 新的 Delimitation Commission

Now, what does it replace it with? Now, that is a little bit less clear. So it replaces it with a delimitation commission, which was one of the other bills had it passed. This commission would be a new constitutional commission. The act had a number of requirements of who would serve on that commission. It's typically a former chief justice of India or Supreme Court justice, a former election commissioner, the Lok Sabha speaker would appoint a few MPs and so on and so forth.

So there's the delimitation commission, which would then determine which census members would be used to then apportion the seats. So if you now switch gears between the constitutional amendment and the delimitation commission bill, the delimitation commission bill simply says the most recent census, which happens to be 2011.

But it's important to note that in India, bills can be amended with simple majority, whereas constitutional amendments require a special majority — which incidentally failed. So by pushing the decision of which census numbers from the constitutional mandate to the delimitation commission, which is based on a statute of parliament and can be changed with simple majority, we've basically really switched things — who makes this decision from a constitutional mandate to more of a political decision making.

It also reopened the question of intrastate constituency boundaries, which would again be based on whatever the delimitation commission decided. This was all true not just for Articles 81 and 82, which is for the parliament, but also Article 170, which is for the state legislature.

(4) 女性保留与女法案的链接

The other major aspect was to link the women's reservation to the current exercise of delimitation. So the original women's reservation constitutional amendment, which was the 106th constitutional amendment, which amended the constitution to add Article 334A — sorry guys —

Milan: Wow. I'm impressed that you could say that.

Shruti: That was supposed to be after an exercise of delimitation process is undertaken for this purpose. So now it's no longer going to be based on the most recent census. It'll be whatever the political compromises.

(5) 联邦属地法案

Now, the third bill in this mix is one on the union territories, because we've so far discussed the states. Union territories usually tend to be quite tiny, but there are two big ones. One is Jammu and Kashmir, which is now a union territory, post all the politics of Article 370. And there is Delhi, and to a lesser extent there is Puducherry. And Delhi and Jammu and Kashmir really have the lion's share of the parliamentary seats. Everything else is just one or two seats. So that's the other thing that needs to be reapportioned — those 15 seats.

Shruti 的 TLDR 与隐藏条款

So this is what the new bills tried to do. My TLDR on this is that they really shift it from the constitutional level and the parliamentary level — the way we have delegated or subordinate legislation making — now we have delegated apportioning in some sense. And it's delegated to the Delimitation Commission, which is not the most robust organization if it's based on what will be introduced and it can decide what census it's based on. So it's not even going to be the most current numbers.

A second thing which is really hidden in there and you need to see quite carefully is it actually limits whether delimitation orders can be challenged in court. And the language very specifically says in the delimitation bill that the new delimitation cannot or shall not be called in question in any court. So that's the other part of the constitutional drama.

And the last part of the constitutional drama is, of course, how we apportion the reserved categories, which you pointed out is both scheduled caste and scheduled tribes, but also women. And the scheduled caste, scheduled tribe is pretty straightforward — it's based on the original constitution as it has been going, based on whichever census the delimitation commission would have chosen had this bill passed. But now you have to also accommodate the women's reservation.

Milan 的复述与确认

Milan: So let me just see, Shruti, if I can give you my TLDR and see how well it matches up — just because there are a lot of moving parts here.

So the Constitutional Amendment Bill does three things mainly: 1. It raises the constitutional ceiling of the Lok Sabha to 850 seats — 815 for states, 35 for union territories. 2. It says that the number of seats for each state in parliament is going to be based on its proportion in the population of states. 3. And I think this is important, and this gets to the point of who is the arbiter — it removes the automatic post-census trigger for reapportionment. So it now gives Parliament the discretion to decide when delimitation will happen and which census to use, which is something that had been kind of hard-coded.

When it comes to the delimitation bill, obviously, in the main, it's kind of providing the operational framework for delimitation. But there is this interesting ambiguity in there, which it says that the seat allocation — how much UP will get, how much Kerala will get, as well as the redrawing of seats within states — is going to be based on, quote, "the latest census figures published," question mark, because we don't really know what that could mean.

And then there's the UT bill, which one of the things it's doing is just essentially ensuring that the framework for reapportionment that would happen at the state level also happens to these three important UTs which also have kind of state legislatures. And then, of course, there's the commencement or the kicking in of the one-third quota for women.

对 2023 年女性保留绑定的疑问

I mean, I think just to take a step back, there was a deliberate decision taken in 2023 when women's reservation was introduced to tie it to delimitation and by extension to the census. And maybe we should just pause and talk about this for a second, Shruti, because — I'm not a constitutional scholar, but it strikes me that if you go back in time to 2023, there was no real need for that to happen.

You could have come up with an alternative legislative framework which said: we are going to take 543 seats, we're going to reserve a third of them in time for the May 2024 general election. I think everyone's reading of that was like: wow, nobody wants to do that because it means people — mostly men — who have those seats are going to be out of a job come next election. I mean, is that a fair assessment, do you think, of what went down in 2023?

Shruti: I think they were setting this up in 2023 as part of some long game. So now let's think through the arithmetic of it. Let's say that you're right and it's really self-interested men who don't want to give up power. It's easier to pass legislation introducing reservation than a constitutional amendment. So the very fact that they managed to whip the votes for a constitutional amendment tells me that there was some other backroom bargain involved given to the very same men — that, hey, if you agree to this constitutional amendment and we link it to delimitation exercise in some way, we will do this later in a way that benefits the same groups.

So I don't see — I'm exactly with you. There was no need for women's reservation in parliament or anywhere else to be hard-coded in the constitution unless they thought future governments would undo it. That's literally the only reason to have done that. And it was much harder to pass the constitutional amendment. So it's a little bit odd. And now in hindsight, it seems like that's exactly what they were setting up — which is some way to bring in women's reservation and then say that, oh, the women in Uttar Pradesh are now going to be less than women in Tamil Nadu or in Kerala, and make this a hot button issue as opposed to what are the other hot button issues. So I have a feeling it was something about narrative building. I still can't figure it out.

五、政治动机：为什么是现在？

Milan: That brings us to the question probably everybody has joined this conversation to ponder with us, which is the politics behind this special session and these bills and why now and what is going on. And I just want to say at the outset that I think it's fair to say you and I are both a little bit puzzled because there are a lot of theories, a lot of hypotheses.

叙事建设的需要

But maybe just to kind of break it down to first principles just to start. Clearly, if you are a party and you are a government in search of a narrative because many other things perhaps are not going as you envisioned — the economy is not firing on all cylinders, some of that may be because of your own doing, some of that may be because of geopolitical factors, the Iran war, Trump's tariffs, what have you. The emotive issues that have powered the BJP and the Sangh Parivar — Article 370, Jammu and Kashmir, Ram Mandir — are taken off the table. You still have the Uniform Civil Code, you have the National Register of Citizens, you have other things, but the outlook for those is not quite as clear.

So if you are looking for narrative building, women's reservation is certainly an issue that you could pick on, especially because the government and the ruling party have made, I think, a priority — certainly in terms of their rhetoric and some would say even in terms of their policies — over the past 12 years to try and cater to women and make that a centerpiece of their broader political strategy. And there are lots of people who've actually done some really interesting work on how and why the BJP has decided to do this.

Now, again, let's just assume for narrative building convenience this was what you settled on. In their minds, they're setting up, I think, a very nice trap, because you put out there this very constitutionally vexing question of delimitation, which has huge political ramifications. You would attach it to women's reservation, which is, I think, broadly popular, broadly all parties in theory support. And then when this package fails, you can blame it on the opposition as saying — they never wanted women's reservation to begin with.

旅程比目的地更重要

And I think, you know, one of the things we were discussing before we started, but I think is true of the BJP historically — and maybe other political parties as well — is that oftentimes the narrative is really about the journey as much as it is about the destination. I mean, Ram Mandir's a great example of that. That was an emotive issue that powered the party from essentially two seats in parliament in 1984 to what it is now. And interestingly, once they actually got the temple built, they didn't really see much political payoff. It really was in the agitation and narrative building. They lost Ayodhya.

So this gives them a juicy narrative for the state elections, which are ongoing — voting has just started in Tamil Nadu and West Bengal — leading all the way to 2029.

第二条线索：选区重划作为政治工具

There's a second reason why they may have wanted to do this, which has to do with the concept of delimitation as a tool to redraw constituency boundaries within states. There have been relatively few allegations prior to the past five years that delimitation has been used as an expressly political tool to redraw either state assembly or parliamentary constituency boundaries for political reasons. There have been a couple of political scientists who have looked at this. None of them have come away with any really convincing evidence to suggest there was some kind of tinkering going on.

However, we have seen two states in recent memory — Assam and Jammu and Kashmir — which never had their boundaries redrawn as part of the overall package in the mid-2000s for different reasons given the sensitivities of those states. In both of those states now, we at least have qualitative suggestive evidence of gerrymandering of different kinds in both places. And so if this is part of what you want to try to embed when you think of all 28 states, then this is a useful thing to do.

口头担保 vs. 文本

Here is where I've reached the limits of my knowledge — which is what the plan was when it comes to statewide representation in Parliament, because it seems to me that they were saying and doing two entirely different things. You have the text of the constitutional amendment bill, which has very clearly: we are going to allocate seats according to population. We're going to rip off this band-aid of this freeze that's been set in motion since the 42nd amendment. And we're going to see that massive divergence — the North's going to gain a lot, the South's going to lose a lot.

Coupled with an oral guarantee, a verbal guarantee that no, no, no, no, we don't want to interfere at all in the statewide allocation of seats. The home minister said that, the prime minister said that, every party spokesperson said that — even to the point that in the final hours of the debate, Amit Shah, the home minister, said: if you want me, I will leave here, come back in an hour with an amendment that codifies this. Whether that was bluffing, whether it was serious, is for the historians to tell us.

So this is where I'm confused about what their real intention was. There is one argument which says this was just all trolling — that they knew this wasn't going to pass, they knew they didn't have a two-thirds majority. You could throw out this promise of an amendment. Paint the opposition as unreasonable. "See, we said we would hardwire not messing with the statewide shares and they didn't go along with it." But obviously, they must have known that these guarantees were not going to be sufficient in the way that they were communicated to really placate the states that would be on the losing end.

So if you step back, maybe one thing they were simply doing was just kind of maintaining negotiating flexibility and painting a narrative that puts the opposition on the back foot, testing political reactions. You got some very useful information. You understood whether the opposition was going to hang together, how strongly the southern states were going to fight back, whether some of your own allies like Chandrababu Naidu and the TDP, who represent a big southern state, were going to stay with you. They did stay with you. And this kind of sets the terms of the debate. I expect that future scenarios are going to kind of use what they put out as some kind of baseline. So anyway, I would love to hear what you think about any or all of that.

Shruti 的反问：BJP 想在南方破局？

Shruti: So I have one question, and I think you're the perfect person to answer it. To me, it seems like they're in a bit of a bind. They want to reapportion the seats in Parliament based on the latest census, which means we know which states will gain — it's really the Hindi heartland states, which are much poorer. We know that West Bengal, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh are all losers in this bargain. Maharashtra maybe loses one seat and so on and so forth.
