Now, to me, it seems like they've been trying to gain a foothold in Tamil Nadu and West Bengal and Kerala for a long time, and the BJP has never really succeeded in those states. So they didn't want to have future election campaigns to be based on — BJP can't win in the state because at the union level they're going to quash our language, they're going to take away all the state's resources and redistribute it to the Hindi heartland and so on. So their way out was to build a larger sort of umbrella campaign around women, which clearly didn't work out.

What do you make of that argument? Because it seems like BJP always tries to build the broader coalition. So they try to do the same thing when it came to caste politics — let's unite all Hindus under one umbrella. Is that the sort of thing going on here, that we can't win these state elections based on language and finances and those kinds of discussions, let's try and build a different coalition?

Milan: I don't know, Shruti. I find it very hard to believe — there is this conventional wisdom out there which is this would all be great for the BJP because their strongholds are in the central states, the northern states, which would gain. They could lose everything else. But if you think about their long-term project — and this has been true for decades and I think it's very much pronounced as we speak right now — their long-term strategy is to build inroads, and they have built inroads (maybe not as significant as they would like) in the east, in the northeast, in the south, where they continue chipping away, increasing their vote share, winning seats, some years more than others.

But I would be surprised if they're willing to forsake all of that for this kind of a victory, which is why I have a hard time believing in their heart of hearts they really wanted this to happen. I think one of the things which seems more likely, and it comes back to something we said earlier, is — you have the text of this bill, but you also have this newly empowered, super empowered, I would say, delimitation commission. And you make them the arbiter and you seek to influence them. And they are the ones who are going to carry out your guarantee of statewide shares or not. But you appoint those people, you control them, you exercise influence as the executive.

Now, as a constitutional scholar, I ask you: this strikes me as an incredibly legally dubious strategy. How will that be held up eventually by the Supreme Court if the text of the bill itself is so black and white in terms of how apportionment should be done?

Shruti: Yeah, it is totally legally dubious, which is why they've also embedded the "can't be challenged in court" part — because I think they expected it would be challenged in court. But even before the delimitation bill, the constitutional amendment would have been challenged in court, for which there is no immunity against that because of Kesavananda Bharati and the basic structure of the Constitution. The Supreme Court can at any point reopen any constitutional amendment question that it wishes to reopen.

So I do agree with you that it is a little bit constitutionally and legally dubious, but in all fairness to them — we have been for 55 years, or 50 years, in a constitutionally dubious place. Why should we place any higher constitutional morality or imagination to this kind of crazy political bargain that happened in the middle of the emergency, the worst time of India's democracy in some sense, and actually give weight to that and continue it for over five decades? So it seems to me like we were already in some political compromise, except the political compromise was made by our grandparents' generation. And the only real difference is now the political compromise will be made by our generation. And I guess we are always more skeptical of how this will play out now because it seems a little bit less clear in terms of the legal text. But if this was always going to be political, then past a point, what difference does it make? That could be one kind of attitude.

六、当下格局与法案受挫后的政治版图

Milan: I think it might be useful just to kind of discuss what the state of play is now. So the government bills stand defeated. So basically the existing ex-ante constitutional framework remains intact. That imposes clear constraints on what can happen next.

So for now: - size of the Lok Sabha remains capped at 550 seats; - longstanding freeze on reapportionment across states continues; - crucially, there can be no fresh delimitation exercise until the results of the next census, which is currently underway — it formally started on April 1st of this year — until those results are published.

Until then, obviously, the map will remain frozen unless the government is able to come back and broker some kind of compromise. So when that delimitation happens after the census results are published, it will be population-based unless the Constitution is amended. And again, that applies both to the allocation of seats across states as well as within them.

And that same sequencing and that sort of schedule is going to apply to women's reservation. Because, again, that implementation is tied to the completion of fresh census delimitation. So even though the government has formally notified the enactment of women's reservation — something that happened as this debate was happening — nothing can really happen until this current census is completed and a delimitation is carried out.

So in the short run, the opposition can claim a victory. It has stymied this rushed, controversial legislation. But the underlying issues — these cross pressures between democracy, one person one vote, and how do you think of the federal compact — those issues are essentially just been deferred. And it's the next census that, again assuming nothing happens in the interim, that's going to set in motion a highly consequential process that's going to determine this negotiation between and within states.

数字想象：今天的得与失

Shruti: So can you give us a sense of what the current map of India looks like if we use numbers from today, which are obviously projections based on the 2011 census and the 2019 projections that were based on the 2011 census and so on? Like, do you have a sense — I mean, we don't have time to go over every single state — but sort of the greatest hits, the biggest winners and the losers?

Milan: Yeah. So let's just assume, for instance, that you have the two baselines.

(1) 以 2011 普查为基线

One is the baseline of the 2011 census. Assume that the 543 remains untouched. - Net losers: Tamil Nadu loses 7 seats, Kerala loses 5 seats, Andhra Pradesh loses 3. - Net gainers: Uttar Pradesh +8, Bihar +6, Rajasthan +5.

There are only two states, according to our calculations, that would be kind of untouched — Assam and Chhattisgarh, which essentially stay the same. Otherwise, everyone at least loses one seat or gains one seat.

(2) 以 2026 投影为基线

What happens when you use more current projections of the 2026 population? Obviously, just those numbers get inflated. So if Tamil Nadu lost seven before, it loses 10. Andhra Pradesh loses five (excuse me). Kerala loses six. So that's 21 seats just with those three southern states that goes away. And then on the flip side, Rajasthan, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh — you have 26 seats that they gain, as opposed to 19 seats in 2011. These are really significant shifts of power amongst significant, large, consequential states.

为什么北方"赢家"反而沉默？

Shruti: And I will add here that one of the biggest reasons why you haven't seen the states that would gain the most say very much about it — for instance, you don't have the chief ministers of Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh and Bihar and Rajasthan sort of building the narrative that their people are getting ripped off and there isn't one person, one vote, and they're losing their voice in parliament.

And the simple reason for that is the states that stand to gain the most are also some of the poorest states in India. And they rely heavily on fiscal transfers from the richer states. So what we really see is some kind of political bargain has been made in the past — that India is very fiscally centralized, Parliament decides how the money pie or the fiscal pie gets split, so the larger the voice in parliament, the more say you have over how to split the fiscal pie. But the money is not getting generated in these states. In fact, these states are net recipients from other states. And it is really Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra who are on the losing side of the seats — if we reapportion with current population numbers — and who are the biggest contributors to the national kitty or to the fiscal finances.

So this again plays into the politics of it: the narrative building has not been a North versus South, because that automatically also sheds light on the rich states versus poor states. And if you want to continue taking these transfers, then you have to, in some sense, give up some political voice. But the core of it is not just that the numbers change — it is that it's the economics behind the numbers. And the economics are simple: the less developed you are, or the later you joined the development party post-liberalization, the later your fertility rates fell, which means the more malapportioned you are today based on a census from 55 years ago. That's just the simple math of it. We can get into the more complicated math.

七、未来情景与方案菜单

Milan: I think it's worth maybe saying what are the scenarios that are out there and that people have kind of talked about.

方案一：继续冻结

So we've talked about a couple already. One is simply extending the freeze. Passing an amendment to the Constitution saying: OK, we don't want to touch this hot potato. We deferred this decision from '76 to 2001, we deferred again, we're just going to keep deferring it for another 25 years. Which actually wouldn't be a surprising outcome in many ways.

方案二：完全按人口重分配

The second is you move towards some full population-based reapportionment. You could do that with the current size — I've kind of given you those numbers. You could also do it with some expanded size. You could take the 850 seats the government mentioned and imagine what the interstate allocation would look like.

方案三：温和扩张（"无邦失席"）

There's also one idea that's been put forward in the past, which is kind of what we call — Louise, Andy and I in this paper — kind of moderated expansion. I believe the principle they followed in the delimitation exercise after the '71 census, where they said: OK, we're going to expand the Lok Sabha just enough to ensure that no single state loses seats. Underrepresented states are going to gain additional seats to bring their representation closer to the population share, but we're not going to take away any seats from any state. We're just going to enlarge the pie.

So if you use the standard, what's called the Webster method, you use your updated population projections, you do the math — that requires expanding the Lok Sabha to about 775 members. So smaller somewhat than what the government envisioned, but still obviously 200+ from what it is today.

What are the pros of that approach? Well, one is it's political — no state would lose a seat, so you wouldn't lose the share.

Shruti: Of course. No person currently occupying.

Milan: Occupying. Exactly. Which is important, actually.

Shruti: That's an important element.

Milan: Which would get thrown out. The second, I guess, is a little bit of a kind of normative thing, where you're moving towards population parity without fully resetting the system. So you do get closer to one person, one vote, but it's not the sharpest discontinuity. And by stopping short of that larger expansion, you maybe also avoid some of the unintended consequences of moving to a really jumbo house with 850 members. Some would say 775 is still pretty jumbo, but you know what I mean.

Now, obviously, the downside is what you said — is this really going to satisfy the concerns of southern states? They want to preserve the federal influence as it exists today. Even if they're not losing seats in absolute terms, certainly the shares would diverge in relative terms.

方案四：混合公式

So there are another set of options which people have thrown out, which are kind of hybrid formulas. One option is what people have called digressive proportionality, where basically more populous states receive more seats but with diminishing returns. So you don't make the adjustment linear with the population. That's kind of a systematic compromise. You could come up with a scheme that says there's a floor — no state is going to fall below the floor — but then on top of that we're going to move to some kind of proportionality. There's also something which I know has come into discussion in the Finance Commission context, which is let's use a blended census formula rather than going from '71 to 2011 or to 2027 (whenever the census is done). Let's use some complicated arithmetic to smooth out some of the rough edges.

Shruti：根本问题是财政

Shruti: Yeah. But none of them really get to the heart of the problem, I think, even now, which is — India in 1976 was a socialist state, but it wasn't a welfare state in the manner it is today, mainly because it didn't have the money or the revenues to be a welfare state. India really became a welfare state after 1991, when state revenue started pouring in and economic growth took off. And a very large part of the reason to be in political power is to be able to redistribute. And a very large part of getting to be in political power is making promises that you are keeping the promises of redistribution in the past.

So if you just look at what is happening in each state, especially the poorer states — and Arvind and Devesh's most recent book, A Sixth of Humanity, is fantastic at documenting this at how much we rely on welfare transfers as opposed to building good old physical and human capital — every interest group starts getting a welfare transfer. The women are the latest and largest of the interest groups, as you pointed out earlier in the conversation. But to be able to do that, you need money. And to be able to have money, you need economic growth. And it doesn't look like some of these larger and poorer states have really gotten their act together in recent times or even in the last 30 years.

Finance Commission 的纵向与横向分配

So I still think we need to pay attention to the economics of it, because if we look at the current Finance Commission devolution — and this is not the proposals that we might have, this is literally what the commission says.

Milan: And has just said. The report has just been tabled.

Shruti: Exactly. And the Finance Commission has two methods of devolution: one is vertical and one is horizontal. So in terms of vertical devolution of the divisible pool — center and the states contribute to a particular divisible pool, and that pool then gets split up between the union and the states. And that's not equal. The states get about 41% from that pool and the union gets the remaining. So that's one part of the split. But the second part is the horizontal —

Milan: How it gets shared by states.

Shruti: Exactly. So now that 41%, how it gets shared by states is based on a weighted average formula. And the weights are poverty and population — the two things that basically stack the odds in favor of the poorer states. And when I say poorer states, I just mean they're the ones who rely on intergovernmental transfers. So from the overall kitty and from the richer states, you have basically this movement of revenue going to these poorer states. And it's going again in proportion of population and poverty — which is exactly what the malapportionment problem was about, too. It was based on population and poverty, because poorer groups saw their fertility rates drop later. So the whole thing seemed a little too interlinked for us to ignore the economics of it.

Shruti 的方案：更大幅度的财政下放

So I would put one more proposal on the table, which is — today, most countries don't have states with equal representation in parliament. As states lose population numbers, they have lower representation, and that seems fair. But in most other federal republics, you don't have this kind of centralized distribution of finances. So if you allow most states to keep their finances — if the union-state vertical devolution was, you know, 90% devolved to the states and left with the union — you would not have the southern states kick off such a big fuss, because their people lose too. Like, let's not forget that they also have poorer groups, they also have marginalized groups which suffer from having constituencies delimited from a 55-year-old census and so on. They are the more progressive states.

So I would also want to put that very much on the table, because I feel like if we don't solve the fiscal bargain in some way, we can't solve the constitutional bargain — in a way that would reflect the principle of one person, one vote.

四股交叉力量

Milan: I think that's exactly right. And it would also require — without going into too much of the details — that divisible pool that you mentioned, which is essentially the money that then gets divvied up: the union government has also worked assiduously to try and shrink that divisible pool by having cesses and surcharges and other things which go around that. So you would have to have a kind of cleaning up of all of that, including maybe increasing the number.

So I think you raise a larger point, which is: you have at least four cross-currents going on at the same time. And we have been talking about them largely in isolation, including much of this conversation.

  1. One is this question of political representation and how that squares with population. That's a ticking time bomb in some sense.
  2. The second is this feeling of resentment around fiscal transfers — a rich state puts in so much into the kitty and they only get so much out. It's unfair because we're subsidizing poorly governed states that can't get their act together. That sense is clearly building up.
  3. The third is the nature of taxation has changed. You now have a unified goods and services tax, which has limited the degree of state autonomy in terms of tax reform, tax innovation, because you've cast your lot with this new system where you don't have as much autonomy or sovereignty. You have a kind of pooled sovereignty where you share tax jurisdiction with the center.
  4. And then the last thing is, of course, interstate migration, where you see a lot of people moving from poorer places to richer places — Maharashtra, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, you name it. And that has helped the economies of certain states, you could argue, but it's also put pressure on them in terms of inadequate infrastructure, welfare, transportation, led to a lot of calls for kind of job reservations for locals, "sons of the soil," quote unquote, and so on and so forth.

So they're all of these things and they're all inextricably linked. So it seems to me that you're at a moment akin to — and other people have kind of said this — of a kind of States Reorganization Commission–like moment, which is not just about state boundaries, but really has to be part of this broader conversation.

福利的可携性

Shruti: No, I could not agree more. And I think in addition to the GST Council and the splitting of finances and so on, there is another conversation to be had about portability of benefits and what kinds of subsidies we give. If most of the subsidies are being given to agriculture, which are highly local and trap people in a particular state and those benefits are not portable, then what you end up seeing is that the money is leaving the richer southern states. It's remaining locked and trapped in these poor agrarian regions in the northern states. Plus you have all these seasonal workers and so on moving to the southern states, and that's what causes this kind of NIMBYism.

If we fundamentally adjust and make these benefits very portable, maybe the southern states would also feel very differently about who is coming to their states. Yamini Iyer has made this point about making welfare entitlements more portable. Lots of people have talked about this. But that's another piece of the puzzle, where maybe migration will take care of it. But for that, we again need to solve the fiscal puzzle.

八、Rajya Sabha 改革的可能性

Milan: Can we just go back for one second to something which I wanted to circle back to? It's come up a couple of times, which is the Rajya Sabha, which I think is important also in this conversation to think about — what the objective of this house is and how to make it a more effective representative of states' interests. After all, it is meant to be a council of states. And I think most people would agree that is not the way that it's turned out.

So there are things that one could do there.

改革一：邦际席位平等化

Number one — it's a radical reform, but changing the composition of the Rajya Sabha to reflect the equality of states rather than their relative size. So take it out of that proportional ballgame. Kind of like the U.S. Senate. The downside — Madhavan has a great piece on this in The Hindu — which is, you can't just give each state two. Because remember, there are times when you're going to have a joint sitting of parliament, when all of a sudden you're going to have a really shrunken Rajya Sabha and potentially a really large Lok Sabha. And it's fine if the Lok Sabha has the upper hand, but it can't be so out of whack. So you'd have to think about what that equalizing number would be.

Shruti: But even more than just the number, I think we can't give states equal numbers because in India you can just create a state very, very easily. We created states by voice vote in the past. Which is Article 3. So even if we're going to go the U.S. Senate way where we give them 15 seats each to solve the Madhavan problem, you still need to adjust how states can be created and destroyed. And we can turn states into union territories. And we have. So I think those sorts of things also need to be baked into the constitutional design. Sorry, please.

改革二：户籍要求与直选

Milan: No, no, totally. Absolutely. And then, again, another thing which people have talked about, I think is really important: there was a 2003 amendment to the Representation of the People Act, which essentially dispensed with the notion that if you were a Rajya Sabha member, you had to be domiciled in the state that you represent. Now, that had already been hollowed out — I mean, I think famously Manmohan Singh was an Assam Rajya Sabha MP. So you would have to not just reinstate the domicile requirement, but do something even stronger. One solution to that, it's indirect, is to say, well, actually make these people directly elected and make them accountable to you as opposed to just indirectly chosen. But there may be other administrative ways of doing it.

改革三：反叛逃法的副作用

There's one final thing to mention, and this actually relates to a question that somebody has asked in the audience, about the Tenth Schedule, which is commonly known as the anti-defection law — an amendment to the Constitution passed in 1985, which says, in short, that if you are a legislator at the state or national levels, and you defy a party whip (which is basically to say you vote in a way that's contrary to how your party has ordered you to vote), not only can you be kicked out of the party, you can be disqualified from parliament.

And so what that has done over time, on many things — one of the things it's done is really concentrated power in the hands of party leaders and really taken away agency from ordinary representatives. And so the question that was asked is: what does it matter if we increase the number of MPs if they really can't vote for themselves? And I think this is particularly an issue in the Rajya Sabha. Because if you want it to be reflective of state interest, these people then have to be able to say, OK, actually it's in Rajasthan's interest or Punjab's interest or so on and so forth. Then right now you really can't do that because you will lose your seat.

Shruti：议员的非投票职能与货币法案问题

Shruti: But, you know, the only purpose of being an elected representative is not just voting in parliament and voting your conscience. There's also a lot of things done on the ground. There are things like MPLAD, which is the money that is dispersed at a constituency level. It is also about aggregating preferences. So let's say they're not voting their conscience in parliament. But if they're self-interested politicians, at the very least, they're taking the preferences within their constituency to the party. So I do think it is important to have better representation, notwithstanding the problems caused by delimitation.

But to go back to your Rajya Sabha point, I think the most important reform we need in the Rajya Sabha, and this is irrespective of the delimitation stuff, is we need to make money bills required to pass in Rajya Sabha. This is essential. Money bills can just bypass Rajya Sabha. And of course, the downstream consequence of that is whenever the government doesn't have the votes to pass something, they add a budgetary or appropriations element to it and they turn it into a money bill, which is what they did with the Aadhaar bill. Remember, Aadhaar was passed as a money bill, which is a little bit hilarious. But if money bills under Article 109 are not required to be tabled and passed in Rajya Sabha, then we have a very big problem when it comes to the representation of states, when it comes to how we split the fiscal pie if you're decentralized.

Milan: Yeah, yeah. The other wrinkle in that also is the fact that what people have designated money bills, at least to a layperson, surely don't seem like money bills. The Supreme Court of India disagrees with that assessment.

But maybe it's time to open up to questions. I think people have already put in some questions in the chat. We will try and take as many as we can in the remaining 18 or so minutes.

九、问答环节

Q1：如何防止 delimitation 沦为党派工具？

Milan: I think we sort of answered the one on the 10th Schedule, which I think is absolutely right — which is, if you increase the number of MPs, you're not maybe getting as much bang for the buck because people can't really vote for their interests.

Shruti, there's another question about the safeguards in delimitation. So how do we ensure that the next delimitation, once a commission is set up, does not become a tool for partisan boundary drawing, especially given the concerns that we mentioned already about Assam and J&K?

Shruti: It's always going to be political. And this is true, not just in India. It's true across the world. The question is, can we minimize capture? I don't think we can eliminate the political nature of the exercise. I do think the way the current delimitation commission was floated through the bill that did not pass or wasn't even put to a vote — it says that the speaker will pick five people from parliament or the state legislature in addition to the election commissioner and Supreme Court judges. I think we explicitly need to create room for opposition.

Milan: Yeah, there should be — they have to be cross-party.

Shruti: They have to be cross-party. So I think that's an important piece of the puzzle. And whatever we're talking about at the parliamentary level is quite symmetric and mirrors at the state level. So the state — same reform would have to happen at the state level. That would be my major concern.

And if you have — for instance, you have a quarter of the seats reserved for SCs and STs, you have a quarter of the seats reserved for women, but that is not reflected in the delimitation commission. So now, do we need to have this kind of reservation or representation all the way through or not? I'm not saying one way or another, but at least it's a topic of conversation. It should be put up for debate.

Milan: I would just say, Shruti, that the reason to me this is such an interesting issue is that India, for most of its post-independence history, has actually managed to create a kind of independent technocratic way of doing redistricting. And you just look at the shenanigans going on in the United States, where it's so broken down and now we're just seeing a kind of arms race with Democratic and Republican states trying to one-up each other because that's the incentive. I don't blame the Democrats at all for doing what they're doing because if you're not going to have mutual disarmament and you just have unilateral disarmament, then that's politically disastrous.

When you pass the delimitation bill, I think there are some hard legal criteria that you do want to encode. Obviously, population equality is one. Geographic compactness is another. You want to have contiguity. You want to have respect for administrative boundaries. If you look at some of these maps that are put out in Assam — you have natural geographic barriers, or you have islands of one enclave of a constituency that's fully occupied on all four sides enclosed by another. And so I think some of that — the other thing they did when they did the redistricting, I believe it was in Assam (someone could fact-check me), is they randomly introduced population density as a measure, which was not something that they had done before. It's complicated in a hilly state to begin with, which is very different in the plains. It seemed very —

Shruti: targeted to get a particular kind of outcome.

Milan: Correct. And so I think one is — there are certain things you can do and insist upon. The second is obviously there should be some modicum of transparency around the publication of draft maps, justifications for close calls and decisions you had to make. One thing I don't have the answer to, Shruti, is — I could be mistaken here — is that I think it has been a norm that the delimitation commission essentially gets a thumbs up, thumbs down vote on parliament. It can't be amended and so on and so forth. The judicial review piece, I'm not sure. You highlighted that as something in this recent bill, which clearly is problematic. You don't necessarily want the judiciary to get involved to influence outcomes. But you certainly do want them involved if there are violations of process.

Shruti: Yeah. And that's the thing. We have so much delegated legislation in India that if you look at the delimitation bill, it's pretty sparse. And then you say the delimitation commission or the relevant authority has the power to make rules. And now those rules have to come under challenge if they violate rule of law principles. If they're arbitrary or discretionary, removing the courts from that ambit is a problem. So I completely agree with you that I don't think the court should be involved in everything. In fact, I think Indian courts talk too much about too many things and literally don't do the job they're supposed to do, which is resolve disputes and conflicts and speak on constitutional matters like electoral bonds or demonetization in a five-year period and so on. But having said that, you do need some check.

So my recommendation to Parliament would be: if you're going to leave the courts out of it, then don't have delegated legislation when it comes to delimitation. Actually flesh out the bill at a level of clarity that when we pass it in Parliament, we have a pretty good sense of what is being passed here.

Q2：BJP 与南方政党之间什么样才算"善意的妥协"？

Milan: I think bottom line — and there are a lot of questions on this, which I think we've kind of answered in terms of this question of judicial review — what does a good faith kind of delimitation commission look like? I think you and I both agree that you want it to be independent. But you need to have some constraints on how the commission exercises discretion, which means you have to have something hard-coded. You also probably have to have some process where you have representatives across the political spectrum who are giving input.

There's a question here, Shruti, which I think you kind of went over in your response on the fiscal federalism side, which is — what is a good faith bargain look like between the BJP and southern regional parties? Is it enough to say, look, we are going to come up with some way of making representation more proportional, but the quid pro quo here is that we're going to take devolution from 41% to 71% or whatever the number is. Do you think that is a bargain that southern states would go for?

Shruti: Yeah. So I would actually call this a bargain, not just between the BJP and DMK or something. I think this is across the board because remember, Haryana is a net contributor. Gujarat is a net contributor. So there are many states which are not dubbed southern progressive states which are part of this puzzle. So I do think they need a larger bargain in parliament across the board.

And the BJP tends to represent the most number of people from the Pure Hindi Heartland Belt. So I think they are involved in that end of the bargain. The opposition has shown that they can come together when required. And I think even within Tamil Nadu, you will see all the parties get together because whenever one of them comes to power, there has to be money to actually govern. So you'll see a lot more solidarity when it comes to the fiscal bargaining than we see in all the other kinds of bargaining over Hindi language or Ram Mandir or any other hot button issue.

So I do think they need to come up with some bargain. And I think the fiscal devolution is probably the best one. Another thing is to hard-code the fiscal devolution into the constitution, maybe have an eclipse clause that — each year, there is going to be greater devolution over a 20-year period, and simultaneously increase the proportional seats in parliament. Because, again, we don't want to leave the UPites and the Biharis without any revenues. This is the poorest group of people in the country. They do need some investment in health and education and physical infrastructure. So that would be the nature of the bargain, I think.

Q3：种姓普查的隐藏维度

Milan: There's a question here about, is there a way to use sub-state data of a more granular kind? I think it's a really interesting question. And certainly, given the kinds of satellite data, terrain mapping and stuff you have, that could be used as an input to ensuring that these maps are done in ways that are contiguous and don't do things like crossover rivers and places that are unnecessary and so forth.

One thing we didn't talk about, Shruti, but it gets to the politics a little bit — and I thought about this in thinking about the sub-state data — is, you could make a plausible argument that one reason the government did this now is because they want to avoid dealing with the caste census. You don't want to be in a world, perhaps, if you're the BJP, where you now have to consider reservations in parliament for OBCs and others if that cuts against your desire to keep Hindus united as one bloc. Obviously, there have been a number of views within the BJP on reservations. But quite a lot of segments of the party have been anti. And so this was one way perhaps to nip it in the bud, because now, assuming nothing happens and status quo prevails, that is going to be an issue, because that is the political context we're in, where there will be a melding together of caste beyond SC and ST and the next delimitation.

Shruti: Yes and no. Because in absolute numbers, the largest number of SCs and STs are in these states which are net receivers of revenue, and also in the states where the BJP is relatively more powerful right now. So in some sense, attaching delimitation to the caste census is the opposition's way of perhaps shooting itself in the foot, which I trust they do very well all the time. So maybe they haven't thought this through quite as well.

I'm not a fan of the caste census at all. This is a very different reason from delimitation. I think the goal was to annihilate caste, and caste census basically constitutionalizes caste. It's like — oh, we're going to have this political and caste identity we can never shake off as long as the republic looks the way that it looks. So I'm against it for many other reasons. But this would be the craziest reason in terms of combining the two. It would be crazy for the southern states to do that. And the northeastern states, let's not forget — that's where most of the scheduled tribes are. And they are huge net receivers of fiscal transfers. So both on the scheduled caste issue, who are the most numerous in the poorer states where fertility dropped later, and in the northeast, which are net receivers — you're in a little bit of trouble if you're the opposition states, opposition parties.

Q4：是不是把不同问题混为一谈？

Milan: Yeah, it's a good point. Somebody has a question about — are we making a mistake somehow by conflating things? Delimitation is about political representation. What does it really have to do with fiscal federalism? Pranay Kotasthane, who's somebody that you and I both read and have had on our various podcasts, tweeted about this after the failure of the bills and linking to a piece he'd done, I think in 2023 or so, where he basically said: the delimitation commission is not the finance commission. It's not the population commission of India. It has a job and it should do it. And then we have to figure out how to adjust. And I think there is — I have some sympathy to that argument.

Shruti: No, just to clarify — I don't think the delimitation should split the fiscal pie at all. That would be a terrible and ridiculous thing to do. I think the fiscal pie needs to be split based on what we have right now with the Finance Commission. And I think Parliament needs to actually discuss and vote on it. Except my simple point is, unless they devolve more, there is no bargain to move towards lesser malapportionment. Sorry, please continue.

Milan: No, no, I mean, I think one of the things that Louise and Andy and I have been working on in a separate piece — that's going to be part of a book that Yamini and I are editing on federalism — is thinking about delimitation both in a kind of theoretical sense as well as an empirical sense. And one of the things that Louise has argued is that — part of why we are where we are is because India is not what federalism scholars have called a kind of "coming together" style federation. It has operated for decades on the basis of an implicit federal bargain and that has never really been explicitly negotiated. And so the delimitation freeze, which obviously preserved representation for states that had, in fertility terms, grown at a slower rate, was a concession that helped to partially offset the Constitution's inbuilt majoritarian design. These were not constitutionally enumerated. They were norms that helped to stabilize this federal model.

And so I do think that's one reason why you and I in this conversation are saying we have to think of all of these things holistically to a certain extent — because, like it or not, there were some norms of an implicit federal bargain which are at risk of unraveling for a whole host of reasons. You have a single dominant party. You have massive growth divergence across states. India is one of those federal countries which has not seen convergence in economic outcomes — we've seen further divergence over time. And so I think that's also important to know.

Shruti: Yeah, no, I completely agree. Do we have time for one more?

Milan: I think we have four minutes.

Shruti: Oh, wow. OK, let's see if we can get one more question.

Q5：财政横向分配中加入 GDP 维度

Milan: I'm just quickly looking through. Lots of — one question here that I'm seeing on fiscal devolution, in terms of moving beyond poverty and population. Shruti, you didn't talk about this, but it was notable and people did note it that the most recent Finance Commission did include a criterion for GDP. So I think that was seen as a fillip towards dynamic states such that part of the formula now is working in their favor, quote unquote, as opposed to the other way.

Shruti: It is, but net, I don't know — I'll have to run the numbers, but I don't know how this plays out. Because given the population weightage and the poverty weightage, I still think this is heavily tilted towards the poorer states when it comes to horizontal devolution. So I agree with you that it's not as lopsided as it was in the past. There is some reward for being economically more dynamic. But I don't think it does enough to set it off.

Q6：南方邦真的需要害怕失去席位吗？

Milan: One thing, Shruti, that somebody asked, and maybe we can end on this, is — if states that have a lot of representation like UP and Bihar have not actually become prosperous by having more MPs, what is the link between representation and development? So to flip it, as this questioner does, do the southern states really have a lot to fear from losing seats?

Shruti: Yes, because the money bill will be voted in Lok Sabha and there's a huge incentive for every state parliamentarian to send more money to their state. That's how you win elections. And I think the BJP has shown that it's extraordinary at this. Of course, the fact that there was an Aadhaar plus UPI revolution and Jan Dhan account revolution in India really helped streamline transfers. So in the time when the delimitation freeze was brought in, most of the welfare entitlements and transfers leaked, and they weren't that large to begin with. Now, actually, the welfare entitlements and transfers reach the people they're intended to reach. They're a huge part of the voting politics in every state election and every union-level election. And if you don't have money to redistribute, you're in trouble. And if the southern and the richer states lose their voice in parliament, more of the money will go to the poorer states. I would not be surprised if that happens at all.

Milan: Yeah. Just one final thing on this. One thing which is true, whether we like it or not, is that members of parliament — and MLAs as well — are not first and foremost lawmakers. They are people who perceive of their jobs, and who are perceived by their constituents, as being intermediaries in the provision of public benefits and services. And so that is the de facto reality in India. And so I think having a constituency size of 2.7 million becomes somewhat unsustainable on all sides. I'm not saying this is what I aspire the link to be between MP and constituent. But there have been great studies from the 70s, 80s, 90s, which have gone around and surveyed MPs and MLAs. And what's interesting is they all say this is what we're there for. And their constituents also kind of say the same thing.

收尾

Shruti: I think this is a good time to end. I'm sure we'll have more of this conversation, because delimitation and apportionment has not been resolved in any way. In fact, we just kicked the hornet's nest with this special session of Parliament. But thank you so much, Milan, for doing this. This was a lot of fun for me. And thank you to everyone who joined the webinar.

Milan: Yeah, thanks, Shruti, for inviting me. And thanks to Mercatus, who put all of this together, did all the heavy lifting. And yeah, look forward to continuing the conversation.

Shruti: Also check out our initiative commemorating 30 years of India's market reforms at the1991project.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的悖论：南方诸邦其实在"赎买"自己的政治声音
最让人意外的观察是——这场争论表面上是"一人一票"的民主原则之争，骨子里却是一场**财政赎罪券交易**。北方贫困邦（UP、比哈尔）选择沉默，不是因为他们高尚地不去争取本可获得的席位，而是因为他们心知肚明：**席位增加的政治筹码，远不如继续维持"穷邦受捐"的财政管道来得实在**。换言之，南方邦今天承受的"代表性赤字"，本质上是他们用议会话语权换取的财政自主权的"价格"。这不是民主危机，而是一场未被言明的、跨越50年的隐形交易——**用席位换钱**。
这里隐藏着一个更深的悖论：印度宪法理论上崇尚"一人一票"，但实际上运行的是"一卢比一票"——你为国家财政贡献得越多，你在议会的相对话语权反而越小，因为发展让你的人口增速放缓。**这是世界上少有的"惩罚成功者"的代议制结构**。
## 跨领域连接：议会席位与"碳排放权交易"的隐秘同构
把这个问题从政治学拉到环境经济学，会出现一个惊人的同构。1971年的人口冻结，本质上和《京都议定书》的"基准年排放权"是同一种制度逻辑——**用一个历史快照锁定权利分配，让后发者承担调整成本**

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Great Irony of Censorship – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

审查者的"近视症"：一个反直觉的认知盲区

Ada Palmer 揭示了一个令人毛骨悚然的规律：每个时代的审查机器都在监视错误的目标。这不是偶然的失误，而是一种结构性的认知缺陷。启蒙时期的宗教裁判所放过了即将颠覆整个欧洲思想的伏尔泰和《百科全书》，却对詹森派关于三位一体的咬文嚼字如临大敌——他们甚至用焚烧詹森派著作来"代偿"那本他们其实舍不得烧的《百科全书》。这个细节本身就是一个绝妙的隐喻：权力机构会下意识地为自己想保护的东西寻找替罪羊。

一个让人意外的观察：威胁感与"语言相似度"成正比，而非"思想颠覆性"成正比

最反直觉的洞见在这里——审查者最害怕的不是离他们最远的思想，而是离他们最近的"方言变体"。教廷把卡尔文、茨温利、路德这些新教神学家用全大写字母标注（相当于今天的红色警报），却对马基雅维利、卢克莱修这种真正改变世界观的作者视若无睹。原因是：新教异端使用的是和天主教相同的"操作系统"，他们的争论可以在教廷的语言框架内被识别为威胁；而唯物主义、世俗政治学是另一套语言，审查者的免疫系统根本不认识它们是病原体。

这个模式有一个跨领域的孪生兄弟：生物学上的"分类群偏见"（taxonomic bias）。免疫系统对近亲变异的细菌反应最激烈（因为受体能识别），但对完全异源的入侵者常常毫无反应——这正是为什么跨物种传播的病毒（如冠状病毒）总能突袭人类。审查制度就是社会的免疫系统，它有同样的盲区结构。

跨领域连接：从西班牙王子的婚事到今天的"AI 安全焦点"

Palmer 在结尾抛出的那句话——"人们说'我们发现了一片新大陆'，他们说'我们不在乎，我们想知道谁要娶查尔斯'"——这不是历史趣闻，这是关于注意力经济的一条永恒定律。每个时代都有它的"西班牙王子婚事"：占据一切官方议程、却终将被历史判定为无关紧要的话题。

把这个框架投射到当下，会有一个让人不寒而栗的对应：今天关于 AI 的公共讨论，多大比例是在烧"詹森派的三位一体论文"？ 监管机构、媒体、学界争论的是"聊天机器人会不会说脏话""模型会不会有偏见"——这些是与现行话语体系同构、因此可识别的"近亲异端"。而真正在重塑认知结构的东西（人类与异质智能共同思考的新范式、知识生产的去中心化、可能的认知主权丧失）——它们使用的是新语言，所以它们和当年的卢克莱修一样，被默认为"博学者读得懂、愚者读不懂、所以无害"。

更深一层的系统性真相：Palmer 自己作为研究审查史的学者，她书的 next 本主题选择本身就是一种元层面的反讽——只有当一个时代的"真正异端"已经成为历史时，下一个时代才有能力识别它。这意味着关于"什么才是真正重要的"，我们永远在事后才知道答案。这不是悲观——这是邀请你在阅读所有官方"重要议题清单"时，习惯性地反过来问一句：他们今天在烧什么书，是为了不烧那本他们其实舍不得烧的《百科全书》？

📝 转录文本

On Studying the History of Censorship

When I study the history of censorship, which I work a lot on — my next non-fiction book is going to be a book on the history of censorship. And whatever they're looking at, they're always wrong from our perspective about what they should be worried about censoring, right? If we had a time machine and our goal was to like go give them advice…

The French Enlightenment Case: Jansenism vs. Voltaire

So here we are in the French Enlightenment. Voltaire and Rousseau and the Marquis de Sade and La Mettrie's articulations of materialist atheism are flying around Europe. And what is the Inquisition worried about? It's worried about Jansenist treatises about the nature of the Trinity.

And Jansenism is sort of like a Calvinist version of Catholicism, right? Do you want to have an incredibly terrifying authoritarian God who hates you and tells you that your soul is a worthless spider that deserves to be hurled into fire, but also have to obey the arbitrary Pope in Rome? Then Jansenism is for you. Has all the grimness of Calvinism and all of the authoritarian centrality of the Roman Catholics. And this was a heresy that was abroad in the Enlightenment. [laughter]

And they are so much more worried about Jansenism than they are about Voltaire. That very chapter in Mathis's book I mentioned where they're raiding the clandestine bookshop — and they're like: Voltaire, fine. The Encyclopédie, which is going to revolutionize all thought in Europe, fine. Letters of Diderot, you know, Rousseau, fine, fine. Jansenist treatises about the nature of the Trinity? Throw the book at these guys. This is the worst thing.

The Encyclopédie and the Substitute Burning

They really are obsessed with this incredibly petty minor heresy — to the degree that when the Encyclopédie is banned by Rome… France likes the Encyclopédie, right? This is Diderot and d'Holbach's big project of universal education: to print an encyclopedia that will collect all world knowledge. They articulated: should a new dark age come upon humankind and even one copy of the encyclopedia survive, it will be sufficient to reconstruct all human progress. Right? That's the goal of this thing.

And it's advancing incredibly radical ideas about biology, about statecraft, about reforming the law to be rational instead of traditional, all sorts of stuff.

When that is banned by Rome, Paris is commanded… you know, Paris loves this book, the king likes this book, the queen likes this book — she's on record saying it was so cool being able to look up the technology that we used to make her silk pantyhose, right? She just loves it. Everybody loves it. France allows it to circulate despite its controversial content, but Rome says, "No, you must ban this book."

And so they agree they're going to have the ceremonial burning — and they march the Encyclopédies about the nature of the Trinity and burn those instead, because they don't want to burn the Encyclopédie. They love it. They want to burn this other thing.

The Pattern Repeats: Machiavelli and Lucretius

And this is always true. If we had a time machine for the Inquisition in the 1540s, we would say like, "Guys, Machiavelli — he's really important. He's really revolutionary. You've got to be looking at this." Or we would say Lucretius's De Rerum Natura, which I did my dissertation on — and many people are familiar with Greenblatt's book The Swerve, which credits a lot of change to the materialist science that this poem articulates. There's a much more complex story, which is told in my book, which refers to Greenblatt's, and if anyone enjoyed The Swerve you would really enjoy the more detailed zoom-in that Inventing the Renaissance has.

But, you know, we would say: "Guys, you should censor this." We literally have letters of inquisitors writing to each other saying: we don't need to bother censoring Lucretius. Only learned people can read it, and they know perfectly well that the false stuff is false, so it'll just circulate and it's fine. What we need to worry about censoring is all of these fine-tuned minutiae of Protestantism.

The 1545 Index: Who Got the All-Caps Treatment

So like, the 1545 edition of the Index of Banned Books says in its introduction: we shall put the names of arch-heretics in all caps. And when I first read that, I was like, "Oh, I want to see all my favorite arch-heretics be in all caps." And I eagerly flipped to M — and Machiavelli is not in all caps. He was not important enough from their position.

The all-caps authors are all minor Protestant theologians. They're all people who are like Calvin and Zwingli and Luther and Melanchthon. They're all doing stuff that we would say does not matter.

The Larger Lesson: Eras Are Always Wrong About What Matters

But an era is always wrong about what ideas and what circulation and what changes are the really big ones, and are always much, much more worried about: Oh my God, the Prince of Spain — which princess is he going to marry? This is going to determine whether Spain is or isn't annexed by Germany. This is the most important thing that has ever happened in the entire history of time. And people are like, "We've discovered another continent." And they're like, "We don't care. We want to know who's going to marry Charles."

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 审查者的"近视症"：一个反直觉的认知盲区
Ada Palmer 揭示了一个令人毛骨悚然的规律：**每个时代的审查机器都在监视错误的目标**。这不是偶然的失误，而是一种结构性的认知缺陷。启蒙时期的宗教裁判所放过了即将颠覆整个欧洲思想的伏尔泰和《百科全书》，却对詹森派关于三位一体的咬文嚼字如临大敌——他们甚至用焚烧詹森派著作来"代偿"那本他们其实舍不得烧的《百科全书》。这个细节本身就是一个绝妙的隐喻：**权力机构会下意识地为自己想保护的东西寻找替罪羊**。
## 一个让人意外的观察：威胁感与"语言相似度"成正比，而非"思想颠覆性"成正比
最反直觉的洞见在这里——**审查者最害怕的不是离他们最远的思想，而是离他们最近的"方言变体"**。教廷把卡尔文、茨温利、路德这些新教神学家用全大写字母标注（相当于今天的红色警报），却对马基雅维利、卢克莱修这种真正改变世界观的作者视若无睹。原因是：**新教异端使用的是和天主教相同的"操作系统"**，他们的争论可以在教廷的语言框架内被识别为威胁；而唯物主义、世俗政治学是另一套语言，**审查者的免疫系统根本不认识它们是病原体**。
这个模式有一个跨

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Plan to Win The Prem, The Ilona Maher Effect & Building a Modern Rugby Club: Bristol Bears CEO
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Bristol Bears 的"低起点"恰恰是它最稀缺的战略资产

大多数人会把 2017 年降级、5000 人空荡荡坐在 27000 座球场里看作劣势——但 Tom 在访谈里有一句话泄露了天机："The bar was very low...it was quite liberating."

这是一种"负资产期权"的现象：当一个组织的历史包袱被降级、财务危机、流浪式主场清空之后，重塑品牌的政治成本几乎为零。Harlequins、Northampton 这类有荣光传统的俱乐部反而背负"老球迷情感否决权"，每一次大胆动作都要付出叙事代价。Bristol 因为"什么都没有"，反而拿到了一张白纸期权——这恰恰是 Quins 即便预算更高、品牌更老，也很难复制 Bears 增长曲线的隐藏原因。

跨领域连接：这与硅谷里"被收购重启的失败创业公司"模式高度相似——失败清空了组织记忆，让创始人能用 Day 0 心态做 Day 1000 的决策。Tom 自己说"we are going to operate a startup"不是修辞，而是组织心理学层面的真实现象。

二、Ilona Maher 不是"营销事件"，而是一次球员主权觉醒的传染源

表层故事：来了一个 TikTok 顶流，门票一票难求，11000 人到场，美国邮编开始下订单球衣。

但 Tom 不经意提到一个更深的现象——Ilona 走了之后，Sarah Bern 开始建立自己的 Instagram 和 TikTok 个人账号。这不是营销 ROI，这是文化感染。

橄榄球过去几十年信奉"team and humility"作为核心价值，结果是球员心理上把"突出个人品牌"等同于"违背队魂"。Ilona 的真正颠覆性贡献，不是带来观众，而是给更衣室里其他球员一个"许可证"——原来你可以又是顶级球员，又是个人品牌经营者，两者并不冲突。

一个让你意外的预测：未来 18 个月内，Bears 女队的商业天花板会比男队先被打破。原因是女队这个"文化突破口"已经打开，而男队还卡在 Tom 说的"需要一两个人砸碎天花板"的等待期。这意味着英超橄榄球俱乐部里，女队可能率先成为利润中心而非成本中心——这与女子足球完全相反的结构。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：Bristol Bears 真正在做的不是"经营橄榄球俱乐部"，而是在对赌一个城市的身份认同基础设施

注意 Tom 反复用的两个词：custodian（受托人） 和 legacy（遗产）。这不是 CEO 的语言，是博物馆馆长或市长的语言。

再看他对法国波尔多模式的羡慕："if you want to do business in Bordeaux, you've got to do business with the rugby club"——他要的不是更多门票收入，他要的是让 Bristol Bears 成为这座城市商业生态的强制路由节点。

这就解释了那个看上去违反财务直觉的策略：损失 560 万英镑，他还坚持"我不是降本派，我是增收派"。因为他真正在建的不是一个橄榄球俱乐部的 P&L，而是一种"城市操作系统的协议层"——一旦让 Bristol Bears 嵌入到本地企业网络、社区情感、青少年身份认同中，未来的现金流是地租级的、近乎复利式的。

跨领域连接：这和早期亚马逊的策略哲学惊人相似——Bezos 反复亏损建仓储和 Prime，因为他押注的不是"零售利润率"，而是"成为美国消费的默认基础设施"。Tom 押注的是 Bristol 这座城市的"默认情感基础设施"。

啊哈时刻：所以"是否拿到奖杯"在他的真实战略里其实不是终点，而是激活那个基础设施的催化剂——奖杯的作用不是 PR，是让 60 万 Bristol 人在心理上正式"完成入网"。这也是为什么他引用 Pat Lamb 那句话：奖杯是泡沫里的瞬间，遗产是人的感受。他不是在抒情，他在描述基础设施的本质。

四、彩蛋观察：Premier Rugby 的"取消升降级"决策，本质是一场风险定价的金融工程

公众讨论都卡在"竞技纯粹性 vs 资本介入"的二元对立。但真正发生的事是：升降级风险像一个永久挂在每家俱乐部头上的或有负债，让任何机构投资人无法对单个俱乐部做合理 DCF 估值。Worcester、London Irish、Wasps 三连暴雷之后，这个或有负债被市场计入了天文数字的折现率。

Simon Massie-Taylor 团队做的事，从金融工程角度看就是——把这个或有负债从俱乐部资产负债表里剥离出来，转移到联盟层面的"准入标准 + 财务监管"框架里。Red Bull 接手 Newcastle、James Dyson 投 Bath、Exeter 寻求外部资本——这些不是巧合，是同一个数学问题的解被找到了之后的资本反应链。

这给所有还在挣扎的非足球职业体育联盟一个启示：你卖的从来不是赛事，你卖的是风险定价合理的资产类别。

📝 转录文本

Bristol Bears CEO Tom Tainton 访谈 · 分段整理

开场：愿景与情感

"The next step on our journey is to win silverware. When you lift a trophy, it's a special moment in time, but it's a moment in time that is enjoyed by those that live in that bubble. The true legacy comes from how you make people feel. We want to leave something where Bristolians walk around with their chest out and they feel good about their team and their city and who they are. I want our team to obsess over that fan journey from when they arrive at the stadium to their experience through the game. How do we make them feel?"

"The players need to better understand the power of their brand. There is a benefit to having values that sit around team and humility and that's important to rugby, but equally we can't sit here and talk about rugby values and watch the league die."

"When Ilona arrived, frankly we underestimated just how impactful she is. Bears Women, we average about a thousand people. When Ilona came, the demand for tickets was so high we had to move a fixture to Ashton Gate. I think we had 11,000 for her debut."

"The key question that we ask every time is: is it moving the dial? Is it different enough to get people to sit up and take notice? Because if it's not, and if it's the same thing that everyone else is doing, what's the point?"

一、欢迎与背景

主持人：Tom, welcome to The Business of Sport. Thanks so much for joining us.

Tom：Thank you so much for having me. It's really great to have you in.

主持人：We've done a few shows with clubs from Prem. We did Laurie at Harlequins and did Julia at Northampton. But your journey at Bristol as CEO at Bristol has been, well, I'd say pretty unique. You've been there for a very long time.

Tom：Yeah, look, I'd say I've been fortunate enough to grow up with the business. And I see that as a real privilege. The perspective that you have from being part of a business in the context of Bristol Bears that has really accelerated over the last 10 years — that perspective of being able to say, well, I know what good looks like, but equally I know what not so good looks like, and I understand the journey we're on and where we've come from. It's given me great context to make decisions for the betterment of the club moving forward.

We've got a really compelling plan around where we're going and what we're trying to achieve, both on and off the field. But I don't think any of that would have been possible without the knowledge and understanding of where this club has come from. Because let's be clear: prior to 2017, when we rebranded, there wasn't a huge amount of success that Bristol Rugby could hang their hat on. We've been around since 1888, but there's been a sparsity of silverware. We've had a number of financial difficulties. We've bounced around different venues and stadia. And really, until we moved to Ashton Gate and had that really consistent footprint, until we've rebranded and come under the Bristol Sport banner, we've not really seen the fruits of the potential that a club of that size, in a city like Bristol, has had. So it's been an exciting journey so far.

二、品牌重塑：从"低起点"到"创业心态"

主持人：How is it when you rebrand? That's something that is incredibly difficult, right, for entities, whether successful or not, because the fans, the people that have had that association with the club previously, are often very protective over what's gone before.

Tom：Yeah, absolutely right. And in a way, it was quite liberating because the bar was very low. We were rebranding at a time when we'd just been relegated from the Prem. I think our average attendance was around 5,000.

I remember sitting in a room with the guys, and our owner articulated it as: the club is in a time warp, and we're going to keep doing the same thing — definition of insanity. And we thought about how can we take some risks and be bold and change the narrative and change the direction of this rugby club? What it needs is a jolt. This wasn't a kind of "here is a progressed transformation plan." It was: we are going to rip the band-aid off, and we are going to do it, and we are going to go hell for leather.

And I think "liberating" is the right word, because it was just exciting. It was like — we are going to operate a startup. We're going to talk to new audiences. We're going to bring people along on the journey with us. And our overriding message was: we're bringing something different to the table. You have not seen what we're going to do in the rugby space. Naturally that's evolved over the last 10 years, and I'd say we've added layers of sophistication to that plan, but in that first iteration in the summer of 2018, it was all about: Is it different? Is it going to move the dial? Is it going to make people sit up and take notice and almost wince and be like, "oh, that's pretty close to the bone"? That's what we wanted, because we wanted that ripple effect.

三、八年回顾：成绩与未竟之业

主持人：If you're looking at it eight years on, what would you say back then that you'd wanted to do that you've achieved now has gone well? And what would you say still needs some work?

Tom：Our overarching vision is to inspire the community through rugby success. What that means is leaving a legacy in our city. That's silverware, that's the work we do in the community, that's the way we inspire young women, young men to grow the game and play the sport.

Over the last eight years, we can point to some really clear data metrics: - Attendances have got bigger year on year. - A higher demographic of female supporters. - A higher demographic of under-12 and under-19 supporters.

All of the data is telling us that we are growing our audience in the right way. We've had success to a point on the field where we've gone from a team that was in the mid-table in the championship to a side that is consistently in and around the top four. We've reached three semifinals. We've won the European Cup in COVID.

But the next step on our journey to really establish that credibility is to win silverware. Our director of rugby, Pat Lamb — a really special and impressive guy — articulates it brilliantly: when you lift a trophy or you win a game, it's a special moment in time, but it's a moment in time that is enjoyed by those that live in that bubble of sport. The true legacy comes from how you make people feel — the hospital visits, the time in the community, what Bears are giving back, the pride that we give people in the city of Bristol, and how they feel when their team on a weekend achieves success.

That's something that gets me out of bed every morning. That's the driver of our team. We want to leave something where Bristolians walk around with their chest out and they feel good about their team and their city and who they are.

四、奖杯的力量：Crystal Palace 类比

主持人：It's so interesting. We had Chris Richards, Crystal Palace centre-back, on the show recently, and he was talking about Crystal Palace in a similar way. Never won a trophy before the FA Cup. He explained what winning that trophy did. They'd been competitive in the Premier League — they'd achieved everything that, sorry Palace fans, a mid-ranking club would ever want to do. But the silverware: looking up in the stands, the grown men crying, families hugging, the taxi that he'll now forever get into and the Palace fan who's the driver turning around going, "you won't believe what you've done for our community." Literally, they'd had 100 years of just never having that, and now they have something that they hold onto.

Taking it from a mid-champ team in rugby — we're not talking about the shifts that go from the championship in football to the Premier League. Taking it from the champ in rugby to now a consistent top-four, top-five team in the Prem, personally, that is a significant achievement. It's just: what next.

Tom：Exactly. You need to be impatient in sport — you're only as good as what's in front of you. If you stand still, you go backwards. I remember 5,000 people coming and bouncing around Ashton Gate in a 27,000-seater arena on the opening day of the 17–18 season. Now we're averaging 20,000. This year we'll get our new record average attendance.

But I'm saying to our team: that means there's 6,000 empty seats. That's a million quid of revenue left on the table every year. So how do we now get to sell out? How do we keep pushing? We have a number of projects ongoing looking at fan engagement, that customer journey. I want our team to obsess over that fan journey from when they arrive at the stadium to their experience through the game. How do we make them feel? How do we talk to them? Because that's the next iteration of sport and event entertainment.

When we sit down and ideate and look at the season and campaign planning, the key question we ask every time is: is it moving the dial? Is it different enough to get people to sit up and take notice? Because if it's not, and if it's the same thing everyone else is doing, what's the point?

That attitude has been shared by the other clubs. There's a far more collective buy-in to how we all grow the league together. There are really interesting, intelligent people now running Prem clubs, and we all want the same thing: a thriving league that in turn helps the clubs grow.

五、Prem Rugby 新结构：可投资性与公开性

主持人：Perfect chance we've got to dive into it. I'll put my flag in the sand: I love the move for Prem Rugby. My challenge — and I've been told off before by Simon — is the phrasing of "franchise." But there's also the part of people understanding what's happening. I don't even want to say "closing a league," because it's not. There are opportunities for teams to come in. What we're really talking about is a structure to allow clubs to rediscover some of the value they need to make them investable sporting products.

I actually think it raises the opportunity to invest in a champ club, even though people say, "yeah, but now they can't get up." It's like — no, they can in this model. There's just a system that they have to adhere to and a quality they have to meet. Which is actually what happened to get into the Football League in the 40s and 50s: you had to have a stadium of a certain size and a foundation. That's super important if you're going to create a system at the top which has teams that can all compete together. So whether anyone cares or not, I think it's great. I'm really excited from the investment standpoint, from the sporting standpoint. How much of this comes into your ability to do this in this current system?

Tom：That's a great question. We've operated hand in glove with Prem Rugby on this. It's not a Prem Rugby strategy — it's all the clubs' shared strategy.

So the removal of promotion-relegation: the question, quite rightly, that people will ask is — well, if you remove that, then you get a ton of dead rubbers, and you kill that inherent value of competitive nature in the sport. But the data tells us that doesn't happen. The data tells us there was only one dead rubber in all of the 24-25 season. As we're talking now, going into the run-in of the Prem season, there are still six teams that can win the competition. There's still plenty to play for. The product of Prem Rugby is excellent. Every team within reason can beat each other on their day.

What Simon and his ExCo have done really well is create an investable structure. We're not talking about the Worcester, London Irish and Wasps scenario, which we would have been doing three, four years ago. We're talking about the fact that: - It's well documented that Exeter are looking to onboard third-party investment. - Bath have just taken on investment from James Dyson. - Red Bull have taken on Newcastle.

There is a swirl of attention now around rugby because it is a product that has appropriate governance and the right parameters for financial sustainability. We've put in really positive steps around financial monitoring, the minimum salary floor, minimum operating standards. If I'm an aspirational champ club, the door is not shut. Opportunities will present themselves when I am in a position that I am commercially and competitively able to compete.

主持人：Completely. If you look at the last few years, we've always talked about open systems, but the last team relegated was Saracens, the last team promoted was Saracens — quite a few years ago in 2021. Fine, the system may have appeared open, but really there were situations every season meaning teams weren't really coming up and going down. Ealing — however many times they've won the champ — they don't have the infrastructure to meet the requirements of the Prem.

So if I'm now going, "do you know what, maybe the time to invest in Ealing — because if I can get a piece of stadia in place and we can keep performance on the pitch really high, suddenly Ealing now has the chance to get up to the Prem." We've got a really good bid. Whereas beforehand, being in the Prem wouldn't have been an attractive proposition for me as an investor, because I still see significant losses across a lot of the clubs. To me, it just feels like that changes it. It's not closing it — it's a different way of presenting opportunity.

Tom：It's sharpening the focus. Rugby as a sport has been guilty of standing on its soapbox and saying, "here are all the things we should do," and then we all pat ourselves on the back and carry on doing the same thing. Now is the time for tangible action. We've gone from saying "we need to do X, Y, Z" to "we are doing X, Y, Z." The decisions being made for the commercial long-term viability of Prem Rugby are really positive. They support the Team England piece. They create better pathways for young English players to get minutes. There is a performance element at international level too.

If I'm an investor looking at the competition and its feasibility, I see a product that is highly competitive. We're now leaning into that competition, gladiatorial, collision sport. There's a great TV deal. We've got a really robust agreement with the RFU. Everything is in place now for the sport at domestic level to really kick on.

六、与英超足球的类比：Pre-92 启示

Tom：It's not without its challenges. We are still very much in the infancy of our professional life, if you like. Everyone looks at the EPL — the English Premier League — as this bastion of what good looks like in sport: an unbelievable TV deal and a great thriving Championship below it. But I'm only just old enough to remember what happened pre-92 in the BSkyB deal. I remember what First Division football looked like in the late 80s — the challenges with hooliganism, the quality of games, infrastructure, pitches. It took some bold decisions to put in place that Premier League. And look where it's gone.

I see Prem Rugby in an equally exciting place now. The foundations are in place. You have 10 teams that all are aligned in wanting the same thing — that's not always been the case. We've always struggled that there are competing factions: what Bristol Bears want is different to what Harlequins want, and so on. But we're now in a place where everyone is in the same room, signed up to the same strategic plan. The next three to five years will generate some real interest in the product.

七、媒体版权：Paywall vs Free-to-Air

主持人：I don't want you to throw your paymasters under the bus. You said "great media deal." Do you think it is a great media deal?

Tom：I think it's a great media deal in the context of where we are now. There's always going to be a debate as to the benefits of a paywall media deal versus free-to-air. But in terms of what the clubs get from central distributions — and the TNT offering and how TNT are growing the game, because I think the production is excellent — it's really positive for the clubs.

主持人：It's interesting. The Premier League media deal has been such a well-documented tool for them to use in a positive way and build the financial opportunity. But it's also meant that for some clubs, particularly close to the bottom, a huge reliance on central media revenue — which they don't have an influence over. Some Premier League teams have maybe 75% of their revenue as Premier League TV money. Great when you're in the Premier League and thriving, but if you're not, or if something happens to that media money, you're very exposed — your business is really £25 million, not £125 million.

While you would, I'm sure, like the media to be a bigger piece of the pie, it's probably also given you an opportunity to really develop business lines beyond media that you actually control as an individual club.

八、本地收入引擎：合作伙伴与商业化

Tom：Absolutely. Our local revenue drivers — partnerships, hospitality, ticketing — that's where we can capitalize and see genuine yield straight away. Partnerships in particular is an area we've really focused our energy on over the last 12 to 18 months. We'll see a 40 to 45% increase in revenue in that space.

主持人：In the last year?

Tom：In the last year.

主持人：Really?

Tom：Yeah. That comes from having a really clear story we've gone to market with, and from us being able to say that Bristol Bears is open for business — that we are a club that has the infantry and the assets to drive value for our partners.

主持人：Is that you upselling existing inventory or creating new opportunities?

Tom：It's both. There are exclusive categories and areas of our sponsorship inventory we obviously can't touch because of the league central deal. I challenge our team all the time: what about events? What about networking? What about client acquisition? What about employee engagement? How do we squeeze every last drop out of our business? How do we really sweat the asset?

Because it's well documented that we are a loss-making business. We rely on Stephen Lansdown and the Lansdown family as our benefactors. But we're also sitting on an incredible opportunity, because Bristol is a really diverse, affluent, tech-savvy, major hub outside of London. A great place to live and work, somewhere we're fiercely proud of. I want people who live and do business in Bristol to feel like Bristol Bears is somewhere aligned to their own business.

I use the analogy of when you go to games in France: the city or town in which you are playing grinds to a halt on match day. The city lives and breathes their club. If you want to do business in Bordeaux, you've got to do business with the rugby club. I love that attitude — everyone buys into the bigger piece.

I am a custodian of Bristol Bears. My job and my legacy is to ensure the club has the sustainable footing to be successful for generations to come. The only way to do that is to create the right stories and narratives now and bring people on the journey. It's very easy to get bogged down in corporate buzzwords and strategy, but when you boil it down, it's about people, relationships, and authenticity. I believe in who Bristol Bears are and what we are selling to the market. My attitude is almost: we're going to get there whether you're with us or not, but if you're with us, we'll be able to supercharge that.

Partnerships gives us the ability to focus our energy on fan engagement and on bringing a really compelling product on match day. The more revenue we generate, the greater ability we have to build a squad, which in turn creates success.

九、可持续性与"增收派 vs 降本派"

主持人：You mentioned two things that are really interesting. One is sustainability; two is ownership structure.

Sustainability is a word thrown around in sport all the time. In the Premier League this season, sustainability has meant you lose £105 million over three years. That's not sustainable. So it's funny how sport has redefined the meaning. But we all know what it really means: people moving in the right direction to reduce losses.

You lost, in the last accounts, £5.6 million against revenue of let's say £12 million. So you're moving in the right direction. But no doubt you want that £5.6m to come down. How much of a prohibitor is it to what you can do? How much focus is put on it versus, "we know we've got to spend a little bit to get better"?

Tom：It's the paradox of professional sport. You want to invest and have the very best product on the pitch. To do that, you increase your losses over here. You're constantly looking at how you can squeeze margin, manage costs, and at the same time grow revenue. The two are sometimes jarring objectives.

Is there room to reduce that £5.5 million home loss? Absolutely. That's a key part of my role. But I don't see that as regressive — let's sit down and work out how we manage headcount, or really go into granular detail on our spend on academy and first team. I want to reduce the losses in a positive way: by growing revenue.

主持人：You strike me as a make-more-revenue guy, not a cut-cost guy.

Tom：100%. The whole point of sport — and the privilege I have of leading a professional sports club — is that it's meant to be fun. You should wake up every morning with a bounce in your step, thinking: right, what can I do today that's going to make a difference?

I should point out that we operate in a multi-sport business. We have a shared ownership model with ourselves and Bristol City Football Club, Bristol Flyers Basketball, and Ashton Gate Stadium. So how we account for that multi-sport business is different to some of our peers in the Prem. It's not a like-for-like comparison in terms of our losses versus a Sale or a Northampton.

The advantage of that shared buying power, working with the other clubs — best practice, shared IP — I can draw a ton of inspiration from football and the excellent governance that comes from, in Bristol City's case, being in the EFL. What inspiration can we take from that and draw down into rugby? And by the same token, what can the football club look at on the rugby side in terms of our culture and the way we engage our community?

主持人：Is it Charlie Boss?

Tom：Charlie Boss, yeah. He's now chief executive. Charlie's been in for a little over a month and he's been a breath of fresh air — come in with new ideas. We've spent a lot of time together, been out for a few beers, shared ideas. I really like what he's trying to build. He has a very different ecosystem to mine, but we have a shared ticketing platform, a shared marketing service. There's a ton of places where we are singing off the same hymn sheet.

十、Lansdown 家族：所有者与社区赞助人

主持人：Tell me about the Lansdowns then — the owners. They've done a huge amount for Bristol Sport, and you mentioned that multi-club ownership group. Quite a unique setup. Just really interested to know how that all works for you.

Tom：Steve, Maggie and John — all of whom have a vested interest in Bears. They come to games. They're present, they're visible, educated and articulate on where our challenges and opportunities are.

As someone who grew up in Bristol — let's park the sports side for a moment — I can't speak highly enough of what the Lansdowns have done for the city, in terms of community engagement, infrastructure. We've got our high-performance centre. Bristol City have theirs. We've got Ashton Gate Stadium, and we're in the process of building the Ashton Gate Sporting Quarter — multi-use conference facilities, events, a basketball arena. The impact the Lansdowns have had on the community — they deeply care about it. That's the most important thing for me: it's authentic. They deeply care about Bristol. They want the city to have clubs it can be proud of. They understand the role sport can play as a vehicle for good in the community.

From a purely rugby perspective, they're engaging and understanding — but they're challenging, and I like that. Challenging in the sense of: how are we being different? We don't want boring. We don't want status quo. We want to constantly be pushing the boundaries.

主持人：They're very happy for you to make mistakes, because it shows you're doing something.

Tom：Yeah, and believe me, I've made a ton of mistakes. That's a really important philosophy in sport, because otherwise you fall into the mire of doing the same things over and over again. Look — I'm relatively young to be in this role, and being in this position has come from the faith that Steve has shown to give me that opportunity. It's not charity. It's sink or swim. If you're not good enough to do the job, we'll find someone that can do the job. From a personal level and from that mentorship piece, the opportunities I've been provided on my own journey have been immeasurable. I feel duty-bound — I want an obligation — to pay the Lansdowns back. The best way I can do that is to deliver a team that supports their objectives.

十一、球员品牌：从 LeBron 到 Ilona Maher

主持人：I want to look at a huge needle-mover in sport, which has been the optimization of talent — to help drive brand and club. We're in such a different environment to where sport was 30 years ago. Individual athletes can be more popular, more followed, than a lot of the teams they play for. Some people have issue with that; some embrace it. Rugby's always had an interesting challenge with the personalities it can promote — you're putting your head above the parapet in dressing rooms. I've had these conversations with players: "no, no, because it's always the team." That's starting to change a little bit.

How do you view the opportunity presented by being able to leverage those amazing players you have, versus the challenges rugby has as a brand and image learning to adjust to this type of environment?

Tom：The best example of how a star profile can drive a sport is LeBron James. People support the Lakers because of LeBron, and when he went back to Cleveland, and so on. Lionel Messi to Miami. Having superstars — out-of-this-world superstars — that can bring an audience with them.

Rugby isn't there yet. That's not to say it can't get there. We had our first taste of it last year when Ilona Maher came in on the women's side. She came to Bristol on a short-term deal. If there is an ability for us to drive value and recognition through that player's brand, then absolutely that should be the cherry on the cake.
