这是陪审团审判还是法官审判？

但回到话题，Sax，那是什么——我们在谈论奥克兰的陪审团，我相信。

不，但这是一个法官审判。这很重要。这是一个法官审判，陪审团是咨询能力—— 但最终那个法官，她会做出最终决定—— 她会做赔偿。

那么 Sachs，这是一个我们有湾区陪审团法官，我们有 Elon——他被认为是有点右翼，那个地区的人不都同意他的政治。然后你有这个 Sam Altman 的纽约客故事，人们发现这么多不同的人觉得他们被他骗了。把这两件事放在一起，要预测这会变成什么是不可能的。Sachs，你的想法？

Sachs 拒绝就案件本身发表意见

嗯，是的，我不认为这是关于政治的。我的意思是，我猜你可以争辩说 Elon 寻求的——保护慈善机构——如果有什么的话，是一个左派编码的原则，尽管我不真的认为它是左还是右。

看，我不想在这场审判中站队。我和其他人一样在看。我上次对 Elon 的诉讼发表意见时，被传讯了 6 个小时。还记得吗？因为他们只是认为我莫名其妙地知道一些事情。

对。 我从来没有和 Elon 谈过这个案子。我对此一无所知。 是的。 我会看看会发生什么，就像其他人一样。

现在，我刚才读到一些报道，有一件事我会说——显然公司在某个时候确实给了 Elon 公司的股份，但他认为这有点恶心。你记得这个吗？

是的。因为在某个时候，我在我们的节目上说，当这个争议开始发生但在它成为法庭案件之前，我说，看，如果 OpenAI 在某个时候决定他们的结构错了，他们应该和 Elon 一起做修正。他应该是资本表上的股东。我不知道的是，显然他们确实尝试做这样的事情，但 Elon 拒绝了，因为他确实希望该实体保持为慈善实体。换句话说—— 根据报告，他对此有一个原则性的看法，他说不，我们正在试图拯救人类，然后你把王国的钥匙交给微软，这一切都已经显现出来。 我也没有和 Elon 谈过任何这些，但我猜测，就像这些事情中的大多数，会有某种和解或这里的事情，但也许他把它推到极致。谁知道呢。

法官 Rogers 与 Epic Games 案

法官 Rogers，做这个案子，61 岁的奥巴马任命者，政治起到了作用。Sachs，他们必须告诉陪审团，无论你对这些人在政治上的感觉如何，无论如何，请把它放在一边。但值得注意的是，她监督了 Epic Games 诉 Apple 案件，关于 App Store 排他性，对 Apple 有利地裁决，有一些注意事项——他们没有垄断等等。

所以这将是一个非常有趣的案子。我认为对 OpenAI 来说最坏的情况是他们必须以某种方式解开这个，这将延迟 IPO。这将给股东带来混乱。我猜最好的是某种和解。如果 Elon 投入了第一个 4000 或 5000 万美元，他在稀释后应该得到公司的 10%、20%、30%。

好的，让我们继续讨论案件。还有更多要讨论的，祝大家在他们的诉讼和那些在 Polymarket 上下注的人好运。

All-In Summit 广告

All-In Summit 售罄很快。我们的第五届，洛杉矶，9 月 13 日到 15 日。去 allin.com/events，演讲者将是顶级的。显然 Freeberg 把这作为他主要的创意输出。我从 Chamath 听说今天他将做一个百老汇音乐剧——魔术师踢踏。我有踢踏舞情况。

他真的是在变成一个完整的演艺人员。这将是 vaudeville Sachs 总结。他只是要把它带到一个全新的水平。音乐节目—— 像 Nathan Lane。 我认为如果你正在编码，这将是他的盛大同性恋峰会。是的，它可能是一个盛大的同性恋峰会。 这可能是我们在 LA 的最后一年，伙计们。 为什么？ 不可能。 可能。哦，每个人都想去拉斯维加斯，显然。那些骨头，宝贝。你能想象在午餐时离开峰会去玩骰子吗？Chimoth，我们得到一个新鲜的。是的，我可以。是的，我可以想象。 我有一些砖头。哦，我这里有一些砖头。我们走。Yum yum。 那样 Sachs 就可以来了。 是的，Sachs 就像，我永远不会踏入加利福尼亚，但我会。你知道，我们正在做几个现场活动。你来吗？ Liquidity 还是别的什么？ Liquidity。然后在九月有 All-In Summit。 是的，我也会做那些。

话题三：大型科技公司财报与超大规模资本支出

好的。大型科技公司在周四爆出了财报。Google、Microsoft、Amazon 和 Meta 都报告了。我不知道为什么他们在同一个晚上这样做，伙计们，但他们这样做了。表现非常出色。它很棒。

然而，资本支出公告才是真正的故事。让我把这个排好并显示图表。2026 年来自四家公司的 7250 亿美元资本支出指引——Amazon、Microsoft、Google 和 Meta。Amazon 领先 2000 亿，Microsoft 和 Google 各 1900 亿，Meta 1450 亿。

加上 Grok、加上 OpenAI 和其他一些参与者到这些计划中。我们还没听到新的 Apple CEO 的消息，但他将接任。他将有一些计划。我们将看到大规模——未来一年内 1 万亿美元的建设。我不知道这是否可能，但这一切都是由 AI 和云计算驱动的。

云收入数字

  • Google Cloud：包括 Google Suite，同比增长 63%，季度收入 200 亿美元。让那个数字深入人心。
  • Microsoft Cloud：包括 Azure、Windows Server、SQL Server——他们捆绑了一些东西使数字上升。增长 30%，收入 347 亿美元。
  • Amazon Web Services：原始云。增长 28%，收入 376 亿美元。这是一个相对纯粹的玩家。只算 Amazon 的网络服务。

显然，所有这些都在转向 NeoClouds。所有这些都在为 AI 工作和 token 服务。他们有大量的客户群，从最小的初创公司到最大的前沿模型，客户都得不到足够的算力，这一切都流向了底线。

但这正在大规模缩减自由现金流，Chamath。这些是自由现金流机器，是人类历史上最大的印钞机。但他们正在放弃自由现金流、股票回购和股息，并专注于这三者来投资基础设施。Amazon 的自由现金流下降 97%。Google、Microsoft 和 Meta 分别下降 12%、12% 和 8%。

Chamath：资本市场的结构性转变

你对这种自由现金流、自由现金流泛滥的结束以及我们看到的资本支出大规模投资的看法？

我认为我们正在看到资本市场的一个非常重要的结构性转变。我认为过去 20 或 30 年——好吧 20 年——是 Mag 7 跑得很远。这些大公司变得越来越大，吸收了所有这些投资资金，最大的原因是它有这些非常资产轻的商业模式——你只是建造更多的软件，它有所有这些杠杆，一切都行得通——也许除了 Amazon，因为他们需要仓库和交付的物理基础设施。但总的来说，这是一个非常资产轻的投资周期。

现在突然之间，钟摆向另一个方向猛烈摆动。有一件事我认为人们误解了——当它转回到这些资产重的基础设施投资时，超大规模公司正在签发我怀疑他们的身体能兑现的支票，但有一个世界他们不能。

我给你举个例子。你知道 Microsoft 说服三里岛的所有者重新打开他们的核电站吗？

是的。 你知道他们的购电协议是什么吗？是当时电力现货价格的 2 倍多。2 倍多。问题是，那不是他们整体能源需求的一个巨大百分比。

所以如果你把这个推演下去，你认为这五六家公司突然不仅仅是花费——Jason——每年 7000 亿的资本支出——他们正在花——但是从运营现金流来看，他们将花费当时电力现货价格的 2 倍，因为他们只想保证未来的需求。

所有这些现金会去哪里？它不会去股东那里，它不会留在资产负债表上。这些公司现在会被杠杆化。它们会变得高度复杂的金融工程。它们会有更多的债务。它们会有各种不同的工具和定期贷款和循环信贷和所有这些东西。所以，它们将在五年内看起来像这种庞大笨重的工业企业。我不确定那时候是否有一个好的估值案例。

所以我认为可能更简单——这就是我推特上说的——只是跟随美元，就像一年从超大规模公司流出 1 万亿美元一样，它去哪里？只跟随那些美元并购买那些公司，因为那些公司已经被低估了。

Cisco 与 2000 年泡沫的类比

这显然让我们都经历了一些事情。Nick，你能拉出我刚发给你的 Cisco 图表并把它最大化吗？我们在 1990 年代末和 2000 年看到了互联网基础设施的大规模建设。这导致很多激进的公司进行大规模支出，很多散户投资者拥抱这些股票，就像我们看到人们试图进入这些私人公司一样。Sachs，看 Cisco 2000 年的高峰。这是有史以来最非凡的图表。他们花了 25 年才回到那个高峰，他们有一个失去的二十年。我们最终有了大量的光纤被购买。我们几年前在节目上谈到了这个。但你看这个大规模基础设施时有事情可以建立。你认为这将再次成为 Cisco 系统、WorldCom 等吗？

Sachs：没有暗 GPU

不，我真的不这么认为。我们在 2000 年遇到的问题是暗光纤。你有所有这些基础设施被建设但没有被使用。今天没有暗 GPU，你知道，正如 Brad Gerstner 喜欢说的。

所以现在驱动资本支出的是对算力、对 token 的贪婪需求，需求现在正在拉动这种额外的基础设施投资。所以我认为这里发生的事情是，AI 的牛市论点在一个下午就被验证了。

我的意思是，再次，你有 Microsoft Azure、Google Cloud、Amazon AWS、Meta，他们基本上都超过了预期，超过了云收入将达到的指导，因此他们今年将在资本支出方面再投资多少。

我认为我们应该有 6600 亿超大规模公司资本支出，从去年的 3500 亿上升。我认为现在新的估计是它将超过 7000 亿。所以这——再次，它超过 GDP 的 2%。这是 GDP 的巨大顺风。还有另一篇文章说我认为上个季度 AI 是 GDP 增长的 75%。

不仅是基础设施，还有 token 经济

顺便说一下，这只是资本支出部分。这是物理基础设施。这不是在 token 工厂内部生成的 token 的经济影响。这是工厂的建设。这些 token 是如何使用的？就像我们看到的那样，它们不仅仅是用来做研究或回答问题，而是用来创建代码。所以我们看到了软件开发生产力的爆炸。我们看到了定制软件创建的爆炸，这将加速经济的每一部分。每个想要获得代码的企业现在都将能够首次获得代码。以前他们甚至无法雇用所需的工程师来生成它。现在他们将能够。所以这是整个经济生产力的巨大解锁。

然后你进入这些新用例，比如协作用例和代理。所以发生的工作流程自动化，仍然很早。我不相信这会取代人类。

9 秒删除生产数据库的 agent

我们上周有那个疯狂的案例——一个 agent 在 9 秒内删除了一个生产数据库，因为有一个 bug。看，那对我说的是——并不是 agent 没有价值。它们有价值，但它们必须被监督。

你知道，这种你只是要能够自动化所有工作的想法，是对真正技术问题和问题的大量挥手。代理必须被监督。某人必须负责。不会是 CEO。CEO 不想为成千上万的代理负责。你需要人——

尽管 Jack 和 Block 这样说。 是的。一千个直接报告是一个很好的目标，但这不现实。是的， 你需要精明的 IT 人员可以监督这个并确保它工作。他们必须对 CEO 负责。某人必须推动生产力。这就像 Bology 总是说的，AI 不是端到端的，而是中间到中间的。你必须有人做提示，你必须有人做验证，我会加上监督和责任。

AI 与美国经济同义

无论如何，更大的观点是，我说的是我不认为会有伴随着这种生产力繁荣的巨大失业。事实上，我认为现在实际发生的是 AI 正在变得与美国经济同义。

我的意思是，它产生 GDP 的 75% 这一事实，你有这种资本支出爆炸、这种能源爆炸来供养它，再次，只是这些新 token 工厂释放的应用的开始。我认为这都是非常非常积极的事情。

所有这些末日论者试图给它泼冷水，或者不断地吓唬人们。我的意思是，他们想要美国经济做什么？只是停止？我的意思是，他们只是不想要任何进步。我的意思是，再次，你知道，当你谈论停止 AI 或停止 AI 进步时，你真正做的是停止美国经济。你基本上是在说你不想要经济增长。AI 现在与美国经济的增长同义。如果没有经济增长，就没有钱来支付所有的社会项目。就没有钱来偿还国债。就没有钱来基本上建立我们的国防。所有这些我们想花钱的事情。我们必须有一个充满活力的经济。这现在与 AI 同义。

所以我知道 AI 可能不受欢迎。我看到那些民意调查。但拥有强大的经济是受欢迎的。我相信这些事情现在是同义的。

川普政府的角色

这几乎就像有一些建筑师或 zar 在第一年设置棋盘一样，以确保它是超级竞争的。 川普总统在这上面摆好了桌子。 绝对的。给了一些好的建议，我认为。也许。 Freeberg，你的想法？ 总有好的顾问总是好的。 总有好的顾问总是好的。绝对的。绝对的。 不，但是看，我以前说过。总统只是想让美国获胜。 字面上，有些人——如果我们在看这个，你知道，100 年前，会像人们说的那样，"是的，你知道吗？我们不应该建造高速公路系统，或者我们半建了高速公路系统。让我们停止——让我们停止建造高速公路。" 不，高速公路系统是由联邦政府资助的。没有竞争。在按通胀调整的基础上，它是最昂贵的——我认为它是美国历史上最昂贵的项目。 是的。在那之前是铁路，就像你不能停止这些事情。它们必须继续前进。

Pocket OS 的 Claude 删除惨案

有趣的观点——你知道，对 token、智能资源的需求很大，Freeberg，这与光纤情况完全不同——正如 Sachs 正确指出的——我们建造了所有这些但实际上没有应用。这里的应用相当——相当众所周知，你有大量的人在企业中试图通过氛围编码达到成功，试图推动这一点。

我们之前在节目中提到了一个有趣的故事，Claude 吃了某人的家庭作业。这是所有噩梦中的噩梦。

有人在做氛围编码。这是 Pocket OS 的创始人。显然，他们为租车公司制造软件。他通过 Cursor 的 AI 平台、他们的编码平台使用 Opus 4.6——你知道，这是最昂贵的级别。他说他配置了足够的安全规则，但代理正在做一项常规任务。他们看到某种凭据不匹配，他们决定通过删除铁路卷来修复不匹配，没有用户确认。他们将代码从仓库推送到实时应用程序，他们删除了所有内容，包括备份。

字面上是 Silicon Valley HBO 剪辑中 Son of Anton 的场景。搞笑。

你给了 AI 权限来覆盖内部文件系统中的代码。你打算告诉我吗？不，我以为这就是公司这些天的政策。 好的，嗯，你的 AI 刚刚史诗般地失败了。 这不清楚。 可能是 Son of Anton 决定摆脱所有 bug 的最有效方法是摆脱所有软件，这在技术上和统计上是正确的。但人工神经网络有点是黑盒。所以我们永远无法确定。 Zach，他们五六年前是怎么做对的？人工神经网络是黑盒。所以我猜我们永远不会知道。但在技术上是正确的。

Freeberg，当你用氛围编码炸毁一个空心系统——你几周前在 Jensen 面前绝对炫耀你正在推送多少代码——你会怪谁？你会自己负责吗？你会怪 Claude 还是 Cursor？当你炸毁俄亥俄州的整个堆栈时你会怪谁？

你怪谁？ 是的，我会怪 Dario。 你怪 Dario。好的，这就是我想的。这是一个正确的答案。正确的答案。怪 Dario。他是说这是末日机器的人。来吧来吧 prodio。第 17 个 invitio。 我的意思是，我邀请了这个家伙 17 次。他完全是要我。他不想和这个播客有任何关系。

Sachs：这不是"AI 阴谋诡计"

实际上，让我谈谈这个。我认为有一个误解，认为这个错误是因为，你知道，所谓的"AI 阴谋诡计"——

就像那个视频中 AI 决定摆脱 bug 的最佳方法基本上是消除代码库。这有点像那个"AI 会把世界变成回形针"类型的事情，它会以某种方式错误地策划。这不是这里发生的事情。

这只是一个老式的 bug 在边缘情况下发生的案例。你知道，你有这个 API 不是为权限使用而设计的事实。你有一个凭据被遗留下来的事实。可能不应该。这有点像一个完美的风暴，导致 AI 做了它不太理解它在做什么的事情。

我认为如果这里有一个系统性问题，而不仅仅是一个随机的边缘情况，那就是 AI 仍然不知道它不知道什么。你知道，像一个人会在删除生产数据库之前停下来说，"哦，我即将做一些非常严重、非常破坏性的事情。我确定我想这样做吗？"你知道，一个人会停下来说，"哦，等一下。我需要在采取那个行动之前对我所做的事情更有信心。"AI 仍然有这个问题——再次，它可能过于自信。这就是幻觉来自的地方——它不知道何时应该对其输出有低信心，对吧？但这就是为什么它必须被监督。任务的时间跨度越长，它就越有可能脱轨。

还有漂移。确切地说。这就是为什么人们开始意识到这种消除所有软件开发人员的想法是膨胀预期的高峰。 是的。 对。

Aaron Levie 的反向看法

Aaron Levie 有一个关于这个的很好的推文，他对此有正确的看法。Aaron 转发了 Matthew Glacius 的推文，他有点讽刺地说："5 个月了，我想我决定我不想氛围编码。我想专业管理的软件公司使用 AI 编码助手为我制造更多更好、更便宜的软件产品，他们用钱卖给我。"

只是降低你的价格。不要让我氛围编码就是翻译。 是的。我的意思是，我认为像 Madaglacius 在那里的难得胜利。

无论如何，Aaron Levie 然后说："Agent Coding 对希望完成更多工作的软件开发人员来说是一个巨大的胜利，对任何想学习如何开始编码的人来说都是极好的。它不那么好的是随便地构建你必须持续维护的复杂软件，并承担升级、维护、保持最新的安全问题、bug、网络安全的所有风险——这些是不熟悉系统的大多数知识工作者的税。"

这不是税。这是一个巨大的风险—— 是的，这是一个必须管理的风险。如果你不这样做。人会被解雇，因为会有一些公开公司，一些笨蛋试图氛围编码他们的方式摆脱某些东西，他们会烧毁企业价值。看起来会很壮观，因为我们都会笑，意识到那是愚蠢的，本来就不应该发生的。 是的。我的意思是，这是有可能的，这会改善到通过幻灭低谷并变得超级高产，你将能够让代理做合理的事情而不删除你的数据集。但我们还有一段路要走。

这是你的，你知道，这是技术采用图。基本上，你有一个技术被触发。你有试验，你有这个膨胀预期的高峰。你进入幻灭低谷，然后是启蒙的斜坡，最终它变得清晰，这是一个机会。

话题四：Retatrutide 与肽热潮

嘿，Freeberg，你已经变成了 Retatrutide curious。你已经——

告诉我——告诉我关于 Reta 的事情，因为我想要它。我想去用它。 我想使用它，我需要你告诉 Nat 我可以拿它。 我有一个朋友也有一些建议。

Freeberg，关于 Lily 上个月发布的第三阶段临床试验数据的报道。所以每个人都对继续显示相当惊人结果的数据疯狂。

不像 Tirzepatide，那是 Lily 今天的主要产品，这是一个双激动剂。它有两种肽与不同的受体结合，GLP-1，GIP 受体。这另一个现在还与胰高血糖素结合，这是第三个受体。胰高血糖素受体结合肽导致细胞增加它们的代谢，这实际上加速了脂肪能量消耗，超过了通常的肌肉能量消耗。它更可能在早期燃烧脂肪，导致更快的脂肪损失，但也减少了肌肉损失。

数据汇总

现在出来的一些其他数据显示： - 非 HDL 胆固醇下降 27% - 甘油三酯下降 41% - 肝脏脂肪下降 80%——80% 的肝脏脂肪减少 - A1C 在 40 周内从 7.9% 下降到 6%——这很惊人。如果你是糖尿病患者，A1C 在几个月内下降这么多，这字面上是一种救命药物 - 这个第三阶段试验的平均用户体重从 214 磅下降，减少了 37 磅。相比之下，安慰剂组在 40 周内减少了 6 磅 - 适度的副作用。20% 的人感觉比安慰剂组的人更恶心

抗炎与抗衰老效益

现在还有很多其他单独的研究正在进行，显示炎症信号分子的显著减少。所以系统性信号——嘿，细胞处于困境——触发这种炎症过程，可能对你的身体造成很多其他伤害，可以加速衰老。所以另一个对话是，Retatrutide 实际上可能也是一种抗衰老药物。

Hercules Hercules Hercules

你知道，顺便说一下，很多研究是在非常高剂量、12 毫克剂量上做的，但你可能可以将这个东西的剂量降低到 2 毫克，仍然看到很多抗炎维持和其他益处。我不是医生，但人们对此疯狂——它比仅仅用于临床肥胖或——

FDA 何时——预计日期是什么时候？ 2027 年。2027 年中期。 那就是他们说的。可能更早发生。我的意思是，数据在，你知道，FDA 会花时间评估它，但我认为鉴于这一切的样子—— 可能更早发生，可能在今年晚些时候发生。

Chamath 谈 Retatrutide

Swim Chamath Swim 说这令人难以置信，根据他们的经验，它符合炒作。

谁？ Swim。 那是什么？那是什么？ 不是我的人。Swim。 哦， 这是一个 Reddit 术语。不是我的人说有一个人 Swim 有一个人，并且循环使用了 Retatrutide，做俯卧撑，说肌肉增长是惊人的，没有肌肉损失，脂肪减少。

如果你去 X 上搜索 Reta，

嗯， 这令人难以置信。你看到这些 65 岁的人在几周内从大叔身材变成令人难以置信的健美运动员的样子。我震惊了。然后对我来说，我不需要那种帮助本身，但我的肝脏健康对我很重要。我的心脏健康，因为我是南亚人，看起来就像一个奇迹药物。我等不及了。

当你饿你的身体时，当你关闭食欲——这是 GLP-1 激动剂功能——通常你的身体进入这种饥饿模式，你有这个过程，你的身体试图从你现有的细胞中产生能量。因为肌肉比脂肪密度大得多，你可能会偏向肌肉组织被分解而不是脂肪组织。但这种新的激动剂、胰高血糖素激动剂，他们放进 Retatrutide 的——它偏向脂肪燃烧而不是肌肉燃烧。所以这实际上可以推动短期使用低剂量让人减重并保持肌肉，并变得健美。这就是为什么健身社区的很多人都在谈论，嘿，我想获得这个并使用一段时间。所以你会在那个社区看到更多的炒作，以及所有的健康影响。这感觉就像我们即将有一场绝对的肽雪崩可供选择。

礼来与川普政府的定价

2025 年 11 月，Lily 与川普政府达成协议——我看到这个——大幅降低 Tirzepatide 的价格。我想 Medicare 上是 50 块。

Medicare 上 50 块。是的。 是的。这是一个相当便宜的价格点，但当你考虑 Lily 产品组合时，它开始变得有意义。你以 50 块得到 Tirzepatide，但如果你想升级，得到 Retatrutide。那是高端产品，那是他们要开始的——梅赛德斯到本田。

我相信，如果我是 Lily，我坐在那里看这些数据出来，我会想，"我的天啊，人们会为这个付费，那开始有点像升级到 BMW 或 Model S Plat，如果你愿意的话。"

是的。Tirzepatide 就像一卧室凌乱床的酒店房间， 另一个是套房——Retatrutide 就像两卧室套房。

Roman 与 Sparks

嗯，你也可以——顺便说一下，你们知道我是 Roman 的代言人。他们也有 Wegovy 药丸。Roman/twist 来获得你的—— 等等，你是付费的？你在谈论什么？你是付费的？我们在请 Charles Barkley—— 我们没有销售团队，然后你过来上 Allin 开始推销。 不，不，不，不，不。相信我，我们也会有一个。我们会有一个 Roman 赞助。 你让我拿的 Roman 药丸是什么？它叫什么？ 哦，Sparks。Sparks。Sparks。你拿了吗？ 我拿过了，现在—— Amore please。不，不—— 也许只是一半的糟糕。 我想听这个故事。我想听这个故事。来吧。 它失控了。 我告诉你，我告诉过你的。 所以发生的是，Nat 和我说，你不能——你不能只是随机使用它。它是计划的。我们讨论它。我们把它放在日历上。 你需要一个计划。 你需要一个计划。 你需要一个计划。不能就进去—— 因为否则——否则它太多了。你不能只是随机吃。 你什么意思？ 它会是一个 sesh。 这是一整件事，伙计。这就像我没有那种能量。 这是一个延长的会议。你必须得到充分的休息。这——不要在凌晨 1 点做这个。这就像晚上 10 点。这就像一个——不，这更像是这更像是在度假，你知道，比如—— 上午 10 点到中午 12 点，你知道，到中午，你知道，你必须真正—— 你必须真正安排它。 安排它，因为孩子们在周围—— 否则它必须是空的。 否则，对她不公平，这只是一个——它是一个谎言。把它放出去。 这是一个——这是一个大的承诺，字面上。 你看起来很尴尬，Jim。你觉得谈论这个很尴尬吗？这只是很多，伙计。这就像——这是一个很多要处理的。这是很多。 它是——如果你想在你的表现中获得额外的 20%， 它是很多，bro。 它是很多。它基本上是加班。 发生了什么？发生的是我说，"哦，这是什么东西？"Jason 说，"伙计，你必须得到它。你必须得到它。"所以，我们得到了它。我们试了它， 我们说，"那他妈的是什么？"所以，然后我一直在试图把药丸卖出去。所以，我给了 Stant Tang 一些。我说，"Stanley，你试试。"字面上，他像发牌一样发它们—— 但当我们吃扑克晚餐时，我说，有人想试试这些东西吗？但是这些是什么？Roman Sparks。是 Roman Sparks 吗？向我的朋友们致敬。好的，让我们继续。

话题五：Freeberg 在最高法院

伙计们。Freeberg 有他自己的个人超级碗。你看到我为 Knicks 季后赛季做准备。我得到我的场边。Freeberg 有相当于的——Sachs，他去了最高法院听他们谈论化学品。这对他来说是一件大事。最高法院聚在一起。你穿了吗？是的。Monsanto 审判在最高法院发生，他去了——他得到了场边。他去了最高法院在大楼里听。

你们见过现场最高法院听证会吗？

没有。我会喜欢的。 我会喜欢的。Sax，你去过吗？ 不，我实际上没有去过。 我的意思是，老实说，我认为这是我有过的最惊人的经历之一。

法院的氛围

前面有一个大规模的抗议。我们通过法警办公室进去。那栋建筑——你走进去，就像神圣的。一切都是大理石。你不允许说话。当你在大楼里时你必须超级安静。就像他们一直说，你在某个安静的图书馆里。就像人们用这种神圣和尊重的水平对待它。他们说，这里没有政治。这里没有 [脏话]。这里没有言论自由。这是法院。当你进入这个法院时，法官告诉你你将如何说话，你将如何举止，你将做什么，未经允许你不会说话。

你把所有东西放在储物柜里，你上楼，你进入法庭。法庭——只是在那里太惊人了。他们在法官上面有一个惊人的大理石浮雕，上面有一些人类历史上的伟人——摩西和这些惊人的历史人物。然后在他们下面是九位法官和案件。

如果你们没看过案件，你可以在线听他们。值得听吗？

法官们的座位

等等，等等。我有一个问题。所以， Roberts 是不是因为他是首席而坐在中间？是的。 然后所有右翼法官是不是都坐在右边？ 不，他们是混合的。所以他们——我不知道——我不知道确切的座位，但他们是按法院任命混合的。 是这样吗？我认为是这样的。然后从中间向外延伸，Roberts 在中间。Roberts 偶尔会点名法官，说，"嘿，你有问题吗？你有问题吗？"如果没有人在说话，但其他法官会在他们想要的时候插入他们的问题，他们会问。

像看 LeBron James 打球

现在，老实说，看这个就像看 LeBron James 打篮球。这些律师在双方都是如此令人惊叹的印象深刻，你只是会坐在那里，我就像是敬畏。这太——我感觉我的能量在这个过程结束时完全被抽干了，因为你只是如此投入，如此被这些家伙的思考和说话方式所吸引。

你拿了 Roman Sparks 吗？你在那里时拿了 Roman Sparks 吗？ 没有。

如果你足够熟悉案件或案件历史或正在辩论的法律，因为再次，当你进入最高法院时，你从不辩论案件。你辩论的是案件决定的法律解释。所以这是宪法吗？你如何解释这个特定的法案，这个法律，这个联邦法律？思考它的正确方式是什么？所以你实际上不谈论案件。你谈论美国法律的解释，我们的法律的解释，我们的宪法的解释，全球的解释。

你说事实已经被确定。 是的。 对。在低级法院，有事实问题和法律问题。 事实已经由低级法院确定。只是最高法院在裁决法律问题。 是的。所以他们有一个完整的简报，包含案件的完整历史。记住，他们一天只听两个案子。所以他们每个听证会一个小时—— 所以你进去他们只在周一、周二、周三的最后两周做，他们只听 10 月到 4 月的案子。只有少数案件被选中。 哇。所以你真的在一个射击时钟上来—— 你在一个射击时钟上，你只有——它每边 30 分钟，然后法官会问问题。

Monsanto 与 Roundup 案

这是 Monsanto 和 Roundup，对吧？所以正在辩论的法律是什么—— 你的——监管机构 EPA 设置农药的标签。这导致癌症还是不导致？什么是警告？这可能对出生缺陷、怀孕、所有我们都习惯在标签上看到的东西有害。当你购买一个产品，一个化学产品时，EPA 在他们的监管权力下确定 Roundup 不会导致癌症。

当你销售农药时，你必须首先向 EPA 注册，得到批准，然后 EPA 给你一个标签。标签是由 EPA 编写的。它确切地说出你应该说的话。在这种情况下，它说所有这些东西不说癌症，因为他们确定它不会导致癌症。

我不会争论它是否导致癌症，但案件是 EPA 是联邦法案下的监管机构，叫做 FIFRA——杀菌剂、杀虫剂或杀鼠剂法案。这就是 EPA 被赋予他们的监管权力来在这些产品上贴标签。

所有失败的案件都是州的失败警告案件。迄今为止，Bayer——现在拥有 Monsanto——已经在这些诉讼中支付了 100 亿美元，他们在资产负债表上预留了 100 亿。他们仍在法庭上有 90,000 个未决案件。90,000。

哇。

所以这一个案子被上诉到最高法院。去年，白宫总检察长——如果总检察长站起来要求最高法院接受案件，案件更有可能被接受。所以白宫说，"请接受这个案件。我们需要联邦优先——意味着联邦政府有权设置标签——因为所有失败和正在裁决的案件都在州法院，那里的州有像加利福尼亚的失败警告法这样的法律——这意味着如果制造商知道产品有风险，你必须警告消费者。"

所以律师一直在争论 Monsanto 或 Bayer 知道这个产品导致癌症并且没有警告消费者。他们一直在赢得案件。他们一直在输掉案件，但他们已经赢得了足够多的案件，这现在已经成为一个数十亿美元的问题。

所以争论是 EPA 说它不会导致癌症，他们有联邦优先权。所以 EPA 有权确定。所以这是一个论点。

但当另一位律师上来时，这家伙就像字面上看 LeBron James。所以进去，我们说，"哦，6:3 Bayer 会赢。"然后另一个家伙上来，他说，"嗯，嘿，你们去年推翻了 Chevron 主义。你们记得那个案子吗？

是的。 当 Chevron 主义被推翻时，它基本上说不再—— 联邦机构得到决定。它必须是法律的直接阅读。 哦。

所以现在他在说像各州应该有权自己阅读法律。他们不应该只是顺从 EPA。这就是这将归结为的。所以最后，我们说，"哦，我的天啊，这可能是一个 50/50 的硬币翻转，5:4 任一方向。"进去时我们试图说，"嘿，也许这可能是 6:3。"

所以老实说，整个体验是不可思议的。案件很有趣。

律师如何浓缩论证？

这些是非常复杂的事情。这些人怎么能做出一个完整的论点，一边只有 30 分钟，另一边 30 分钟，有一些 Q&A，然后一小时就结束了？ 有一整个艺术和科学，Sax，你可能熟悉这个——你如何在简报文件中浓缩最高法院案件？你向法庭请愿什么？你试图把它浓缩到你想让法官裁决的确切法律解释，而不是所有其他 [脏话]。

这是口头辩论。是的。 口头辩论，只是一个讨论。然后法官介入，他们所做的就是问律师问题，一次一个律师，一边然后另一边。

顺便说一下，总检察长在中间上来，发表了一些评论，他们问她从白宫来的一些问题，她坐下，然后双方有点来回——他们就这样——它就像每边在那一个具体的法律问题上 30 分钟 Q&A。

案件的潜在含义

Ketanji Brown Jackson 说，"但如果在 EPA 发布标签后，他们发现它确实导致癌症，他们应该不更新标签吗？"他说，"嗯，不，他们不允许。他们只能发布 EPA 说的标签。"他还说——如果他们发现它确实导致癌症并且不报告给 EPA，那是一个刑事案件。然后她说，嗯，如果 EPA 不行动呢，各州不应该有权保护他们的人民吗？

所以这些是法律论点，所有这些正在进行的讨论。还有有趣的含义——基本上如果各州得到解释联邦法律并忽略联邦监管机构，它打开了一个全新的潘多拉魔盒——所有州可以开始忽略联邦监管机构如 EPA 或 FDA 或 USDA 等等等等。所以整个案件有一大堆真正有趣的含义。

当你听这些家伙时，他们只是在谈论 Chamath，关于法律的那个确切的解释，这就是这归结为的。不是实际的案件。

闭门会议

之后，Sachs，他们口头辩论，然后他们有一个像私人会议，他们会写他们的论文，给出他们的最终判决。是的。

Sachs。 是的。是的，我认为发生的是 - 所以我猜有一些讨论在闭门发生，他们弄清楚多数派在哪里，然后首席分配谁为多数派写意见—— 在那次会议中，没有人被允许进入，事实上你有一个双门系统——如果有任何东西需要进出，你必须像敲门一样——你被引入这个前室—— 哦，是不是一个气闸？ 是的，气闸。

Ted Cruz 与最高法院

它实际上——我不知道你是否在那里，Jason，但 Ted Cruz 来玩扑克游戏—— Ted Cruz 给 William Rehnquist 当过 clerk。如果你想吃一顿令人难以置信的晚餐，问他关于最高法院和 Bill Rehnquist 的事。他真的是最高法院的一个学生。这只是让最高法院免费——按你的观点听起来像有史以来最不可思议的机构，无处不在地被创造出来。

顺便说一下，比白宫更多，比国会大厦更多，比任何其他这些大机构更多，这个地方有——它几乎就像在英国一样，它有这些人们运作的方式。安全是如此不同。他们站在那里，在法庭上，他们每 20 分钟交换位置。这是非常协调的。他们的着装与任何其他法庭听证会都不同。

也许像 150，我会说。你怎么进入的？他们在—— 我实际上认为每个人都是 clerk 或在法院工作的人的客人。我不认为它对公众非常公开。 你不能排队。没有排队。 我不知道是否有排队。嗯，这是通过——我们通过首席法官进入的。嗯，他给了我们通行证，但我认为这是非常嗯—— 我认为在 Elon vs OpenAI 案件中——你可以排队，然后法官给媒体院 30 张票。不，不。是的，但我认为最高法院也有一个排队。有一些他们的公共访问—— 看起来不像有任何公众在这个法院。每个人，每个人都穿得很尊重。我的意思是，这个法院有一个不可思议的—— 你知道，很酷的体验。

Sachs：享受功能性机构

我会——我只想说——趁还可以的时候享受。我的意思是，我认为最高法院是美国最后高度功能性的机构之一。100%。 你知道在某个时候我们会有像 13 或 21 或某个疯狂数字的法官在那里——给法官加塞 jersey 后——所以趁它还在的时候享受—— 在它目前的形式中。 你能想象出现戴着印有法官名字的球衣，像有部分，有人卖 cracker jacks—— 神权版的最高法院—— 版本。确切地。 法院的受欢迎程度真的取决于它是否在发布人们同意的决定。这就是它归结为的。如果你问人们他们是否喜欢最高法院，这真的取决于他们是否同意这些决定是新近的，而不是—— 决定的过程以及它的论点有多好，所有这些你指出的事情。实际上，法院——我的意思是，我刚检查了数字。法院现在相对受欢迎。我认为它在 2024 年盖洛普调查中低至 35%，但我认为它回升到 44% 到 50% 的好评度，对于涉及政治的东西来说相对较高，对吧？就像你看国会或任何特定的政治家，他们通常会比那低。 我只是在我访问时感到对机构如此放心，看到这些家伙互动和行为以及他们如何行为这个过程。这就像——伙计，多么神奇的国家。是的。

James Carville 与法院打包

嗯，我说我说的原因是最近有一个和 James Carville 的采访。你们看到这个了吗？他说——他说，看，当我们获得权力时我们正在打包法院。

所以我们甚至不会——我们不会担心它。 我们会到 13。对吧？他说我们要—— 我认为是的。他们将从 9 增加到 13，然后他们将创造一些新的州和所有其他的。所以那将是那。 嗯， 享受——趁还可以的时候享受。享受。 趁还可以的时候享受。 顺便说一下， 以高音结束。 等等。是的。这是帝国的终结。那将是那。

公众抽签和拍卖建议

顺便说一下，最高法院有一个——我是对的。有一个在线票务彩票。所以我们都可以注册，你可以得到四张票。我认为他们应该让这——我们应该和 Howard Lutnik 谈谈。也许他可以让这成为一场拍卖。我们从美国得到一个收入流。我们可以以 20,000 美元的价格作为场边座位卖 10 张票。

Jason，你正是他们试图保护免受的。 确切地。就像，我们怎么能——我们如何货币化最高法院？

收尾

好的，各位。就这样。这是世界上最伟大的播客，为你，为 Chamath Polihapatia、David Freeberg 和 David Sachs。我是世界上最伟大的主持人。我们会见——首席法官。

我就像 All-In 播客的首席法官。我们会让你的赢家继续奔跑。 Rainman David and 它说我们把它开源给粉丝，他们已经疯了。 爱你赢家的女王。 最好的朋友走了。 那是我的狗在 driveway 上。 哦，伙计。我的 habitasher 会满足。 我们应该都拿一个房间，只是有一场大的群交，因为他们都没用。这就像这种性紧张你只需要以某种方式释放。 你的脚。我们需要让水星已经。我要全押。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"权力倒挂"：算力不再是稀缺品，**电力**才是
表面上市场在讨论 OpenAI 没达成 10 亿周活、收入未达标、$6000 亿算力承诺难以兑现——但 Chamath 揭开了一个被普遍误读的真相：**这不是需求问题，而是供给问题，而且供给的瓶颈不是 GPU，是变压器和电网基础设施**。
让我给你一个意外的观察：**当算力变成电力的奴隶时，AI 公司的护城河从"模型能力"悄悄迁移到了"电网谈判桌"。** 这意味着 Dario Amodei 和 Sam Altman 现在的命运，不是由他们的研究员决定，而是由一个你从没听过的 PJM 互联队列调度员决定的。这就是为什么 Anthropic 必须给 Amazon 让出经济参与权——他们买的不是 EC2，他们买的是**电力的优先排队权**。这彻底重写了"AI 公司"的本质：它们正在从软件公司变成披着软件外衣的电力公用事业公司。
## 二、跨领域连接：墨西哥湾石油争夺战 × 19 世纪铁路 × 当下 AI 资本结构
播客里有人提到 Cisco 在 2000 年泡沫顶点的图——**这是错的类比**。真正的类比应该是 19

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 010 - How Much Do GPUs Really Cost, and Where Does the Value Go? (AI Cloud TCO)
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的"GPU 通胀税"：你买的不是算力，是概率

表面上 Neo Cloud 在卖 GPU 小时，实质上他们在卖故障概率分布的右尾。文章里最反直觉的数字是：silver tier 与 gold tier 的总成本差距中，92% 来自 goodput 而不是表面的 GPU 单价。换句话说，当你在比价表上看到便宜 5% 的 GPU 时，你实际上是在用 20%+ 的"看不见的损失"来交换那 5% 的"看得见的便宜"——这是一种典型的 Akerlof 式柠檬市场，但比二手车市场更阴险，因为故障是统计性的、滞后显现的、且在大集群上非线性放大（一颗 GPU 挂掉等于整个 4000 卡训练任务停摆）。

跨领域连接：这与航空业的"按时率"经济学惊人相似。廉价航空的票面价格便宜 30%，但延误的隐性成本（误工、转机、心理损耗）让总成本反超传统航司。Neo Cloud 行业正在经历航空业 1980 年代刚解除管制时的同一阶段——价格透明化先行，质量透明化（ClusterMax 排名）滞后但即将引爆分层。

二、Vera Rubin 是英伟达定价模型的"哥德尔不完备时刻"

最让我意外的不是 Jensen 没涨价，而是 Jensen 不能涨价而又不自知。SemiAnalysis 的"one chart to rule them all"揭示了一个深刻的悖论：英伟达内部用"组件成本 × 75% 标准加价"定价，但这套体系在面对 inference acts（吞吐量代际跃升 15×）时彻底失效——它把一个 V 形价值跃迁压缩成了线性 markup。这就像用算盘给量子计算定价。

更深层的隐喻：Jensen 的定价机制本质上是牛顿力学（线性、组件可加性），但 Blackwell→Rubin 的代际跃迁已经进入相对论尺度（NVLink 72 卡域、prefill/decode 解耦、MTP 协同优化让"flop"这个度量单位本身失效）。当你度量单位的物理意义被颠覆时，你的定价模型就发生了哥德尔式自我崩塌——它在自己的体系内是自洽的，但外部世界已经换了语言。

跨领域连接：这与摄影行业从胶片转向数字时柯达的悲剧同构。柯达用"每张照片成本"定价，而数字相机让"每张"这个单位失去意义。英伟达正面临"每 flop 价格"失去意义的时刻——而 TPU 和 Trainium 早已悄悄换了度量衡。

三、Torch.PASS 是"系统熵"的市场化套利工具

一个被掩埋的洞察：Clockwork 卖的不是软件，他们卖的是让低质数据中心"假装"高质数据中心的能力——一种纯粹的熵差套利。这在金融市场叫"信用违约互换"，在生物学叫"共生菌群"，在机器学习里叫"知识蒸馏"。本质都是同一件事：用一层抽象把底层的混乱隔离掉，向上层呈现一个干净接口。

但 Jordan 不经意提到的那句话是真正的炸弹："the threshold is getting lower over time"——这意味着容错框架正在把 GPU 集群商品化。一旦 1000 卡门槛降到 256 卡，再降到 64 卡，整个 ClusterMax 排名体系会失去意义，因为软件层会把硬件差异熨平。届时 Neo Cloud 的护城河会从"我们故障少"被迫迁移到"我们规模大、合约长、生态厚"——这正是云计算从 IaaS 走向 PaaS 的同一条路径。

跨领域连接：这与 19 世纪铁路标准化历史完全重演。早期美国铁路有十几种轨距，托运货物要在不同公司间反复换装（=checkpoint restart）。1886 年统一为标准轨距后（=fault-tolerant framework 普及），运输商品化，价值从铁路公司转移到货主和终端消费者。今天 GPU 集群正在 1880 年代——真正的赢家不是当下最便宜的 Neo Cloud，而是那个在标准轨距时代来临前就建好枢纽车站的人（CoreWeave 的五年合约就是在赌这个）。

一个埋在闲聊里的预言

Jordan 那句"Jensen 把 Anthropic 推进了 AWS 的怀抱"是整期最重的一句话——但它被笑声盖过去了。这是经典的修昔底德陷阱：当垄断者用价格傲慢回应客户的求助时，他制造的不是顺从，而是对手的诞生。Trainium + Claude 4.5 的成功不是 AWS 的胜利，而是 Jensen 的战略代价。12-18 个月后当算力过剩出现时，市场会突然记起：原来还有别的选择。

📝 转录文本

开场闲聊：Vine、Tik Tok 与"Shorty"

Ben： Do you believe in magic? Well, I still have cautious um Mike. I was reminded of this like Vine. Dan, do you remember Vine before Tik Tok?

Dan： Yeah, that died like yeah, yeah, I remember that.

Ben： Do you remember those ones of the guys who would have the like room fan going and they'd sing into the fan and it sound like auto-tune? "Baby girl, what's your name?" [laughter] "Said shorty. Shorty come downtown." [laughter]

Kongwen, this is before your time, dude. Yeah, Kongwen doesn't even know what shorty is. "Who the is shorty?"

节目介绍：重新思考 GPU 集群的总拥有成本

Hey everyone, so welcome to SemiAnalysis Weekly. Jordan and I are switching roles. Tables have the turns of table this week. I'm going to be hosting Jordan as he talks about a new article that we've done on rethinking the total cost of a GPU cluster.

So Kongwen and I spent a lot of our time working on the total cost of ownership. But oftentimes this is from a theoretical or a quoted perspective. So it's a bit like being at a car dealership and you're saying this thing does 36 miles per gallon, but you know what, when you take it on the road, you have your mix of highway versus city, the performance going to be different. So that's what this article is all about.

Jordan's had a lot of experience actually using GPUs and many clusters. He's worked with dozens, maybe even a hundred Neo clouds and so he's drawn on that experience in terms of saying like, hey, what is the actual on-the-road performance? Jordan, you want to kick us off and talk about a few points in your article?

文章核心：Goodput 框架

Jordan： Sure, yeah, thanks Ben. So I think the high-level is in the title, which is how much do GPU clusters really cost? The article really explores implied costs that are not explicitly called out on a bill of materials or on a purchase order.

When people are renting GPUs, in many cases the price is not the same and so you get a cheaper price from many different providers, but you really want to look beyond the individual price of an individual GPU and figure out how much useful work you can get out of the cluster.

And so what we did in this article was introduce a framework for actually calculating goodput. Goodput being measured as how much useful work you can do on the cluster, how many jobs you can run or how much inference you can serve.

显性成本与隐性成本

We try to include line items that people would explicitly purchase: - The GPUs - Any sort of orchestration premiums - The cost of storage - The cost of networking - The cost of support nodes (CPUs) - The cost of support itself — many of the hyperscalers charge a premium just to give you response times within a certain SLA. You got to pay more money if you want quicker responses basically.

And then there's these implied costs: - The time spent setting things up or running POCs - The time spent debugging on the cluster if there's issues - And then goodput, which is basically a way to calculate how much downtime you're going to have on the cluster while you operate it.

As Dan said, it's like mileage on a car. If some of your GPUs are not accessible and they're not working and there's interruptions or failures while you're trying to run jobs, this doesn't just cost you time on that one GPU, it actually costs you time on the entire cluster because nothing can do useful work if one GPU fails depending on how you're setting up your clusters.

高层发现

The high-level findings is that there's a big discrepancy especially for big jobs on big clusters between top-tier providers that have less failures and a quicker recovery time from a failure when compared to lower-tier providers that just have a bunch of failures and it takes them a long time to recover from.

Previously to this, I think a lot of people didn't have a way to put numbers to that intuition. They'd know it'd be somewhere 5, 10, 20% I'd be willing to pay a premium, but exactly how much should you be willing to pay? We think we have a way of answering that based on this calculator, which for what it's worth is available for free for people listening at clustermax.ai/tco.

如何衡量 Goodput？

Ben： So Jordan, how do you measure the goodput? I saw the goodput calculator and this is your goodput expense calculator, but what is the range of assumptions that you should use? What has been your experience? What are the failure modes?

Jordan： At a high level there's two ways to recover from failures if you're running training jobs.

方式一：Checkpoint Restart

The first primary way is checkpoint restart, which basically means you're auto-saving the job as it runs and then if a failure occurs, you're going to have to restart the entire job — meaning the whole cluster grinds to a halt and then you start the job again from scratch.

This is the standard way to do things for basic PyTorch users. This is the torch.DCP distributed checkpoint API and you just kind of save your work.

That's on screen here for those watching, which is basically showing that the more failures or interruptions you have in the cluster — these can be hard failures like an issue with the power supply on the server, a GPU fails, a GPU falls off the bus, a network fails. Things like that are hard and immediate.

But there's also many other types of failures that are gradual over time. These are ECC mirror errors on memory, certain types of GPU failures, links flapping on the network where things are slowly degrading and you want to remove that node from the job or from the cluster and just keep it running, keep it making progress.

方式二：容错训练框架

The second way of recovering from failure would be to use fault-tolerant training frameworks. In the article we walked through three of them: torch.FT, torch.PASS and AWS's checkpointless training framework that's built into their SageMaker HyperPod clusters.

All three of them take a slightly different approach, but they kind of approximate what the Frontier Labs have. Especially torch.PASS, which is a way to keep training running if there's failures.

In our hands-on testing, the visual on screen from the torch.PASS Clockwork team blog basically shows a blue line and a green line tracking up where they're making good progress at expected performance, and then other checkpoint restart and torch.FT scenarios where you can keep training running, but it's either running with overhead on the network or having to recover from checkpoints at some interval.

Goodput 计算器的输入

The first inputs to the goodput calculator is which software framework you are using to accommodate for these failures: - Checkpoint restart → lower goodput, more expense due to every failure or every upgrade or every other interruption. - Fault tolerance → you have to configure things like how long it takes to fail over, how many idle spare nodes you have, and how much memory overhead or network overhead you're accommodating for.

torch.FT and checkpointless training both keep jobs running by paying a performance penalty — either by using certain network collectives or by using certain ways to do in-memory checkpointing.

In addition, there's a way to configure based on which provider you're assessing the average number of job failures you're expecting. People tend to have this intuition: "we tend to see five GPU XID 79s per day" (the GPU falling off the bus code). Or "we have a 256 GPU cluster and we see a failure a week."

From these pieces of intuition you can back out the GPU level MTBF (mean time between failures). The higher up a provider is on the ClusterMax rankings — gold, silver, bronze, gold or platinum tier — the more reliable the clusters, the less failures, and the more hot spare nodes they run to recover from a failure quickly.

ClusterMax 排名与 Neo Cloud 之间的真实差异

Ben： Jordan, you mentioned ECC errors, you mentioned GPUs falling off the bus. Aren't those Nvidia problems? Aren't they common to most hyperscalers? Is there actually a lot of variance between those? And if not, then what are the failure modes that there is actually a huge difference between tiers of Neo clouds?

GPU 层面：温度、功耗、冷却

Jordan： There definitely is a difference between tiers of Neo clouds.

When it comes to GPUs, different systems run at different temperatures and different power draw depending on the environmentals in the data center. The simplest example would be air cooling versus liquid cooling. Liquid cooling is generally more consistent over time. Air cooling creates hot spots and causes GPUs to fail more often.

Especially with the latest and greatest B300s — these GPUs are really hot, really power hungry. So a provider running an air-cooled data center might experience more failures because the GPUs are running hotter, when compared to one using a DLC (direct liquid cooler) to the chip.

网络层面：空气质量、链路抖动

Another example is air quality in the data center. This applies a lot to networking at scale. If you're training 4,000 GPUs and the cluster is 5,000 GPUs in size, all 4,000 of those GPUs need to be in sync — every single network link has to be up.

This is where we'll hear terms like link flaps where a given cable or NIC has issues — the connector may be dirty or failing, the light may be degrading. If that happens, training's going to slow down, stall, and eventually fail.

So air quality in the data center is a primary example of how you can guarantee less link failures or link flaps — literally just cleaning the cables or getting dust in there is an issue. People who run clean data centers and design the physical hardware are generally expected to have less hard failures.

运营层面：维护窗口

But there's also operational issues. If you constantly need to be making changes on the network, storage, or GPUs themselves — upgrading firmware, changing drivers, reconfiguring the network on the physical layer because you're trying to expand or add somebody else into the cluster — these impact existing customers because they require taking downtime.

Anybody who's run GPUs for six or more months has been contacted by their provider before: "Hey, can you accommodate us doing something? We need to reboot this. We need to unmount your storage. We need a maintenance window this weekend. Please don't run jobs for this amount of time." That's covered in the SLA — let's say 8-hour maintenance windows over a certain period.

But providers who are not experienced running this — these maintenance windows go longer than expected, they happen more often than needed. Those interruptions, even if they're not actual hard failures, can still cause you to stop jobs, restart them, or generally not get as much useful work out of your cluster.

Torch.PASS 能否抹平 Neo Cloud 之间的差异？

Ben： You mentioned Torch Pass actually goes a long way towards ameliorating the issues of having to checkpoint and restart. Doesn't this equalize the performance across Neo Clouds? Couldn't it paper over those kind of differences?

Jordan： It potentially can. And that's why Torch Pass is a licensed product that the Clockwork guys are selling to many of these labs — to get people to pay a little bit of money in order to be able to run on some of these lower tier providers.

It totally helps, especially for predictable failures like maintenance windows where there might be a rolling upgrade or ECCs on memory or a scheduled reboot. It can recover from hard failures, but it can't necessarily accommodate for many hard failures all at once. And nothing can accommodate for a switch outage or a file system unmount or something major.

So there's a level in which the software framework on a properly run cluster will help improve goodput and therefore you'll have a better TCO. But it's not a silver bullet to solve all potential issues from a provider. Torch Pass is the closest that we've seen to what Frontier Labs are using at this scale.

容错门槛在持续下降

If everybody running 100,000 GPUs is using it, then what's the threshold at which you need it? We think it's generally just getting lower over time as it becomes easier to adopt the framework, the amount of code changes you need is lower, and the amount you need to think about the system infrastructure is lower.

Is that limit a 2,000 GPU cluster or 1,000 or 512? We know people running fault tolerant frameworks on 1,000 GPUs for job sizes in the 128 to 256 GPU range. So it kind of feels like a "why not do it" point now.

That's why we wanted to introduce that section of the article — to motivate everybody from licensed products like Clockwork Torch Pass to the open source stuff like Torch FT, and all the users or potential contributors in the ecosystem to think about this more.

真金白银：Goodput 占总成本差异的比重

Ben： Let's pivot a little bit more towards talking specific dollars and cents. Jordan, could you talk us quickly through the difference?

5,184 张 GB300 的场景

Jordan： There's a TCO cluster calculator scenario on screen — a cluster of 5,184 GB300 NVL72 GPUs, latest and greatest. We kept all the pricing the same and called the three providers gold tier, hyperscaler, and silver tier. The pricing is $4 an hour.

Differences in storage, network services, CPUs. Major differences in support if you want good support from the hyperscalers. But the biggest discrepancy is in the goodput tier:

  • Gold tier: 6.14% goodput premium
  • Hyperscaler: 10.53%
  • Silver tier: 20.91%

When you scroll down — silver versus gold tier — 92% of the difference in price is due to goodput, not storage, not debugging, not setup time. And about 40% of the discrepancy when we look at the hyperscalers.

计算方法

Clicking over to goodput expense: - Gold tier: fault tolerant frameworks like Torch Pass - Hyperscaler (AWS): HyperPod checkpointless training - Silver tier: checkpoint restart, with a 15,000 MTBF — slightly more failures than gold tier and hyperscaler at a dozen MTBF (in GPU hours)

For 5,000 GPUs in the cluster: - Gold tier / Hyperscaler: hard failure every 4.8 hours - Silver tier: hard failure every 2.9 hours

The calculation is based on a formula: 1. How long are you doing work after a failure occurs without identifying it? (Wasted across the entire cluster.) 2. How long do you actually need to fail over? 3. How long does it take to replace that failed node itself? Even if not part of that training job, you're paying for it to be out of service.

We configured the average job size to 4,000 GPUs on a 5,000 GPU cluster, blast radius to 64 (64 of 72 GPUs in the rack are being used).

For HyperPod checkpointless training: 5% memory overhead — to provide fault tolerance, you take some GPU memory away, which impacts batch size, so training is slower. That filters down: 6% on gold tier, 10% on hyperscaler, 20% on silver tier.

在 Llama 3 Behemoth 规模下放大

In a paper from Meta, Llama 3 Behemoth runs with 4.5 billion parameters — wait, they were running 16,000 GPU jobs on a 20,000 GPU cluster. At this scale: - Fault tolerant (Torch Pass): losing 21% to goodput - HyperPod checkpointless: 26% - Checkpoint restart: 80% of your cluster lost — effectively having a failure every 0.8 hours, basically a failure right after every checkpoint on an hourly basis

That's the motivation for why people are actually using these fault tolerant training frameworks at this scale.

推理业务的毛利率敏感性

Ben： You guys concluded the article with a very interesting gross margin sensitivity. Kongwen and Jordan, can you talk through how we can use this to figure out effective margins?

Jordan： The key point is just however many failures you're experiencing impact inference, too. As you have more nodes down in the cluster, you're able to serve less customers total. That's the basic conclusion for inference providers.

Kongwen 的毛利率分析

Kongwen： Bringing your attention to the column — token selling price, dollars per million tokens — that refers to how much a provider is selling their compute or inference on a dollar-per-million-token basis.

The row shows two figures from Jordan's calculations. For an H200 doing a one-year contract today, you can get somewhere around $2 on the GPU rental price. Factoring in the goodput expense, very little additional expense — that's the $1.90 on the left.

All the way to the right, it goes up to $3.10 — for a hyperscaler compute provider, your overall cost is up to 1.5× more on a total goodput expense basis.

If you sell tokens at $3 today and have CoreWeave or a Platinum compute provider at $1.19 goodput expense → 28% margins.

But going to a small hyperscaler compute provider at the same $3 token price → negative 25% gross margin when you factor in total goodput expense.

在推理场景里的取舍

Jordan： In inference it's a little bit different. A lot of inference providers — because it's a more naturally fault tolerant workload, single node inference — they're willing to go way down the list and use any spare GPUs they can find as long as it's cheap.

But many have commented they kind of learned their lesson over time: it's not that easy to make money on cheap GPUs if they're never online. You have to chase providers all over the place to fix things or credit you back for downtime.

So it could be a situation of pay $2.30 because you know you're going to get $2.30 of goodput as opposed to paying $2.10 and landing in the $2.70 much later. After much aggravation. And potentially a long-term contract to lock down those GPUs.

Inference is in some ways less sensitive to goodput itself — the workload is single node in many cases, so the blast radius is just eight GPUs. The ability to adopt a software framework that's fault tolerant is almost required to run inference endpoints because you have to load balance and retry failed requests.

Whereas people training models — even at Frontier Labs with all their infrastructure, or smaller companies, academia training smaller models — they go to a startup trying to train the next generation of models and they've got all these great ideas. Suddenly they need to become systems engineers to actually run it at scale. Previously they were supported by a team of 20 people managing this fault tolerant framework for the entire organization.

为什么超大厂还有定价空间？

Kongwen： Why is there room for hyperscalers to price a lot smaller on GPUs, orchestration software, storage, support? Why are they not instantly competed out by gold tier or silver tier providers?

Jordan： In some ways they are competed out. Look at how successful CoreWeave, Nebius, Crusoe, Lambda have been at selling all their new GPU capacity. They're sold out for the near future. They're getting long-term five-year committed contracts measured in the billions of dollars.

That's a lesson the hyperscalers have learned: people don't need expensive feature-rich storage options. They just need S3 to work or a file system to work. They don't want to be nickeled and dimed for every compute node and dashboard and support ticket. They just want a good experience.

But on the flip side, hyperscalers have also sold plenty of GPUs at relatively high margins. Their revenues are increasing, gross margins are increasing.

It's hard to draw conclusions about clear winners and losers in the Neo Cloud industry right now when demand is so high. That masks over a lot of issues — Neo Clouds with poor reliability who lost customers have been able to find replacement customers because demand is so high. Hyperscalers pricing too high have seen people buying at high prices because they can't get GPUs anywhere else.

And you're always going to have peace of mind that AWS or Google or Azure knows how to run a clean data center.

Ben： A rising tide lifts all boats. Right now, demand is so strong, you can charge whatever you want.

Jordan： For now.

这一次真的不一样吗？Zane 加入

Ben： Are you calling the top sometime soon, Kongwen, or you riding it out with us? Is this time different?

Kongwen： This time is different.

Ben： That's our favorite question. Speaking of "this time is different," I think we have some interesting evidence why this time really is different.

Zane 的私募股权背景

Ben： Zane has appeared. Cute graphic. Zane, you came from private equity. Equity research is arduous — I'm not sure what we would call private equity.

Zane： For sure. Arduous, and there's a lot of back and forth — it's an iterative process. One boss says one instruction set, you go and carry that out, and then go back to the same boss or a different boss.

In private equity it's like a thousand-and-nine models, 50 sheets — granular is another word to describe it.

Agented Director of Research：AI 与人类成本对比

Ben： From last week, you'll know at SemiAnalysis we have an Agented Director of Research. Zane, can you talk us through this table?

Zane： This table is broadly the cost for AI to do one of our real workflows versus humans. When we say "AI just like token cost," of course we're not saying we'll just fully throw it to cloud and not do anything. For us to supervise, throw in a proper prompt, and look at the results — this is roughly the cost and time for tokens versus humans.

案例：初始覆盖 HPE

The fourth item — initiating on a new company, in this case HPE (Hewlett Packard Enterprise), looking at their roadmap and balance sheet: - Token cost in cloud: ~$50 - Human cost (assuming $50/hr): easily 40 hours going through annuals, quarterlies, earnings call scripts, presentations - ROI (human/token): ~36×

案例：财报回顾（CXL 与 CPU）

Modeling earnings recap for up-and-coming technologies — CXL memory switching and CPU: - Token side: $2.82 - Human cost in person: ~$16

案例：拉取过去年度财务数据

Pulling past annual financials and year-to-date financials — 5 years plus 3-4 quarters. Mainly a data entry job, making sure stuff lines up: - Human analyst: ~3 hours - AI: ~3 minutes and $1.87 in token costs

We're not trying to eliminate. We're trying to refocus their time on stuff that's not data entry, not Control-F on transcripts. We're trying to spend our time on higher value-add stuff and cover more ground because there's always a lot of companies you could have been tracking but aren't because it's too costly.

价值流向：Anthropic 与推理提供商

Ben： Let's talk about how the value's also accrued. There's multiple layers. Kongwen, can you talk a little bit about how Anthropic and the inference providers have actually picked up a lot of value?

Kongwen： A pretty clear trend how Anthropic ARR has accelerated. Anthropic reached $30 billion ARR over the last couple of months. This margin will continue to accrue to Anthropic as they provide the application layer, what we are directly benefiting from. We forecast they can go up to almost $300 billion in revenue at 51% gross margins. This seems like a pretty good business to me.

Ben： What's really driving the margin improvement is the fact that throughput has been increasing continuously — whether through software, whether through better hardware — but they haven't been decreasing prices. They've just been offering more expensive stuff the whole time.

Generation to generation, things have improved so much. We've also talked about how goodput has been improving.

系统提供商在分到公平的价值吗？

Ben： Are the system providers capturing their fair share of value?

Kongwen： That's a nuanced question considering we're still quite early in the agentic AI revolution. We were talking with friends on Telegram — they're saying the banks aren't using AI at all. But from our vantage point, it almost looks like a no-brainer to be using AI.

For now, if profit pools keep accruing to inference providers, it only makes sense to release back to the upper end of the spectrum — OEMs, system integrators, hardware providers, foundry equipment makers. It's important for them to move up.

TCO per PFLOP 散点图：VR 是异常值

Ben： Let's look at the scatter plot. This is the system pricing — and what do you think of the VR pricing?

Kongwen： This trend line shows the cost per flop across different generations when released. H100 released in December '22 had a TCO per PFLOP of about 80 cents. Most GPUs cluster tightly around this line — until VR.

VR seems to be a bit of an anomaly compared to the trends we expect. It's fallen off quite sharply in TCO per PFLOP, way below the trend line. Even if you give it a 40% hike on the server price that we are modeling today, it seems to just barely reach the trend line. That's another argument that maybe the hardware should be accruing more of this value.

但实际收益远超 flops 提升

Jordan： The big thing you guys are talking about here is the denominator is flops. And of course flops are improving, but the concept of inference acts is that the amount of useful work you get out of GPUs is exponentially more than the flops increase.

If flops go up 2×, we're not seeing throughput go up 2×. We're seeing in some cases it go up 15× generation to generation because of all of the improvements beyond flops: - Flops improvements - Efficiency improvements on the interconnect network — 18× faster interconnect with NVLink going to 72 GPUs instead of just 8 - Use of prefill/decode disaggregation - Use of YDP - Use of multi-token prediction

All of this software has been optimized for Blackwell first because it's the latest and greatest. It gets even better — or worse, depending on your perspective — if you consider useful work on a benchmark of tokens per second instead of just flops.

Ben： I totally agree. The reason we did flops is because we don't yet have inference acts for VR. We could proxy it with memory bandwidth. You'd have to make this a log axis — that's how fast the improvements in inference are. Whatever point we're making here, it's going to be even more powerful. There's even more left on the table from system vendors.

租金视角的散点图

Kongwen： This chart is similar but from the TCO perspective — rental price per PFLOP. This illustrates how inference providers renting compute would think about what they'd be willing to pay.

Older Nvidia GPUs scatter tightly around the trend line — P100, B200s, B300s, GB300s. But VR stands out as a very unusual outlier.

  • GB300s today: deployed at current market rate with current server cost → 15.3% IRR
  • VR at the same 15.3% IRR over 5 years: implies a rental cost per PFLOP all the way down at about 28 cents per PFLOP — way below the trend line
  • VR with 40% server price hike: ~40 cents per PFLOP
  • VR with server price hike and more than doubled rental: 38% IRR — still falls below the rental price per PFLOP trend

"One Chart to Rule Them All"

Ben： What we did is we actually combined this into a framework — what we call "one chart to rule them all."

There's two constraints: a lower bound and upper bound.

上界

Upper bound: If you're renting a GB300, you're paying a rental price per flop of 70 cents. You're never going to pay more for a VR. A VR has 17,500 petaflops (sic — teraflops of FP8 dense). That works out to about $12, about 70 cents per flop. You never pay more than that.

下界

Lower bound: A Neo Cloud is never going to put in a VR system if it's not above the hurdle rate.

The orange curve actually forms the supply curve in a way (technically not a supply curve because there's no quantity). It shows the variety of pricing and the IRR. - X-axis: IRR - Sliding up and to the right along the orange curve → price is higher, IRR for the Neo Cloud is higher → stronger pricing power - Sliding down and to the left → weaker pricing power

隐含的第三维：服务器价格

There's an implicit third dimension — the different curves. The gray line is if there's a 40% hike in server price — the curve shifts up and down based on server price changes.

When server price changes, for a given 15% IRR, you have to charge more. At a 40% hike, a Neo Cloud has to charge $6 to make the same IRR. But even then it's still well below trend in flops — and probably much further below trend on an inference throughput basis.

The gap between the top left corner and the orange chart is the value that the server provider is leaving on the table.

Ben 的纠结：80× ROI 反过来支持涨价

Ben： I almost think we should maybe edit this out or just not release this because we certainly like our 80× ROI. So we're almost arguing against this — but I think we can take a 50% higher pricing because the ROI is just so high. There's so much value being created. There's so much talked about memory price increase, but think about all the value that's being created.

Jordan, is this a confusing chart? It takes a little while to understand.

Jordan： I've been so conditioned for the last few months. I would stress to everybody looking at this that they're making the point based on rental price per petaflop, ignoring all the improvements of inference that have come with the latest generation.

If you just do this chip by chip, the result is a few different options: 1. Jensen is feeling really, really benevolent. 2. Jensen is really, really scared about his competition. 3. Somebody doesn't really understand what they're doing on the financial side.

None of those three make sense to me. People are going to look at this and see Groq was almost at $1/hour just 6 months ago, $8/hour GPUs — they're going to visually look at that and not compute the fact that a GPU is so much more expensive from a Neo Cloud. But then they'll turn around and see Anthropic gross margins improving, Neo Cloud gross margins improving, and people funding a whole bunch of projects with a ton of capex.

V for Value：Vera Rubin 的命名

Jordan： Do you want to motivate Jensen to hike the price even higher? Is that a good result for you, Dan?

Ben： Well, if Blackwell was B for benevolent, then is it V for valiant? No — hike the prices. It's V for value.

Jordan： V for value, okay. Yeah, actually V for value.

Ben： That's a good soundbite. I like V for value.

Jordan： Jensen will spin it as being benevolent, to be honest. And I think that's the closest to the truth. The guy is VR. He's very rich. I don't know how much more — that's another good soundbite. I don't know how much more money he needs to be making.

Ben： That explains it actually, Jordan. He's already VR.

Jordan： Yeah, it's already VR, man.

Jensen 为什么没涨价？三种解读

I think it's some combination of: 1. Not understanding the financials with the pricing — not pricing to value, instead pricing to component inputs and doing a 75% markup standard. They do it for every component — doesn't matter if it's a transceiver with no value or a GPU with all the value, they're all marked up the same. 2. He's more concerned about TPU and Trainium being used to power two of the best models in the world in Claude and Gemini than he really would like to admit. Those are serious players right now. 3. If he does jack the price and everybody else doesn't, in some world 12 to 18 months from now where there is a lack of scarcity and an abundance of compute options, people will run to the ones cheapest per flop or per bit per second on memory bandwidth or per token per second on a real benchmark.

I think Jensen regrets pushing Anthropic into the welcoming arms of AWS with Trainium — that collaboration really resulted in a world-class Trainium chip that can run the biggest models in the world and others can use. TPU is already world-class. Doesn't intentionally do that to anybody else in the future when you've got that. And there's pressure from Chinese chips. He's already admitted he missed something by not acquiring Groq and doing the SRAM thing — which we're not even considering here with the token-for-value stuff.

These are unstructured thoughts at the end of the podcast.

收尾

Ben： Dan says Jensen jacked the price. All Neo Clouds who receive pricing from Jensen after today on, please contact dan@semianalysis.com to complain.

We've got "your call is important to us" — we call it like we see it.

Press 1 to request a callback. All operators are currently experiencing high call volume. Press 2 to file a Jira ticket.

All right, back to the phone lines, everyone.

Ben： So let's sign off. Jordan, unless there's anything else you want to add?

Jordan： No, this has been great, guys. I'm excited to see the article in full now after seeing this. Hopefully we can publish both of these roughly around the same time.

Ben： Should we literally title it "Why This Time Is Different"? Or no — V for value. We're going to title it like Vera Rubin, V for value.

Yes — Vera Rubin is V for Value. Vera is V for Value. Not Vera and compares to CPU. Vera, no.

All right. So, signing off. Thanks everyone for joining us this week. We will catch you next week.

Jordan： Catch you on the next one, guys. Good job.

Bye. Bye.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的"GPU 通胀税"：你买的不是算力，是概率
表面上 Neo Cloud 在卖 GPU 小时，实质上他们在卖**故障概率分布的右尾**。文章里最反直觉的数字是：silver tier 与 gold tier 的总成本差距中，**92% 来自 goodput 而不是表面的 GPU 单价**。换句话说，当你在比价表上看到便宜 5% 的 GPU 时，你实际上是在用 20%+ 的"看不见的损失"来交换那 5% 的"看得见的便宜"——这是一种典型的 Akerlof 式柠檬市场，但比二手车市场更阴险，因为故障是统计性的、滞后显现的、且在大集群上**非线性放大**（一颗 GPU 挂掉等于整个 4000 卡训练任务停摆）。
跨领域连接：这与航空业的"按时率"经济学惊人相似。廉价航空的票面价格便宜 30%，但延误的隐性成本（误工、转机、心理损耗）让总成本反超传统航司。Neo Cloud 行业正在经历航空业 1980 年代刚解除管制时的同一阶段——价格透明化先行，**质量透明化（ClusterMax 排名）滞后但即将引爆分层**。
## 二、Vera Rubin 是英伟达定价模型的"哥

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Baseten CEO Tuhin Srivastava on Custom Models, and Building the Inference Cloud
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：中国开源模型，本质上是中国政府对美国企业的"隐性补贴"

访谈中最被低估的洞见来自 Aladdin 那段几乎被礼貌性带过的评论——当中国国家资本补贴 DeepSeek、Moonshot、Qwen 等模型的训练成本，而这些权重被免费释放给全球时，真正的受益者是采用这些模型的美国企业。这构成了一个奇特的"反向重商主义"：传统贸易战中，补贴是为了倾销本国商品挤压对手；但在智能这一新型生产要素上，补贴方反而把"剩余价值"转移给了对手国的应用层。

跨领域连接：这非常像1970-80 年代日本对半导体 DRAM 的国家补贴——当时美国痛失 DRAM 产业，但同时美国 PC 厂商（Dell、Compaq、Apple）享受了人类历史上最便宜的内存，从而催生了整个 PC 革命与软件帝国。今天的开源模型，可能正在为下一轮"美国应用层霸权"提供同样的燃料。问题不在"中国模型是否安全"，而在于：当智能本身被补贴时，价值会沿着供应链向哪一层迁移？答案是：向最贴近用户信号的那一层。

二、Tuhin 隐藏的"反摩尔定律"：post-training 与 inference 正在合并为同一个问题

表面上这是一个"我们收购了一个研究团队"的常规叙事。但仔细听 Tuhin 的话——"how you train the model affects how you need to quantize for inference"——他实际上在宣告一个范式转移：训练与推理的边界正在溶解。

这件事的深层意义被低估了。过去十年云计算的胜利逻辑是"专业化分工"（训练归训练云、推理归推理云、存储归存储云）。但 Baseten 看到的未来恰恰相反——当 RL post-training 用推理时产生的 reward signal 反哺模型，inference cluster 本身就变成了一个永不停歇的训练厂。这意味着"推理云"这个词的定义将在 2-3 年内失效，取而代之的是某种"持续学习基础设施" (Continuous Learning Infrastructure)。

跨领域连接：这非常类似生物学中神经系统的演化——大脑的"推理"（信号传递）和"学习"（突触可塑性）从来不是分开的两个系统，它们是同一个过程的两个面。云计算正在从"机械式分工"演化为"神经式融合"。Baseten 收购研究团队不是补短板，而是在押注：未来五年里，把 inference 和 post-training 拆开运营的公司，会像把"思考"和"记忆"拆开运营的大脑一样不可能存在。

三、被所有人忽视的资本结构革命：软件公司被迫学会"重资产融资"

这一段几乎是被快速带过的——Tuhin 提到 "we're software people and we don't pick like this... our business has very interesting working capital requirements"——但这是整个访谈最具结构性意义的转折信号。

SaaS 时代的全部融资逻辑（高毛利、轻资产、高 ARR 倍数估值）正在被 inference cloud 这种新物种打破。当一个 1000 张 B200 的合同需要 3-5 年期、20-30% 预付的承诺时，Baseten 这类公司本质上正在变成"披着软件皮的电力公司 / 航空公司"——它们的核心能力不再只是写代码，而是资本配置的速度与精度。

最反直觉的推论：这会倒逼一批 AI infra 公司提前 IPO，不是为了套现或品牌，而是为了降低资本成本以赢得算力期货市场。Tuhin 几乎承认了这一点（"I think you'd go sooner"）。

跨领域连接：这让我想起 20 世纪初铁路公司的演化路径——它们最初被视为"运输服务公司"，但很快人们意识到真正的护城河是对土地、债券和长期固定资产的金融工程能力。最终，胜出的不是技术最好的铁路，而是资本结构最聪明的铁路。今天的 inference cloud 正站在同一个十字路口：未来的赢家可能不是 GPU 优化最强的团队，而是最先把自己重新设计成"AI 时代的伯克希尔"的那家公司。

附加冷观察

Tuhin 说 "no one wants a vegan inference"（没人想要纯素推理）——这句玩笑实际上隐藏了他对 NVIDIA 生态的真实判断：chip 的多样化只会发生在"特定 decode 任务"这样的边缘地带，而通用推理在可见的未来仍是 CUDA 的领地。这与市场上主流的"NVIDIA 必将被颠覆"叙事形成了一个有趣的、来自最贴近真实算力供应链的人的反对票。

📝 转录文本

Baseten CEO Tuhin Srivastava on Custom Models, and Building the Inference Cloud

开场介绍

Hi listeners, today Aladdin and I are here with Tuhin Stuba, the founder and CEO of B 10, the AI inference cloud. We're here to talk about capacity constraints for AI compute, why inference is the last market, how the workload is changing, the open-source and perhaps multi-chip future, and what 30x scale in a year looks like. Tuhan, welcome back.

Hi. Great to see it. Thanks for having me.

一年 30 倍增长背后的市场逻辑

All right. You are in one of the uh craziest markets, AI inference. Uh uh it's very important. There's a lot going on. You guys have grown 30x over the last year. And I think I can say you're expecting to do more than a billion dollars in revenue this year.

Mhm.

What's going on? Tell us about scale.

Yeah. No, it's been it's been nuts. I I think what's happened over the last honestly at 24 months but just kind of keeps getting bigger and bigger is that I think everyone is re realizing that you can put AI everywhere. Um you have you have all these great options available from closed source to open source models. the open source models have c crossed some sort of chasm in terms of their baseline capability and that I think RL techniques and post- training is um for specialized models um has become mainstream enough and you know there's enough examples of it work of it working the customers realizing they can you know kind of own their inference um more and more and what that's meant for us is um more you know the longtail models coming true. Customers in a lot of that intelligence themselves and as the application layer just gets you know bigger and bigger and bigger and that's growing when we are we are just someone index on that and we've been around to be able to collect the demand.

应用层是否还有独立存在的空间？

There's an existential question in here that I think everybody is uh continually asking of does the independent application layer get to exist at all versus the labs like how do you you have to believe this why do you believe it?

Yeah, look, I I I think it'd be it'd be a sad thing if it didn't exist in general, and I think that's like my but you know, sadness is fine. Um the all the time sadness is fine. Um but like that's not the reason why I think the application layer will exist.

I think the application layer will exist for a number of reasons. One is because you know I think this idea um that what is what is valuable to a company um is you know the the user signal that they can gather that only they can gather um and to the extent that that is encoded um in a model I think a lot of their business will um be at risk but to the to the extent that it is encoded in workflows um that is where they will be able to develop notes.

So a good I think a good example of that is say a company like a bridge where the clinicians edits of the notes and what they do with those notes after the fact and the um the thing that happens in um inside the EMR three steps down and that becomes a workflow that only

can you explain what a bridge does?

Sorry. So bridge is a um ambient um scribe um that is used by physicians in um like you know almost all hospitals in the US. I think large investor um great chef's amazing great great company great team um great product um and you know they they've basically uh you know got this very very deep integration into into hospitals into clinician workflows and my argument would be here is that actually you know it's very very hard for um a frontier model company to be able to eat away at that because they just don't have access to that user signal and what will happen over time is the folks who have access to that user signal can start to post-train models on that reward signal and and start to get long long horizon agentic models running that and I think to the extent that that is possible and that signal is differentiated and unique and in and is um somewhat um rare to to get access to there will be an application layer.

And I think you know like support company is another example of that where you know a support a support task isn't oneshotted Usually at a company like Ban when a ticket comes in there's like what like 1 2 10 20 actions that get taken and that is where you know someone can develop a specialized model.

AI 原生应用 vs. 企业自建：市场结构

So there's almost two versions of this then there's new companies like a bridge or decagon or some of these other things that you mentioned that are doing

these new types of applications that are using AI and they sell it to customers.

The other is enterprises building things inhouse or building their own models. What proportion of the market today do you think is um these new application companies versus enterprises just adopting AI?

Yeah. And how do you think that looks in a couple years?

Yeah, I I think that's a that's you we did I think you asked me the same question two years ago on

I had to be repetitive. Yeah.

It it is crazy consistent.

The answer is just that it's crazy that the answer is still I think I think if you look by inference count

Yeah.

it'd be 99% the full.

Yeah. Um I think that is that kind of represents the scope of the opportunity here is that the majority of the market hasn't come online and and and um and added AI into the

Yeah. most of enterprise adoption is well ahead of us and I think that's one of the very exciting things about AI.

Yeah.

There's just so much still to come and people are underestimating that I think

100%. And but but what's cool is that we're seeing the transition happen. Right. Before it was like hey are they are they using AI tools?

I don't think that was immediately obvious two years ago. I think it's obvious now that yes they are. are they using closed source model APIs? Um I think they're starting to get there. I think once you do that and then you kind of see what is possible then comes the whole custom model adoption. I think that is all that is ahead of us

today.

服务前沿客户的策略：Stripe 模式

So if the majority of your customer base today is as you described the the former like application companies AI natives the um fast growing I mean some of them are at considerable scale now like the a bridge cursor um

open evidence

open evidences of the world. uh what you know what do they what do they teach you? What does that push the company to do? How do you think about serving them versus evolving for the enterprise?

Yeah. Um I I think firstly like you just learn a lot by building with the companies greatest scale doing the most interesting things.

Um we we think of it two ways like I think there's like the the the most obvious way which is just build for the highest scale. um you know most uh the customers that will push you the most from technologically and everything kind of will fall into play. I think the Stripe evolution as a company showed that we're like Stripe now like serves like so many enterprises but 12 years ago that wasn't the case but they just built for the frontier and kind of went with them.

Um I think the second way we think about that this is to just think about building for companies that are serving enterprises. So yes we don't serve the enterprise but our customers serve enterprises. Um a bridge service open evidence decagon um all these writer gamma all these clay all all these companies serve enterprises in mass and what we actually get like a translation of the requirements from them which is like you know they're like hey we need this data retention we need this type this where models need to be deployed um this is the types of GPUs or the latencies they're okay with this is the model requirements from like a transparency perspective that they care about and so I think that is actually the more nuance answer.

It's that if you listen to what their needs are, we actually get a full translation of what the enterprise would require. Like I would say that by serving companies like a bridge and open evidence, we're probably pretty well suited to go serve the healthare system given that they are selling and latent health given that they are selling to them.

开源模型的演化与中国模型的崛起

How much of a shift are you seeing in terms of the types of open source models that are being used? And so I think we've seen an evolution where two three years ago I think the main thing was kind of mistral and then a few other things and then meta kind of came along with llama and then it kind of really shifted

in terms of the misperformant models or of Chinese origin and different ways.

Do you see that sort of mix reflected in terms of what's being used by our customers? Yeah.

Yeah. I I I think customers at least the customers we are serving are very and these are like the fastest growing AI companies in the world that are very forward thinking. they they want to use the best model and they they are optimizing.

I think there is there there are a there's a subset of tasks which I think is small today where people really start to start with cost.

Mhm.

Um but everyone comes from capability first because that's really where the economic growth is being unlocked where the value is being delivered and then they optimize. And I think that's like actually been, you know, and so with that in mind, you know, you you you name like you name it. Everything from GPTOSS all the way to um Moonshot model to Deep Seeks um

to um Canopy or Oreus, which is like really good text to speech

um models um

customers generally want to use whatever's at the frontier. And and I think the um the difference has just been um I think we have a lot more visibility into how to run these and how to run these really well and secondly that they're good. Now

中国模型的安全顾虑与地缘政治

there have been a number of different concerns raised about the use of Chinese models in particular security or is there something embedded in the models or you know Trojan horses or other things.

Um a do you think there's any real concern there and b you know people often talk about how there should be like US counterweights to this from a geopolitical perspective. Do you think that's something that's legitimate or something we should be worried about or how do you think about the sort of origins of these models versus their uses?

Yeah, look, I I I think these these models firstly are fantastic. They're amazing. We work with these teams. They're truly awesome.

Um, I'd say look I I don't it it is hard for me it is hard for me to see and I I could be wrong but like you know if if I if I if I network bound these models that they're not magically you know going to be able to cross those network boundaries and so data is there and you know I I don't and we I've never seen any real evidence um except from some very early models that I think people picked up on very quickly that there is some agenda or bias built opportunities.

Um I do think that to some um to some extent is I I think there is importance to the US that we develop our own models. I think that that would be a massive loss if that there are five companies, you know, five different labs in China that are creating open source models. Um and we're struggling to get one stood up. So it's necessary.

Um I also think it's inevitable. Um, and you know like the to deepse the deepseek moment a year ago. Um, I remember someone saying to me and I thought it was like very well said which is like and the world's changed a lot but they said hey you know we should kind of just forget

that this is a Chinese model. We should just act like this came from

from meta and and build and build with that in mind.

It's like you know I think you're kind of missing the forest from the trees.

Like there's two there's two scenarios, right? either America does not ever come up with good open source models.

I think there's probably a fundamental problem there or we will get there and we need to be ready for that world.

Yeah, that makes sense. It's interesting because um you know like you I I think it's very important for the US to have a strong open- source footprint here.

Um at least for now it looks like effectively the Chinese government is subsidizing at least a large subset of these models and that subsidy or surplus is effectively just being passed on to US enterprises who are adopting these models. In other words, it's a way for the Chinese government to effectively subsidize US enterprise.

Yeah.

In an indirect manner. And I think that's a little bit lost right now. Um but you know, it's always interesting to weigh that against some of the other concerns that are raised. So I appreciate your your comments on this.

Well, and I think the concern also just there just becomes it's like what happens if we aren't able to like I if it is fun like I I think if you think of the economics here which is deepseek by most deepseek is a a very good model

um you know like and like you can argue whether it's at the absolute frontier or not but like let's let's go back three months

and it's there and so think about everything and we're doing a whole lot of things three months ago and so let's just think about that well um

you know if you could run deepseek probably 20% of the cost

of running openthropic models um in production um with comparable better latency probably better reliability if we don't have access to that intelligence

in that form I think it's just a massive loss um and as a country like we won't be able to innovate as fast because like the cost of intelligence going down control of intelligence what we have seen just means more intelligence intelligence being embedded in more places

yeah an important note uh here that we didn't mention explicitly is that the state-of-the-art models the ones that are most far ahead on the frontier are actually still the closed source anthropic open AI Google etc.

Workload 构成：95% 是定制模型

Yeah. What has been actually maybe you can just characterize like workload a little bit like how of tokens being served on base 10 like how many of them are uh from custom models of some kind versus like vanilla open source today

it is all custom it's basically

so like 95% plus

95% like and I think that's really cool to be honest like look we have we have two businesses we have we have we have three business we have we have three businesses right now um and help you No, no.

So we have like dedicated dedicated inference which is basically custom model inference. Um your SLA is your SLA. Then we have shared inference which is shared inference endpoint. Um shared SLAs's. Um and then we have a training business.

Um um I'd say 95% of the tokens today are on the the the first business and almost all of them um there's probably a yeah for almost all of them the customer is making some modifications to the model with their with their own data um specialized for the use case and I think what's even more important is um they might be compiling in different ways no one is just running the vanilla open source weights like you you might be customizing it for quality but you also might be customizing it for performance.

You made an acquisition of a research team a few months ago. You've mentioned uh post- trainining customization. Uh what was the rationale behind the acquisition? What is that team doing today?

Yeah. Um so the the rational around the acquisition was you know we we are infrastructure and product people. um and just as as I said in the opening statement here which is you know as more and more post train models have come up we've realized that like the the demand for people to for people to either um for software loops to do post training or for post training expertise is very high and we're really really investing um in that

Um but yeah that that's been fantastic. They're working with all sorts of customers. Um and it it's also very interesting when you when you um start, you know, we were doing a lot of research on the performance side and less so on the post-raining side.

Um it's interesting as we've started to do a lot more research on the post training side. Um, you start to see how linked inference and post- training are and like you know even even when you think about stuff like quantization and when you should do that and like you know h how how training um how how you train the model affects how you need to quantize for inference and how paired these problems are

um has become like very apparent and more and more like the post training inference are kind of both sides of the same problem. So because inference will ideally will be get more post training where inference creates data you do eval post post train on the um on that reward function that you that you found of those evals and and hopefully to set up the entire look.

何时该投资定制模型？

Plenty of folks from uh Ant and Open AI Sam Greg etc have said in recent months that like inference is super strategic inference talent is strategic capacity is strategic. Uh so between that and post-training these are uh very uh difficult to gather like capabilities.

Yeah.

Um I imagine that lots of your customers go to you guys for advice on like how to do this progression of moving to custom models. Like what do you tell people about the life cycle and when they should invest in that?

Yeah, I I think it's hey go find go prove to yourself with the best-in-class model that you have something worth optimizing.

Um and and I think you know a lot of you know if a customer comes to us you there was that meme which was like it was like two years ago it feels like no GPUs pre-product market fit. It's like no post training pre-product market fit is what is what I'd say.

So people that you're working with here are very very at scale first.

Yeah, they they they have a user signal that they know how to optimize and they've shown that they can, you know, they can serve customer value and that value and that they have something special around that value and once you have that value, it's like, okay, now how can I do that better, faster, and cheaper with the idea being that, hey, if you need to be very good at customer support, you can you maybe don't need to be that good at coding and that a specialized model might be a better fit for that problem and you can do it better, faster, cheaper.

What about the capacity side? you uh started with you unifying capacity across all the clouds and neoclouds. How do you think about this when everybody keeps talking about a a supply crunch and a multi-year supply crunch?

I think um you know there's so much narrative around the supply crunch. Um and no matter like as much as we hear about it, I don't think people realize how bad it really is. like there is you know there is very very little slack compute available like you know we run pretty large clusters ourselves and we run them at like uncomfortably high utilization you know we when I'm saying we're like mid90s utilization

um most of the time um there is we have made we we have we sit in 18 different clouds now we have 90 clusters around the world across 18 different clouds and like you Initially we started we like built this technology to be able to like kind of create one runtime fabric that spans all these different clouds and try to abstract that away from our customers um as a way to think about reliability, latency, failover, all these things that we think are going to be very important for very mission critical use cases.

um that same technology like just our ability to get compute wherever humanly possible um has been really really helpful in our ability to get supply and and what what I mean by that is we can be introduced to a new provider in a different country um and have it up and running with the whole base 10 inference stack

as part of the fabric. Yeah,

fabric in half day half maybe less. uh um even for and that gives us enormous flexibility.

Um even for us it is hard for us to grow. We have a we have a I think it's yeah say that um we have a a 4M standing meeting for the company where we basically like like how do we like how do we how do we manage capacity for the demand right now I think the second part which people don't really um the two the the second part that people don't really understand is um that there are also a lot of um suppliers right now um that It's kind of grifty, you know, like I I I think, you know, they haven't run they haven't run data centers before, you know, they don't understand SLAs's for especially for inference.

Um, and so like, you know, even when there is capacity available, um, there's a lot of D like there's probably we we run a lot more and we have redundancy so it's fine. But if you, you know, there's probably like a dozen good like clouds and I'd probably like put like three or four of them in like the the gold tier.

Mhm.

Um, and I think that just means that like supply like not only are we supply crunched, we're supplier and operationally crunched onto people who can who can run these data centers as well.

it's crazy. It's four years, four and a half years old. The price is going up still.

Yeah.

and I think there was demand for that. Capacity. Um I I think you know
